Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A semantic-structure regularized unsupervised domain adaptation method for driving scene parsing that uses class prototypes, entropy-based pseudo-label filtering, and pixel-level attention to improve cross-domain performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the synthetic-to-real domain gap in driving scene parsing and the limited modeling of semantic relationships in existing UDA methods.

Method: Introduce a framework that enforces inter-class separation and intra-class compactness via class-specific prototypes, employs entropy-based noise filtering for pseudo labels, and uses pixel-level attention to refine feature alignment.

Result: Empirical results on standard benchmarks show consistent improvements over recent state-of-the-art methods, validating the effectiveness of preserving semantic structure during adaptation.

Conclusion: Preserving semantic structure in the feature space is crucial for robust synthetic-to-real adaptation in driving scene parsing and leads to more discriminative and coherent feature representations.

Abstract: Driving scene parsing is critical for autonomous vehicles to operate reliably in complex real-world traffic environments. To reduce the reliance on costly pixel-level annotations, synthetic datasets with automatically generated labels have become a popular alternative. However, models trained on synthetic data often perform poorly when applied to real-world scenes due to the synthetic-to-real domain gap. Despite the success of unsupervised domain adaptation in narrowing this gap, most existing methods mainly focus on global feature alignment while overlooking the semantic structure of the feature space. As a result, semantic relations among classes are insufficiently modeled, limiting the model's ability to generalize. To address these challenges, this study introduces a novel unsupervised domain adaptation framework that explicitly regularizes semantic feature structures to significantly enhance driving scene parsing performance in real-world scenarios. Specifically, the proposed method enforces inter-class separation and intra-class compactness by leveraging class-specific prototypes, thereby enhancing the discriminability and structural coherence of feature clusters. An entropy-based noise filtering strategy improves the reliability of pseudo labels, while a pixel-level attention mechanism further refines feature alignment. Extensive experiments on representative benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods. These results underscore the importance of preserving semantic structure for robust synthetic-to-real adaptation in driving scene parsing tasks.

</details>


### [27] [Interact3D: Compositional 3D Generation of Interactive Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16085)
*Hui Shan,Keyang Luo,Ming Li,Sizhe Zheng,Yanwei Fu,Zhen Chen,Xiangru Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Interact3D introduces a two-stage 3D composition pipeline to generate physically plausible interacting 3D objects from single images. It uses generative priors to assemble a unified scene, a global-to-local registration to anchor the main object, a differentiable SDF-based optimization to merge additional objects while penalizing intersections, and a VLM-guided closed-loop refinement to iteratively correct errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of assembling 3D compositional objects from single images under occlusion, where hidden regions lose geometric detail and object-object spatial relationships are not preserved.

Method: 1) Generate high-quality individual assets with a unified 3D guidance scene via generative priors. 2) Two-stage composition: (a) anchor the primary object with global-to-local registration; (b) integrate subsequent geometries using differentiable Signed Distance Field (SDF) optimization with explicit collision penalties. 3) Closed-loop refinement using a Vision-Language Model (VLM) that analyzes multi-view renderings, emits corrective prompts, and guides an image-editing module to iteratively refine the pipeline.

Result: Extensive experiments show Interact3D achieves collision-aware compositions with improved geometric fidelity and consistent object-object spatial relationships compared to baselines.

Conclusion: Interact3D provides a robust, end-to-end framework combining generative priors, differentiable geometry optimization, and VLM-driven refinement to produce physically plausible interacting 3D objects from single images, particularly under occlusion.

Abstract: Recent breakthroughs in 3D generation have enabled the synthesis of high-fidelity individual assets. However, generating 3D compositional objects from single images--particularly under occlusions--remains challenging. Existing methods often degrade geometric details in hidden regions and fail to preserve the underlying object-object spatial relationships (OOR). We present a novel framework Interact3D designed to generate physically plausible interacting 3D compositional objects. Our approach first leverages advanced generative priors to curate high-quality individual assets with a unified 3D guidance scene. To physically compose these assets, we then introduce a robust two-stage composition pipeline. Based on the 3D guidance scene, the primary object is anchored through precise global-to-local geometric alignment (registration), while subsequent geometries are integrated using a differentiable Signed Distance Field (SDF)-based optimization that explicitly penalizes geometry intersections. To reduce challenging collisions, we further deploy a closed-loop, agentic refinement strategy. A Vision-Language Model (VLM) autonomously analyzes multi-view renderings of the composed scene, formulates targeted corrective prompts, and guides an image editing module to iteratively self-correct the generation pipeline. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Interact3D successfully produces promising collsion-aware compositions with improved geometric fidelity and consistent spatial relationships.

</details>


### [28] [Parallel In-context Learning for Large Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16092)
*Shin'ya Yamaguchi,Daiki Chijiwa,Tamao Sakao,Taku Hasegawa*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Parallel-ICL enables near full-context MM-ICL performance with significant speedups by parallelizing chunked demonstrations and fusing logits via a weighted PoE, guided by diversity and relevance-based chunking/weighting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate the quadratic complexity of Transformer attention with long context in multi-modal in-context learning, which degrades inference speed as more demonstrations are added.

Method: Divide the long demonstration context into multiple short chunks, process them in parallel, and fuse their outputs at the logit level using a weighted Product-of-Experts ensemble. Two principled strategies guide chunking: (1) clustering-based context chunking to maximize inter-chunk diversity, and (2) similarity-based weighting to emphasize chunks more relevant to the query.

Result: Experiments on VQA, image captioning, and classification show that Parallel-ICL achieves performance comparable to full-context MM-ICL while significantly reducing inference time.

Conclusion: Parallel-ICL provides an effective solution to the accuracy-efficiency trade-off in MM-ICL, enabling dynamic task adaptation with substantially reduced inference overhead.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) employ multi-modal in-context learning (MM-ICL) to adapt to new tasks by leveraging demonstration examples. While increasing the number of demonstrations boosts performance, they incur significant inference latency due to the quadratic computational cost of Transformer attention with respect to the context length. To address this trade-off, we propose Parallel In-Context Learning (Parallel-ICL), a plug-and-play inference algorithm. Parallel-ICL partitions the long demonstration context into multiple shorter, manageable chunks. It processes these chunks in parallel and integrates their predictions at the logit level, using a weighted Product-of-Experts (PoE) ensemble to approximate the full-context output. Guided by ensemble learning theory, we introduce principled strategies for Parallel-ICL: (i) clustering-based context chunking to maximize inter-chunk diversity and (ii) similarity-based context compilation to weight predictions by query relevance. Extensive experiments on VQA, image captioning, and classification benchmarks demonstrate that Parallel-ICL achieves performance comparable to full-context MM-ICL, while significantly improving inference speed. Our work offers an effective solution to the accuracy-efficiency trade-off in MM-ICL, enabling dynamic task adaptation with substantially reduced inference overhead.

</details>


### [29] [LICA: Layered Image Composition Annotations for Graphic Design Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16098)
*Elad Hirsch,Shubham Yadav,Mohit Garg,Purvanshi Mehta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Large-scale, multi-layer graphic design dataset (LICA) with rich per-element metadata and a video extension, enabling structure-based design understanding and generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond pixel-based models toward structured, layer-aware understanding and generation of graphic layouts; provide large-scale, richly annotated data that capture real-world design structures.

Method: Dataset comprises 1,550,244 multi-layer compositions with typed components (text, image, vector, group) and per-element metadata (geometry, typography, opacity, visibility); 971,850 templates; across 20 design categories. A new graphic design video dataset with 27,261 animated layouts, per-component keyframes, and motion parameters. Proposes tasks such as layer-aware inpainting, structured layout generation, controlled design editing, and temporally-aware generative modeling; emphasizes models that operate on design structure rather than pixels.

Result: A large, richly annotated dataset and accompanying video collection enabling research on structured design tasks; broad category and template coverage; establishes baselines and directions for layer-aware and temporally-aware generation.

Conclusion: LICA establishes a new paradigm for graphic design research, providing resources and tasks that support structure-centric understanding and generation of layouts and animations.

Abstract: We introduce LICA (Layered Image Composition Annotations), a large-scale dataset of 1,550,244 multi-layer graphic design compositions designed to advance structured understanding and generation of graphic layouts1. In addition to ren- dered PNG images, LICA represents each design as a hierarchical composition of typed components including text, image, vector, and group elements, each paired with rich per-element metadata such as spatial geometry, typographic attributes, opacity, and visibility. The dataset spans 20 design categories and 971,850 unique templates, providing broad coverage of real-world design structures. We further introduce graphic design video as a new and largely unexplored challenge for current vision-language models through 27,261 animated layouts annotated with per-component keyframes and motion parameters. Beyond scale, LICA establishes a new paradigm of research tasks for graphic design, enabling structured investiga- tions into problems such as layer-aware inpainting, structured layout generation, controlled design editing, and temporally-aware generative modeling. By repre- senting design as a system of compositional layers and relationships, the dataset supports research on models that operate directly on design structure rather than pixels alone.

</details>


### [30] [OneWorld: Taming Scene Generation with 3D Unified Representation Autoencoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16099)
*Sensen Gao,Zhaoqing Wang,Qihang Cao,Dongdong Yu,Changhu Wang,Tongliang Liu,Mingming Gong,Jiawang Bian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OneWorld diffuses directly in a 3D latent space using a 3D Unified Representation Autoencoder (3D-URAE) to improve cross-view consistency, enhanced by token-level Cross-View-Correspondence loss and Manifold-Drift Forcing (MDF). It outperforms 2D-based diffusion methods in cross-view fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcoming cross-view and geometric inconsistencies that arise when diffusion-based 3D generation operates in 2D latent spaces; leverage pretrained 3D foundation models to create a unified 3D latent space that captures geometry, appearance, and semantics.

Method: Introduce 3D-URAE to encode scenes into a unified 3D latent space leveraging 3D foundation models; perform diffusion directly in this 3D space; enforce cross-view structural alignment with token-level Cross-View-Correspondence (CVC) loss; mitigate train-inference exposure bias with Manifold-Drift Forcing (MDF) by mixing drifted and original representations to shape a robust 3D manifold.

Result: Extensive experiments show OneWorld achieves high-quality 3D scenes with superior cross-view consistency relative to state-of-the-art 2D-based diffusion methods.

Conclusion: Diffusing in a coherent 3D latent space with 3D-URAE, augmented by CVC and MDF, yields robust, cross-view-consistent 3D generation; code will be released.

Abstract: Existing diffusion-based 3D scene generation methods primarily operate in 2D image/video latent spaces, which makes maintaining cross-view appearance and geometric consistency inherently challenging. To bridge this gap, we present OneWorld, a framework that performs diffusion directly within a coherent 3D representation space. Central to our approach is the 3D Unified Representation Autoencoder (3D-URAE); it leverages pretrained 3D foundation models and augments their geometry-centric nature by injecting appearance and distilling semantics into a unified 3D latent space. Furthermore, we introduce token-level Cross-View-Correspondence (CVC) consistency loss to explicitly enforce structural alignment across views, and propose Manifold-Drift Forcing (MDF) to mitigate train-inference exposure bias and shape a robust 3D manifold by mixing drifted and original representations. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that OneWorld generates high-quality 3D scenes with superior cross-view consistency compared to state-of-the-art 2D-based methods. Our code will be available at https://github.com/SensenGao/OneWorld.

</details>


### [31] [Reevaluating the Intra-Modal Misalignment Hypothesis in CLIP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16100)
*Jonas Herzog,Yue Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Reexamines the alleged intra-modal misalignment in CLIP-like models. Theoretical claims show no degrees of freedom for image-embedding distances; empirical indicators yield similar results for CLIP-like and image-image models. Intra-modal task performance is driven by task ambiguity, not misalignment, per retrieval and few-shot classification analyses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenge the widely cited hypothesis that inter-modal alignment losses undermine intra-modal image representations. Assess the theoretical basis, supporting indicators, and performance metrics.

Method: Theoretical analysis arguing there are no free dimensions to vary image-image distances. Empirical comparison between language-image trained models (CLIP, SigLIP) and image-image trained models (DINO, SigLIP2) using standard intra-modal tasks (retrieval, few-shot classification) and evaluation of task ambiguity as a driver of performance.

Result: Theoretical constraints eliminate degrees of freedom for adjusting image embedding distances. Empirical indicators show similar behavior across CLIP-like and image-image training regimes. Improved intra-modal task performance aligns with mitigating task ambiguity rather than correcting misalignment.

Conclusion: Intra-modal misalignment is not the primary bottleneck for image-only tasks. Task design and disambiguation drive performance; addressing task ambiguity yields better results than focusing on inter-modal alignment alone.

Abstract: Recent research suggested that the embeddings produced by CLIP-like contrastive language-image training are suboptimal for image-only tasks. The main theory is that the inter-modal (language-image) alignment loss ignores intra-modal (image-image) alignment, leading to poorly calibrated distances between images. In this study, we question this intra-modal misalignment hypothesis. We reexamine its foundational theoretical argument, the indicators used to support it, and the performance metrics affected. For the theoretical argument, we demonstrate that there are no such supposed degrees of freedom for image embedding distances. For the empirical measures, our findings reveal they yield similar results for language-image trained models (CLIP, SigLIP) and image-image trained models (DINO, SigLIP2). This indicates the observed phenomena do not stem from a misalignment specific to the former. Experiments on the commonly studied intra-modal tasks retrieval and few-shot classification confirm that addressing task ambiguity, not supposed misalignment, is key for best results.

</details>


### [32] [NanoGS: Training-Free Gaussian Splat Simplification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16103)
*Butian Xiong,Rong Liu,Tiantian Zhou,Meida Chen,Zhiwen Fan,Andrew Feng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free, CPU-friendly NanoGS simplifies Gaussian Splat scenes by locally merging Gaussians with mass-preserving moment matching to reduce primitives while preserving appearance and structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D Gaussian Splat (3DGS) supports real-time, high-fidelity view synthesis but requires millions of splats, driving storage/transmission costs; existing compression often needs GPU-intensive, image-based optimization, hindering deployment.

Method: NanoGS performs local pairwise merging on a sparse spatial graph. It approximates two Gaussians with a single primitive via mass-preserved moment matching, evaluates a merge cost against the original mixture, restricts candidates to local neighborhoods, and efficiently selects compatible pairs. It operates directly on existing Gaussian Splat models, preserves standard 3DGS parameterization, and runs on CPU without image-based supervision.

Result: Significant reduction in primitive count with maintained rendering fidelity; effective, practical, and easy to integrate into existing rendering pipelines; demonstrated on Gaussian Splat models with CPU-based processing.

Conclusion: NanoGS offers a training-free, lightweight solution for Gaussian Splat simplification, enabling practical deployment by reducing storage/transmission needs without sacrificing appearance or structure.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splat (3DGS) enables high-fidelity, real-time novel view synthesis by representing scenes with large sets of anisotropic primitives, but often requires millions of Splats, incurring significant storage and transmission costs. Most existing compression methods rely on GPU-intensive post-training optimization with calibrated images, limiting practical deployment. We introduce NanoGS, a training-free and lightweight framework for Gaussian Splat simplification. Instead of relying on image-based rendering supervision, NanoGS formulates simplification as local pairwise merging over a sparse spatial graph. The method approximates a pair of Gaussians with a single primitive using mass preserved moment matching and evaluates merge quality through a principled merge cost between the original mixture and its approximation. By restricting merge candidates to local neighborhoods and selecting compatible pairs efficiently, NanoGS produces compact Gaussian representations while preserving scene structure and appearance. NanoGS operates directly on existing Gaussian Splat models, runs efficiently on CPU, and preserves the standard 3DGS parameterization, enabling seamless integration with existing rendering pipelines. Experiments demonstrate that NanoGS substantially reduces primitive count while maintaining high rendering fidelity, providing an efficient and practical solution for Gaussian Splat simplification. Our project website is available at https://saliteta.github.io/NanoGS/.

</details>


### [33] [PathGLS: Evaluating Pathology Vision-Language Models without Ground Truth through Multi-Dimensional Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16113)
*Minbing Chen,Zhu Meng,Fei Su*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PathGLS is a reference-free evaluation framework for pathology vision-language models, integrating Grounding, Logic, and Stability to assess fine-grained alignment, entailment consistency, and robustness to perturbations, at patch and whole-slide levels. It correlates with expert error hierarchies, detects hallucinations better than baselines, and supports private dataset benchmarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lack of reliable, automated, reference-free metrics for evaluating pathology VLMs; existing metrics fail to identify subtle failures like hallucinations and domain shifts, hindering clinical deployment.

Method: PathGLS comprises three axes: Grounding (fine-grained visual-text alignment), Logic (entailment-graph consistency via Natural Language Inference), and Stability (output variance under adversarial visual-semantic perturbations). It provides patch-level and whole-slide image (WSI) analysis and outputs a composite trust score.

Result: Empirical evaluation on Quilt-1M, TCGA, REG2025, PathMMU, and TCGA-Sarcoma demonstrates PathGLS superiority. On Quilt-1M, it reveals a 40.2% sensitivity drop for hallucinated reports (vs 2.1% for BERTScore). It achieves strong Spearman correlation with expert error hierarchies (ρ=0.71, p<0.0001), outperforming LLM-based approaches (Gemini 3.0 Pro: ρ=0.39, p<0.0001). PathGLS provides a robust, reference-free metric for benchmarking on private datasets and informing safe deployment; code available at GitHub.

Conclusion: PathGLS is a robust, reference-free evaluation framework for pathology VLMs that quantifies hallucination rates and domain shift robustness across grounding, logic, and stability, enabling safer deployment and benchmarking on private data.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) offer significant potential in computational pathology by enabling interpretable image analysis, automated reporting, and scalable decision support. However, their widespread clinical adoption remains limited due to the absence of reliable, automated evaluation metrics capable of identifying subtle failures such as hallucinations. To address this gap, we propose PathGLS, a novel reference-free evaluation framework that assesses pathology VLMs across three dimensions: Grounding (fine-grained visual-text alignment), Logic (entailment graph consistency using Natural Language Inference), and Stability (output variance under adversarial visual-semantic perturbations). PathGLS supports both patch-level and whole-slide image (WSI)-level analysis, yielding a comprehensive trust score. Experiments on Quilt-1M, TCGA, REG2025, PathMMU and TCGA-Sarcoma datasets demonstrate the superiority of PathGLS. Specifically, on the Quilt-1M dataset, PathGLS reveals a steep sensitivity drop of 40.2% for hallucinated reports compared to only 2.1% for BERTScore. Moreover, validation against expert-defined clinical error hierarchies reveals that PathGLS achieves a strong Spearman's rank correlation of $ρ=0.71$ ($p < 0.0001$), significantly outperforming Large Language Model (LLM)-based approaches (Gemini 3.0 Pro: $ρ=0.39$, $p < 0.0001$). These results establish PathGLS as a robust reference-free metric. By directly quantifying hallucination rates and domain shift robustness, it serves as a reliable criterion for benchmarking VLMs on private clinical datasets and informing safe deployment. Code can be found at: https://github.com/My13ad/PathGLS

</details>


### [34] [Out-of-Distribution Object Detection in Street Scenes via Synthetic Outlier Exposure and Transfer Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16122)
*Sadia Ilyas,Annika Mütze,Klaus Friedrichs,Thomas Kurbiel,Matthias Rottmann*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SynOE-OD uses synthetic outlier exposure to train a single detector that detects OOD objects alongside ID, leveraging Stable Diffusion and OVODs to generate semantically meaningful object-level outliers and achieve state-of-the-art AP on an OOD detection benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OOD object detection is underexplored and often misses OOD objects (treated as background). A unified ID+OOD detector is desirable but existing methods rely on complex architectures and fail to treat ID and OOD in a unified framework.

Method: Generate semantically meaningful object-level outliers via a generative model (e.g., Stable Diffusion) and OVODs to produce training data; apply transfer learning to maintain strong ID performance while incorporating OOD robustness, resulting in a single detector capable of identifying OOD objects.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art average precision on a standard OOD object detection benchmark; emphasizes that OVODs like GroundingDINO have limited zero-shot OOD detection in street scenes, which SynOE-OD improves.

Conclusion: A unified detector with synthetic outlier exposure can effectively handle both ID and OOD detection without heavy architectural changes, leveraging generative models to create meaningful outliers and improving OOD detection while preserving ID performance.

Abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) object detection is an important yet underexplored task. A reliable object detector should be able to handle OOD objects by localizing and correctly classifying them as OOD. However, a critical issue arises when such atypical objects are completely missed by the object detector and incorrectly treated as background. Existing OOD detection approaches in object detection often rely on complex architectures or auxiliary branches and typically do not provide a framework that treats in-distribution (ID) and OOD in a unified way. In this work, we address these limitations by enabling a single detector to detect OOD objects, that are otherwise silently overlooked, alongside ID objects. We present \textbf{SynOE-OD}, a \textbf{Syn}thetic \textbf{O}utlier-\textbf{E}xposure-based \textbf{O}bject \textbf{D}etection framework, that leverages strong generative models, like Stable Diffusion, and Open-Vocabulary Object Detectors (OVODs) to generate semantically meaningful, object-level data that serve as outliers during training. The generated data is used for transfer-learning to establish strong ID task performance and supplement detection models with OOD object detection robustness. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art average precision on an established OOD object detection benchmark, where OVODs, such as GroundingDINO, show limited zero-shot performance in detecting OOD objects in street-scenes.

</details>


### [35] [Boosting Quantitive and Spatial Awareness for Zero-Shot Object Counting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16129)
*Da Zhang,Bingyu Li,Feiyu Wang,Zhiyuan Zhao,Junyu Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QICA is a zero-shot object counting framework that unifies quantity perception with spatially robust aggregation via numerically conditioned prompts, a Cost Aggregation Decoder operating on vision-text similarity maps, and a multi-level quantity alignment loss. It achieves competitive results on FSC-147 and strong zero-shot generalization to unseen domains (CARPK, ShanghaiTech-A).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ZSOC methods treat counting as coarse retrieval and suffer from limited fine-grained quantity awareness, spatial insensitivity, and feature-space distortion during adaptation. A method that tightly couples semantic recognition with quantitative reasoning while preserving transferability is needed.

Method: Synergistic Prompting Strategy (SPS) adapt vision and language encoders with numerically conditioned prompts; Cost Aggregation Decoder (CAD) refines vision-text similarity maps through spatial aggregation to avoid overfitting; Multi-level Quantity Alignment loss (L_MQA) enforces numerical consistency across the pipeline.

Result: Competitive performance on FSC-147; zero-shot evaluation on CARPK and ShanghaiTech-A demonstrates superior generalization to unseen domains.

Conclusion: QICA effectively couples quantity perception with spatial aggregation, enabling accurate zero-shot counting and improved transferability. The approach shows promise for broader applications in open-category counting and cross-domain robustness.

Abstract: Zero-shot object counting (ZSOC) aims to enumerate objects of arbitrary categories specified by text descriptions without requiring visual exemplars. However, existing methods often treat counting as a coarse retrieval task, suffering from a lack of fine-grained quantity awareness. Furthermore, they frequently exhibit spatial insensitivity and degraded generalization due to feature space distortion during model adaptation.To address these challenges, we present \textbf{QICA}, a novel framework that synergizes \underline{q}uantity percept\underline{i}on with robust spatial \underline{c}ast \underline{a}ggregation. Specifically, we introduce a Synergistic Prompting Strategy (\textbf{SPS}) that adapts vision and language encoders through numerically conditioned prompts, bridging the gap between semantic recognition and quantitative reasoning. To mitigate feature distortion, we propose a Cost Aggregation Decoder (\textbf{CAD}) that operates directly on vision-text similarity maps. By refining these maps through spatial aggregation, CAD prevents overfitting while preserving zero-shot transferability. Additionally, a multi-level quantity alignment loss ($\mathcal{L}_{MQA}$) is employed to enforce numerical consistency across the entire pipeline. Extensive experiments on FSC-147 demonstrate competitive performance, while zero-shot evaluation on CARPK and ShanghaiTech-A validates superior generalization to unseen domains.

</details>


### [36] [EPOFusion: Exposure aware Progressive Optimization Method for Infrared and Visible Image Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16130)
*Zhiwei Wang,Yayu Zheng,Defeng He,Li Zhao,Xiaoqin Zhang,Yuxing Li,Edmund Y. Lam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces EPOFusion, an exposure-aware IR-visible fusion framework with guidance-based encoding, iterative multiscale decoder, and adaptive loss, plus a new IVOE dataset for overexposed regions; shows superior performance and preserves IR cues in bright areas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overexposure in practical scenarios leads to loss of critical information; existing infrared–visible fusion methods struggle in highly bright regions, hindering both visual quality and downstream tasks; a dataset with explicit overexposure annotations is needed to study and improve exposure-aware fusion.

Method: 1) Guidance module to help encoder extract fine-grained infrared features from overexposed regions. 2) Iterative decoder with a multiscale context fusion module to progressively refine the fused image. 3) Adaptive loss that dynamically balances the modalities under varying exposure. 4) IVOE dataset with high-quality infrared-guided annotations for overexposed regions to facilitate exposure-aware training and evaluation.

Result: Extensive experiments show EPOFusion outperforms existing methods, preserving infrared cues in overexposed regions and delivering visually faithful fusion in non-overexposed areas, with gains in visual fidelity and downstream task performance.

Conclusion: EPOFusion provides exposure-aware fusion for infrared–visible data, addressing overexposure challenges, supported by a dedicated IVOE dataset; code and data will be released to enable reproducibility and comparison.

Abstract: Overexposure frequently occurs in practical scenarios, causing the loss of critical visual information. However, existing infrared and visible fusion methods still exhibit unsatisfactory performance in highly bright regions. To address this, we propose EPOFusion, an exposure-aware fusion model. Specifically, a guidance module is introduced to facilitate the encoder in extracting fine-grained infrared features from overexposed regions. Meanwhile, an iterative decoder incorporating a multiscale context fusion module is designed to progressively enhance the fused image, ensuring consistent details and superior visual quality. Finally, an adaptive loss function dynamically constrains the fusion process, enabling an effective balance between the modalities under varying exposure conditions. To achieve better exposure awareness, we construct the first infrared and visible overexposure dataset (IVOE) with high quality infrared guided annotations for overexposed regions. Extensive experiments show that EPOFusion outperforms existing methods. It maintains infrared cues in overexposed regions while achieving visually faithful fusion in non-overexposed areas, thereby enhancing both visual fidelity and downstream task performance. Code, fusion results and IVOE dataset will be made available at https://github.com/warren-wzw/EPOFusion.git.

</details>


### [37] [DualPrim: Compact 3D Reconstruction with Positive and Negative Primitives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16133)
*Xiaoxu Meng,Zhongmin Chen,Bo Yang,Weikai Chen,Weixiao Liu,Lin Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DualPrim uses positive and negative superquadrics to form a compact, structured 3D representation that can both build and carve geometry, enabling topology-aware holes and concavities; trained end-to-end with a volumetric differentiable renderer and exact mesh export via boolean difference; achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and structured outputs suitable for downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural reconstructions often trade structure for fidelity, yielding dense, unstructured meshes with irregular topology and weak part boundaries that hinder editing, animation, and downstream asset reuse. A topology-aware, compact, differentiable representation is needed.

Method: Represent shapes with positive (building) and negative (carving) superquadrics; apply a differentiable operator to carve local volumes; embed in a volumetric differentiable renderer for end-to-end learning from multi-view images; enable seamless mesh export via closed-form boolean difference.

Result: State-of-the-art accuracy; outputs are compact, structured, and interpretable; better satisfy downstream needs than additive-only alternatives.

Conclusion: The additive-subtractive design increases representational power without sacrificing compactness or differentiability, enabling practical, topology-aware 3D reconstruction with end-to-end learning and clean mesh export.

Abstract: Neural reconstructions often trade structure for fidelity, yielding dense and unstructured meshes with irregular topology and weak part boundaries that hinder editing, animation, and downstream asset reuse. We present DualPrim, a compact and structured 3D reconstruction framework. Unlike additive-only implicit or primitive methods, DualPrim represents shapes with positive and negative superquadrics: the former builds the bases while the latter carves local volumes through a differentiable operator, enabling topology-aware modeling of holes and concavities. This additive-subtractive design increases the representational power without sacrificing compactness or differentiability. We embed DualPrim in a volumetric differentiable renderer, enabling end-to-end learning from multi-view images and seamless mesh export via closed-form boolean difference. Empirically, DualPrim delivers state-of-the-art accuracy and produces compact, structured, and interpretable outputs that better satisfy downstream needs than additive-only alternatives.

</details>


### [38] [When Generative Augmentation Hurts: A Benchmark Study of GAN and Diffusion Models for Bias Correction in AI Classification Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16134)
*Shesh Narayan Gupta,Nik Bear Brown*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: In low-data regimes, diffusion-based augmentation (Stable Diffusion + LoRA) outperforms traditional transforms and GAN-based augmentation (FastGAN); FastGAN can increase classifier bias and show mode collapse; a sample-size boundary around 20–50 images per class.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand failure modes of generative augmentation under data scarcity and identify robust augmentation strategies for imbalanced, fine-grained classification tasks.

Method: Benchmark on Oxford-IIIT Pet Dataset with eight artificially underrepresented breeds. Compare three augmentation strategies: traditional transforms, FastGAN, and Stable Diffusion 1.5 fine-tuned with LoRA. Evaluate macro F1 and bias gap across three random seeds; analyze embedding structure with t-SNE to detect mode collapse; experiments run on consumer-grade GPUs (6–8 GB).

Result: FastGAN augmentation increases bias (bias gap +20.7%; Cohen's d = 5.03; p = 0.013) and exhibits mode collapse, with tight isolated clusters for severe-minority breeds in t-SNE. Stable Diffusion + LoRA achieves the highest macro F1 (0.9125 ± 0.0047) and reduces bias gap by 13.1% relative to the unaugmented baseline. Indicates a sample-size boundary between 20 and 50 training images per class below which GAN augmentation can be detrimental.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based augmentation with LoRA provides more robust performance in low-data, imbalanced, fine-grained classification than GAN-based augmentation, which can harm accuracy and increase bias; broader validation across domains is warranted.

Abstract: Generative models are widely used to compensate for class imbalance in AI training pipelines, yet their failure modes under low-data conditions are poorly understood. This paper reports a controlled benchmark comparing three augmentation strategies applied to a fine-grained animal classification task: traditional transforms, FastGAN, and Stable Diffusion 1.5 fine-tuned with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). Using the Oxford-IIIT Pet Dataset with eight artificially underrepresented breeds, we find that FastGAN augmentation does not merely underperform at very low training set sizes but actively increases classifier bias, with a statistically significant large effect across three random seeds (bias gap increase: +20.7%, Cohen's d = +5.03, p = 0.013). The effect size here is large enough to give confidence in the direction of the finding despite the small number of seeds. Feature embedding analysis using t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding reveals that FastGAN images for severe-minority breeds form tight isolated clusters outside the real image distribution, a pattern consistent with mode collapse. Stable Diffusion with Low-Rank Adaptation produced the best results overall, achieving the highest macro F1 (0.9125 plus or minus 0.0047) and a 13.1% reduction in the bias gap relative to the unaugmented baseline. The data suggest a sample-size boundary somewhere between 20 and 50 training images per class below which GAN augmentation becomes harmful in this setting, though further work across additional domains is needed to establish where that boundary sits more precisely. All experiments run on a consumer-grade GPU with 6 to 8 GB of memory, with no cloud compute required.

</details>


### [39] [Rethinking UMM Visual Generation: Masked Modeling for Efficient Image-Only Pre-training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16139)
*Peng Sun,Jun Xie,Tao Lin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage Image-Only Training for UMMs (IOMM) reduces data and compute needs while achieving SOTA on GenEval/WISE; Stage 1 uses unlabeled images; Stage 2 uses unlabeled plus small text-image set; IOMM-B 3.6B trained in ~1050 GPU hours.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UMMs' visual generation pre-training is bottlenecked by inefficient paradigms and reliance on scarce high-quality text-image paired data; removing these bottlenecks could yield more data-efficient, scalable multimodal models.

Method: Two-stage training: (1) pre-train the visual generative component exclusively on abundant unlabeled image data; (2) fine-tune with a mix of unlabeled images and a small curated set of text-image pairs to improve instruction alignment and generative quality.

Result: IOMM-B (3.6B) trained from scratch in ~1050 H800 GPU hours (1000h for image-only pre-training). Achieves GenEval 0.89 and WISE 0.55, outperforming BAGEL-7B (0.82, 0.55) and BLIP3-o-4B (0.84, 0.50). Code released at provided repo.

Conclusion: Demonstrates data-efficient training for UMMs with competitive or state-of-the-art performance; substantial efficiency gains and reduced reliance on paired data; enables scalable multimodal generation research.

Abstract: Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) are often constrained by the pre-training of their $\textbf{visual generation components}$, which typically relies on inefficient paradigms and scarce, high-quality text-image paired data. In this paper, we systematically analyze pre-training recipes for $\textbf{UMM visual generation}$ and identify these two issues as the major bottlenecks.
  To address them, we propose $\textbf{Image-Only Training for UMMs (IOMM)}$, a data-efficient two-stage training framework.
  The first stage pre-trains the visual generative component $\textbf{exclusively}$ using abundant unlabeled image-only data, thereby removing the dependency on paired data $\textbf{for this costly phase}$. The second stage fine-tunes the model using a mixture of unlabeled images and a small curated set of text-image pairs, leading to improved instruction alignment and generative quality.
  Extensive experiments show that IOMM not only improves training efficiency but also achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance.
  For example, our IOMM-B (3.6B) model was trained from scratch using only $\sim \textbf{1050}$ H800 GPU hours (with the vast majority, $\textbf{1000}$ hours, dedicated to the efficient $\textbf{image-only pre-training stage}$). It achieves $\textbf{0.89}$ on GenEval and $\textbf{0.55}$ on WISE--surpassing strong baselines such as BAGEL-7B (0.82 & 0.55) and BLIP3-o-4B (0.84 & 0.50).
  Code is available $\href{https://github.com/LINs-lab/IOMM}{https://github.com/LINs-lab/IOMM}$.

</details>


### [40] [EFF-Grasp: Energy-Field Flow Matching for Physics-Aware Dexterous Grasp Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16151)
*Yukun Zhao,Zichen Zhong,Yongshun Gong,Yilong Yin,Haoliang Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A flow-matching-based, deterministic ODE framework for physics-aware dexterous grasp generation (EFF-Grasp) that uses energy-guided guidance to enforce feasibility with fewer sampling steps than diffusion methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based methods yield rich grasp distributions but suffer from slow sampling, trajectory instability, and potential physical infeasibility; there is a need for faster, stable generation that respects physical constraints without heavy simulation.

Method: Propose EFF-Grasp: reformulate grasp synthesis as a deterministic ODE via flow matching; introduce training-free physics-aware energy guidance using adapted explicit energy functions; estimate guidance term via local Monte Carlo during inference; no additional physics-based training or simulation feedback.

Result: Outperforms diffusion baselines on five benchmark datasets in grasp quality and physical feasibility, with substantially fewer sampling steps.

Conclusion: Deterministic flow-based grasp generation with energy-guided physics constraints yields efficient, stable, physically feasible grasp synthesis and can generalize to other physics-constrained generation tasks.

Abstract: Denoising generative models have recently become the dominant paradigm for dexterous grasp generation, owing to their ability to model complex grasp distributions from large-scale data. However, existing diffusion-based methods typically formulate generation as a stochastic differential equation (SDE), which often requires many sequential denoising steps and introduces trajectory instability that can lead to physically infeasible grasps. In this paper, we propose EFF-Grasp, a novel Flow-Matching-based framework for physics-aware dexterous grasp generation. Specifically, we reformulate grasp synthesis as a deterministic ordinary differential equation (ODE) process, which enables efficient and stable generation through smooth probability flows. To further enforce physical feasibility, we introduce a training-free physics-aware energy guidance strategy. Our method defines an energy-guided target distribution using adapted explicit physical energy functions that capture key grasp constraints, and estimates the corresponding guidance term via a local Monte Carlo approximation during inference. In this way, EFF-Grasp dynamically steers the generation trajectory toward physically feasible regions without requiring additional physics-based training or simulation feedback. Extensive experiments on five benchmark datasets show that EFF-Grasp achieves superior performance in grasp quality and physical feasibility, while requiring substantially fewer sampling steps than diffusion-based baselines.

</details>


### [41] [GATS: Gaussian Aware Temporal Scaling Transformer for Invariant 4D Spatio-Temporal Point Cloud Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16154)
*Jiayi Tian,Jiaze Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A dual invariant 4D backbone for point cloud videos, called Gaussian Aware Temporal Scaling (GATS), combining Uncertainty Guided Gaussian Convolution (UGGC) and Temporal Scaling Attention (TSA). It addresses distributional irregularities and temporal scale bias, offering robustness to density variation, noise, and occlusion, with improved efficiency over Transformer baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 4D point cloud videos suffer from temporal scale bias across varying frame rates and distributional uncertainty due to irregular point cloud densities. Existing CNN/Transformer approaches face limited receptive fields or quadratic complexity and often ignore these distortions.

Method: Two modules: (1) Uncertainty Guided Gaussian Convolution (UGGC) uses local Gaussian statistics and uncertainty-aware gating for robust neighborhood aggregation under density variation; (2) Temporal Scaling Attention (TSA) learns a scaling factor to normalize temporal distances, achieving frame partition invariance and stable velocity estimation across frame rates. The modules are complementary: time interval normalization precedes Gaussian estimation, and Gaussian modeling enhances robustness to irregular distributions.

Result: On MSR-Action3D, NTU RGBD, and Synthia4D the framework reports gains: +6.62% accuracy on MSR-Action3D, +1.4% accuracy on NTU RGBD, and +1.8% mIoU on Synthia4D, indicating improved accuracy, robustness, and scalability relative to Transformer-based backbones.

Conclusion: GATS provides a principled, efficient approach for invariant 4D point cloud video understanding, achieving higher accuracy and robustness under density variation, noise, occlusion, and frame-rate changes, with favorable scalability compared to Transformer-based methods.

Abstract: Understanding 4D point cloud videos is essential for enabling intelligent agents to perceive dynamic environments. However, temporal scale bias across varying frame rates and distributional uncertainty in irregular point clouds make it highly challenging to design a unified and robust 4D backbone. Existing CNN or Transformer based methods are constrained either by limited receptive fields or by quadratic computational complexity, while neglecting these implicit distortions. To address this problem, we propose a novel dual invariant framework, termed \textbf{Gaussian Aware Temporal Scaling (GATS)}, which explicitly resolves both distributional inconsistencies and temporal. The proposed \emph{Uncertainty Guided Gaussian Convolution (UGGC)} incorporates local Gaussian statistics and uncertainty aware gating into point convolution, thereby achieving robust neighborhood aggregation under density variation, noise, and occlusion. In parallel, the \emph{Temporal Scaling Attention (TSA)} introduces a learnable scaling factor to normalize temporal distances, ensuring frame partition invariance and consistent velocity estimation across different frame rates. These two modules are complementary: temporal scaling normalizes time intervals prior to Gaussian estimation, while Gaussian modeling enhances robustness to irregular distributions. Our experiments on mainstream benchmarks MSR-Action3D (\textbf{+6.62\%} accuracy), NTU RGBD (\textbf{+1.4\%} accuracy), and Synthia4D (\textbf{+1.8\%} mIoU) demonstrate significant performance gains, offering a more efficient and principled paradigm for invariant 4D point cloud video understanding with superior accuracy, robustness, and scalability compared to Transformer based counterparts.

</details>


### [42] [AI-Generated Figures in Academic Publishing: Policies, Tools, and Practical Guidelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16159)
*Davie Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Survey of major publishers' policies on AI-generated figures; proposes best-practice guidelines for compliant, transparent use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing use of AI-generated imagery in scientific figures raises policy, reproducibility, authorship, and misinformation concerns; need clarity and practical guidance.

Method: Review of policies from Nature, Science, Cell Press, Elsevier, and PLOS; analysis of publisher concerns; consideration of tools like SciDraw; development of best-practice guidelines for researchers.

Result: Identifies key concerns (reproducibility, authorship attribution, visual misinformation); notes policy variability across publishers; offers guidelines and disclosure protocols; argues that AI figures can accelerate scientific communication with proper quality control.

Conclusion: With appropriate disclosure and quality control, AI-generated figures can meaningfully accelerate scientific communication without compromising integrity.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of generative AI has introduced a new class of tools capable of producing publication-quality scientific figures, graphical abstracts, and data visualizations. However, academic publishers have responded with inconsistent and often ambiguous policies regarding AI-generated imagery. This paper surveys the current stance of major journals and publishers -- including Nature, Science, Cell Press, Elsevier, and PLOS -- on the use of AI-generated figures. We identify key concerns raised by publishers, including reproducibility, authorship attribution, and potential for visual misinformation. Drawing on practical examples from tools such as SciDraw, an AI-powered platform designed specifically for scientific illustration, we propose a set of best-practice guidelines for researchers seeking to use AI figure-generation tools in a compliant and transparent manner. Our findings suggest that, with appropriate disclosure and quality control, AI-generated figures can meaningfully accelerate scientific communication without compromising integrity.

</details>


### [43] [Segmentation-before-Staining Improves Structural Fidelity in Virtual IHC-to-Multiplex IF Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16160)
*Junhyeok Lee,Han Jang,Heeseong Eum,Joon Jang,Kyu Sung Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Supervision-free conditioning with a soft nucleus prior and variance-preserving regularization improves nuclei fidelity and perceptual quality in virtual staining across multiple models, without task-specific tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current mIF translators prioritize pixel-level fidelity but can distort nuclear morphology, leading to errors in nuclei counts and downstream metrics (e.g., Ki67). Clinical relevance demands accurate nuclei structure preservation.

Method: Inject a continuous cell probability map from a pretrained nuclei segmentation model as an explicit input prior; apply a variance-preserving regularization to match local intensity statistics; use a soft prior to retain boundary information; architecture-agnostic approach evaluated on Pix2Pix (U-Net and ResNet backbones), deterministic regression U-Net, and conditional diffusion; tested on two independent datasets.

Result: Across models and datasets, the proposed conditioning and regularization yield consistent improvements in nuclei-count fidelity and perceptual quality with no additional task-specific tuning.

Conclusion: The approach enhances nuclear- morphology fidelity in virtual staining, improving potential clinical utility; code will be publicly released upon acceptance.

Abstract: Multiplex immunofluorescence (mIF) enables simultaneous single-cell quantification of multiple biomarkers within intact tissue architecture, yet its high reagent cost, multi-round staining protocols, and need for specialized imaging platforms limit routine clinical adoption. Virtual staining can synthesize mIF channels from widely available brightfield immunohistochemistry (IHC), but current translators optimize pixel-level fidelity without explicitly constraining nuclear morphology. In pathology, this gap is clinically consequential: subtle distortions in nuclei count, shape, or spatial arrangement propagate directly to quantification endpoints such as the Ki67 proliferation index, where errors of a few percent can shift treatment-relevant risk categories. This work introduces a supervision-free, architecture-agnostic conditioning strategy that injects a continuous cell probability map from a pretrained nuclei segmentation foundation model as an explicit input prior, together with a variance-preserving regularization term that matches local intensity statistics to maintain cell-level heterogeneity in synthesized fluorescence channels. The soft prior retains gradient-level boundary information lost by binary thresholding, providing a richer conditioning signal without task-specific tuning. Controlled experiments across Pix2Pix with U-Net and ResNet generators, deterministic regression U-Net, and conditional diffusion on two independent datasets demonstrate consistent improvements in nuclei count fidelity and perceptual quality, as the sole modifications. Code will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [44] [STARK: Spatio-Temporal Attention for Representation of Keypoints for Continuous Sign Language Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16163)
*Suvajit Patra,Soumitra Samanta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified spatio-temporal attention encoder for CSLR with substantial parameter reduction (70-80%) and competitive performance on Phoenix-14T, using spatial attention across keypoints and local-window temporal attention to form a local context-aware representation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce model size and computational complexity of existing keypoint-based CSLR architectures (GNNs/attention encoders with 1D temporal convs) while maintaining comparable accuracy.

Method: Introduce a unified spatio-temporal attention network that computes attention scores across spatial keypoints and temporally within local windows, aggregating features into a local context-aware spatio-temporal representation.

Result: Achieves ~70-80% fewer parameters than state-of-the-art models and maintains competitive performance relative to keypoint-based methods on the Phoenix-14T CSLR dataset.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that parameter-efficient CSLR is possible with a local-window spatio-temporal attention architecture, offering a practical alternative to heavier encoder/decoder designs.

Abstract: Continuous Sign Language Recognition (CSLR) is a crucial task for understanding the languages of deaf communities. Contemporary keypoint-based approaches typically rely on spatio-temporal encoding, where spatial interactions among keypoints are modeled using Graph Convolutional Networks or attention mechanisms, while temporal dynamics are captured using 1D convolutional networks. However, such designs often introduce a large number of parameters in both the encoder and the decoder. This paper introduces a unified spatio-temporal attention network that computes attention scores both spatially (across keypoints) and temporally (within local windows), and aggregates features to produce a local context-aware spatio-temporal representation. The proposed encoder contains approximately $70-80\%$ fewer parameters than existing state-of-the-art models while achieving comparable performance to keypoint-based methods on the Phoenix-14T dataset.

</details>


### [45] [Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Consistency progressive Re-ranking for Visible-Infrared Person Re-identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16165)
*Yiming Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A progressive modal relationship re-ranking method (HHCR) for cross-modal person re-identification, with a consistency re-ranking inference network (CRI) baseline, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-modal re-ID suffers from large inter-modal gaps and intra-modal variations; current re-ranking methods fail to address both simultaneously, motivating a unified re-ranking framework that exploits both cross-modal and intra-modal relationships.

Method: Two-module framework: heterogeneous consistency re-ranking (cross-modal relationships between query and gallery) and homogeneous consistency re-ranking (intra-modal relationships within each modality). Introduces CRI as the baseline network for consistency re-ranking.

Result: Extensive experiments show the method generalizes across settings and achieves state-of-the-art performance for both the re-ranking approach and the CRI baseline.

Conclusion: The proposed HHCR framework with CRI baseline is effective and generalizable for cross-modal person re-identification.

Abstract: Visible-infrared person re-identification faces greater challenges than traditional person re-identification due to the significant differences between modalities. In particular, the differences between these modalities make effective matching even more challenging, mainly because existing re-ranking algorithms cannot simultaneously address the intra-modal variations and inter-modal discrepancy in cross-modal person re-identification. To address this problem, we propose a novel Progressive Modal Relationship Re-ranking method consisting of two modules, called heterogeneous and homogeneous consistency re-ranking(HHCR). The first module, heterogeneous consistency re-ranking, explores the relationship between the query and the gallery modalities in the test set. The second module, homogeneous consistency reranking, investigates the intrinsic relationship within each modality between the query and the gallery in the test set. Based on this, we propose a baseline for cross-modal person re-identification, called a consistency re-ranking inference network (CRI). We conducted comprehensive experiments demonstrating that our proposed re-ranking method is generalized, and both the re-ranking and the baseline achieve state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [46] [360° Image Perception with MLLMs: A Comprehensive Benchmark and a Training-Free Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16179)
*Huyen T. T. Tran,Van-Quang Nguyen,Farros Alferro,Kang-Jun Liu,Takayuki Okatani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces 360Bench, a 7K-resolution 360° VQA benchmark, and Free360, a training-free, scene-graph–based method for high-resolution 360° VQA. The study reveals gaps in current MLLMs’ perception of 360° imagery and demonstrates that Free360 consistently improves base models without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 360° images capture full environments but introduce geometric distortion and complex spatial relations. Existing multimodal LLMs struggle to perceive and reason about such data, necessitating a dedicated benchmark and robust, training-free methods to evaluate and improve 360° VQA capabilities.

Method: 360Bench provides 7K-resolution 360° images with seven VQA-oriented tasks, annotated by humans, and evaluates seven MLLMs and six enhancement methods. Free360 is a training-free, modular framework that performs stepwise reasoning with adaptive spherical transformations per step and constructs a unified scene-graph to generate answers.

Result: Free360 consistently improves the base MLLMs on 360° VQA tasks and offers a strong training-free solution. The authors plan to publicly release the dataset and code upon acceptance.

Conclusion: 360Bench exposes deficiencies in current MLLMs’ 360° perception, while Free360 demonstrates that a modular, graph-based, training-free approach can significantly boost 360° VQA performance. The work lays groundwork for future benchmarks and techniques in holistic 360° understanding.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown impressive abilities in understanding and reasoning over conventional images. However, their perception of 360° images remains largely underexplored. Unlike conventional images, 360° images capture the entire surrounding environment, enabling holistic spatial reasoning but introducing challenges such as geometric distortion and complex spatial relations. To comprehensively assess MLLMs' capabilities to perceive 360° images, we introduce 360Bench, a Visual Question Answering (VQA) benchmark featuring 7K-resolution 360° images, seven representative (sub)tasks with annotations carefully curated by human annotators. Using 360Bench, we systematically evaluate seven MLLMs and six enhancement methods, revealing their shortcomings in 360° image perception. To address these challenges, we propose Free360, a training-free scene-graph-based framework for high-resolution 360° VQA. Free360 decomposes the reasoning process into modular steps, applies adaptive spherical image transformations to 360° images tailored to each step, and seamlessly integrates the resulting information into a unified graph representation for answer generation. Experiments show that Free360 consistently improves its base MLLM and provides a strong training-free solution for 360° VQA tasks. The source code and dataset will be publicly released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [47] [KidsNanny: A Two-Stage Multimodal Content Moderation Pipeline Integrating Visual Classification, Object Detection, OCR, and Contextual Reasoning for Child Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16181)
*Viraj Panchal,Tanmay Talsaniya,Parag Patel,Meet Patel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage multimodal content moderation (KidsNanny) combines ViT + object detector for fast visual screening and OCR+text-based 7B LLM for contextual reasoning, achieving competitive accuracy with low latency on UnsafeBench Sexual category; notable gains in text-aware threats but limited text-only data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address efficient, accurate child-safety content moderation leveraging both vision and text, reducing reliance on pixel-level processing and enabling faster, text-aware decisions.

Method: Stage 1: Vision Transformer integrated with object detector for visual screening; outputs routed as text to Stage 2. Stage 2: OCR to extract text, then a 7B language model for contextual reasoning; total pipeline ~120 ms.

Result: Stage 1: 80.27% accuracy, 85.39% F1 at 11.7 ms. Multimodal full pipeline: 81.40% accuracy, 86.16% F1 at 120 ms. Baselines: ShieldGemma-2 64.80% acc, 1,136 ms; LlavaGuard 80.36% acc, 4,138 ms. Text-only subset: 25/25 recall (100%), 75.76% precision; text-aware analysis shows OCR-based reasoning improves recall-precision on text-embedded threats.

Conclusion: Dedicated OCR-based reasoning can improve recall-precision on text-embedded threats with lower latency; however, small text-only sample limits generalizability; the architecture contributes to efficient multimodal moderation for child safety.

Abstract: We present KidsNanny, a two-stage multimodal content moderation architecture for child safety. Stage 1 combines a vision transformer (ViT) with an object detector for visual screening (11.7 ms); outputs are routed as text not raw pixels to Stage 2, which applies OCR and a text based 7B language model for contextual reasoning (120 ms total pipeline). We evaluate on the UnsafeBench Sexual category (1,054 images) under two regimes: vision-only, isolating Stage 1, and multimodal, evaluating the full Stage 1+2 pipeline. Stage 1 achieves 80.27% accuracy and 85.39% F1 at 11.7 ms; vision-only baselines range from 59.01% to 77.04% accuracy. The full pipeline achieves 81.40% accuracy and 86.16% F1 at 120 ms, compared to ShieldGemma-2 (64.80% accuracy, 1,136 ms) and LlavaGuard (80.36% accuracy, 4,138 ms). To evaluate text-awareness, we filter two subsets: a text+visual subset (257 images) and a text-only subset (44 images where safety depends primarily on embedded text). On text-only images, KidsNanny achieves 100% recall (25/25 positives; small sample) and 75.76% precision; ShieldGemma-2 achieves 84% recall and 60% precision at 1,136 ms. Results suggest that dedicated OCR-based reasoning may offer recall-precision advantages on text-embedded threats at lower latency, though the small text-only subset limits generalizability. By documenting this architecture and evaluation methodology, we aim to contribute to the broader research effort on efficient multimodal content moderation for child safety.

</details>


### [48] [ECHO: Edge-Cloud Humanoid Orchestration for Language-to-Motion Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16188)
*Haozhe Jia,Jianfei Song,Yuan Zhang,Honglei Jin,Youcheng Fan,Wenshuo Chen,Wei Zhang,Yutao Yue*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A cloud–edge system (ECHO) enables language-driven whole-body control of humanoid robots via a diffusion-based text-to-motion generator on the cloud and an edge RL tracker on the robot, using a compact 38-D motion representation for direct PD control, with real-world validation on a Unitree G1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable natural-language guidance for robust, real-time humanoid motion in real-world settings, bridging cloud-based generation and edge execution with sim-to-real transfer.

Method: Cloud diffusion-based text-to-motion generator (1D UNet with cross-attention conditioned on CLIP features; DDIM sampling with 10 denoising steps and classifier-free guidance) outputs 38-D robot-native motion representation. Edge tracker uses a teacher–student RL paradigm with evidential adaptation for sim-to-real transfer, symmetry constraints, and domain randomization. An autonomous fall-recovery module uses onboard IMU and a motion library. Evaluation uses a robot-domain unified evaluator on a retargeted HumanML3D benchmark; real robot tests on Unitree G1.

Result: Quantitative: FID 0.029 and R-Precision Top-1 0.686 on the robot-domain evaluation. Qualitative: strong motion generation quality, high safety and trajectory consistency, and stable real-world execution without hardware fine-tuning.

Conclusion: ECHO convincingly integrates cloud-based motion generation with edge execution to deliver language-guided, safe, and software-efficient humanoid motion control with real-world validation.

Abstract: We present ECHO, an edge--cloud framework for language-driven whole-body control of humanoid robots. A cloud-hosted diffusion-based text-to-motion generator synthesizes motion references from natural language instructions, while an edge-deployed reinforcement-learning tracker executes them in closed loop on the robot. The two modules are bridged by a compact, robot-native 38-dimensional motion representation that encodes joint angles, root planar velocity, root height, and a continuous 6D root orientation per frame, eliminating inference-time retargeting from human body models and remaining directly compatible with low-level PD control. The generator adopts a 1D convolutional UNet with cross-attention conditioned on CLIP-encoded text features; at inference, DDIM sampling with 10 denoising steps and classifier-free guidance produces motion sequences in approximately one second on a cloud GPU. The tracker follows a Teacher--Student paradigm: a privileged teacher policy is distilled into a lightweight student equipped with an evidential adaptation module for sim-to-real transfer, further strengthened by morphological symmetry constraints and domain randomization. An autonomous fall recovery mechanism detects falls via onboard IMU readings and retrieves recovery trajectories from a pre-built motion library. We evaluate ECHO on a retargeted HumanML3D benchmark, where it achieves strong generation quality (FID 0.029, R-Precision Top-1 0.686) under a unified robot-domain evaluator, while maintaining high motion safety and trajectory consistency. Real-world experiments on a Unitree G1 humanoid demonstrate stable execution of diverse text commands with zero hardware fine-tuning.

</details>


### [49] [Reliable Reasoning in SVG-LLMs via Multi-Task Multi-Reward Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16189)
*Haomin Wang,Qi Wei,Qianli Ma,Shengyuan Ding,Jinhui Yin,Kai Chen,Hongjie Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CTRL-S proposes chain-of-thought reinforcement learning to improve SVG generation, introducing SVG-Sophia dataset and a multi-reward, multi-task framework that enhances structure, code quality, and visual fidelity beyond prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SVG generation methods suffer from limited generalization, redundant code paths, and no explicit reasoning. A structured, traceable reasoning process (chain-of-thought) could improve coherence and fidelity in SVG outputs.

Method: Introduce a chain-of-thought mechanism for SVG generation; construct SVG-Sophia with 145K samples across refinement, Text-to-SVG, and Image-to-SVG tasks; train for group-level structured SVG code; apply GRPO algorithm with a multi-reward objective including DINO, image-text similarity, format, and code efficiency; employ multi-task training.

Result: CTRL-S significantly improves task success rates, SVG code quality, and visual fidelity compared with existing methods across evaluated tasks.

Conclusion: Incorporating chain-of-thought reasoning and a multi-reward, multi-task RL framework yields more coherent, faithful SVG generation and better overall performance than prior approaches.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of vision-language models, an increasing number of studies have explored their potential for SVG generation tasks. Although existing approaches improve performance by constructing large-scale SVG datasets and introducing SVG-specific tokens, they still suffer from limited generalization, redundant paths in code outputs, and a lack of explicit reasoning. In this work, we present CTRL-S (Chain-of-Thought Reinforcement Learning for SVG), a unified framework that introduces a chain-of-thought mechanism to explicitly expose the model's reasoning process during SVG generation. To support this structured reasoning, we construct SVG-Sophia, a high-quality dataset containing 145K samples across SVG code refinement, Text-to-SVG, and Image-to-SVG tasks. By training the model to generate group-level structured SVG code, CTRL-S significantly improves structural coherence and visual fidelity. Furthermore, we adopt the GRPO algorithm and design a multi-reward optimization framework, incorporating DINO, image-text similarity, format, and code efficiency rewards. Through joint multi-reward optimization and multi-task training, our approach systematically enhances overall generation capabilities. Extensive experiments show that CTRL-S outperforms existing methods, achieving higher task success rates, superior SVG code quality, and exceptional visual fidelity.

</details>


### [50] [S-VAM: Shortcut Video-Action Model by Self-Distilling Geometric and Semantic Foresight](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16195)
*Haodong Yan,Zhide Zhong,Jiaguan Zhu,Junjie He,Weilin Yuan,Wenxuan Song,Xin Gong,Yingjie Cai,Guanyi Zhao,Xu Yan,Bingbing Liu,Ying-Cong Chen,Haoang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: S-VAM is a single-pass shortcut video-action model using self-distillation from diffusion-generated multi-step videos to map noisy one-step features to teacher targets, enabling real-time, high-fidelity robot manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video action models struggle to balance real-time inference with high foresight fidelity: multi-step video generation is slow; one-step features are noisy; need a method to achieve coherent geometric/semantic foresight in one pass.

Method: Propose S-VAM architecture: single forward pass to predict coherent representations; self-distillation: distills priors from multi-step denoising into one-step inference; teacher targets are VFM representations extracted from diffusion model's multi-step generated videos; lightweight decouplers learn to map noisy one-step features to targets.

Result: Experiments in simulation and real world show S-VAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods, enabling efficient and precise manipulation in complex environments.

Conclusion: S-VAM provides an efficient, accurate, and stable blueprint for action prediction in robotics by combining shortcut foreseeing with self-distillation; practical implications for real-time robotic manipulation; project page provided.

Abstract: Video action models (VAMs) have emerged as a promising paradigm for robot learning, owing to their powerful visual foresight for complex manipulation tasks. However, current VAMs, typically relying on either slow multi-step video generation or noisy one-step feature extraction, cannot simultaneously guarantee real-time inference and high-fidelity foresight. To address this limitation, we propose S-VAM, a shortcut video-action model that foresees coherent geometric and semantic representations via a single forward pass. Serving as a stable blueprint, these foreseen representations significantly simplify the action prediction. To enable this efficient shortcut, we introduce a novel self-distillation strategy that condenses structured generative priors of multi-step denoising into one-step inference. Specifically, vision foundation model (VFM) representations extracted from the diffusion model's own multi-step generated videos provide teacher targets. Lightweight decouplers, as students, learn to directly map noisy one-step features to these targets. Extensive experiments in simulation and the real world demonstrate that our S-VAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods, enabling efficient and precise manipulation in complex environments. Our project page is https://haodong-yan.github.io/S-VAM/

</details>


### [51] [Leveling3D: Leveling Up 3D Reconstruction with Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting and Geometry-Aware Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16211)
*Yiming Huang,Baixiang Huang,Beilei Cui,Chi Kit Ng,Long Bai,Hongliang Ren*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Leveling3D proposes a geometry-aware pipeline that couples feed-forward 3D reconstruction with diffusion-based, geometry-consistent generation to fill extrapolated view artifacts, using a lightweight leveling adapter, palette filtering, and test-time masking, yielding state-of-the-art results on novel-view synthesis and depth estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based fixes for corrupted rendering results in 3D vision often ignore geometric consistency, leaving gaps in extrapolated views. There is a need for holistic systems that jointly reconstruct 3D structure and generate appearance in a geometry-aware manner.

Method: Introduce a geometry-aware leveling adapter that aligns diffusion-model priors with feed-forward geometry; employ palette filtering to diversify generation; apply test-time masking refinement to smooth boundaries at fixing regions; enable enhanced extrapolated views to serve as inputs for feed-forward 3DGS, achieving a joint reconstruction-and-generation pipeline.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on public datasets for tasks including novel-view synthesis and depth estimation, demonstrating improved geometric consistency and artifact filling in extrapolated views and enabling better downstream 3DGS results.

Conclusion: Leveling3D successfully integrates feed-forward 3D reconstruction with geometry-aware diffusion-based generation, providing holistic, geometrically consistent extrapolation and improving downstream 3D vision tasks. 

Abstract: Feed-forward 3D reconstruction has revolutionized 3D vision, providing a powerful baseline for downstream tasks such as novel-view synthesis with 3D Gaussian Splatting. Previous works explore fixing the corrupted rendering results with a diffusion model. However, they lack geometric concern and fail at filling the missing area on the extrapolated view. In this work, we introduce Leveling3D, a novel pipeline that integrates feed-forward 3D reconstruction with geometrical-consistent generation to enable holistic simultaneous reconstruction and generation. We propose a geometry-aware leveling adapter, a lightweight technique that aligns internal knowledge in the diffusion model with the geometry prior from the feed-forward model. The leveling adapter enables generation on the artifact area of the extrapolated novel views caused by underconstrained regions of the 3D representation. Specifically, to learn a more diverse distributed generation, we introduce the palette filtering strategy for training, and a test-time masking refinement to prevent messy boundaries along the fixing regions. More importantly, the enhanced extrapolated novel views from Leveling3D could be used as the inputs for feed-forward 3DGS, leveling up the 3D reconstruction. We achieve SOTA performance on public datasets, including tasks such as novel-view synthesis and depth estimation.

</details>


### [52] [PureCLIP-Depth: Prompt-Free and Decoder-Free Monocular Depth Estimation within CLIP Embedding Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16238)
*Ryutaro Miya,Kazuyoshi Fushinobu,Tatsuya Kawaguchi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Prompt-free, decoder-free monocular depth estimation in the CLIP embedding space; learns an RGB-to-depth mapping inside CLIP features; claims SOTA among CLIP-based methods for indoor and outdoor data; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage the semantic and cross-modal representations in CLIP to estimate depth without geometry-centric features or heavy decoders/prompts, aiming for a simpler, unified embedding-space approach.

Method: Train a direct RGB-to-depth mapping strictly within the CLIP embedding space, avoiding traditional decoders and geometric feature reliance; computations occur entirely in CLIP embeddings.

Result: Outperforms prior CLIP-embedding-based models on both indoor and outdoor datasets, achieving state-of-the-art within the CLIP-based literature.
