To overcome these limitations, we propose Meta-TTRL, a metacognitive test-time reinforcement learning framework. Meta-TTRL performs test-time parameter optimization guided by model-intrinsic monitoring signals derived from the meta-knowledge of UMMs, achieving self-improvement and capability-level improvement at test time. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Meta-TTRL generalizes well across three representative UMMs, including Janus-Pro-7B, BAGEL, and Qwen-Image, achieving significant gains on compositional reasoning tasks and multiple T2I benchmarks with limited data. We provide the first comprehensive analysis to investigate the potential of test-time reinforcement learning (TTRL) for T2I generation in UMMs. Our analysis further reveals a key insight underlying effective TTRL: metacognitive synergy, where monitoring signals align with the model's optimization regime to enable self-improvement.

</details>


### [157] [OMNIFLOW: A Physics-Grounded Multimodal Agent for Generalized Scientific Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15797)
*Hao Wu,Yongheng Zhang,Yuan Gao,Fan Xu,Fan Zhang,Ruobing Xie,Ruijian Gou,Yuxuan Liang,Xiaomeng Huang,Xian Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OMNIFLOW introduces a neuro-symbolic, domain-agnostic framework to ground frozen multimodal LLMs in physical laws for PDE-driven dynamics, using Semantic-Symbolic Alignment and Physics-Guided CoT to achieve physically consistent zero-shot and few-shot performance with interpretable reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs exhibit strong logical reasoning but struggle with continuous spatiotemporal dynamics governed by PDEs, leading to non-physical hallucinations. Domain-specific fine-tuning is costly and harms cross-domain generalization and interpretability. A parameter-free grounding approach in fundamental physical laws is needed for cross-domain, interpretable results.

Method: Two main components: (1) Semantic-Symbolic Alignment (SSA) that projects high-dimensional flow tensors into topological linguistic descriptors to emphasize physical structures over raw pixels; (2) Physics-Guided Chain-of-Thought (PG-CoT) with dynamic constraint injection (e.g., mass conservation) and iterative reflexive verification, enabling reasoning within frozen multimodal LLMs without domain-specific updates.

Result: Empirical evaluation across microscopic turbulence, theoretical Navier-Stokes, and macroscopic weather forecasting shows OMNIFLOW outperforms traditional deep learning baselines in zero-shot generalization and few-shot adaptation, and provides transparent, physically consistent reasoning reports.

Conclusion: OMNIFLOW enables interpretable, physics-grounded reasoning without fine-tuning, improving cross-domain generalization and offering a paradigm shift from black-box fitting to scientifically grounded inference.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional logical reasoning capabilities but frequently struggle with the continuous spatiotemporal dynamics governed by Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), often resulting in non-physical hallucinations. Existing approaches typically resort to costly, domain-specific fine-tuning, which severely limits cross-domain generalization and interpretability. To bridge this gap, we propose OMNIFLOW, a neuro-symbolic architecture designed to ground frozen multimodal LLMs in fundamental physical laws without requiring domain-specific parameter updates. OMNIFLOW introduces a novel \textit{Semantic-Symbolic Alignment} mechanism that projects high-dimensional flow tensors into topological linguistic descriptors, enabling the model to perceive physical structures rather than raw pixel values. Furthermore, we construct a Physics-Guided Chain-of-Thought (PG-CoT) workflow that orchestrates reasoning through dynamic constraint injection (e.g., mass conservation) and iterative reflexive verification. We evaluate OMNIFLOW on a comprehensive benchmark spanning microscopic turbulence, theoretical Navier-Stokes equations, and macroscopic global weather forecasting. Empirical results demonstrate that OMNIFLOW significantly outperforms traditional deep learning baselines in zero-shot generalization and few-shot adaptation tasks. Crucially, it offers transparent, physically consistent reasoning reports, marking a paradigm shift from black-box fitting to interpretable scientific reasoning.

</details>


### [158] [Time-Aware Prior Fitted Networks for Zero-Shot Forecasting with Exogenous Variables](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15802)
*Andres Potapczynski,Ravi Kiran Selvam,Tatiana Konstantinova,Shankar Ramasubramanian,Malcolm Wolff,Kin G. Olivares,Ruijun Ma,Mengfei Cao,Michael W. Mahoney,Andrew Gordon Wilson,Boris N. Oreshkin,Dmitry Efimov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A time-aware prior-data fitted network (PFN) that natively incorporates exogenous covariates via synthetic data generation and time-biased architecture, achieving state-of-the-art forecasts on exogenous-rich benchmarks (e.g., M5, electric prices).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exogenous covariates drive spikes, regime shifts, and discontinuities in many time series. Ignoring them degrades accuracy and many TS foundation models only use history. A PFN that is both time-aware and exogenous-capable is needed.

Method: Two main advances: (i) a synthetic data generation procedure tailored to address failure modes when applying tabular PFNs to time series; (ii) time-aware architectural modifications that embed inductive biases for temporal context; integration of exogenous covariates into the PFN within the prior-data fitted framework.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on exogenous-information-rich benchmarks, such as M5 and electric price forecasting.

Conclusion: A time-aware PFN that leverages exogenous covariates via targeted synthetic data and architecture design yields leading forecasting performance on exogenous-rich time series.

Abstract: In many time series forecasting settings, the target time series is accompanied by exogenous covariates, such as promotions and prices in retail demand; temperature in energy load; calendar and holiday indicators for traffic or sales; and grid load or fuel costs in electricity pricing. Ignoring these exogenous signals can substantially degrade forecasting accuracy, particularly when they drive spikes, discontinuities, or regime and phase changes in the target series. Most current time series foundation models (e.g., Chronos, Sundial, TimesFM, TimeMoE, TimeLLM, and LagLlama) ignore exogenous covariates and make forecasts solely from the numerical time series history, thereby limiting their performance. In this paper, we develop ApolloPFN, a prior-data fitted network (PFN) that is time-aware (unlike prior PFNs) and that natively incorporates exogenous covariates (unlike prior univariate forecasters). Our design introduces two major advances: (i) a synthetic data generation procedure tailored to resolve the failure modes that arise when tabular (non-temporal) PFNs are applied to time series; and (ii) time-aware architectural modifications that embed inductive biases needed to exploit the time series context. We demonstrate that ApolloPFN achieves state-of-the-art results across benchmarks, such as M5 and electric price forecasting, that contain exogenous information.

</details>


### [159] [Mask Is What DLLM Needs: A Masked Data Training Paradigm for Diffusion LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15803)
*Linrui Ma,Yufei Cui,Kai Han,Yunhe Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A density-aware noise scheduling approach for discrete diffusion LLMs that targets information-dense regions, using Complementary Priority Masking to separate reasoning and syntax samples; yields ~4% average accuracy gains on code/math benchmarks and reduces contextual collapse; dataset provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniform random noise schedulers fail to align with non-uniform information density in real sequences, wasting optimization on low-density regions and under-optimizing high-density logical pivots.

Method: Information Density Driven Smart Noise Scheduler, identify information-dense hubs, apply Complementary Priority Masking, decouple training instance into reasoning and syntax samples, use probabilistic priority masking to mitigate contextual collapse.

Result: Improved average accuracy by ~4% across four Code and Math benchmarks; outperforms uniform baselines; mechanistic analyses show effectiveness in reducing contextual collapse.

Conclusion: Density-aware masking-based training is an efficient, low-cost paradigm for Diffusion LLMs; opens a new masked data training paradigm; dataset provided.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models offer global context awareness and flexible parallel generation. However, uniform random noise schedulers in standard DLLM training overlook the highly non-uniform information density inherent in real-world sequences. This wastes optimization resources on low-density structural glues while leaving high-density logical pivot points severely under-optimized. To address this, we propose an Information Density Driven Smart Noise Scheduler. By extracting information-dense hubs and applying Complementary Priority Masking, our method decouples a single training instance into mutually reinforcing reasoning and syntax samples, forcing the model to master both logical deduction and foundational sequence structure. Experiments demonstrate that our approach improves average accuracy by ~4\% across four Code and Math reasoning benchmarks, significantly outperforming uniform baselines. Mechanistic analyses further reveal that probabilistic priority masking effectively mitigates contextual collapse during block diffusion training. Overall, this density-aware strategy efficiently unlocks the reasoning potential of diffusion language models at minimal annotation cost, emerging as a promising new masked data training paradigm for Diffusion LLMs. Our processed dataset can be found at https://huggingface.co/datasets/malr07/opc-sft-stage2-dense-extracted.

</details>


### [160] [Longitudinal Risk Prediction in Mammography with Privileged History Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15814)
*Banafsheh Karimian,Alexis Guichemerre,Soufiane Belharbi,Natacha Gillet,Luke McCaffrey,Mohammadhadi Shateri,Eric Granger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Privileged History Distillation (PHD) trains horizon-specific teachers on full longitudinal mammography history and distills their prognostic signals into a student model that only requires the current exam at inference, achieving long-horizon risk prediction comparable to full-history models while handling missing histories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In real-world screening, prior exams are often missing, irregular, or unavailable, which degrades longitudinal risk models. A training-time privileged approach is needed to leverage history without requiring it at deployment.

Method: A privileged multi-teacher distillation scheme with horizon-specific teachers trained on full longitudinal history for each prediction horizon. The student learns from a reconstructed history derived from the current exam, effectively inheriting horizon-dependent risk cues without past exams at inference.

Result: On the CSAW-CC longitudinal mammography dataset with multi-year outcomes, the method improves time-dependent AUC for long horizons versus no-history baselines and is comparable to full-history models, despite using only the current exam at inference.

Conclusion: Privileged History Distillation enables practical, horizon-aware long-horizon risk prediction under missing-history at deployment by transferring rich longitudinal signals from privileged training into a deployable student model.

Abstract: Breast cancer remains a leading cause of cancer-related mortality worldwide. Longitudinal mammography risk prediction models improve multi-year breast cancer risk prediction based on prior screening exams. However, in real-world clinical practice, longitudinal histories are often incomplete, irregular, or unavailable due to missed screenings, first-time examinations, heterogeneous acquisition schedules, or archival constraints. The absence of prior exams degrades the performance of longitudinal risk models and limits their practical applicability. While substantial longitudinal history is available during training, prior exams are commonly absent at test time. In this paper, we address missing history at inference time and propose a longitudinal risk prediction method that uses mammography history as privileged information during training and distills its prognostic value into a student model that only requires the current exam at inference time. The key idea is a privileged multi-teacher distillation scheme with horizon-specific teachers: each teacher is trained on the full longitudinal history to specialize in one prediction horizon, while the student receives only a reconstructed history derived from the current exam. This allows the student to inherit horizon-dependent longitudinal risk cues without requiring prior screening exams at deployment. Our new Privileged History Distillation (PHD) method is validated on a large longitudinal mammography dataset with multi-year cancer outcomes, CSAW-CC, comparing full-history and no-history baselines to their distilled counterparts. Using time-dependent AUC across horizons, our privileged history distillation method markedly improves the performance of long-horizon prediction over no-history models and is comparable to that of full-history models, while using only the current exam at inference time.

</details>


### [161] [Hypothesis Class Determines Explanation: Why Accurate Models Disagree on Feature Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15821)
*Thackshanaramana B*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Prediction-equivalent models can yield different explanations; explanations depend on hypothesis class, leading to an 'Explanation Lottery' where cross-class models disagree; a reliability score R(x) predicts stability; implications for model selection and auditing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explainable AI practices assume explanations transfer across models with identical predictions; this assumption risks misleading audits, policy compliance, and model selection.

Method: Empirical study across 24 datasets and multiple model classes; measure attribution agreement for identical predictions; analyze agreement within vs across hypothesis classes; formal analysis showing Agreement Gap with interactions; propose post-hoc reliability score; theoretical justification.

Result: Within-class models show strong attribution agreement; cross-class pairs show reduced agreement near lottery threshold; Agreement Gap persists with interaction structure; introduction of Explanation Reliability Score R(x) to predict stability; Explanation Lottery identified as structural driver.

Conclusion: Model deployment is not explanation-neutral; the chosen hypothesis class determines feature attributions; need reliability diagnostics for explanations; guidelines for audits and regulation; future work on robust explanations across architectures.

Abstract: The assumption that prediction-equivalent models produce equivalent explanations underlies many practices in explainable AI, including model selection, auditing, and regulatory evaluation. In this work, we show that this assumption does not hold. Through a large-scale empirical study across 24 datasets and multiple model classes, we find that models with identical predictive behavior can produce substantially different feature attributions.
  This disagreement is highly structured: models within the same hypothesis class exhibit strong agreement, while cross-class pairs (e.g., tree-based vs. linear) trained on identical data splits show substantially reduced agreement, consistently near or below the lottery threshold. We identify hypothesis class as the structural driver of this phenomenon, which we term the Explanation Lottery.
  We theoretically show that the resulting Agreement Gap persists under interaction structure in the data-generating process. This structural finding motivates a post-hoc diagnostic, the Explanation Reliability Score R(x), which predicts when explanations are stable across architectures without additional training.
  Our results demonstrate that model selection is not explanation-neutral: the hypothesis class chosen for deployment can determine which features are attributed responsibility for a decision.

</details>


### [162] [When Stability Fails: Hidden Failure Modes Of LLMS in Data-Constrained Scientific Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15840)
*Nazia Riasat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A controlled behavioral framework separates stability, correctness, prompt sensitivity, and output validity in LLM-driven decision tasks; results show LLMs can be highly stable yet misaligned with ground truth under relaxed thresholds or subtle prompt changes, underscoring the need for explicit ground-truth validation and output checks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stability/reproducibility are not sufficient guarantees of correctness in data-constrained scientific workflows; there is a risk of systematic deviation from statistical ground truth despite stable results.

Method: Develop four-dimensional evaluation framework; apply to a statistical gene prioritization task with differential expression analysis across prompt regimes (strict/relaxed thresholds, borderline ranking, minor wording changes); evaluate multiple LLMs on these tasks under fixed statistical inputs.

Result: LLMs exhibit near-perfect run-to-run stability while systematically diverging from ground truth, such as over-selecting under relaxed thresholds, showing high sensitivity to minor prompt wording, and generating syntactically plausible gene identifiers not present in the input.

Conclusion: Stability does not guarantee agreement with statistical ground truth; explicit ground-truth validation and output validity checks are essential when deploying LLMs in automated or semi-automated scientific workflows.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as decision-support tools in data-constrained scientific workflows, where correctness and validity are critical. However, evaluation practices often emphasize stability or reproducibility across repeated runs. While these properties are desirable, stability alone does not guar- antee agreement with statistical ground truth when such references are available. We introduce a controlled behavioral evaluation framework that explicitly sep- arates four dimensions of LLM decision-making: stability, correctness, prompt sensitivity, and output validity under fixed statistical inputs. We evaluate multi- ple LLMs using a statistical gene prioritization task derived from differential ex- pression analysis across prompt regimes involving strict and relaxed significance thresholds, borderline ranking scenarios, and minor wording variations. Our ex- periments show that LLMs can exhibit near-perfect run-to-run stability while sys- tematically diverging from statistical ground truth, over-selecting under relaxed thresholds, responding sharply to minor prompt wording changes, or producing syntactically plausible gene identifiers absent from the input table. Although sta- bility reflects robustness across repeated runs, it does not guarantee agreement with statistical ground truth in structured scientific decision tasks. These findings highlight the importance of explicit ground-truth validation and output validity checks when deploying LLMs in automated or semi-automated scientific work- flows.

</details>


### [163] [Informationally Compressive Anonymization: Non-Degrading Sensitive Input Protection for Privacy-Preserving Supervised Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15842)
*Jeremy J Samuelson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes ICA and VEIL for privacy-preserving ML that uses a supervised encoder in a trusted environment to produce low-dimensional, irreversibly anonymized representations, enabling high-utility ML without noise or encryption, backed by topological/information-theoretic proofs of non-invertibility and strong post-quantum privacy guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address privacy, security, and regulatory risks in ML posed by sensitive data, where traditional ppML methods like Differential Privacy and Homomorphic Encryption incur performance, complexity, or overhead costs; seek a design where privacy comes from architecture and theory rather than noise or crypto.

Method: Introduce Informationally Compressive Anonymization (ICA) with a supervised, multi-objective encoder in a trusted Source Environment that outputs low-dimensional, task-aligned latent representations exported to untrusted Training/Inference Environments. Provide mathematical/topological proofs of non-invertibility and adversarial intractability, and position VEIL as a scalable architecture with privacy-by-design guarantees.

Result: Encodings are structurally non-invertible; inversion is logically impossible under the proposed assumptions. In realistic deployments, attacker conditional entropy over original data diverges, driving reconstruction probability to zero. Predictive utility is preserved, enabling low-latency, high-performance ML without gradient clipping, noise budgets, or at-inference encryption. VEIL supports multi-region deployment with strict trust boundaries and aligns with post-quantum considerations.

Conclusion: Provides a novel architectural and theoretical foundation for enterprise ppML—secure, performant, and safe-by-design—by avoiding cryptographic leakage and noise by design, and is resilient to post-quantum threats through its non-invertible encodings and trusted environment boundaries.

Abstract: Modern machine learning systems increasingly rely on sensitive data, creating significant privacy, security, and regulatory risks that existing privacy-preserving machine learning (ppML) techniques, such as Differential Privacy (DP) and Homomorphic Encryption (HE), address only at the cost of degraded performance, increased complexity, or prohibitive computational overhead. This paper introduces Informationally Compressive Anonymization (ICA) and the VEIL architecture, a privacy-preserving ML framework that achieves strong privacy guarantees through architectural and mathematical design rather than noise injection or cryptography. ICA embeds a supervised, multi-objective encoder within a trusted Source Environment to transform raw inputs into low-dimensional, task-aligned latent representations, ensuring that only irreversibly anonymized vectors are exported to untrusted Training and Inference Environments. The paper rigorously proves that these encodings are structurally non-invertible using topological and information-theoretic arguments, showing that inversion is logically impossible, even under idealized attacker assumptions, and that, in realistic deployments, the attackers conditional entropy over the original data diverges, driving reconstruction probability to zero. Unlike prior autoencoder-based ppML approaches, ICA preserves predictive utility by aligning representation learning with downstream supervised objectives, enabling low-latency, high-performance ML without gradient clipping, noise budgets, or encryption at inference time. The VEIL architecture enforces strict trust boundaries, supports scalable multi-region deployment, and naturally aligns with privacy-by-design regulatory frameworks, establishing a new foundation for enterprise ML that is secure, performant, and safe by construction, even in the face of post-quantum threats.

</details>


### [164] [FlashSampling: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15854)
*Tomas Ruiz,Zhen Qin,Yifan Zhang,Xuyang Shen,Yiran Zhong,Mengdi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlashSampling is an exact sampling primitive that fuses sampling into the LM-head matmul, avoiding logits materialization and achieving up to 19% token-time speedups in vLLM, across multiple GPUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In large-vocabulary decoding, sampling triggers memory traffic and extra kernels after the LM head; there is a need for exact, efficient sampling integrated into computation to reduce bandwidth and postprocessing overhead.

Method: Compute logits tile-by-tile on-chip, add Gumbel noise, keep only one maximizer per row and per vocabulary tile, and finish with a small reduction over tiles. The fused tiled kernel is exact because argmax decomposes over a partition; grouped variants for online and tensor-parallel settings are exact by hierarchical factorization of the categorical distribution.

Result: Across H100, H200, B200, and B300 GPUs, FlashSampling speeds up kernel-level decode workloads. In end-to-end vLLM experiments, it reduces time per output token by up to 19% on the models tested.

Conclusion: Exact sampling, with no approximation, can be integrated into the matmul itself, turning a bandwidth-bound postprocessing step into a lightweight epilogue.

Abstract: Sampling from a categorical distribution is mathematically simple, but in large-vocabulary decoding, it often triggers extra memory traffic and extra kernels after the LM head. We present FlashSampling, an exact sampling primitive that fuses sampling into the LM-head matmul and never materializes the logits tensor in HBM. The method is simple: compute logits tile-by-tile on chip, add Gumbel noise, keep only one maximizer per row and per vocabulary tile, and finish with a small reduction over tiles. The fused tiled kernel is exact because $\argmax$ decomposes over a partition; grouped variants for online and tensor-parallel settings are exact by hierarchical factorization of the categorical distribution. Across H100, H200, B200, and B300 GPUs, FlashSampling speeds up kernel-level decode workloads, and in end-to-end vLLM experiments, it reduces time per output token by up to $19%$ on the models we test. These results show that exact sampling, with no approximation, can be integrated into the matmul itself, turning a bandwidth-bound postprocessing step into a lightweight epilogue. Project Page: https://github.com/FlashSampling/FlashSampling.

</details>


### [165] [Evaluating Black-Box Vulnerabilities with Wasserstein-Constrained Data Perturbations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15867)
*Adriana Laurindo Monteiro,Jean-Michel Loubes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Optimal Transport (Wasserstein) projection of input distributions under a constraint is used to analyze ML model responses to distribution shifts, with convergence guarantees and empirical validation in regression and classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address lack of explainability and robustness in ML by quantifying how model outputs respond to distributional changes via Wasserstein distances and constrained projection.

Method: Compute the Wasserstein-closest distribution to the input distribution that satisfies a given constraint, study its impact on model behavior, prove convergence of the projected distribution, and illustrate with real-world regression and classification datasets.

Result: The paper provides convergence results for the projected distribution and demonstrates the approach with examples and real-world data in both regression and classification tasks.

Conclusion: OT-based distribution projection is a viable framework for understanding and potentially mitigating the effects of distributional shifts on ML models, applicable across regression and classification domains.

Abstract: The massive use of Machine Learning (ML) tools in industry comes with critical challenges, such as the lack of explainable models and the use of black-box algorithms. We address this issue by applying Optimal Transport theory in the analysis of responses of ML models to variations in the distribution of input variables. We find the closest distribution, in the Wasserstein sense, that satisfies a given constraintt and examine its impact on model behavior. Furthermore, we establish convergence results for this projected distribution and demonstrate our approach using examples and real-world datasets in both regression and classification settings.

</details>


### [166] [Counteractive RL: Rethinking Core Principles for Efficient and Scalable Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15871)
*Ezgi Korkmaz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a counteractive-action-based learning paradigm for high-dimensional MDPs that accelerates training with zero extra computational cost and improved sample efficiency, validated on Arcade Learning Environment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of exponential state spaces in high-dimensional reinforcement learning, which creates a trade-off between computational complexity and policy performance; seek a scalable, efficient learning paradigm with minimal overhead.

Method: Proposes a theoretically-founded paradigm built on experiences from counteractive actions during environment interaction; emphasizes zero additional computational complexity.

Result: Theoretical analysis supported by extensive experiments in high-dimensional ALE tasks showing significant performance gains and strong sample efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed counteractive-action framework offers efficient, scalable, and accelerated learning without extra computation, achieving substantial gains in high-dimensional RL tasks.

Abstract: Following the pivotal success of learning strategies to win at tasks, solely by interacting with an environment without any supervision, agents have gained the ability to make sequential decisions in complex MDPs. Yet, reinforcement learning policies face exponentially growing state spaces in high dimensional MDPs resulting in a dichotomy between computational complexity and policy success. In our paper we focus on the agent's interaction with the environment in a high-dimensional MDP during the learning phase and we introduce a theoretically-founded novel paradigm based on experiences obtained through counteractive actions. Our analysis and method provide a theoretical basis for efficient, effective, scalable and accelerated learning, and further comes with zero additional computational complexity while leading to significant acceleration in training. We conduct extensive experiments in the Arcade Learning Environment with high-dimensional state representation MDPs. The experimental results further verify our theoretical analysis, and our method achieves significant performance increase with substantial sample-efficiency in high-dimensional environments.

</details>


### [167] [Electrodermal Activity as a Unimodal Signal for Aerobic Exercise Detection in Wearable Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15880)
*Rena Mira Krishna,Ramya Sankar,Shadi Ghiasi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EDA-alone features can moderately distinguish rest from sustained aerobic exercise in a subject-independent setting; phasic dynamics and event timing aid classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify EDA's standalone discriminative power for wearable activity-state inference and provide a conservative benchmark for unimodal EDA performance, given prior multimodal success.

Method: Use a publicly available dataset of 30 healthy individuals. Extract EDA features and evaluate with benchmark machine learning models using leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) cross-validation.

Result: EDA-only classifiers achieved moderate subject-independent performance across models; phasic temporal dynamics and event timing contributed to class separation.

Conclusion: EDA is not a replacement for multimodal sensing but provides a benchmark for its unimodal discriminative power in activity-state inference.

Abstract: Electrodermal Activity (EDA) is a non-invasive physiological signal widely available in wearable devices and reflects sympathetic nervous system (SNS) activation. Prior multi-modal studies have demonstrated robust performance in distinguishing stress and exercise states when EDA is combined with complementary signals such as heart rate and accelerometry. However, the ability of EDA to independently distinguish sustained aerobic exercise from low-arousal states under subject-independent evaluation remains insufficiently characterized.
  This study investigates whether features derived exclusively from EDA can reliably differentiate rest from sustained aerobic exercise. Using a publicly available dataset collected from thirty healthy individuals, EDA features were evaluated using benchmark machine learning models with leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) validation. Across models, EDA-only classifiers achieved moderate subject-independent performance, with phasic temporal dynamics and event timing contributing to class separation. Rather than proposing EDA as a replacement for multimodal sensing, this work provides a conservative benchmark of the discriminative power of EDA alone and clarifies its role as a unimodal input for wearable activity-state inference.

</details>


### [168] [PhasorFlow: A Python Library for Unit Circle Based Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15886)
*Dibakar Sigdel,Namuna Panday*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new unit-circle based computational framework (PhasorFlow) with a Phasor Circuit model, a variational extension, and a Phasor Transformer, enabling unitary-based learning on classical hardware; validated across diverse tasks; open-source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a deterministic, unitary computing paradigm on classical hardware that preserves global norm and enables gradient-based learning, offering an alternative to traditional neural networks and quantum circuits by operating on the unit circle with complex phasors.

Method: Formalize the Phasor Circuit model (N unit-circle threads, M gates) with a 22-gate library spanning Standard Unitary, Non-Linear, Neuromorphic, and Encoding operations and full matrix algebra simulation. Introduce a Variational Phasor Circuit (VPC) to optimize continuous phase parameters for ML tasks. Develop the Phasor Transformer, a parameter-free, DFT-based token mixer replacing QK^T V attention. Validate on non-linear spatial classification, time-series prediction, financial volatility detection, and neuromorphic tasks (neural binding, oscillatory associative memory).

Result: PhasorFlow demonstrates that unit circle computing can serve as a lightweight, deterministic alternative to classical neural networks and quantum circuits on standard hardware, leveraging unitary dynamics and continuous phase optimization to perform diverse learning tasks.

Conclusion: PhasorFlow establishes a principled, open-source framework for unit circle computing with formalized circuits, variational optimization, and a fast transform-based attention-like mechanism, offering a new computational paradigm with broad applicability and hardware compatibility.

Abstract: We present PhasorFlow, an open-source Python library introducing a computational paradigm operating on the $S^1$ unit circle. Inputs are encoded as complex phasors $z = e^{iθ}$ on the $N$-Torus ($\mathbb{T}^N$). As computation proceeds via unitary wave interference gates, global norm is preserved while individual components drift into $\mathbb{C}^N$, allowing algorithms to natively leverage continuous geometric gradients for predictive learning. PhasorFlow provides three core contributions. First, we formalize the Phasor Circuit model ($N$ unit circle threads, $M$ gates) and introduce a 22-gate library covering Standard Unitary, Non-Linear, Neuromorphic, and Encoding operations with full matrix algebra simulation. Second, we present the Variational Phasor Circuit (VPC), analogous to Variational Quantum Circuits (VQC), enabling optimization of continuous phase parameters for classical machine learning tasks. Third, we introduce the Phasor Transformer, replacing expensive $QK^TV$ attention with a parameter-free, DFT-based token mixing layer inspired by FNet. We validate PhasorFlow on non-linear spatial classification, time-series prediction, financial volatility detection, and neuromorphic tasks including neural binding and oscillatory associative memory. Our results establish unit circle computing as a deterministic, lightweight, and mathematically principled alternative to classical neural networks and quantum circuits. It operates on classical hardware while sharing quantum mechanics' unitary foundations. PhasorFlow is available at https://github.com/mindverse-computing/phasorflow.

</details>


### [169] [Federated Learning for Privacy-Preserving Medical AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15901)
*Tin Hoang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Privacy-preserving federated learning for Alzheimer's disease MRI classification using site-aware partitioning and Adaptive Local Differential Privacy (ALDP); achieves strong privacy-utility trade-offs with FedProx, up to 80.4% accuracy in two-client setups, outperforming fixed-noise DP


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address realistic data partitioning, stronger privacy guarantees, and benchmarking gaps in multi-institutional medical imaging FL to enable practical deployment

Method: Introduce site-aware data partitioning that preserves institutional boundaries; develop Adaptive Local Differential Privacy (ALDP) that tunes privacy parameters during training; leverage FedProx and other federated optimizers; evaluate across multiple client federations and privacy budgets with ablation studies

Result: ALDP improves privacy-utility trade-off over fixed-noise DP; FedProx performance matches or exceeds centralized training; two-client accuracy up to 80.4% with 5-7 percentage-point gains over fixed-noise DP; improved training stability and robust benchmarking across budgets

Conclusion:  Advances the state-of-the-art in privacy-preserving federated learning for medical imaging; provides methodological foundations and empirical benchmarks to guide real-world deployment and future research in privacy-compliant healthcare AI

Abstract: This dissertation investigates privacy-preserving federated learning for Alzheimer's disease classification using three-dimensional MRI data from the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI). Existing methodologies often suffer from unrealistic data partitioning, inadequate privacy guarantees, and insufficient benchmarking, limiting their practical deployment in healthcare. To address these gaps, this research proposes a novel site-aware data partitioning strategy that preserves institutional boundaries, reflecting real-world multi-institutional collaborations and data heterogeneity. Furthermore, an Adaptive Local Differential Privacy (ALDP) mechanism is introduced, dynamically adjusting privacy parameters based on training progression and parameter characteristics, thereby significantly improving the privacy-utility trade-off over traditional fixed-noise approaches. Systematic empirical evaluation across multiple client federations and privacy budgets demonstrated that advanced federated optimisation algorithms, particularly FedProx, could equal or surpass centralised training performance while ensuring rigorous privacy protection. Notably, ALDP achieved up to 80.4% accuracy in a two-client configuration, surpassing fixed-noise Local DP by 5-7 percentage points and demonstrating substantially greater training stability. The comprehensive ablation studies and benchmarking establish quantitative standards for privacy-preserving collaborative medical AI, providing practical guidelines for real-world deployment. This work thereby advances the state-of-the-art in federated learning for medical imaging, establishing both methodological foundations and empirical evidence necessary for future privacy-compliant AI adoption in healthcare.

</details>


### [170] [Game-Theory-Assisted Reinforcement Learning for Border Defense: Early Termination based on Analytical Solutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15907)
*Goutam Das,Michael Dorothy,Kyle Volle,Daigo Shishika*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hybrid RL + game-theoretic approach using Apollonius Circle to compute post-detection equilibrium and terminate episodes early, improving search efficiency and RL performance in a border defense game with limited perception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Game-theoretic guarantees can be brittle under imperfect information, while RL is adaptable but data-inefficient in large domains. The work aims to combine the strengths of both to improve training efficiency and maintain optimal continuation after detection in adversarial settings.

Method: Leverage Apollonius Circle to compute equilibrium in the post-detection phase and apply early termination of RL episodes, so RL concentrates on learning search strategies while guaranteeing optimal continuation after detection. The approach is evaluated in single- and multi-defender settings.

Result: RL achieves 10-20% higher rewards, faster convergence, and more efficient search trajectories, demonstrated through extensive experiments.

Conclusion: A hybrid method that integrates game-theoretic equilibrium computation with RL improves training efficiency and performance under limited perception, validating the practical benefits of combining these paradigms.

Abstract: Game theory provides the gold standard for analyzing adversarial engagements, offering strong optimality guarantees. However, these guarantees often become brittle when assumptions such as perfect information are violated. Reinforcement learning (RL), by contrast, is adaptive but can be sample-inefficient in large, complex domains. This paper introduces a hybrid approach that leverages game-theoretic insights to improve RL training efficiency. We study a border defense game with limited perceptual range, where defender performance depends on both search and pursuit strategies, making classical differential game solutions inapplicable. Our method employs the Apollonius Circle (AC) to compute equilibrium in the post-detection phase, enabling early termination of RL episodes without learning pursuit dynamics. This allows RL to concentrate on learning search strategies while guaranteeing optimal continuation after detection. Across single- and multi-defender settings, this early termination method yields 10-20% higher rewards, faster convergence, and more efficient search trajectories. Extensive experiments validate these findings and demonstrate the overall effectiveness of our approach.

</details>


### [171] [The Agentic Researcher: A Practical Guide to AI-Assisted Research in Mathematics and Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15914)
*Max Zimmer,Nico Pelleriti,Christophe Roux,Sebastian Pokutta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A practical, open-source framework and guide for AI-assisted research in math and ML, featuring a five-level integration taxonomy, an autonomous-agent framework built from CLI coding agents, sandboxed execution, and case studies; designed to augment researchers rather than replace them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To clarify how AI tools fit into everyday research practice, address guardrails and responsible use, and provide a scalable, reproducible workflow for AI-assisted experimentation in mathematics and deep learning.

Method: Three-part design: (I) five-level taxonomy of AI integration into research; (II) an open-source framework that encodes methodological rules as agent prompts enabling CLI coding agents (e.g., Claude Code, Codex CLI) to operate as autonomous research assistants within a sandboxed container; (III) case studies in deep learning and mathematics. The framework is containerized, CLI-agent-compatible, and scalable from laptop to multi-node clusters, aiming for minimal setup and reproducibility.

Result: Demonstrates autonomous experimentation sessions, with a longest run over 20 hours across multiple nodes without human intervention; provides a working implementation and documentation; code available at GitHub.

Conclusion: The framework aims to augment researchers by providing autonomous capabilities while preserving human-in-the-loop oversight; emphasizes guardrails, reproducibility, and accessibility; scalable and easy-to-install open-source solution.

Abstract: AI tools and agents are reshaping how researchers work, from proving theorems to training neural networks. Yet for many, it remains unclear how these tools fit into everyday research practice. This paper is a practical guide to AI-assisted research in mathematics and machine learning: We discuss how researchers can use modern AI systems productively, where these systems help most, and what kinds of guardrails are needed to use them responsibly. It is organized into three parts: (I) a five-level taxonomy of AI integration, (II) an open-source framework that, through a set of methodological rules formulated as agent prompts, turns CLI coding agents (e.g., Claude Code, Codex CLI, OpenCode) into autonomous research assistants, and (III) case studies from deep learning and mathematics. The framework runs inside a sandboxed container, works with any frontier LLM through existing CLI agents, is simple enough to install and use within minutes, and scales from personal-laptop prototyping to multi-node, multi-GPU experimentation across compute clusters. In practice, our longest autonomous session ran for over 20 hours, dispatching independent experiments across multiple nodes without human intervention. We stress that our framework is not intended to replace the researcher in the loop, but to augment them. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/ZIB-IOL/The-Agentic-Researcher.

</details>


### [172] [Auto Researching, not hyperparameter tuning: Convergence Analysis of 10,000 Experiments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15916)
*Xiaoyi Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM-guided combinatorial ML experiment design shows genuine architecture search rather than hyperparameter tuning; architectural choices explain ~94% of performance variance, hyperparameters ~6%; validated across a second dataset with different backbones; identifies best architecture (V-JEPA2 with Zipformer) and demonstrates efficient search focusing in productive architectural regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether autonomous LLM agents performing ML experiment design perform genuine architecture search or mostly tune hyperparameters within a narrow space, and to establish a large-scale empirical framework for LLM-driven combinatorial design.

Method: ANOVA decomposition across 108,000 discrete configuration cells sampled over 27 days for dashcam collision detection using two LLM agents (Claude Opus, Gemini 2.5 Pro); evaluate architectural choices vs hyperparameters; cross-task validation on a second collision dataset with different winner backbone; measure AP; analyze search dynamics via entropy cycles and Jensen–Shannon specialization; compare against baselines (random search, Bayesian).

Result: Architectural choices explain 94% of performance variance (F=1324, η^2=0.94); hyperparameter variation within fixed architecture explains 6%; cross-task validation shows 75% architecture-explained variance with a different backbone; identified V-JEPA2 video features with Zipformer encoders achieving 0.9245 AP (a configuration not proposed by humans); at N=50, LLM-guided search achieves AP=0.985 vs 0.965 for from-scratch random search; post-bugfix convergence follows a power law (c=0.11, R^2=0.93); multi-agent search dynamics characterized by entropy cycles and Jensen–Shannon specialization, establishing the first large-scale empirical framework for LLM-guided combinatorial ML experiment design.

Conclusion: The study provides compelling evidence that LLM agents perform genuine architecture discovery in combinatorial ML experiment design, not merely hyperparameter tuning; architectural search concentrates productivity, and LLMs can identify novel, high-performing backbones and configurations, supported by robust cross-task validation and analysis of search dynamics.

Abstract: When LLM agents autonomously design ML experiments, do they perform genuine architecture search -- or do they default to hyperparameter tuning within a narrow region of the design space? We answer this question by analyzing 10,469 experiments executed by two LLM agents (Claude Opus and Gemini 2.5 Pro) across a combinatorial configuration space of 108,000 discrete cells for dashcam collision detection over 27 days. Through ANOVA decomposition, we find that \textbf{architectural choices explain 94\% of performance variance} ($F = 1324$, $η^2 = 0.94$), while hyperparameter variation within a fixed architecture explains only 6\%. Cross-task validation on a second collision dataset confirms this finding (75\% architecture-explained variance) with a \emph{different} winning backbone, confirming genuine architecture discovery. The agents' key contribution is discovering that V-JEPA\,2 video features with Zipformer temporal encoders achieve 0.9245 AP -- a configuration no human proposed -- and concentrating search on productive architectural regions: at $N = 50$, LLM-guided search reaches AP $= 0.985$ versus $0.965$ for from-scratch random search. Post-bugfix convergence follows a power law ($c = 0.11$, $R^2 = 0.93$); the low exponent reflects the cost of broad exploration, not inefficiency, since the LLM discovers qualitatively better regions than random or Bayesian baselines. We characterize multi-agent search dynamics via entropy cycles and Jensen--Shannon specialization, providing the first large-scale empirical framework for LLM-guided combinatorial ML experiment design.

</details>


### [173] [Generative Inverse Design with Abstention via Diagonal Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15925)
*Miguel de Campos,Werner Krebs,Hanno Gottschalk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diag-CFM introduces a zero-anchoring diagonal flow matching scheme to solve permutation-order and scaling sensitivity in inverse-design, yielding large accuracy gains and new uncertainty metrics for candidate selection, abstention, and OOD detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address instability of standard conditional flow matching when adapting to inverse design by pairing labels with design parameters; enforce invariance to coordinate permutations and scaling; enable practical uncertainty-driven decision making.

Method: Zero-anchoring strategy that pairs design coordinates with noise and labels with zero, yielding a provably permutation-invariant learning problem; Diagonal Flow Matching (Diag-CFM); develops architecture-intrinsic uncertainty metrics (Zero-Deviation and Self-Consistency).

Result: Order-of-magnitude improvements in round-trip accuracy over CFM and invertible neural networks for design dimensions up to P=100; improved performance across airfoil, gas turbine combustor, and analytical benchmarks; metrics enable best-candidate selection, abstention, and OOD detection, outperforming ensembles and general-purpose methods.

Conclusion: Diag-CFM provides a stable, scalable inverse-design framework with provable invariance to coordinate permutation, coupled with practical uncertainty measures that enhance decision making and reliability in multi-physics design tasks.

Abstract: Inverse design aims to find design parameters $x$ achieving target performance $y^*$. Generative approaches learn bidirectional mappings between designs and labels, enabling diverse solution sampling. However, standard conditional flow matching (CFM), when adapted to inverse problems by pairing labels with design parameters, exhibits strong sensitivity to their arbitrary ordering and scaling, leading to unstable training. We introduce Diagonal Flow Matching (Diag-CFM), which resolves this through a zero-anchoring strategy that pairs design coordinates with noise and labels with zero, making the learning problem provably invariant to coordinate permutations. This yields order-of-magnitude improvements in round-trip accuracy over CFM and invertible neural network baselines across design dimensions up to $P{=}100$. We develop two architecture-intrinsic uncertainty metrics, Zero-Deviation and Self-Consistency, that enable three practical capabilities: selecting the best candidate among multiple generations, abstaining from unreliable predictions, and detecting out-of-distribution targets; consistently outperforming ensemble and general-purpose alternatives across all tasks. We validate on airfoil, gas turbine combustor, and an analytical benchmark with scalable design dimension.

</details>


### [174] [Evaluating Causal Discovery Algorithms for Path-Specific Fairness and Utility in Healthcare](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15926)
*Nitish Nagesh,Elahe Khatibi,Thomas Hughes,Mahdi Bagheri,Pratik Gajane,Amir M. Rahmani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evaluates PC, GES, and FCI causal-discovery algorithms using expert-constructed proxy graphs for synthetic Alzheimer's and heart failure data, focusing on structural recovery and path-specific fairness; PC excels structurally on synthetic data, while FCI yields highest utility on heart-failure data; path-specific effects (e.g., ejection fraction) influence fairness-utility trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ground truth for causal structure is typically unknown in health data; expert-driven proxy ground-truth graphs enable benchmarking of causal-discovery methods and a more nuanced, path-specific fairness assessment.

Method: Construct proxy ground-truth graphs with clinical experts; benchmark three algorithms (PC, GES, FCI) on synthetic AD data and heart-failure patient records; evaluate structural recovery and path-specific fairness decomposition, beyond aggregate fairness metrics.

Result: PC achieved the best structural recovery on synthetic data; FCI achieved the highest utility on heart-failure data. For path-specific effects, ejection fraction contributed 3.37 percentage points to the indirect effect in the ground truth, driving variations in the fairness-utility ratio across algorithms.

Conclusion: Graph-aware fairness evaluation and fine-grained path-specific analysis are necessary when deploying causal discovery in clinical settings; reliance on aggregate fairness may obscure method-specific trade-offs.

Abstract: Causal discovery in health data faces evaluation challenges when ground truth is unknown. We address this by collaborating with experts to construct proxy ground-truth graphs, establishing benchmarks for synthetic Alzheimer's disease and heart failure clinical records data. We evaluate the Peter-Clark, Greedy Equivalence Search, and Fast Causal Inference algorithms on structural recovery and path-specific fairness decomposition, going beyond composite fairness scores. On synthetic data, Peter-Clark achieved the best structural recovery. On heart failure data, Fast Causal Inference achieved the highest utility. For path-specific effects, ejection fraction contributed 3.37 percentage points to the indirect effect in the ground truth. These differences drove variations in the fairness-utility ratio across algorithms. Our results highlight the need for graph-aware fairness evaluation and fine-grained path-specific analysis when deploying causal discovery in clinical applications.

</details>


### [175] [Discovery of interaction and diffusion kernels in particle-to-mean-field multi-agent systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15927)
*Giacomo Albi,Alessandro Alla,Elisa Calzola*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Data-driven, sparse-regression framework to learn interaction and diffusion kernels in stochastic multi-agent systems from trajectory data; uses random-batch and mean-field strategies to handle latent interactions and partial observations; validated on benchmark models with accurate kernel reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify nonlocal interaction and diffusion terms without prior structure, directly from trajectories, despite latent pairwise interactions and limited data.

Method: Start from discrete stochastic binary-interaction model; formulate inverse problem as a sequence of sparse regression tasks in finite-dimensional spaces spanned by compactly supported basis functions (e.g., piecewise linear polynomials). Two strategies: (1) random-batch sampling to preserve statistical structure and account for latent interactions; (2) mean-field approximation using empirical density to define a continuous nonlocal regression problem.

Result: Numerical experiments demonstrate accurate reconstruction of both interaction and diffusion kernels even with partial observability; comparable accuracy between the two strategies across benchmark models including bounded-confidence and attraction-repulsion dynamics.

Conclusion: The framework provides a robust, data-driven route to recover nonlocal interaction and diffusion kernels from sparse/partially observed trajectory data, with flexibility to accommodate different interaction regimes via the two complementary strategies.

Abstract: We propose a data-driven framework to learn interaction kernels in stochastic multi-agent systems. Our approach aims at identifying the functional form of nonlocal interaction and diffusion terms directly from trajectory data, without any a priori knowledge of the underlying interaction structure. Starting from a discrete stochastic binary-interaction model, we formulate the inverse problem as a sequence of sparse regression tasks in structured finite-dimensional spaces spanned by compactly supported basis functions, such as piecewise linear polynomials. In particular, we assume that pairwise interactions between agents are not directly observed and that only limited trajectory data are available. To address these challenges, we propose two complementary identification strategies. The first based on random-batch sampling, which compensates for latent interactions while preserving the statistical structure of the full dynamics in expectation. The second based on a mean-field approximation, where the empirical particle density reconstructed from the data defines a continuous nonlocal regression problem. Numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed framework, showing accurate reconstruction of both interaction and diffusion kernels even from partially observed. The method is validated on benchmark models, including bounded-confidence and attraction-repulsion dynamics, where the two proposed strategies achieve comparable levels of accuracy.

</details>


### [176] [Data-Local Autonomous LLM-Guided Neural Architecture Search for Multiclass Multimodal Time-Series Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15939)
*Emil Hardarson,Luka Biedebach,Ómar Bessi Ómarsson,Teitur Hrólfsson,Anna Sigridur Islind,María Óskarsdóttir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A data-local, LLM-guided NAS framework for multimodal time-series learning that preserves patient data on-premise while enabling remote orchestration via trial summaries; improves a modular baseline on UEA30 and SleepEDFx and reduces manual intervention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy- and security-constrained domains (e.g., healthcare) require automated model/search workflows that do not expose raw data or intermediate representations, especially for multimodal time-series.

Method: Joint search over modality-specific preprocessing and one-vs-rest binary experts per class with a lightweight fusion MLP; LLM-guided search controlling a remote process while all training/evaluation runs locally under a fixed protocol; controller observes only trial-level descriptors (descriptors, metrics, learning curves, logs) and never accesses raw samples or features.

Result: The modular baseline is strong; LLM-guided NAS yields further improvements. Models achieve performance within published ranges on both datasets, across UEA30 and SleepEDFx, and the approach reduces manual intervention by enabling unattended architecture search with data kept on-site.

Conclusion: A viable approach for privacy-preserving automated NAS in multimodal time-series, enabling on-premise training with remote guidance; appropriate for sensitive domains and comparable to reported baselines across benchmarks.

Abstract: Applying machine learning to sensitive time-series data is often bottlenecked by the iteration loop: Performance depends strongly on preprocessing and architecture, yet training often has to run on-premise under strict data-local constraints. This is a common problem in healthcare and other privacy-constrained domains (e.g., a hospital developing deep learning models on patient EEG). This bottleneck is particularly challenging in multimodal fusion, where sensor modalities must be individually preprocessed and then combined. LLM-guided neural architecture search (NAS) can automate this exploration, but most existing workflows assume cloud execution or access to data-derived artifacts that cannot be exposed.
  We present a novel data-local, LLM-guided search framework that handles candidate pipelines remotely while executing all training and evaluation locally under a fixed protocol. The controller observes only trial-level summaries, such as pipeline descriptors, metrics, learning-curve statistics, and failure logs, without ever accessing raw samples or intermediate feature representations. Our framework targets multiclass, multimodal learning via one-vs-rest binary experts per class and modality, a lightweight fusion MLP, and joint search over expert architectures and modality-specific preprocessing.
  We evaluate our method on two regimes: UEA30 (public multivariate time-series classification dataset) and SleepEDFx sleep staging (heterogeneous clinical modalities such as EEG, EOG, and EMG). The results show that the modular baseline model is strong, and the LLM-guided NAS further improves it. Notably, our method finds models that perform within published ranges across most benchmark datasets. Across both settings, our method reduces manual intervention by enabling unattended architecture search while keeping sensitive data on-premise.

</details>


### [177] [MobileLLM-Flash: Latency-Guided On-Device LLM Design for Industry Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15954)
*Hanxian Huang,Igor Fedorov,Andrey Gromov,Bernard Beckerman,Naveen Suda,David Eriksson,Maximilian Balandat,Rylan Conway,Patrick Huber,Chinnadhurai Sankar,Ayushi Dalmia,Zechun Liu,Lemeng Wu,Tarek Elgamal,Adithya Sagar,Vikas Chandra,Raghuraman Krishnamoorthi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MobileLLM-Flash: a family of on-device LLMs (350M–1.4B) optimized via hardware-in-the-loop architecture search with attention skipping, enabling near real-time mobile performance without custom kernels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time AI on resource-constrained devices requires OD-LLMs that (i) run on common mobile hardware, (ii) deliver fast latency for interactive experiences, and (iii) scale deployment without bespoke kernels or runtimes.

Method: A hardware-in-the-loop architecture search under mobile latency constraints, jointly optimizing architecture (layers and dimensions) and attention patterns. Candidates are pruned versions of a pretrained backbone with inherited weights. A staged evaluation uses a latency model to quickly estimate performance and then finds the Pareto frontier across latency and quality. The design avoids specialized attention and uses attention skipping for long-context acceleration, and targets compatibility with standard mobile runtimes like Executorch.

Result: Introduction of MobileLLM-Flash family (350M, 650M, 1.4B) supporting up to 8k context. Achieves up to 1.8x faster prefill and 1.6x faster decode on mobile CPUs with comparable or superior quality. Provides actionable Pareto-frontier design insights for OD-LLM development.

Conclusion: A latency-conscious, hardware-in-the-loop, attention-skipping strategy yields deployable on-device LLMs across multiple scales with broad hardware compatibility and practical performance gains, plus design principles for OD-LLM optimization.

Abstract: Real-time AI experiences call for on-device large language models (OD-LLMs) optimized for efficient deployment on resource-constrained hardware. The most useful OD-LLMs produce near-real-time responses and exhibit broad hardware compatibility, maximizing user reach. We present a methodology for designing such models using hardware-in-the-loop architecture search under mobile latency constraints. This system is amenable to industry-scale deployment: it generates models deployable without custom kernels and compatible with standard mobile runtimes like Executorch. Our methodology avoids specialized attention mechanisms and instead uses attention skipping for long-context acceleration.
  Our approach jointly optimizes model architecture (layers, dimensions) and attention pattern. To efficiently evaluate candidates, we treat each as a pruned version of a pretrained backbone with inherited weights, thereby achieving high accuracy with minimal continued pretraining. We leverage the low cost of latency evaluation in a staged process: learning an accurate latency model first, then searching for the Pareto-frontier across latency and quality.
  This yields MobileLLM-Flash, a family of foundation models (350M, 650M, 1.4B) for efficient on-device use with strong capabilities, supporting up to 8k context length. MobileLLM-Flash delivers up to 1.8x and 1.6x faster prefill and decode on mobile CPUs with comparable or superior quality. Our analysis of Pareto-frontier design choices offers actionable principles for OD-LLM design.

</details>


### [178] [GASP: Guided Asymmetric Self-Play For Coding LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15957)
*Swadesh Jana,Cansu Sancaktar,Tomáš Daniš,Georg Martius,Antonio Orvieto,Pavel Kolev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Grounded curriculum-guided asymmetric self-play (GASP) for LMs; uses real-data goalpost questions; teacher creates an easier-to-harder curriculum to approach hard goals, improving pass@20 on LiveCodeBench by 2.5% vs unguided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of grounding and informativeness in goal-agnostic asymmetric self-play: not all hard problems drive learning; need data-grounded curricula to steer exploration toward informative tasks.

Method: Introduce Guided Asymmetric Self-Play (GASP). A teacher uses real-data goalpost questions to define a curriculum. During self-play, the teacher first generates an easier variant of a hard question, then a harder variant of that easier question, aiming to progressively close the gap to the goalpost.

Result: Pass@20 on LiveCodeBench improves by 2.5% over unguided asymmetric self-play; the constructed curriculum enables solving hard goalpost questions that exceed baseline capabilities.

Conclusion: Grounding asymmetric self-play with a data-driven curriculum improves data efficiency and task reach, yielding measurable gains and enabling access to previously unreachable goals.

Abstract: Asymmetric self-play has emerged as a promising paradigm for post-training large language models, where a teacher continually generates questions for a student to solve at the edge of the student's learnability. Although these methods promise open-ended data generation bootstrapped from no human data, they suffer from one major problem: not all problems that are hard to solve are interesting or informative to improve the overall capabilities of the model. Current asymmetric self-play methods are goal-agnostic with no real grounding. We propose Guided Asymmetric Self-Play (GASP), where grounding is provided by real-data goalpost questions that are identified to pose a hard exploration challenge to the model. During self-play, the teacher first generates an easier variant of a hard question, and then a harder variant of that easier question, with the goal of gradually closing the gap to the goalpost throughout training. Doing so, we improve pass@20 on LiveCodeBench (LCB) by 2.5% over unguided asymmetric self-play, and through the curriculum constructed by the teacher, we manage to solve hard goalpost questions that remain out of reach for all baselines.

</details>


### [179] [Deriving Hyperparameter Scaling Laws via Modern Optimization Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15958)
*Egor Shulgin,Dimitri von Rütte,Tianyue H. Zhang,Niccolò Ajroldi,Bernhard Schölkopf,Antonio Orvieto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Derives principled hyperparameter scaling laws for first-order optimizers using convergence bounds from Linear Minimization Oracle (LMO) methods, yielding closed-form power-law schedules for learning rate, momentum, and batch size as a function of iteration or token budget; highlights momentum–batch-size interactions and suggests multiple viable scaling strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hyperparameter transfer across training regimes (model size, batch size, compute horizon) is essential for scalable, efficient training. A unified, theory-driven framework is sought to explain and guide scaling rules beyond empirical heuristics.

Method: Utilizes convergence bounds from LMO-based optimization methods (normalized SGD, signSGD approximating Adam, Muon) as a proxy objective. By minimizing these bounds across different tuning regimes with fixed model size, derives closed-form power-law schedules for key hyperparameters.

Result: Obtains closed-form power-law schedules for learning rate, momentum, and batch size as functions of iteration or token budget. Reproduces many existing empirical scaling insights within a unified theoretical framework; reveals a nontrivial interaction between momentum and batch-size scaling and suggests multiple viable scaling strategies for optimal performance.

Conclusion: Provides a unified, principled perspective on hyperparameter scaling for modern optimizers under a fixed model size. Opens directions for future work, particularly in exploring the momentum–batch-size interaction and validating the proposed scaling laws across diverse architectures and workloads.

Abstract: Hyperparameter transfer has become an important component of modern large-scale training recipes. Existing methods, such as muP, primarily focus on transfer between model sizes, with transfer across batch sizes and training horizons often relying on empirical scaling rules informed by insights from timescale preservation, quadratic proxies, and continuous-time approximations. We study hyperparameter scaling laws for modern first-order optimizers through the lens of recent convergence bounds for methods based on the Linear Minimization Oracle (LMO), a framework that includes normalized SGD, signSGD (approximating Adam), and Muon. Treating bounds in recent literature as a proxy and minimizing them across different tuning regimes yields closed-form power-law schedules for learning rate, momentum, and batch size as functions of the iteration or token budget. Our analysis, holding model size fixed, recovers most insights and observations from the literature under a unified and principled perspective, with clear directions open for future research. Our results draw particular attention to the interaction between momentum and batch-size scaling, suggesting that optimal performance may be achieved with several scaling strategies.

</details>


### [180] [Determinism in the Undetermined: Deterministic Output in Charge-Conserving Continuous-Time Neuromorphic Systems with Temporal Stochasticity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15987)
*Jing Yan,Kang You,Zhezhi He,Yaoyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified continuous-time, charge-conserving SNN framework yields deterministic, timing-invariant outputs in acyclic networks and exact representational equivalence to quantized ANNs, enabling deterministic asynchronous neuromorphic computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome intrinsic temporal stochasticity in asynchronous neuromorphic hardware and provide a theoretical basis for deterministic computation by unifying spiking dynamics with charge conservation.

Method: Develop a continuous-time framework that enforces charge conservation with minimal neuron constraints; prove terminal state depends solely on aggregate input charge; demonstrate spike-timing invariance in acyclic networks; analyze effect of recurrence; establish an exact mapping between charge-conserving SNNs and quantized artificial neural networks.

Result: Terminal state is invariant to spike timing in acyclic networks; recurrent connections can introduce temporal sensitivity; there exists an exact representational correspondence between charge-conserving SNNs and quantized ANNs, enabling no-approximation transfer between continuous-time and static neural representations.

Conclusion: Provides a rigorous theoretical basis for designing continuous-time neuromorphic systems that combine asynchronous processing efficiency with algorithmic determinism and enables precise alignment with quantized neural networks.

Abstract: Achieving deterministic computation results in asynchronous neuromorphic systems remains a fundamental challenge due to the inherent temporal stochasticity of continuous-time hardware. To address this, we develop a unified continuous-time framework for spiking neural networks (SNNs) that couples the Law of Charge Conservation with minimal neuron-level constraints. This integration ensures that the terminal state depends solely on the aggregate input charge, providing a unique cumulated output invariant to temporal stochasticity. We prove that this mapping is strictly invariant to spike timing in acyclic networks, whereas recurrent connectivity can introduce temporal sensitivity. Furthermore, we establish an exact representational correspondence between these charge-conserving SNNs and quantized artificial neural networks, bridging the gap between static deep learning and event-driven dynamics without approximation errors. These results establish a rigorous theoretical basis for designing continuous-time neuromorphic systems that harness the efficiency of asynchronous processing while maintaining algorithmic determinism.

</details>


### [181] [W2T: LoRA Weights Already Know What They Can Do](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15990)
*Xiaolong Han,Ferrante Neri,Zijian Jiang,Fang Wu,Yanfang Ye,Lu Yin,Zehong Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Weight2Token (W2T) canonically encodes LoRA updates by removing factorization ambiguity (via QR and SVD) and turns them into tokenized components processed by a Transformer to produce a weight-space embedding, enabling readout of model behavior from LoRA weights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA updates can be factorized in infinitely many ways, making it impossible to deduce the underlying update or model behavior from weights alone without access to training data. A canonical representation is needed for interpretability and auditing.

Method: Compute a canonical form of each LoRA update by applying QR decomposition followed by SVD to obtain a unique representation. Tokenize the resulting components and feed them through a Transformer to produce a weight-space embedding.

Result: W2T achieves strong results across language and vision LoRA collections on tasks such as attribute classification, performance prediction, and adapter retrieval, demonstrating that LoRA weights reliably indicate model behavior once factorization ambiguity is removed.

Conclusion: Canonicalizing LoRA updates enables reliable interpretation of what the adapter encodes directly from weights; W2T provides a practical pipeline and code for reading model behavior from LoRA components.

Abstract: Each LoRA checkpoint compactly stores task-specific updates in low-rank weight matrices, offering an efficient way to adapt large language models to new tasks and domains. In principle, these weights already encode what the adapter does and how well it performs. In this paper, we ask whether this information can be read directly from the weights, without running the base model or accessing training data. A key obstacle is that a single LoRA update can be factorized in infinitely many ways. Without resolving this ambiguity, models trained on the factors may fit the particular factorization rather than the underlying update. To this end, we propose \methodfull, which maps each LoRA update to a provably canonical form via QR decomposition followed by SVD, so that all equivalent factorizations share the same representation. The resulting components are then tokenized and processed by a Transformer to produce a weight-space embedding. Across language and vision LoRA collections, W2T achieves strong results on attribute classification, performance prediction, and adapter retrieval, demonstrating that LoRA weights reliably indicate model behavior once factorization ambiguity is removed. Code is available at https://github.com/xiaolonghan2000/Weight2Token.

</details>


### [182] [The Importance of Being Smoothly Calibrated](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16015)
*Parikshit Gopalan,Konstantinos Stavropoulos,Kunal Talwar,Pranay Tankala*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Generalizes smooth calibration and links it to omniprediction for bounded proper losses; shows that smoothing plus post-processing yields guarantees against smoothed benchmarks; introduces an earth mover distance-based characterization of smooth calibration; proves fundamental estimation limits for upper distance to calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify and extend smooth calibration, make it a robust calibration measure, and enable omniprediction where predictions support low regret for downstream decision makers with unknown proper losses.

Method: Add noise to predictions (smooth the predictor), compare against smoothed benchmarks, and post-process arbitrarily. Bound omniprediction error by the predictor's smooth calibration error plus the earth mover's distance to the closest perfectly calibrated joint distribution. Provide constructive instances showing the bound is tight in general. Characterize smooth calibration via earth mover's distance to the closest perfectly calibrated (prediction, label) joint distribution. Prove upper distance to calibration cannot be estimated within a quadratic factor with sample complexity independent of support size, contrasting with results for the standard distance to calibration.

Result: A new omniprediction guarantee for all bounded proper losses; a crisp earth mover distance-based characterization of smooth calibration; a proof of tightness for the dependence on smooth calibration and earth mover distance; a separation result on sample complexity for estimating upper distance to calibration.

Conclusion: The work unifies and extends prior results on omniprediction from smooth calibration, provides a simpler proof framework via earth mover distances, and establishes fundamental limits on estimating calibration-related distances from samples.

Abstract: Recent work has highlighted the centrality of smooth calibration [Kakade and Foster, 2008] as a robust measure of calibration error.
