We generalize, unify, and extend previous results on smooth calibration, both as a robust calibration measure, and as a step towards omniprediction, which enables predictions with low regret for downstream decision makers seeking to optimize some proper loss unknown to the predictor.
  We present a new omniprediction guarantee for smoothly calibrated predictors, for the class of all bounded proper losses. We smooth the predictor by adding some noise to it, and compete against smoothed versions of any benchmark predictor on the space, where we add some noise to the predictor and then post-process it arbitrarily. The omniprediction error is bounded by the smooth calibration error of the predictor and the earth mover's distance from the benchmark. We exhibit instances showing that this dependence cannot, in general, be improved. We show how this unifies and extends prior results [Foster and Vohra, 1998; Hartline, Wu, and Yang, 2025] on omniprediction from smooth calibration.
  We present a crisp new characterization of smooth calibration in terms of the earth mover's distance to the closest perfectly calibrated joint distribution of predictions and labels. This also yields a simpler proof of the relation to the lower distance to calibration from [Blasiok, Gopalan, Hu, and Nakkiran, 2023].
  We use this to show that the upper distance to calibration cannot be estimated within a quadratic factor with sample complexity independent of the support size of the predictions. This is in contrast to the distance to calibration, where the corresponding problem was known to be information-theoretically impossible: no finite number of samples suffice [Blasiok, Gopalan, Hu, and Nakkiran, 2023].

</details>


### [183] [Residual Stream Duality in Modern Transformer Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16039)
*Yifan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-axis Transformer perspective (Transformer^2) reveals a operator-level duality between depth-wise residual processing and sequence-wise short-window attention, guiding architectural choices: use Deep Delta Learning (DDL) to modify residual shortcuts, or sequence-axis ShortSWA for local adaptive mixing; practical guidelines depend on hardware and goal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Organize the Transformer design space by two ordered axes—sequence position and layer depth—to understand how residual connections interact with attention, and to unify disparate approaches under a common framework (the residual stream duality).

Method: Conceptual analysis of the residual stream as a depth-axis operator and self-attention as a sequence-axis operator; formalizes a duality between depth-wise residual reading and short-window attention; situates existing methods (ELC-BERT, DenseFormer, Vertical Attention, DCA, MUDDFormer, Attention Residuals) within this framework; discusses hardware implications and intervention strategies (DDL vs ShortSWA).

Result: Identifies an operator-level duality: depth-wise residual read is equivalent in behavior to causal ShortSWA when viewed along the depth axis; clarifies that this duality does not imply system-level symmetry; provides design guidance: favor ShortSWA for hardware-friendly sequence locality, and prefer Deep Delta Learning to modify the residual shortcut itself; integrates with existing literature through concrete examples.

Conclusion: A practical blueprint for Transformer design: use DDL when the target is the residual shortcut; use sequence-axis ShortSWA for local adaptive mixing; the two-axis framework (Transformer^2) clarifies how to navigate residual pathways and attention to improve efficiency and expressivity.

Abstract: Recent work has made clear that the residual pathway is not mere optimization plumbing; it is part of the model's representational machinery. We agree, but argue that the cleanest way to organize this design space is through a two-axis view of the Transformer. A decoder evolves information along two ordered dimensions: sequence position and layer depth. Self-attention already provides adaptive mixing along the sequence axis, whereas the residual stream usually performs fixed addition along the depth axis. If we fix a token position and treat layer index as the ordered variable, then a causal depth-wise residual attention read is exactly the same local operator as causal short sliding-window attention (ShortSWA), except written over depth rather than over sequence. This is the core residual stream duality behind Transformer$^2$. This perspective also clarifies the recent literature. ELC-BERT and DenseFormer already show that learned aggregation over depth can outperform uniform residual accumulation, while Vertical Attention, DeepCrossAttention (DCA), MUDDFormer, and Attention Residuals move further toward explicit attention-based routing over earlier layers. The key point, however, is that operator-level duality does not imply systems-level symmetry. For large-scale autoregressive models, sequence-axis ShortSWA is usually the more hardware-friendly placement because it reuses token-side sliding-window kernels, KV-cache layouts, and chunked execution. If the goal is instead to change the shortcut itself, Deep Delta Learning (DDL) is the cleaner intervention because it modifies the residual operator directly rather than adding a separate cross-layer retrieval path. Our recommendation is therefore simple: use DDL when the shortcut is the object of interest, and use sequence-axis ShortSWA when the goal is local adaptive mixing.

</details>


### [184] [Collaborative Temporal Feature Generation via Critic-Free Reinforcement Learning for Cross-User Sensor-Based Activity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16043)
*Xiaozhou Ye,Feng Jiang,Zihan Wang,Xiulai Wang,Yutao Zhang,Kevin I-Kai Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Transformer-based autoregressive feature generator (CTFG) with Group-Relative Policy Optimization achieves state-of-the-art cross-user HAR on DSADS and PAMAP2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-user variability in HAR due to physiology, habits, and sensor placement; existing domain generalization methods lack temporal dependencies or require target-domain annotations; need stable, critic-free optimization.

Method: Transformer-based autoregressive generator produces feature token sequences conditioned on input; Group-Relative Policy Optimization uses intra-group normalization to compare generated sequences to in-sample alternatives, no critic; tri-objective reward aligning class discrimination, cross-user invariance, and temporal fidelity.

Result: State-of-the-art cross-user accuracy (88.53% on DSADS, 75.22% on PAMAP2); reduced variance, faster convergence; robust generalization across varying action-space dimensionalities.

Conclusion: CTFG demonstrates effective generalizable feature extraction via collaborative sequential generation and critic-free optimization, mitigating distribution shift and preserving temporal structure; promising for scalable HAR across diverse users.

Abstract: Human Activity Recognition using wearable inertial sensors is foundational to healthcare monitoring, fitness analytics, and context-aware computing, yet its deployment is hindered by cross-user variability arising from heterogeneous physiological traits, motor habits, and sensor placements. Existing domain generalization approaches either neglect temporal dependencies in sensor streams or depend on impractical target-domain annotations. We propose a different paradigm: modeling generalizable feature extraction as a collaborative sequential generation process governed by reinforcement learning. Our framework, CTFG (Collaborative Temporal Feature Generation), employs a Transformer-based autoregressive generator that incrementally constructs feature token sequences, each conditioned on prior context and the encoded sensor input. The generator is optimized via Group-Relative Policy Optimization, a critic-free algorithm that evaluates each generated sequence against a cohort of alternatives sampled from the same input, deriving advantages through intra-group normalization rather than learned value estimation. This design eliminates the distribution-dependent bias inherent in critic-based methods and provides self-calibrating optimization signals that remain stable across heterogeneous user distributions. A tri-objective reward comprising class discrimination, cross-user invariance, and temporal fidelity jointly shapes the feature space to separate activities, align user distributions, and preserve fine-grained temporal content. Evaluations on the DSADS and PAMAP2 benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art cross-user accuracy (88.53\% and 75.22\%), substantial reduction in inter-task training variance, accelerated convergence, and robust generalization under varying action-space dimensionalities.

</details>


### [185] [Adaptive regularization parameter selection for high-dimensional inverse problems: A Bayesian approach with Tucker low-rank constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16066)
*Qing-Mei Yang,Da-Qing Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A variational Bayesian method with Tucker decomposition for large-scale inverse problems; adaptive per-mode regularization; data-driven noise estimation; outperforms standard criteria; scalable but sensitive to Tucker rank; needs theoretical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle computational bottlenecks of variational inference in high-dimensional inverse problems and enable anisotropic regularization aligned with physical structure; estimate noise without fixed priors.

Method: Integrates Tucker decomposition to compress the parameter space into a core tensor; introduces per-mode precision (regularization) parameters for adaptive, anisotropic support; jointly estimates noise level from data; evaluates against discrepancy principle and common model-selection criteria; applies to 2D deblurring, 3D heat conduction, and Fredholm integral equations.

Result: Shows consistent improvements in quantitative metrics (PSNR, SSIM) and qualitative error maps compared with L-curve, GCV, UPRE, and DP; scales to about 110k variables; gains of ~0.73–2.09 dB in deblurring and ~6.75 dB in 3D heat conduction.

Conclusion: Proposes a Bayesian-sparse, scalable framework bridging theory and computation for large-scale inverse problems; promising for imaging, remote sensing, and scientific computing; limitations include sensitivity to Tucker rank selection and lack of theoretical guarantees; future work to automate rank selection and provide formal guarantees.

Abstract: This paper introduces a novel variational Bayesian method that integrates Tucker decomposition for efficient high-dimensional inverse problem solving. The method reduces computational complexity by transforming variational inference from a high-dimensional space to a lower-dimensional core tensor space via Tucker decomposition. A key innovation is the introduction of per-mode precision parameters, enabling adaptive regularization for anisotropic structures. For instance, in directional image deblurring, learned parameters align with physical anisotropy, applying stronger regularization to critical directions (e.g., row vs. column axes). The method further estimates noise levels from data, eliminating reliance on prior knowledge of noise parameters (unlike conventional benchmarks such as the discrepancy principle (DP)). Experimental evaluations across 2D deblurring, 3D heat conduction, and Fredholm integral equations demonstrate consistent improvements in quantitative metrics (PSNR, SSIM) and qualitative visualizations (error maps, precision parameter trends) compared to L-curve criterion, generalized cross-validation (GCV), unbiased predictive risk estimator (UPRE), and DP. The approach scales to problems with 110,000 variables and outperforms existing methods by 0.73-2.09 dB in deblurring tasks and 6.75 dB in 3D heat conduction. Limitations include sensitivity to rank selection in Tucker decomposition and the need for theoretical analysis. Future work will explore automated rank selection and theoretical guarantees. This method bridges Bayesian theory and scalable computation, offering practical solutions for large-scale inverse problems in imaging, remote sensing, and scientific computing.

</details>


### [186] [MDM-Prime-v2: Binary Encoding and Index Shuffling Enable Compute-optimal Scaling of Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16077)
*Chen-Hao Chao,Wei-Fang Sun,Junwei Qua,Chun-Yi Lee,Rahul G. Krishnan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MDM-Prime addresses limitations in token granularity guidance and subtokenizer likelihood with BPE; MDM-Prime-v2 introduces Binary Encoding and Index Shuffling to tighten the variational bound and boost efficiency, achieving state-rich perplexity and zero-shot reasoning gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two limitations: (1) lack of tools to guide token granularity in subtokenizers for MDM-Prime; (2) subtokenizer function form with BPE reduces likelihood estimates. The work aims to improve theory (variational bound tightness) and practice (more efficient encoding) and to quantify compute efficiency.

Method: The authors analyze the tightness of the variational bound in MDM-Prime and propose MDM-Prime-v2, which uses Binary Encoding and Index Shuffling. They perform scaling analyses to compare compute efficiency against autoregressive models, and benchmark perplexity on OpenWebText, plus zero-shot commonsense tasks at 1.1B parameters.

Result: MDM-Prime-v2 is 21.8× more compute-efficient than autoregressive models. In compute-optimal settings, it attains 7.77 perplexity on OpenWebText, better than ARM (12.99), MDM (18.94), and MDM-Prime (13.41). At 1.1B parameters, it shows superior zero-shot accuracy on various commonsense reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: MDM-Prime-v2 resolves key limitations of MDM-Prime by adopting Binary Encoding and Index Shuffling, yielding tighter variational bounds, higher computational efficiency, and improved downstream performance, suggesting a promising direction for diffusion-based language modeling with subtokenizers.

Abstract: Masked diffusion models (MDM) exhibit superior generalization when learned using a Partial masking scheme (Prime). This approach converts tokens into sub-tokens and models the diffusion process at the sub-token level. We identify two limitations of the MDM-Prime framework. First, we lack tools to guide the hyperparameter choice of the token granularity in the subtokenizer. Second, we find that the function form of the subtokenizer significantly degrades likelihood estimation when paired with commonly used Byte-Pair-Encoding (BPE) tokenizers. To address these limitations, we study the tightness of the variational bound in MDM-Prime and develop MDM-Prime-v2, a masked diffusion language model which incorporates Binary Encoding and Index Shuffling. Our scaling analysis reveals that MDM-Prime-v2 is 21.8$\times$ more compute-efficient than autoregressive models (ARM). In compute-optimal comparisons, MDM-Prime-v2 achieves 7.77 perplexity on OpenWebText, outperforming ARM (12.99), MDM (18.94), and MDM-Prime (13.41). When extending the model size to 1.1B parameters, our model further demonstrates superior zero-shot accuracy on various commonsense reasoning tasks.

</details>


### [187] [A Depth-Aware Comparative Study of Euclidean and Hyperbolic Graph Neural Networks on Bitcoin Transaction Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16080)
*Ankit Ghimire,Saydul Akbar Murad,Nick Rahimi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A controlled comparison of Euclidean and tangent-space hyperbolic GNNs for node classification on a large Bitcoin transaction graph shows that embedding geometry and neighborhood depth influence performance, with stability of high-dimensional hyperbolic embeddings requiring careful joint tuning of learning rate and curvature; results inform practical deployment of hyperbolic GNNs in large-scale transactional networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how embedding geometry (Euclidean vs hyperbolic) interacts with receptive field size in GNNs applied to large-scale transaction networks, and to derive practical guidance for deploying hyperbolic GNNs for fraud detection in blockchain data.

Method: Systematic, controlled experiments that vary neighborhood depth while keeping model architecture and dimensionality fixed; compare Euclidean GNNs and tangent-space hyperbolic GNNs on a large Bitcoin transaction graph; analyze embedding geometry, optimization behavior, and the effect of curvature and learning rate on stability.

Result: Found measurable differences between Euclidean and hyperbolic embedding spaces; joint selection of learning rate and curvature is crucial for stabilizing high-dimensional hyperbolic embeddings; the study yields practical insights into how embedding geometry and neighborhood depth affect performance in large-scale transaction networks.

Conclusion: Embedding geometry and neighborhood depth significantly affect performance and stability in GNNs for large blockchain graphs; with careful tuning of curvature and learning rate, hyperbolic GNNs can be effectively deployed for node classification in cryptocurrency networks.

Abstract: Bitcoin transaction networks are large scale socio- technical systems in which activities are represented through multi-hop interaction patterns. Graph Neural Networks(GNNs) have become a widely adopted tool for analyzing such systems, supporting tasks such as entity detection and transaction classification. Large-scale datasets like Elliptic have allowed for a rise in the analysis of these systems and in tasks such as fraud detection. In these settings, the amount of transactional context available to each node is determined by the neighborhood aggregation and sampling strategies, yet the interaction between these receptive fields and embedding geometry has received limited attention. In this work, we conduct a controlled comparison of Euclidean and tangent-space hyperbolic GNNs for node classification on a large Bitcoin transaction graph. By explicitly varying the neighborhood while keeping the model architecture and dimensionality fixed, we analyze the differences in two embedding spaces. We further examine optimization behavior and observe that joint selection of learning rate and curvature plays a critical role in stabilizing high-dimensional hyperbolic embeddings. Overall, our findings provide practical insights into the role of embedding geometry and neighborhood depth when modeling large-scale transaction networks, informing the deployment of hyperbolic GNNs for computational social systems.

</details>


### [188] [Functorial Neural Architectures from Higher Inductive Types](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16123)
*Karen Sargsyan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decoders that are functorial (strict monoidal) enable compositional generalization; standard softmax attention is not functorial; HIT-inspired architectures compiled via monoidal functors show empirical gains across spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the systematic failure of neural nets on compositional generalization by recasting decoding as a functorial, category-theoretic problem; provide guarantees, impossibility results, and a formal framework.

Method: Build decoders as monoidal functors from the path groupoid of a target space to a category of parametric maps. Map path constructors to generator networks; let composition become structural concatenation; treat 2-cells (group relations) as learned natural transformations. Prove that decoders assembled by structural concatenation are strict monoidal (compositional by construction) and that softmax self-attention is not functorial for non-trivial tasks. Formalize in Cubical Agda and compile Higher Inductive Type (HIT) specifications into neural architectures. Validate the full hierarchy empirically on spaces: torus Z^2, wedge of circles S^1 ∨ S^1 (free group F2), and Klein bottle Z ⋊ Z, comparing functorial vs non-functorial decoders; report accuracy/improvement numbers.

Result: Demonstrate that functorial decoders outperform non-functorial ones by 2–2.7x on the torus; the type-A/B gap widens to 5.5–10x on the wedge of circles; on the Klein bottle, a learned 2-cell reduces a 46% error gap for words exercising the group relation.

Conclusion: Formalizing decoders as (strict) monoidal functors provides compositional guarantees and a clear hierarchy for neural architectures; HIT-inspired compilation and category-theoretic design offer practical improvements and a solid theoretical basis for compositional generalization, with empirical validation across topological spaces.

Abstract: Neural networks systematically fail at compositional generalization -- producing correct outputs for novel combinations of known parts. We show that this failure is architectural: compositional generalization is equivalent to functoriality of the decoder, and this perspective yields both guarantees and impossibility results. We compile Higher Inductive Type (HIT) specifications into neural architectures via a monoidal functor from the path groupoid of a target space to a category of parametric maps: path constructors become generator networks, composition becomes structural concatenation, and 2-cells witnessing group relations become learned natural transformations. We prove that decoders assembled by structural concatenation of independently generated segments are strict monoidal functors (compositional by construction), while softmax self-attention is not functorial for any non-trivial compositional task. Both results are formalized in Cubical Agda. Experiments on three spaces validate the full hierarchy: on the torus ($\mathbb{Z}^2$), functorial decoders outperform non-functorial ones by 2-2.7x; on $S^1 \vee S^1$ ($F_2$), the type-A/B gap widens to 5.5-10x; on the Klein bottle ($\mathbb{Z} \rtimes \mathbb{Z}$), a learned 2-cell closes a 46% error gap on words exercising the group relation.

</details>


### [189] [Noisy Data is Destructive to Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16140)
*Yuxuan Zhu,Daniel Kang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR claims robust learning from noisy labels are invalidated after data contamination is corrected; with verified data, noise harms performance and current RLVR methods fail to mitigate it; high-quality data remains essential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the claim that RLVR can learn effectively from incorrect annotations; determine if previous improvements were due to data leakage (contaminated data) rather than algorithmic robustness.

Method: Re-verify the noisy dataset with a rigorous pipeline; compare RLVR improvements to a baseline (GRPO); evaluate on mathematical reasoning benchmarks and a Text2SQL task with real-world noise; quantify performance gaps between noisy and clean data.

Result: After correction, noise is destructive to RLVR; RLVR improvements do not outperform basic baselines; models trained on truly incorrect annotations perform 8-10% worse on math benchmarks and 5-12% worse on Text2SQL than models trained on clean data.

Conclusion: Current RLVR methods cannot compensate for poor data quality; emphasis should be on obtaining and verifying high-quality data.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has driven recent capability advances of large language models across various domains. Recent studies suggest that improved RLVR algorithms allow models to learn effectively from incorrect annotations, achieving performance comparable to learning from clean data. In this work, we show that these findings are invalid because the claimed 100% noisy training data is "contaminated" with clean data. After rectifying the dataset with a rigorous re-verification pipeline, we demonstrate that noise is destructive to RLVR. We show that existing RLVR algorithm improvements fail to mitigate the impact of noise, achieving similar performance to that of the basic GRPO. Furthermore, we find that the model trained on truly incorrect annotations performs 8-10% worse than the model trained on clean data across mathematical reasoning benchmarks. Finally, we show that these findings hold for real-world noise in Text2SQL tasks, where training on real-world, human annotation errors cause 5-12% lower accuracy than clean data. Our results show that current RLVR methods cannot yet compensate for poor data quality. High-quality data remains essential.

</details>


### [190] [HIPO: Instruction Hierarchy via Constrained Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16152)
*Keru Chen,Jun Luo,Sen Lin,Yingbin Liang,Alvaro Velasquez,Nathaniel Bastian,Shaofeng Zou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Treats hierarchical instruction following as a constrained RL (CMDP). Enforces system prompts as explicit boundaries via primal-dual safe RL, yielding higher compliance and user utility across models; mechanistic analysis shows attention shifts to long-range system tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard alignment methods (RLHF, DPO) optimize a single objective and often fail to enforce the priority of system prompts in hierarchical instruction following. There is a need to encode priority asymmetry algorithmically to ensure system-level constraints are respected.

Method: Formulate Hierarchical Instruction Following as a Constrained Markov Decision Process. Apply a primal-dual safe reinforcement learning approach to enforce system prompts as explicit constraints while maximizing user utility within the feasible region. Treat system prompts as algorithmic boundaries shaping the policy. Evaluate across diverse model architectures (e.g., Qwen, Phi, Llama).

Result: HIPO significantly improves both system compliance and user utility across multiple model families. Mechanistic analysis reveals the constrained optimization biases the model toward attending to long-range system tokens, offering a principled foundation for reliable LLM deployment in complex workflows.

Conclusion: HIPO provides a principled constrained-optimization framework for enforcing hierarchical instruction constraints in LLMs, achieving improved compliance and user utility while enabling more reliable deployments.

Abstract: Hierarchical Instruction Following (HIF) refers to the problem of prompting large language models with a priority-ordered stack of instructions. Standard methods like RLHF and DPO typically fail in this problem since they mainly optimize for a single objective, failing to explicitly enforce system prompt compliance. Meanwhile, supervised fine-tuning relies on mimicking filtered, compliant data, which fails to establish the priority asymmetry at the algorithmic level. In this paper, we introduce \textsc{HIPO}, a novel alignment framework that formulates HIF as a Constrained Markov Decision Process. \textsc{HIPO} elevates system prompts from mere input context to strict algorithmic boundaries. Using a primal-dual safe reinforcement learning approach, the algorithm dynamically enforces system prompt compliance as an explicit constraint, maximizing user utility strictly within this feasible region. Extensive evaluations across diverse model architectures (e.g., Qwen, Phi, Llama) demonstrate that \textsc{HIPO} significantly improves both system compliance and user utility. Furthermore, mechanistic analysis reveals that this constrained optimization autonomously drives the model to shift its attention toward long-range system tokens, providing a principled foundation for reliable LLM deployment in complex workflows.

</details>


### [191] [DyJR: Preserving Diversity in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards via Dynamic Jensen-Shannon Replay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16157)
*Long Li,Zhijian Zhou,Tianyi Wang,Weidi Xu,Zuming Huang,Wei Chu,Zhe Wang,Shirui Pan,Chao Qu,Yuan Qi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic Jensen-Shannon Replay (DyJR) combines a time-sensitive dynamic replay buffer with Jensen-Shannon divergence regularization to preserve diversity in policy updates for on-policy RL with LLMs, improving sample efficiency and outperforming GRPO and baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-policy RL for large language model reasoning is sample-inefficient because past rollouts are discarded; existing experience replay reuses samples but can cause high compute costs and mode collapse from overfitting. The work argues that maintaining diversity in historical data is more important than simply reinforcing accuracy.

Method: 1) Time-Sensitive Dynamic Buffer: FIFO-based, adaptive sizing to keep temporally proximal samples aligned with model evolution. 2) Jensen-Shannon Divergence Regularization: replaces direct gradient updates with a distributional constraint using a dynamic reference distribution from recent trajectories to prevent diversity collapse.

Result: DyJR significantly outperforms GRPO and baselines such as RLEP and Ex-GRPO on mathematical reasoning and Text-to-SQL benchmarks, with training efficiency comparable to GRPO. Rank-k token probability evolution analysis shows enhanced diversity and reduced over-reliance on Rank-1 tokens, clarifying how DyJR's sub-modules affect training dynamics.

Conclusion: DyJR effectively stabilizes and diversifies learning in on-policy RL for LLM reasoning by coupling a temporally aware replay buffer with a distributional regularization objective, delivering stronger performance with comparable efficiency and providing insights into module-level contributions to policy evolution.

Abstract: While Reinforcement Learning (RL) enhances Large Language Model reasoning, on-policy algorithms like GRPO are sample-inefficient as they discard past rollouts. Existing experience replay methods address this by reusing accurate samples for direct policy updates, but this often incurs high computational costs and causes mode collapse via overfitting. We argue that historical data should prioritize sustaining diversity rather than simply reinforcing accuracy. To this end, we propose Dynamic Jensen-Shannon Replay (DyJR), a simple yet effective regularization framework using a dynamic reference distribution from recent trajectories. DyJR introduces two innovations: (1) A Time-Sensitive Dynamic Buffer that uses FIFO and adaptive sizing to retain only temporally proximal samples, synchronizing with model evolution; and (2) Jensen-Shannon Divergence Regularization, which replaces direct gradient updates with a distributional constraint to prevent diversity collapse. Experiments on mathematical reasoning and Text-to-SQL benchmarks demonstrate that DyJR significantly outperforms GRPO as well as baselines such as RLEP and Ex-GRPO, while maintaining training efficiency comparable to the original GRPO. Furthermore, from the perspective of Rank-$k$ token probability evolution, we show that DyJR enhances diversity and mitigates over-reliance on Rank-1 tokens, elucidating how specific sub-modules of DyJR influence the training dynamics.

</details>


### [192] [Execution-Grounded Credit Assignment for GRPO in Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16158)
*Abhijit Kumar,Natalya Kumar,Shikhar Gupta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EGCA localizes credit assignment in GRPO by using execution traces to attribute failures to the earliest semantic divergence during execution, yielding higher pass@1 on HumanEval and MBPP with modest overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Critic-free RL updates (GRPO-style) suffer from coarse credit assignment when failures are localized; need a mechanism to connect outcomes to specific program regions.

Method: Run the candidate and a canonical reference solution under identical instrumentation; for cases where tests fail but algorithmic constraints are met, locate the earliest semantic divergence and assign advantage only to the corresponding token span, masking downstream tokens; use offline curated reference for analysis but not supervision; no critic, auxiliary loss, or learned verifier is introduced.

Result: 69-82% range reported: 82.1% pass@1 on HumanEval (+3.1 over GRPO); 68.9% on MBPP (+1.5) with ~18% wall-clock overhead.

Conclusion: EGCA provides a drop-in improvement to GRPO without extra components, improving credit assignment precision and code generation performance with modest runtime cost.

Abstract: Critic-free reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) improves code generation by optimizing unit-test pass rates, but GRPO-style updates suffer from coarse credit assignment: a single outcome signal is spread uniformly across long programs even when failure stems from a localized semantic error. We propose Execution-Grounded Credit Assignment (EGCA), which localizes GRPO updates using execution traces. For programs that satisfy algorithmic constraints but fail tests, EGCA executes the candidate and a canonical reference solution (curated once offline; used for analysis, not supervision) under identical instrumentation, identifies the earliest semantic divergence, and assigns advantage only to the corresponding token span while masking downstream tokens. EGCA is a drop-in modification requiring no critic, auxiliary loss, or learned verifier, yielding 82.1% pass@1 on HumanEval (+3.1 over GRPO) and 68.9% on MBPP (+1.5) with 18% wall-clock overhead.

</details>


### [193] [The Finetuner's Fallacy: When to Pretrain with Your Finetuning Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16177)
*Christina Baek,Ricardo Pio Monti,David Schwab,Amro Abbas,Rishabh Adiga,Cody Blakeney,Maximilian Böther,Paul Burstein,Aldo Gael Carranza,Alvin Deng,Parth Doshi,Vineeth Dorna,Alex Fang,Tony Jiang,Siddharth Joshi,Brett W. Larsen,Jason Chan Lee,Katherine L. Mentzer,Luke Merrick,Haakon Mongstad,Fan Pan,Anshuman Suri,Darren Teh,Jason Telanoff,Jack Urbanek,Zhengping Wang,Josh Wills,Haoli Yin,Aditi Raghunathan,J. Zico Kolter,Bogdan Gaza,Ari Morcos,Matthew Leavitt,Pratyush Maini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Specialized pretraining (SPT) repeats a small domain dataset during pretraining, boosting domain-specific performance while preserving general knowledge, outperforming standard pretraining in underrepresented domains and reducing compute tokens needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world deployments require strong performance on narrow domains with scarce data. Finetuning can overfit and forget general knowledge; a pretraining strategy that leverages domain data early may improve both specialization and generalization.

Method: Pretrain with SPT by reusing a small domain dataset (typically reserved for finetuning) as a fraction of tokens during pretraining. Evaluate on ChemPile, MusicPile, and ProofPile; compare against standard pretraining. Derive overfitting scaling laws to guide domain-data repetition for given compute budgets; analyze the finetuner's fallacy.

Result: SPT yields better domain performance and preserves general capabilities after finetuning versus standard pretraining. It reduces the pretraining tokens required to reach a given domain performance by up to 1.75x. In underrepresented domains, a 1B SPT model can outperform a 3B standard pretrained model. Gains are larger when the domain is far from web text. SPT also improves specialization and reduces forgetting, leading to stronger results with fewer parameters and compute when amortized over inference.

Conclusion: Incorporating domain data early is advantageous. SPT challenges the reliance on finetuning alone for domain adaptation by providing better domain performance, stronger generalization, and compute efficiency; practitioners should consider repeating domain data during pretraining (SPT) to optimize domain adaptation under limited data.

Abstract: Real-world model deployments demand strong performance on narrow domains where data is often scarce. Typically, practitioners finetune models to specialize them, but this risks overfitting to the domain and forgetting general knowledge. We study a simple strategy, specialized pretraining (SPT), where a small domain dataset, typically reserved for finetuning, is repeated starting from pretraining as a fraction of the total tokens. Across three specialized domains (ChemPile, MusicPile, and ProofPile), SPT improves domain performance and preserves general capabilities after finetuning compared to standard pretraining. In our experiments, SPT reduces the pretraining tokens needed to reach a given domain performance by up to 1.75x. These gains grow when the target domain is underrepresented in the pretraining corpus: on domains far from web text, a 1B SPT model outperforms a 3B standard pretrained model. Beyond these empirical gains, we derive overfitting scaling laws to guide practitioners in selecting the optimal domain-data repetition for a given pretraining compute budget. Our observations reveal the finetuner's fallacy: while finetuning may appear to be the cheapest path to domain adaptation, introducing specialized domain data during pretraining stretches its utility. SPT yields better specialized domain performance (via reduced overfitting across repeated exposures) and better general domain performance (via reduced forgetting during finetuning), ultimately achieving stronger results with fewer parameters and less total compute when amortized over inference. To get the most out of domain data, incorporate it as early in training as possible.

</details>


### [194] [Sample-Efficient Adaptation of Drug-Response Models to Patient Tumors under Strong Biological Domain Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16185)
*Camille Jimenez Cortes,Philippe Lalanda,German Vega*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A staged transfer-learning framework using unsupervised pretraining to learn transferable representations of cellular and drug data, enabling faster, data-efficient adaptation to patient tumor drug response with few-shot supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the large biological gap between preclinical cell lines and patient tumors that hampers accurate drug-response prediction, aiming to reduce required clinical supervision.

Method: Unsupervised autoencoder-based representation learning on large unlabeled pharmacogenomic data to build cell and drug representations; align representations with drug-response labels on cell-line data; adapt to patient tumors using few-shot supervision.

Result: Unsupervised pretraining provides limited benefit when source and target domains overlap, but yields clear gains for adapting to patient tumors with very limited labeled data; faster improvements in few-shot patient-level adaptation while maintaining comparable accuracy to single-phase baselines on cell-line benchmarks.

Conclusion: Learning structured, transferable representations from unlabeled molecular profiles can substantially reduce the supervision needed for effective drug-response prediction and facilitate data-efficient preclinical-to-clinical translation.

Abstract: Predicting drug response in patients from preclinical data remains a major challenge in precision oncology due to the substantial biological gap between in vitro cell lines and patient tumors. Rather than aiming to improve absolute in vitro prediction accuracy, this work examines whether explicitly separating representation learning from task supervision enables more sample-efficient adaptation of drug-response models to patient data under strong biological domain shift. We propose a staged transfer-learning framework in which cellular and drug representations are first learned independently from large collections of unlabeled pharmacogenomic data using autoencoder-based representation learning. These representations are then aligned with drug-response labels on cell-line data and subsequently adapted to patient tumors using few-shot supervision. Through a systematic evaluation spanning in-domain, cross-dataset, and patient-level settings, we show that unsupervised pretraining provides limited benefit when source and target domains overlap substantially, but yields clear gains when adapting to patient tumors with very limited labeled data. In particular, the proposed framework achieves faster performance improvements during few-shot patient-level adaptation while maintaining comparable accuracy to single-phase baselines on standard cell-line benchmarks. Overall, these results demonstrate that learning structured and transferable representations from unlabeled molecular profiles can substantially reduce the amount of clinical supervision required for effective drug-response prediction, offering a practical pathway toward data-efficient preclinical-to-clinical translation.

</details>


### [195] [Online Semi-infinite Linear Programming: Efficient Algorithms via Function Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16200)
*Yiming Zong,Jiashuo Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OSILP with constraint-reduction via function approximation; dual-based solver with regret bounds independent of the constraint count; two-stage and general-function extensions; empirical results show improvement when constraints are large/infinite.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online semi-infinite linear programming faces a potentially infinite set of streaming constraints; traditional online LP regret grows with the number of constraints, hindering scalability. A reduction to a constant q of effective constraints aims to decouple regret from constraint count.

Method: Introduce a novel LP formulation where function approximation reduces the number of active constraints to a fixed q. Develop a dual-based algorithm with adaptable potential functions. Analyze under stochastic input and random permutation models, yielding regret bounds independent of the (potentially infinite) constraint count. Propose a two-stage algorithm to achieve improved regret O(q log T + q/ε) under stronger assumptions. Extend the framework to general function settings and validate via experiments against baselines with many constraints.

Result: Regret bounds: O(q√T) under stochastic inputs; O((q+q log T)√T) under random permutation. Two-stage method achieves O(q log T + q/ε). Extended to general function setting. Experiments show the proposed methods outperform existing approaches when the constraint set is large or infinite.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates that constraint-count independence is achievable in OSILP via constraint reduction and dual-based optimization, enabling scalable performance with streaming or oracle-driven constraints. The method broadens applicability and offers provable guarantees under classic input models, with empirical validation supporting practical efficacy.

Abstract: We consider the dynamic resource allocation problem where the decision space is finite-dimensional, yet the solution must satisfy a large or even infinite number of constraints revealed via streaming data or oracle feedback. We model this challenge as an Online Semi-infinite Linear Programming (OSILP) problem and develop a novel LP formulation to solve it approximately. Specifically, we employ function approximation to reduce the number of constraints to a constant $q$. This addresses a key limitation of traditional online LP algorithms, whose regret bounds typically depend on the number of constraints, leading to poor performance in this setting. We propose a dual-based algorithm to solve our new formulation, which offers broad applicability through the selection of appropriate potential functions. We analyze this algorithm under two classical input models-stochastic input and random permutation-establishing regret bounds of $O(q\sqrt{T})$ and $O\left(\left(q+q\log{T})\sqrt{T}\right)\right)$ respectively. Note that both regret bounds are independent of the number of constraints, which demonstrates the potential of our approach to handle a large or infinite number of constraints. Furthermore, we investigate the potential to improve upon the $O(q\sqrt{T})$ regret and propose a two-stage algorithm, achieving $O(q\log{T} + q/ε)$ regret under more stringent assumptions. We also extend our algorithms to the general function setting. A series of experiments validates that our algorithms outperform existing methods when confronted with a large number of constraints.

</details>


### [196] [Offline Exploration-Aware Fine-Tuning for Long-Chain Mathematical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16206)
*Yongyu Mu,Jiali Zeng,Fandong Meng,JingBo Zhu,Tong Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Offline Exploration-Aware (OXA) fine-tuning improves initial SFT to foster exploration in RLVR by promoting low-confidence verified data and suppressing high-confidence incorrect self-distillation, yielding consistent math reasoning gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SFT memory of chain-of-thought trajectories influences subsequent exploration in RLVR, but prior work emphasizes exploration during RLVR training rather than shaping the SFT phase; exploration-aware SFT could provide a better initialization for exploration.

Method: OXA optimizes two objectives during offline fine-tuning: (1) promote low-confidence verified teacher-distillation data to capture previously uncaptured reasoning patterns, and (2) suppress high-confidence incorrect self-distillation data to redistribute probability mass toward potentially correct candidates.

Result: Across 6 benchmarks, OXA achieves consistent improvements over conventional SFT, notably an average +6 Pass@1 and +5 Pass@k on Qwen2.5-1.5B-Math; it also increases initial policy entropy, with performance gains persisting through extensive RLVR training.

Conclusion: OXA demonstrates that exploration-aware initialization in SFT can yield durable improvements in mathematical reasoning under RLVR, highlighting the long-term value of shaping the exploration landscape during pretraining/fine-tuning.

Abstract: Through encouraging self-exploration, reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced the mathematical reasoning capabilities of large language models. As the starting point for RLVR, the capacity of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to memorize new chain-of-thought trajectories provides a crucial initialization that shapes the subsequent exploration landscape. However, existing research primarily focuses on facilitating exploration during RLVR training, leaving exploration-aware SFT under-explored. To bridge this gap, we propose Offline eXploration-Aware (OXA) fine-tuning. Specifically, OXA optimizes two objectives: promoting low-confidence verified teacher-distillation data to internalize previously uncaptured reasoning patterns, and suppressing high-confidence incorrect self-distillation data to redistribute probability mass of incorrect patterns toward potentially correct candidates. Experimental results across 6 benchmarks show that OXA consistently improves mathematical reasoning performance, especially achieving an average gain of $+6$ Pass@1 and $+5$ Pass@$k$ points compared to conventional SFT on the Qwen2.5-1.5B-Math. Crucially, OXA elevates initial policy entropy, and performance gains persist throughout extensive RLVR training, demonstrating the long-term value of OXA.

</details>


### [197] [Dual Consensus: Escaping from Spurious Majority in Unsupervised RLVR via Two-Stage Vote Mechanism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16223)
*Kaixuan Du,Meng Cao,Hang Zhang,Yukun Wang,Xiangzhou Huang,Ni Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DCRL is a self-supervised two-stage consensus RL approach for LLMs that uses an anchor and explorer signals and harmonic mean to improve reasoning without labels, achieving better Pass@1 over majority vote across eight benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Label-free RLVR methods in LLMs often rely on pseudo-labels, tending to converge on spurious, popular answers and trapping models in a dominant mode, which limits further gains.

Method: DCRL introduces a two-stage consensus: (1) anchor stage where the model generates dominant responses, and (2) explorer stage where a temporary unlearning process yields diverse auxiliary signals. The final training target is the harmonic mean of the two signal sets; the method is fully self-supervised and does not use external models.

Result: Across eight benchmarks and diverse domains, DCRL consistently improves Pass@1 over majority vote and exhibits more stable training dynamics.

Conclusion: DCRL demonstrates a scalable path toward stronger reasoning without labels.

Abstract: Current label-free RLVR approaches for large language models (LLMs), such as TTRL and Self-reward, have demonstrated effectiveness in improving the performance of LLMs on complex reasoning tasks. However, these methods rely heavily on accurate pseudo-label estimation and converge on spurious yet popular answers, thereby trapping in a dominant mode and limiting further improvements. Building on this, we propose Dual Consensus Reinforcement Learning (DCRL), a novel self-supervised training method which is capable of generating more reliable learning signals through a two-stage consensus mechanism. The model initially acts as an anchor, producing dominant responses; then it serves as an explorer, generating diverse auxiliary signals via a temporary unlearning process. The final training target is derived from the harmonic mean of these two signal sets. Notably, the process operates entirely without external models or supervision. Across eight benchmarks and diverse domains, DCRL consistently improves Pass@1 over majority vote while yielding more stable training dynamics. These results demonstrate that DCRL establishes a scalable path toward stronger reasoning without labels.

</details>


### [198] [Physics-integrated neural differentiable modeling for immersed boundary systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16277)
*Chenglin Li,Hang Xu,Jianting Chen,Yanfei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A physics-integrated differentiable framework for long-horizon simulation of immersed-boundary flows that combines a PDE-based intermediate-velocity module and a multi-direct-forcing immersed boundary module with a learned pressure-projection correction, enabling stable coarse-grid autoregressive rollouts trained with single-step supervision. It achieves ~200x faster inference than a high-resolution solver and outperforms purely data-driven, physics-loss-constrained, and coarse-grid baselines on cylinder-flow benchmarks at Re=100.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the high computational cost and instability of long-horizon, near-wall fluid-flow simulations. Purely numerical solvers demand fine grids/small time steps; data-driven surrogates lack robustness, especially under extrapolation. Integrating physical principles into differentiable surrogates aims to deliver accurate, stable, and efficient long-horizon predictions.

Method: An end-to-end differentiable architecture that embeds a PDE-based intermediate velocity module and a multi-direct- forcing immersed boundary model, both following a pressure-projection framework for incompressible flow. Replaces the expensive pressure projection with a learned implicit correction using ConvResNet blocks. Introduces a sub-iteration strategy to decouple stability requirements of the embedded physics module from the surrogate model's time step, enabling stable coarse-grid autoregressive rollouts with large effective time increments. Training uses single-step supervision, avoiding long-horizon backpropagation, and achieves training on a single GPU in under one hour.

Result: The proposed model consistently outperforms purely data-driven, physics-loss-constrained, and coarse-grid numerical baselines in flow-field fidelity and long-horizon stability. It delivers approximately 200-fold inference speedup over the high-resolution solver on benchmark cases of flow past a stationary cylinder and a rotationally oscillating cylinder at Re=100.

Conclusion: Integrating physical priors into a differentiable surrogate with a learned pressure correction and sub-iteration stability strategy yields robust, long-horizon predictions for immersed-boundary flows, combining accuracy with substantial efficiency gains and suggesting strong potential for scalable, high-fidelity, real-time fluid simulations.

Abstract: Accurately, efficiently, and stably computing complex fluid flows and their evolution near solid boundaries over long horizons remains challenging. Conventional numerical solvers require fine grids and small time steps to resolve near-wall dynamics, resulting in high computational costs, while purely data-driven surrogate models accumulate rollout errors and lack robustness under extrapolative conditions. To address these issues, this study extends existing neural PDE solvers by developing a physics-integrated differentiable framework for long-horizon prediction of immersed-boundary flows. A key design aspect of the framework includes an important improvement, namely the structural integration of physical principles into an end-to-end differentiable architecture incorporating a PDE-based intermediate velocity module and a multi-direct forcing immersed boundary module, both adhering to the pressure-projection procedure for incompressible flow computation. The computationally expensive pressure projection step is substituted with a learned implicit correction using ConvResNet blocks to reduce cost, and a sub-iteration strategy is introduced to separate the embedded physics module's stability requirement from the surrogate model's time step, enabling stable coarse-grid autoregressive rollouts with large effective time increments. The framework uses only single-step supervision for training, eliminating long-horizon backpropagation and reducing training time to under one hour on a single GPU. Evaluations on benchmark cases of flow past a stationary cylinder and a rotationally oscillating cylinder at Re=100 show the proposed model consistently outperforms purely data-driven, physics-loss-constrained, and coarse-grid numerical baselines in flow-field fidelity and long-horizon stability, while achieving an approximately 200-fold inference speedup over the high-resolution solver.

</details>


### [199] [Laya: A LeJEPA Approach to EEG via Latent Prediction over Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16281)
*Saarang Panchavati,Uddhav Panchavati,Corey Arnold,William Speier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Latent predictive SSL with LeJEPA (Laya) yields stronger linear-probe transfer than reconstruction-based SSL for EEG foundation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconstruction-based SSL often biases representations toward high-variance artifacts and may limit transferability; EEG foundation models need objectives that capture task-relevant neural structure.

Method: Introduce Laya, an EEG foundation model trained with LeJEPA (latent representation prediction rather than signal reconstruction). Compare against reconstruction-based baselines across multiple EEG benchmarks using linear probing.

Result: Laya shows improved linear-probing performance over reconstruction-based baselines across EEG benchmarks, supporting latent predictive objectives for transferable EEG representations.

Conclusion: Latent predictive SSL (JEPA/LeJEPA) is a promising direction for EEG foundation models, potentially yielding better transferability than reconstruction-based SSL; further work on stability and broader benchmarks could consolidate these gains.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a widely used tool for studying brain function, with applications in clinical neuroscience, diagnosis, and brain-computer interfaces (BCIs). Recent EEG foundation models trained on large unlabeled corpora aim to learn transferable representations, but their effectiveness remains unclear; reported improvements over smaller task-specific models are often modest, sensitive to downstream adaptation and fine-tuning strategies, and limited under linear probing. We hypothesize that one contributing factor is the reliance on signal reconstruction as the primary self-supervised learning (SSL) objective, which biases representations toward high-variance artifacts rather than task-relevant neural structure. To address this limitation, we explore an SSL paradigm based on Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA), which learn by predicting latent representations instead of reconstructing raw signals. While earlier JEPA-style methods often rely on additional heuristics to ensure training stability, recent advances such as LeJEPA provide a more principled and stable formulation. We introduce Laya, the first EEG foundation model based on LeJEPA. Across a range of EEG benchmarks, Laya demonstrates improved performance under linear probing compared to reconstruction-based baselines, suggesting that latent predictive objectives offer a promising direction for learning transferable, high-level EEG representations.

</details>


### [200] [Decoding the Critique Mechanism in Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16331)
*Hoang Phan,Quang H. Nguyen,Hung T. Q. Le,Xiusi Chen,Heng Ji,Khoa D. Doan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LRMs exhibit a hidden critique mechanism that detects and corrects reasoning errors, enabling correct final answers even when intermediate steps are flawed; a steerable critique vector in latent space allows improving error detection and test-time scaling without extra training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether self-verification in Large Reasoning Models relies on an internal critique capability, quantify it, and assess its practical utility for reliability and scaling.

Method: Inject arithmetic mistakes into intermediate chain-of-thought, observe error propagation and final answers, perform feature-space analysis to extract an interpretable critique vector, steer latent representations using this vector, and evaluate across multiple model scales/families with no additional training. Code is released.

Result: Observed that models can recover from intermediate errors, producing correct final answers; identified a highly interpretable critique vector in latent space; steering representations with this vector improves error-detection and enhances test-time scaling performance without extra training.

Conclusion: The study reveals a latent critique mechanism underlying self-verification in LRMs, and demonstrates a practical approach—latent-space steering via a critique vector—to control and enhance this behavior, with code available for replication.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) exhibit backtracking and self-verification mechanisms that enable them to revise intermediate steps and reach correct solutions, yielding strong performance on complex logical benchmarks. We hypothesize that such behaviors are beneficial only when the model has sufficiently strong "critique" ability to detect its own mistakes. This work systematically investigates how current LRMs recover from errors by inserting arithmetic mistakes in their intermediate reasoning steps. Notably, we discover a peculiar yet important phenomenon: despite the error propagating through the chain-of-thought (CoT), resulting in an incorrect intermediate conclusion, the model still reaches the correct final answer. This recovery implies that the model must possess an internal mechanism to detect errors and trigger self-correction, which we refer to as the hidden critique ability. Building on feature space analysis, we identify a highly interpretable critique vector representing this behavior. Extensive experiments across multiple model scales and families demonstrate that steering latent representations with this vector improves the model's error detection capability and enhances the performance of test-time scaling at no extra training cost. Our findings provide a valuable understanding of LRMs' critique behavior, suggesting a promising direction to control and improve their self-verification mechanism. Our code is available at https://github.com/mail-research/lrm-critique-vectors.

</details>


### [201] [Behavioral Steering in a 35B MoE Language Model via SAE-Decoded Probe Vectors: One Agency Axis, Not Five Traits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16335)
*Jia Qing Yap*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Nine sparse autoencoders trained on the residual stream of a 35B Mixture-of-Experts LLM identify and steer five agentic traits via linear probes projected back into activation space for continuous at-inference control; autonomy is the dominant axis, with trait-specific effects as secondary modulations; evidence suggests commitments form during prefill, not decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable fine-grained, retraining-free control of agentic behaviors in large LLMs by decoding latent representations into continuous steering vectors and applying them at inference-time.

Method: Train nine sparse autoencoders on the LLM residual stream; train linear probes on SAE latent activations; project probe weights back through the SAE decoder to obtain continuous steering vectors in the model's native space; apply them during inference to bias behavior without retraining; evaluate 1,800 rollouts across 50 scenarios and 36 conditions; analyze five agentic traits; test effects when applied during autoregressive decoding vs prefill.

Result: Autonomy steering at multiplier 2 yields Cohen's d = 1.01 (p < 0.0001), shifting from user-asking 78% of the time to proactive code execution and web search; all five traits mostly modulate a single dominant axis (independence vs deference); tool-use vector d = 0.39; risk-calibration vector suppresses; steering during autoregressive decoding has no effect, implying commitments are computed during prefill in GatedDeltaNet.

Conclusion: Behavioral commitments in this setup are largely centralized around a single agency axis, with cross-trait interference and trait-specific effects appearing as secondary modulations; the method demonstrates a retraining-free pathway to steer agentic behavior at inference time, with implications for controllability and safety, and highlights prefill-stage computation as the locus of commitment.

Abstract: We train nine sparse autoencoders (SAEs) on the residual stream of Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B, a 35-billion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model with a hybrid GatedDeltaNet/attention architecture, and use them to identify and steer five agentic behavioral traits. Our method trains linear probes on SAE latent activations, then projects the probe weights back through the SAE decoder to obtain continuous steering vectors in the model's native activation space. This bypasses the SAE's top-k discretization, enabling fine-grained behavioral intervention at inference time with no retraining. Across 1,800 agent rollouts (50 scenarios times 36 conditions), we find that autonomy steering at multiplier 2 achieves Cohen's d = 1.01 (p < 0.0001), shifting the model from asking the user for help 78% of the time to proactively executing code and searching the web. Cross-trait analysis, however, reveals that all five steering vectors primarily modulate a single dominant agency axis (the disposition to act independently versus defer to the user), with trait specific effects appearing only as secondary modulations in tool-type composition and dose-response shape. The tool-use vector steers behavior (d = 0.39); the risk-calibration vector produces only suppression. We additionally show that steering only during autoregressive decoding has zero effect (p > 0.35), providing causal evidence that behavioral commitments are computed during prefill in GatedDeltaNet architectures.

</details>


### [202] [DynamicGate MLP Conditional Computation via Learned Structural Dropout and Input Dependent Gating for Functional Plasticity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16367)
*Yong Il Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DynamicGate-MLP unifies dropout-like regularization with input-dependent conditional computation by learning gate probabilities that decide unit/block usage. At training, a budget penalty and STE optimize a continuous-to-discrete gate mask; at inference, a discrete path is executed. Evaluated across several datasets, the method emphasizes compute efficiency using gate activation and MAC-based metrics rather than wall-clock latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reconcile regularization (e.g., dropout) with conditional computation in neural networks, enabling sample-dependent execution that reduces unnecessary computation while preserving or improving accuracy.

Method: Introduce continuous gate probabilities for MLP units/blocks. Train with a penalty on expected gate usage to control compute, and optimize a discrete execution mask via Straight-Through Estimator. At inference, sample a discrete mask to select an execution path. Evaluate compute efficiency with gate activation ratios and a layer-weighted relative MAC metric across datasets.

Result: Demonstrates compute efficiency gains by suppressing unnecessary units, outperforming or matching MLP baselines and MoE-like variants under a consistent compute-measure framework. Evaluated on MNIST, CIFAR-10, Tiny-ImageNet, Speech Commands, and PBMC3k.

Conclusion: DynamicGate-MLP provides a unified framework that combines regularization and conditional computation, enabling sample-dependent execution while maintaining competitive accuracy, with a hardware-agnostic metric-centric evaluation of compute efficiency.

Abstract: Dropout is a representative regularization technique that stochastically deactivates hidden units during training to mitigate overfitting. In contrast, standard inference executes the full network with dense computation, so its goal and mechanism differ from conditional computation, where the executed operations depend on the input. This paper organizes DynamicGate-MLP into a single framework that simultaneously satisfies both the regularization view and the conditional-computation view. Instead of a random mask, the proposed model learns gates that decide whether to use each unit (or block), suppressing unnecessary computation while implementing sample-dependent execution that concentrates computation on the parts needed for each input. To this end, we define continuous gate probabilities and, at inference time, generate a discrete execution mask from them to select an execution path. Training controls the compute budget via a penalty on expected gate usage and uses a Straight-Through Estimator (STE) to optimize the discrete mask. We evaluate DynamicGate-MLP on MNIST, CIFAR-10, Tiny-ImageNet, Speech Commands, and PBMC3k, and compare it with various MLP baselines and MoE-style variants. Compute efficiency is compared under a consistent criterion using gate activation ratios and a layerweighted relative MAC metric, rather than wall-clock latency that depends on hardware and backend kernels.

</details>


### [203] [FederatedFactory: Generative One-Shot Learning for Extremely Non-IID Distributed Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16370)
*Andrea Moleri,Christian Internò,Ali Raza,Markus Olhofer,David Klindt,Fabio Stella,Barbara Hammer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FederatedFactory reframes federated learning around generative priors instead of discriminative weights, enabling one-shot exchange of generative modules to synthesize balanced data, eliminate gradient conflicts, and match centralized performance, with modular unlearning capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-iid, mutually exclusive local label distributions in standard FL cause conflicting optimization trajectories and degraded performance. Reliance on pretrained foundation models introduces additional, sometimes unrealistic biases. A zero-dependency, modular FL approach is needed to handle heterogeneity without external priors.

Method: Invert the unit of federation from discriminative parameters to generative priors. Exchange generative modules in a single communication round to enable ex nihilo synthesis of universally class-balanced datasets. This eliminates gradient conflicts and external prior bias, and enables exact modular unlearning via deterministic deletion of modules.

Result: Empirical results on MedMNIST and ISIC2019 show recovery of centralized upper-bound performance. In pathology-heavy heterogeneity (CIFAR-10), accuracy improves from 11.36% to 90.57%; ISIC2019 AUROC also reaches 90.57%. The framework supports exact modular unlearning by deleting specific generative modules.

Conclusion: FederatedFactory provides a zero-dependency, modular FL framework that overcomes gradient conflicts from data heterogeneity, enables data synthesis with generative priors, delivers centralized-level performance in diverse settings, and supports exact unlearning through modular deletion.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables distributed optimization without compromising data sovereignty. Yet, where local label distributions are mutually exclusive, standard weight aggregation fails due to conflicting optimization trajectories. Often, FL methods rely on pretrained foundation models, introducing unrealistic assumptions. We introduce FederatedFactory, a zero-dependency framework that inverts the unit of federation from discriminative parameters to generative priors. By exchanging generative modules in a single communication round, our architecture supports ex nihilo synthesis of universally class balanced datasets, eliminating gradient conflict and external prior bias entirely. Evaluations across diverse medical imagery benchmarks, including MedMNIST and ISIC2019, demonstrate that our approach recovers centralized upper-bound performance. Under pathological heterogeneity, it lifts baseline accuracy from a collapsed 11.36% to 90.57% on CIFAR-10 and restores ISIC2019 AUROC to 90.57%. Additionally, this framework facilitates exact modular unlearning through the deterministic deletion of specific generative modules.

</details>


### [204] [Prior-Informed Neural Network Initialization: A Spectral Approach for Function Parameterizing Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16376)
*David Orlando Salazar Torres,Diyar Altinses,Andreas Schwung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A prior-informed Bag-of-Functions architecture uses FFT-derived seasonal priors and residual trend modeling to guide initialization and depth, enabling smaller encoders, faster convergence, and improved efficiency without altering the training procedure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate sensitivity to data-agnostic initializations in function-parameterization networks by embedding intrinsic spectral-temporal structure as priors to improve convergence, interpretability, and efficiency.

Method: Extract dominant seasonal components via Fast Fourier Transform to inform model depth and initial states; apply a residual-based regression for trend components; align architecture with data structure to reduce encoder dimensionality; provide finite-sample trend estimation theory; keep standard training protocol.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show accelerated convergence, reduced trial-to-trial performance variability, and better computational efficiency; the method outperforms standard initialization baselines while yielding more compact and interpretable models, without changing the core training procedure.

Conclusion: Integrating data-driven priors into BoF-style neural architectures yields compact, interpretable models with improved convergence and efficiency, and offers principled guidance for trend estimation under finite samples.

Abstract: Neural network architectures designed for function parameterization, such as the Bag-of-Functions (BoF) framework, bridge the gap between the expressivity of deep learning and the interpretability of classical signal processing. However, these models are inherently sensitive to parameter initialization, as traditional data-agnostic schemes fail to capture the structural properties of the target signals, often leading to suboptimal convergence. In this work, we propose a prior-informed design strategy that leverages the intrinsic spectral and temporal structure of the data to guide both network initialization and architectural configuration. A principled methodology is introduced that uses the Fast Fourier Transform to extract dominant seasonal priors, informing model depth and initial states, and a residual-based regression approach to parameterize trend components. Crucially, this structural alignment enables a substantial reduction in encoder dimensionality without compromising reconstruction fidelity. A supporting theoretical analysis provides guidance on trend estimation under finite-sample regimes. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that embedding data-driven priors significantly accelerates convergence, reduces performance variability across trials, and improves computational efficiency. Overall, the proposed framework enables more compact and interpretable architectures while outperforming standard initialization baselines, without altering the core training procedure.

</details>


### [205] [Age Predictors Through the Lens of Generalization, Bias Mitigation, and Interpretability: Reflections on Causal Implications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16377)
*Debdas Paul,Elisa Ferrari,Irene Gravili,Alessandro Cellerino*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes an adversarial invariant representation learning framework to improve out-of-distribution generalization for age prediction by removing exogenous attribute information; demonstrates on mouse transcriptomics; results align with Elamipretide study; cautions on causal interpretation from predictive models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exogenous attributes (e.g., race, gender, tissue) cause distributional shifts and bias in age/predictive models. Learning invariant representations can enhance OOD robustness, enable bias mitigation in predictive settings, and support fairer inferences in causal analyses. The work frames these ideas in a coherent, theoretically grounded neural-network approach.

Method: Develop an interpretable neural network model using adversarial representation learning to enforce invariance to protected attributes. Provide theoretical scaffolding and compare its behavior to conventional ML models using publicly available mouse transcriptomic datasets, with qualitative alignment to published Elamipretide effects.

Result: The model learns representations that are invariant to exogenous attributes, showing robust behavior under distribution shifts and aligning its predictions with the reported effects of Elamipretide on mouse muscle. Relative to baselines, the invariant model demonstrates favorable invariance properties and comparable predictive performance on in-distribution data.

Conclusion: Invariant predictive modeling can enhance OOD generalization and bias mitigation, but it cannot fully establish causal relationships from purely predictive models. Causal interpretation requires additional assumptions or interventional data.

Abstract: Chronological age predictors often fail to achieve out-of-distribution (OOD) gen- eralization due to exogenous attributes such as race, gender, or tissue. Learning an invariant representation with respect to those attributes is therefore essential to improve OOD generalization and prevent overly optimistic results. In predic- tive settings, these attributes motivate bias mitigation; in causal analyses, they appear as confounders; and when protected, their suppression leads to fairness. We coherently explore these concepts with theoretical rigor and discuss the scope of an interpretable neural network model based on adversarial representation learning. Using publicly available mouse transcriptomic datasets, we illustrate the behavior of this model relative to conventional machine learning models. We observe that the outcome of this model is consistent with the predictive results of a published study demonstrating the effects of Elamipretide on mouse skeletal and cardiac muscle. We conclude by discussing the limitations of deriving causal interpretation from such purely predictive models.

</details>


### [206] [Trained Persistent Memory for Frozen Encoder--Decoder LLMs: Six Architectural Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16413)
*Hong Jeong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A pilot study demonstrating feasible persistent memory in the continuous latent space of a frozen LLM via adapters, showing a design-space with six methods across injection points and write mechanisms; memory capacity critically affects retention, with positive recall at higher capacity and degradation at low capacity; scalable memory bank promises larger-scale improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the statelessness of frozen encoder–decoder LMs by enabling persistent memory in the latent space without full end-to-end training, aiming for conversational learning and memory consolidation under strict resource limits.

Method: Train only adapters to augment a frozen Flan-T5-XL backbone. Implement six architectural methods across three injection points and four differentiable read/write mechanisms on dense latent vectors. Memory reads/writes are differentiable and stored in a compact memory bank that accumulates at inference without gradients. Evaluate forgetting on LoCoMo at two capacity scales (1× and 10×).

Result: At 10× capacity, all six adapters yield positive memory-recall curves after training; at 1×, three methods collapse and the baseline remains at zero. The memory bank remains compact and scalable, enabling persistent memory without backpropagation. The study identifies capacity as a critical design parameter and provides a taxonomy of methods and injection points for future work.

Conclusion: Persistent latent-space memory in frozen LMs is feasible and promising. The work establishes a design-space taxonomy and baseline feasibility, suggesting that larger models, data, and memory capacities could yield stronger results with end-to-end training in future work.

Abstract: Frozen encoder--decoder language models are stateless: the latent representation is discarded after every forward pass, so no information persists across sessions. This paper presents a \textbf{proof-of-concept pilot study} showing that persistent memory in the \emph{continuous latent space} of a frozen LLM is feasible -- even under severe resource constraints (a single frozen Flan-T5-XL backbone, small trainable adapters, a single dataset). We implement six architectural methods spanning three injection points and four write mechanisms; unlike text-level memory systems, every write and read is a differentiable operation on dense vectors. After training only the adapter, the memory bank continues to accumulate at inference time without gradients, enabling \emph{conversational learning}. Under a forgetting-curve evaluation on LoCoMo at two capacity scales (1$\times$ and 10$\times$), the stateless baseline scores exactly zero; at 10$\times$ all six trained adapters produce positive memory-recall curves; at 1$\times$ three methods collapse, revealing capacity as a critical design parameter. Because the memory bank is a compact numerical array, it can be scaled to arbitrarily large capacity without altering the backbone.
