We argue that full end-to-end training with larger models, larger data, and orders-of-magnitude larger memory will yield substantially stronger results; this pilot study establishes the feasibility baseline and design-space taxonomy that such efforts require.

</details>


### [207] [DISCOVER: A Solver for Distributional Counterfactual Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16436)
*Yikai Gu,Lele Cao,Bo Zhao,Lei Lei,Lei You*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DISCOVER is a model-agnostic solver for distributional counterfactual explanations (DCE) that replaces gradient-based optimization with a sparse propose-and-select search. It maintains the DCE objective and statistical certification while using per-row transport objective decomposition, a top-k intervention budget, and OT-guided cone sampling to handle non-differentiable tabular pipelines. Demonstrated on multiple tabular datasets with open-source code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DCE achieves distributional explanations with gradient-based optimization, which limits applicability to non-differentiable or black-box models common in real-world tabular pipelines. A model-agnostic, efficient search method is needed to extend DCE to these settings while preserving the theoretical guarantees.

Method:  DISCOVER uses a sparse propose-and-select search to replace gradient descent. It decomposes the transport objective per row to compute individual impact scores and enforces a top-k intervention budget to concentrate edits on influential samples. An OT-guided cone sampling primitive, driven by input-side transport geometry, guides candidate generation without predictor gradients while preserving the original DCE objective and its certification.

Result: Empirical evaluation on several tabular datasets shows strong joint alignment of input and output distributions, extending distributional counterfactual reasoning to black-box pipelines. A code repository is provided for reproducibility.

Conclusion: DISCOVER enables model-agnostic distributional counterfactual explanations without gradient information, making DCE applicable to a broader class of tabular models while retaining theoretical guarantees and interpretability through per-row contributions and targeted interventions.

Abstract: Counterfactual explanations (CE) explain model decisions by identifying input modifications that lead to different predictions. Most existing methods operate at the instance level. Distributional Counterfactual Explanations (DCE) extend this setting by optimizing an optimal transport objective that balances proximity to a factual input distribution and alignment to a target output distribution, with statistical certification via chance constrained bounds. However, DCE relies on gradient based optimization, while many real-world tabular pipelines are dominated by non-differentiable models. We propose DISCOVER, a model-agnostic solver for distributional counterfactual explanations. DISCOVER preserves the original DCE objective and certification while replacing gradient descent with a sparse propose-and-select search paradigm. It exploits a sample-wise decomposition of the transport objective to compute per-row impact scores and enforce a top-$k$ intervention budget, focusing edits on the most influential samples. To guide candidate generation without predictor gradients, DISCOVER introduces an OT-guided cone sampling primitive driven by input-side transport geometry. Experiments on multiple tabular datasets demonstrate strong joint alignment of input and output distributions, extending distributional counterfactual reasoning to modern black box learning pipelines. A code repository is available at https://github.com/understanding-ml/DCE.

</details>


### [208] [Capability-Guided Compression: Toward Interpretability-Aware Budget Allocation for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16440)
*Rishaank Gupta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Capability-Guided Compression (CGC) using SAE-derived capability density to allocate per-component compression budgets, enabling component-level phase-transition prediction and revealing orthogonality to Wanda scores; shows PPL-based compression is unreliable in the tested setting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compression methods currently allocate budgets without accounting for the functional role of model components, risking loss of reasoning capabilities and abrupt performance shifts; need a capability-aware budgeting framework that aligns compression with component utility.

Method: Define capability density as a scalar combining feature breadth, activation entropy, and cross-input consistency of Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) feature activations. The paper proves that high-capacity-density components have less redundancy and hit phase-transition points at lower compression ratios, enabling pre-compression phase-transition prediction. Empirical evaluation on GPT-2 Medium assesses independence from Wanda scores (Spearman ρ = -0.054, n = 384 heads) and reports a negative result for PPL-based compression comparisons, with a diagnosis that GPT-2 Medium may be an insufficient test bed.

Result: Capability density is statistically independent of Wanda importance scores (ρ = -0.054, n = 384 heads), establishing a novel orthogonal compression signal. The study reports a negative result for perplexity-based compression comparisons, highlighting evaluation challenges and the need for broader test beds. The authors present a theoretical framework, a density formalism, and an orthogonality finding that underpin capability-aware compression research.

Conclusion: This work lays a foundational framework for capability-aware compression, introducing a new density-based signal to guide component-level budgeting and predict phase transitions, and highlighting the necessity for diverse test beds and further empirical validation to generalize beyond GPT-2 Medium.

Abstract: Large language model compression has made substantial progress through pruning, quantization, and low-rank decomposition, yet a fundamental limitation persists across all existing methods: compression budgets are allocated without any representation of what individual model components functionally encode. We term this the capability-blind compression problem and argue it is a root cause of two well-documented failures -- the insensitivity of perplexity-based evaluation to reasoning capability loss, and the abrupt phase transitions in model performance recently characterized by Ma et al. (2026). We propose Capability-Guided Compression (CGC), a framework that addresses this by using Sparse Autoencoder (SAE)-derived capability density maps to allocate differential compression budgets across transformer components. Capability density is a formally defined scalar measure combining the feature breadth, activation entropy, and cross-input consistency of a component's SAE feature activation distribution. We prove theoretically that components with higher capability density exhibit lower structural redundancy and reach their individual phase transition points at lower compression ratios, providing the first pre-compression mechanism for component-level phase transition prediction. Experiments on GPT-2 Medium confirm that capability density is statistically independent of Wanda importance scores (Spearman rho = -0.054, n = 384 heads), establishing it as a genuinely novel compression signal orthogonal to all existing importance metrics. We report a negative result on PPL-based compression comparison and provide a principled diagnosis identifying GPT-2 Medium as an insufficient test bed for the full CGC hypothesis. The theoretical framework, density formalism, and orthogonality finding constitute a foundation for capability-aware compression research.

</details>


### [209] [Optimal uncertainty bounds for multivariate kernel regression under bounded noise: A Gaussian process-based dual function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16481)
*Amon Lahr,Anna Scampicchio,Johannes Köhler,Melanie N. Zeilinger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A tight, distribution-free uncertainty bound for multi-output kernel-based estimates is proposed, using an unconstrained duality-based formulation that mirrors Gaussian process confidence bounds and integrates easily into optimization. It generalizes existing results and is illustrated with a quadrotor-learning example.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To obtain non-conservative, distribution-free uncertainty bounds for latent functions estimated from noisy data in multi-output kernel methods, addressing limitations of existing bounds that assume specific noise models, are overly conservative, or poorly scalable.

Method: An unconstrained, duality-based formulation yields a tight bound for multi-output kernel estimates. The bound has the same structure as GP confidence bounds and can be incorporated into downstream optimization. The approach is distribution-free and generalizes many prior results.

Result: The bound is tight and broadly applicable to multi-output kernel-based estimates, generalizes existing results, and is demonstrated within a quadrotor dynamics learning scenario, illustrating practical applicability and integration into control pipelines.

Conclusion: The proposed bound provides reliable, scalable uncertainty quantification for learning-based control with kernel methods, enabling safer and more robust optimization across multi-output problems, with broad potential applications.

Abstract: Non-conservative uncertainty bounds are essential for making reliable predictions about latent functions from noisy data--and thus, a key enabler for safe learning-based control. In this domain, kernel methods such as Gaussian process regression are established techniques, thanks to their inherent uncertainty quantification mechanism. Still, existing bounds either pose strong assumptions on the underlying noise distribution, are conservative, do not scale well in the multi-output case, or are difficult to integrate into downstream tasks. This paper addresses these limitations by presenting a tight, distribution-free bound for multi-output kernel-based estimates. It is obtained through an unconstrained, duality-based formulation, which shares the same structure of classic Gaussian process confidence bounds and can thus be straightforwardly integrated into downstream optimization pipelines. We show that the proposed bound generalizes many existing results and illustrate its application using an example inspired by quadrotor dynamics learning.

</details>


### [210] [Bridging the High-Frequency Data Gap: A Millisecond-Resolution Network Dataset for Advancing Time Series Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16497)
*Subina Khanal,Seshu Tirupathi,Merim Dzaferagic,Marco Ruffini,Torben Bach Pedersen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A millisecond-scale wireless/traffic dataset for 5G enables high-frequency TSFMs, revealing current models' limitations and the need for targeted pre-training and architectures to handle high-frequency data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TSFMs are typically trained on lower-frequency time series; a lack of high-frequency data and wireless-domain samples hampers generalization to millisecond-scale signals and new domains. The dataset addresses this gap and aims to improve pre-training and robustness for TSFMs.

Method: Collect and curate millisecond-resolution wireless and traffic measurements from an operational 5G deployment; define forecasting tasks with horizons from 100 ms (1 step) to 9.6 s (96 steps); benchmark traditional ML models and TSFMs in zero-shot and fine-tuned settings.

Result: Most TSFM configurations perform poorly on the high-frequency wireless data distribution in both zero-shot and fine-tuned settings, highlighting a distribution shift from existing pretraining corpora. The data suggests the need for high-frequency pretraining, domain-specific architectures, and refined fine-tuning strategies to enhance generalization and robustness.

Conclusion: Incorporating high-frequency datasets during pre-training and forecasting is crucial to improve TSFM performance, generalization, and robustness in real-world applications, and to extend TSFMs to the wireless-domain.

Abstract: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) require diverse, real-world datasets to adapt across varying domains and temporal frequencies. However, current large-scale datasets predominantly focus on low-frequency time series with sampling intervals, i.e., time resolution, in the range of seconds to years, hindering their ability to capture the nuances of high-frequency time series data. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel dataset that captures millisecond-resolution wireless and traffic conditions from an operational 5G wireless deployment, expanding the scope of TSFMs to incorporate high-frequency data for pre-training. Further, the dataset introduces a new domain, wireless networks, thus complementing existing more general domains like energy and finance. The dataset also provides use cases for short-term forecasting, with prediction horizons spanning from 100 milliseconds (1 step) to 9.6 seconds (96 steps). By benchmarking traditional machine learning models and TSFMs on predictive tasks using this dataset, we demonstrate that most TSFM model configurations perform poorly on this new data distribution in both zero-shot and fine-tuned settings. Our work underscores the importance of incorporating high-frequency datasets during pre-training and forecasting to enhance architectures, fine-tuning strategies, generalization, and robustness of TSFMs in real-world applications.

</details>


### [211] [From the Inside Out: Progressive Distribution Refinement for Confidence Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16500)
*Xizhong Yang,Yinan Xia,Huiming Wang,Mofei Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DistriTTRL uses distribution priors of the model’s confidence to guide self-reward signals in reinforcement learning, coupled with diversity penalties to combat reward hacking in voting-based Test-Time Training, achieving reported improvements across multiple models/ benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the mismatch between test-time and training-time internal information for self-reward RL and mitigate reward hacking in voting-based Test-Time Training.

Method: Leverage the distribution prior of the model’s confidence to progressively optimize the self-reward signal instead of relying on single-query rollouts; impose diversity-targeted penalties to reduce reward hacking.

Result: Significant performance improvements observed across multiple models and benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed approach enables complementary interaction between model capability and self-reward signals and mitigates reward hacking, demonstrating effectiveness in test-time RL settings.

Abstract: Leveraging the model's internal information as the self-reward signal in Reinforcement Learning (RL) has received extensive attention due to its label-free nature. While prior works have made significant progress in applying the Test-Time Scaling (TTS) strategies to RL, the discrepancy in internal information between test and training remains inadequately addressed. Moreover, Test-Time Training based on voting-based TTS strategies often suffers from reward hacking problems. To address these issues, we propose DistriTTRL, which leverages the distribution prior of the model's confidence during RL to progressively optimize the reward signal, rather than relying solely on single-query rollouts. Additionally, we mitigate the phenomenon of consistent reward hacking caused by the voting-based TTS strategies through diversity-targeted penalties. Benefiting from this training mechanism where model capability and self-reward signals complement each other, and the mitigation of reward hacking, DistriTTRL has achieved significant performance improvements across multiple models and benchmarks.

</details>


### [212] [FEAT: A Linear-Complexity Foundation Model for Extremely Large Structured Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16513)
*Zhenghang Song,Tang Qian,Lu Chen,Yushuai Li,Zhengke Hu,Bingbing Fang,Yumeng Song,Junbo Zhao,Sheng Zhang,Tianyi Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FEAT is a linear-complexity foundation model for extremely large structured data that replaces quadratic self-attention with a hybrid linear encoding using AFMB for local dependencies and Conv-GLA for global memory, enabling linear scaling and up to 40x faster inference while improving zero-shot performance across 11 real datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current large structured-data models rely on sample-wise self-attention with O(N^2) complexity, suffer from compressed representations and artificial biases from linear models, and often rely on synthetic pre-training that poorly matches real-world distributions. There is a need for linear-complexity, robust, real-data-pretrained LDMs for structured data.

Method: Introduce FEAT with a multi-layer dual-axis architecture that substitutes quadratic attention with hybrid linear encoding. It combines adaptive-fusion bi-Mamba-2 (AFBM) for local sample dependencies and convolutional gated linear attention (Conv-GLA) for global memory, plus a hybrid structural causal model pipeline and a stable reconstruction objective.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 11 real-world datasets shows FEAT outperforms baselines in zero-shot tasks, scales linearly with data, and achieves up to 40x faster inference.

Conclusion: FEAT demonstrates that linear-complexity LDMs can effectively handle extremely large structured data, delivering robust performance and significant efficiency gains over quadratic-attention baselines.

Abstract: Structured data is foundational to healthcare, finance, e-commerce, and scientific data management. Large structured-data models (LDMs) extend the foundation model paradigm to unify heterogeneous datasets for tasks such as classification, regression, and decision support. However, existing LDMs face major limitations. First, most rely on sample-wise self-attention, whose O(N^2) complexity limits the sample count. Second, linear sequence models often degrade representations due to hidden-state compression and artificial causal bias. Third, synthetic-only pre-training often fails to match real-world distributions. We propose FEAT, a linear-complexity foundation model for extremely large structured data. FEAT introduces a multi-layer dual-axis architecture that replaces quadratic attention with hybrid linear encoding. The architecture combines adaptive-fusion bi-Mamba-2 (AFBM) for local sample dependencies and convolutional gated linear attention (Conv-GLA) for global memory. This design enables linear-complexity cross-sample modeling while preserving expressive representations. To improve robustness, FEAT adopts a hybrid structural causal model pipeline and a stable reconstruction objective. Experiments on 11 real-world datasets show that FEAT consistently outperforms baselines in zero-shot performance, while scaling linearly and achieving up to 40x faster inference.

</details>


### [213] [SympFormer: Accelerated attention blocks via Inertial Dynamics on Density Manifolds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16535)
*Viktor Stein,Wuchen Li,Gabriele Steidl*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Accelerated attention blocks for Transformers via inertial density dynamics yield Hamiltonian momentum attention; linear self-attention approximates a Stein variational gradient flow with a bilinear kernel; preserves elliptically contoured distributions; faster convergence with same oracle calls.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend the mean-field interpretation of attention as gradient flows on Wasserstein-2 spaces to accelerated, inertial dynamics, and develop Hamiltonian-structured attention blocks to speed up convergence without increasing oracle cost.

Method: Introduce tokens carrying spatial (feature) and velocity variables, discretize accelerated density dynamics, derive Hamiltonian momentum attention blocks; for linear self-attention, establish approximation to a Stein variational gradient flow with a bilinear kernel; prove distributional invariants and provide implementable particle-based algorithms.

Result: Accelerated attention blocks converge faster than classical attention blocks while preserving the number of oracle calls; elliptically contoured distributions are preserved under the dynamics; practical particle-based algorithms are developed.

Conclusion: The framework provides faster, theoretically grounded attention mechanisms via inertial density dynamics and Hamiltonian structure, extending the mean-field viewpoint of attention and enabling efficient, implementable accelerated architectures.

Abstract: Transformers owe much of their empirical success in natural language processing to the self-attention blocks. Recent perspectives interpret attention blocks as interacting particle systems, whose mean-field limits correspond to gradient flows of interaction energy functionals on probability density spaces equipped with Wasserstein-$2$-type metrics. We extend this viewpoint by introducing accelerated attention blocks derived from inertial Nesterov-type dynamics on density spaces. In our proposed architecture, tokens carry both spatial (feature) and velocity variables. The time discretization and the approximation of accelerated density dynamics yield Hamiltonian momentum attention blocks, which constitute the proposed accelerated attention architectures. In particular, for linear self-attention, we show that the attention blocks approximate a Stein variational gradient flow, using a bilinear kernel, of a potential energy. In this setting, we prove that elliptically contoured probability distributions are preserved by the accelerated attention blocks. We present implementable particle-based algorithms and demonstrate that the proposed accelerated attention blocks converge faster than the classical attention blocks while preserving the number of oracle calls.

</details>


### [214] [Manifold-Matching Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16568)
*Laurent Cheret,Vincent Létourneau,Isar Nejadgholi,Chris Drummond,Hussein Al Osman,Maia Fraser*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Manifold-Matching Autoencoder (MMAE) regularizes unsupervised learning by matching pairwise latent-space distances to input-space distances; extendable to lower-dimensional latents, scalable like MDS, and improves geometry-preserving metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Preserve the geometric structure of data in autoencoders by using pairwise distances instead of raw coordinates, enabling flexible latent dimensionality and improving local/topological structure preservation.

Method: Introduce MMAE, an unsupervised regularization term that minimizes the mean squared error between pairwise distances in the latent space and the corresponding pairwise distances in the input data space during autoencoder training; applicable to both standard and lower-dimensional latent representations, yielding a scalable MDS-like approximation.

Result: MMAE outperforms comparable regularization approaches on metrics for nearest-neighbor preservation and persistent homology-based measures; it provides a scalable approximation to Multi-Dimensional Scaling (MDS).

Conclusion: MMAE is an effective, flexible geometry-preserving regularizer for autoencoders that can operate in reduced latent dimensions and scales similarly to MDS, enhancing manifold-learning capabilities.

Abstract: We study a simple unsupervised regularization scheme for autoencoders called Manifold-Matching (MMAE): we align the pairwise distances in the latent space to those of the input data space by minimizing mean squared error. Because alignment occurs on pairwise distances rather than coordinates, it can also be extended to a lower-dimensional representation of the data, adding flexibility to the method. We find that this regularization outperforms similar methods on metrics based on preservation of nearest-neighbor distances and persistent homology-based measures. We also observe that MMAE provides a scalable approximation of Multi-Dimensional Scaling (MDS).

</details>


### [215] [Deep Tabular Representation Corrector](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16569)
*Hangting Ye,Peng Wang,Wei Fan,Xiaozhuang Song,He Zhao,Dandan Gun,Yi Chang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A model-agnostic, post-hoc representation corrector (TRC) for deep tabular models that re-estimates and maps representations to reduce shift and redundancy, improving performance without changing base model parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep tabular models suffer from representation shift and redundancy in learned features. Existing in-learning or pre-learning paradigms are resource-intensive or restrictive. A lightweight, post-hoc, model-agnostic method is desired to enhance representations without retraining the base model.

Method: Introduce TRC with two tasks: (i) Tabular Representation Re-estimation: train a shift estimator to quantify inherent shifts in tabular representations and re-estimate them; (ii) Tabular Space Mapping: a coordinate estimator maps the re-estimated representations to a light-embedding space while preserving predictive information. The approach does not modify the original model parameters (model-agnostic) and aims for high efficiency.

Result: Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art deep tabular models combined with TRC across diverse tabular benchmarks show consistent improvements, demonstrating the method's effectiveness.

Conclusion: TRC provides a lightweight, model-agnostic mechanism to enhance representations of deep tabular models without altering their parameters, yielding improved predictive performance and efficiency.

Abstract: Tabular data have been playing a mostly important role in diverse real-world fields, such as healthcare, engineering, finance, etc. The recent success of deep learning has fostered many deep networks (e.g., Transformer, ResNet) based tabular learning methods. Generally, existing deep tabular machine learning methods are along with the two paradigms, i.e., in-learning and pre-learning. In-learning methods need to train networks from scratch or impose extra constraints to regulate the representations which nonetheless train multiple tasks simultaneously and make learning more difficult, while pre-learning methods design several pretext tasks for pre-training and then conduct task-specific fine-tuning, which however need much extra training effort with prior knowledge. In this paper, we introduce a novel deep Tabular Representation Corrector, TRC, to enhance any trained deep tabular model's representations without altering its parameters in a model-agnostic manner. Specifically, targeting the representation shift and representation redundancy that hinder prediction, we propose two tasks, i.e., (i) Tabular Representation Re-estimation, that involves training a shift estimator to calculate the inherent shift of tabular representations to subsequently mitigate it, thereby re-estimating the representations and (ii) Tabular Space Mapping, that transforms the above re-estimated representations into a light-embedding vector space via a coordinate estimator while preserves crucial predictive information to minimize redundancy. The two tasks jointly enhance the representations of deep tabular models without touching on the original models thus enjoying high efficiency. Finally, we conduct extensive experiments on state-of-the-art deep tabular machine learning models coupled with TRC on various tabular benchmarks which have shown consistent superiority.

</details>


### [216] [When and Why Does Unsupervised RL Succeed in Mathematical Reasoning? A Manifold Envelopment Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16578)
*Zelin Zhang,Fei Cheng,Chenhui Chu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unsupervised intrinsic rewards to enforce concise, certain generation in outcome-based RL for LLM mathematical reasoning; investigates model priors and uses geometric diagnostics to explain stability and breakdown.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outcome-based RL is powerful but relies on costly ground-truth annotations; unsupervised RL with intrinsic rewards offers scalability yet suffers from opaque dynamics and instability; the work aims to design intrinsic rewards to enforce concise, certain outputs and to understand when this approach succeeds or fails.

Method: Design and evaluate a suite of intrinsic rewards that explicitly promote concise and certain generation; assess performance across base models with different intrinsic reasoning capabilities; introduce a geometric diagnostic framework to analyze training dynamics and identify manifolds that characterize stable regimes; analyze breakdown cases.

Result: Intrinsic rewards can boost mathematical reasoning under unsupervised RL; model priors strongly influence success, defining a boundary for effective deployment; successful configurations are characterized by geometric manifolds; the work demystifies stability conditions and where the approach collapses, offering diagnostics for when and why.

Conclusion: Enforcing conciseness and certainty via intrinsic rewards can enhance mathematical reasoning in LLMs under unsupervised RL, but stability depends on the model’s priors and geometry; the study provides practical guidelines and a diagnostic lens to predict and explain when the approach will succeed or fail.

Abstract: Although outcome-based reinforcement learning (RL) significantly advances the mathematical reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), its reliance on computationally expensive ground-truth annotations imposes a severe scalability bottleneck. Unsupervised RL guided by intrinsic rewards offers a scalable alternative, yet it suffers from opaque training dynamics and catastrophic instability, such as policy collapse and reward hacking. In this paper, we first design and evaluate a suite of intrinsic rewards that explicitly enforce concise and certain generation. Second, to discover the boundaries of this approach, we test base models across a spectrum of intrinsic reasoning capabilities, revealing how a model's foundational logical prior dictates its success or failure. Finally, to demystify why certain configurations stabilize while others collapse, we introduce a novel geometric diagnostic lens, showing that successful cases are enveloped by manifolds. Ultimately, our work goes beyond merely demonstrating that enforcing concise and certain responses successfully boosts mathematical reasoning; we reveal when this unsupervised approach breaks down and geometrically diagnose why.

</details>


### [217] [Trajectory-Optimized Time Reparameterization for Learning-Compatible Reduced-Order Modeling of Stiff Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16583)
*Joe Standridge,Daniel Livescu,Paul Cizmas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Trajectory-Optimized Time Reparameterization (TOTR) for neural ODE reduced-order models to mitigate stiffness by optimizing arc-length traversal speed to smooth training dynamics; yields improved learnability and large loss reductions on stiff systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stiff dynamical systems hinder ML-ROMs: explicit time integration becomes unstable, implicit learning loops are costly. A learnable, efficient time map is needed to enable stable training.

Method: Formulates time reparameterization as an optimization in arc-length coordinates; selects a traversal-speed profile that penalizes acceleration in stretched time; produces smoother reparameterized trajectories and improved conditioning; compares against existing TR approaches on standard stiff problems.

Result: TOTR yields smoother reparameterizations and better physics-time predictions under the same training regimen; achieves 1–2 orders of magnitude loss reduction relative to benchmark TR methods across a stiff linear system, van der Pol oscillator, and HIRES chemical kineticsmodel.

Conclusion: Effective stiffness mitigation hinges on the regularity and learnability of the time map; optimization-based TR provides a robust framework for explicit reduced-order modeling of multiscale dynamical systems.

Abstract: Stiff dynamical systems present a challenge for machine-learning reduced-order models (ML-ROMs), as explicit time integration becomes unstable in stiff regimes while implicit integration within learning loops is computationally expensive and often degrades training efficiency. Time reparameterization (TR) offers an alternative by transforming the independent variable so that rapid physical-time transients are spread over a stretched-time coordinate, enabling stable explicit integration on uniformly sampled grids. Although several TR strategies have been proposed, their effect on learnability in ML-ROMs remains incompletely understood. This work investigates time reparameterization as a stiffness-mitigation mechanism for neural ODE reduced-order modeling and introduces a trajectory-optimized TR (TOTR) formulation. The proposed approach casts time reparameterization as an optimization problem in arc-length coordinates, in which a traversal-speed profile is selected to penalize acceleration in stretched time. By targeting the smoothness of the training dynamics, this formulation produces reparameterized trajectories that are better conditioned and easier to learn than existing TR methods. TOTR is evaluated on three stiff problems: a parameterized stiff linear system, the van der Pol oscillator, and the HIRES chemical kinetics model. Across all cases, the proposed approach yields smoother reparameterizations and improved physical-time predictions under identical training regimens than other TR approaches. Quantitative results demonstrate loss reductions of one to two orders of magnitude compared to benchmark algorithms. These results highlight that effective stiffness mitigation in ML-ROMs depends critically on the regularity and learnability of the time map itself, and that optimization-based TR provides a robust framework for explicit reduced-order modeling of multiscale dynamical systems.

</details>


### [218] [Simplex-to-Euclidean Bijection for Conjugate and Calibrated Multiclass Gaussian Process](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16621)
*Bernardo Williams,Harsha Vardhan Tetali,Arto Klami,Marcelo Hartmann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conjugate, calibrated Gaussian process for multi-class classification using Aitchison geometry on the probability simplex to map to an unconstrained Euclidean space, enabling GP regression with fewer latent dimensions, conjugate inference, and scalable, well-calibrated predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve calibration and scalability in multi-class GP classification by avoiding distributional approximations and exploiting simplex geometry to reduce latent dimensionality.

Method: Map simplex-valued class probabilities via Aitchison geometry to an unconstrained Euclidean latent space; perform Gaussian process regression in this space; obtain conjugate inference; integrate with standard sparse GP frameworks for scalability; fewer latent dimensions than conventional multi-class GP models.

Result: Empirical evaluation indicates well-calibrated predictive probabilities and competitive accuracy on both synthetic and real-world datasets, with scalability enabled by sparse GP techniques.

Conclusion: The approach provides a principled, scalable framework for calibrated multi-class classification by combining simplex geometry with conjugate GP inference, producing reliable probabilities without heavy distributional approximations.

Abstract: We propose a conjugate and calibrated Gaussian process (GP) model for multi-class classification by exploiting the geometry of the probability simplex. Our approach uses Aitchison geometry to map simplex-valued class probabilities to an unconstrained Euclidean representation, turning classification into a GP regression problem with fewer latent dimensions than standard multi-class GP classifiers. This yields conjugate inference and reliable predictive probabilities without relying on distributional approximations in the model construction. The method is compatible with standard sparse GP regression techniques, enabling scalable inference on larger datasets. Empirical results show well-calibrated and competitive performance across synthetic and real-world datasets.

</details>


### [219] [Self-Aware Markov Models for Discrete Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16661)
*Gregor Kornhardt,Jannis Chemseddine,Christian Wald,Gabriele Steidl*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a learnable Markov transition kernel for masked diffusion with self-correction and adaptive stopping, enabling remasking and variable computation, achieving state-of-the-art performance on reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard masked diffusion models suffer from fixed computation (fixed denoising steps) and cannot correct earlier mistakes on the masking path, hindering reasoning tasks.

Method: Train a Markov transition kernel on its own outputs; allow tokens to be remasked; replace fixed time schedule with a learned stopping criterion; add two lightweight prediction heads to enable reuse/fine-tuning of existing pretrained models.

Result: Sudoku-Extreme achieves 95% validity, outperforming flow-based methods; Countdown-4 requires on average ~10 steps to solve ~96% of instances, with many solved in 2 steps.

Conclusion: Adaptive computation with self-correction via remasking improves reasoning performance and is compatible with pretrained models, offering efficient, task-dependent inference for reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Standard masked discrete diffusion models face limitations in reasoning tasks due to their inability to correct their own mistakes on the masking path. Since they rely on a fixed number of denoising steps, they are unable to adjust their computation to the complexity of a given problem. To address these limitations, we introduce a method based on learning a Markov transition kernel that is trained on its own outputs. This design enables tokens to be remasked, allowing the model to correct its previous mistakes. Furthermore, we do not need a fixed time schedule but use a trained stopping criterion. This allows for adaptation of the number of function evaluations to the difficulty of the reasoning problem. Our adaptation adds two lightweight prediction heads, enabling reuse and fine-tuning of existing pretrained models. On the Sudoku-Extreme dataset we clearly outperform other flow based methods with a validity of 95%. For the Countdown-4 we only need in average of 10 steps to solve almost 96% of them correctly, while many problems can be solved already in 2 steps.

</details>


### [220] [Grid-World Representations in Transformers Reflect Predictive Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16689)
*Sasha Brenner,Thomas R. Knösche,Nico Scherf*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Demonstrates that optimal prediction in a toy stochastic process yields low-dimensional predictive representations in transformer models, and that learned activations align with analytically derived sufficient statistics of the process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how world-model-like representations in next-token predictors relate to the geometry of the data-generating process, by using a controlled stochastic environment where the sufficient statistics are analytically known.

Method: Use constrained random walks on a 2D lattice ending at a fixed endpoint after a fixed number of steps. Derive sufficient vectors from the analytic predictive distribution dependent on position and time horizon. Train decoder-only transformers on prefixes drawn from the exact walk distribution. Analyze hidden activations across layers to compare with the sufficient vectors.

Result: Found strong alignment between learned representations and ground-truth predictive vectors across models and layers; representations are often low-dimensional; evidence that world-model-like structures emerge from predictive geometry of data.

Conclusion: Even in a simplified toy system, geometric representations enabling optimal prediction can be traced to data's predictive structure, suggesting a lens for understanding how neural networks internalize grammatical/structural constraints; potential generalization to more complex world models and grammar.

Abstract: Next-token predictors often appear to develop internal representations of the latent world and its rules. The probabilistic nature of these models suggests a deep connection between the structure of the world and the geometry of probability distributions. In order to understand this link more precisely, we use a minimal stochastic process as a controlled setting: constrained random walks on a two-dimensional lattice that must reach a fixed endpoint after a predetermined number of steps. Optimal prediction of this process solely depends on a sufficient vector determined by the walker's position relative to the target and the remaining time horizon; in other words, the probability distributions are parametrized by the world's geometry. We train decoder-only transformers on prefixes sampled from the exact distribution of these walks and compare their hidden activations to the analytically derived sufficient vectors. Across models and layers, the learned representations align strongly with the ground-truth predictive vectors and are often low-dimensional. This provides a concrete example in which world-model-like representations can be directly traced back to the predictive geometry of the data itself. Although demonstrated in a simplified toy system, the analysis suggests that geometric representations supporting optimal prediction may provide a useful lens for studying how neural networks internalize grammatical and other structural constraints.

</details>


### [221] [Learning Lineage-guided Geodesics with Finsler Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16708)
*Aaron Zweig,Mingxuan Zhang,David A. Knowles,Elham Azizi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a Finsler metric that blends geometry with classification to integrate continuous and discrete priors for trajectory inference, improving interpolation performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of using only continuous geometric priors (Riemannian) or only discrete prior knowledge by enabling joint use of both in trajectory inference.

Method: Introduce a Finsler metric that combines geometry with a classification component encoding admissible transitions; integrate both priors into the trajectory inference framework.

Result: Demonstrates improved interpolation accuracy on synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: Jointly integrating geometric and discrete priors via a Finsler metric enhances trajectory inference and offers a principled approach to fusing priors in dynamical-system analysis.

Abstract: Trajectory inference investigates how to interpolate paths between observed timepoints of dynamical systems, such as temporally resolved population distributions, with the goal of inferring trajectories at unseen times and better understanding system dynamics. Previous work has focused on continuous geometric priors, utilizing data-dependent spatial features to define a Riemannian metric. In many applications, there exists discrete, directed prior knowledge over admissible transitions (e.g. lineage trees in developmental biology). We introduce a Finsler metric that combines geometry with classification and incorporate both types of priors in trajectory inference, yielding improved performance on interpolation tasks in synthetic and real-world data.

</details>


### [222] [Novelty-Driven Target-Space Discovery in Automated Electron and Scanning Probe Microscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16715)
*Utkarsh Pratiush,Kamyar Barakati,Boris N. Slautin,Catherine C. Bodinger,Christopher D. Lowe,Brandi M. Cossairt,Sergei V. Kalinin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A deep-kernel-learning BEACON framework for discovery in target space that guides experimental search strategies in automated microscopy, supported by offline benchmarking and real-STEM deployment with reproducible notebooks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In automated microscopy, key information often resides in the target space (sequences of spectra or responses) rather than visible image features. This necessitates discovery-driven strategies that actively seek new behaviors rather than merely optimizing predefined objectives.

Method: Develop BEACON with deep-kernel learning to model structure-property relationships during experiments and steer exploration toward diverse response regimes. Validate via demonstration workflows on pre-acquired ground-truth data, benchmarking against classical acquisition strategies using monitoring functions for exploration quality, target-space coverage, and surrogate-model behavior. Build a reusable benchmarking framework, transition from offline validation to real-STEM implementation, and provide notebooks for reproducibility and adaptation.

Result: Demonstrated on offline ground-truth datasets with benchmarks against traditional acquisitions; defined monitoring metrics enabling transparent assessment of exploration and surrogate-model behavior; successfully ported to STEM for real experimental deployment; notebooks support reproducibility and adaptation by the broader community.

Conclusion: The BEACON framework offers a practical, extensible platform for discovery-driven experiments in automated microscopy, with transparent benchmarks and reproducible workflows that bridge offline validation and real-world instrumentation, enabling broader adoption and extension by the research community.

Abstract: Modern automated microscopy faces a fundamental discovery challenge: in many systems, the most important scientific information does not reside in the immediately visible image features, but in the target space of sequentially acquired spectra or functional responses, making it essential to develop strategies that can actively search for new behaviors rather than simply optimize known objectives. Here, we developed a deep-kernel-learning BEACON framework that is explicitly designed to guide discovery in the target space by learning structure-property relationships during the experiment and using that evolving model to seek diverse response regimes. We first established the method through demonstration workflows built on pre-acquired ground-truth datasets, which enabled direct benchmarking against classical acquisition strategies and allowed us to define a set of monitoring functions for comparing exploration quality, target-space coverage, and surrogate-model behavior in a transparent and reproducible manner. This benchmarking framework provides a practical basis for evaluating discovery-driven algorithms, not just optimization performance. We then operationalized and deployed the workflow on STEM, showing that the approach can transition from offline validation to real experimental implementation. To support adoption and extension by the broader community, the associated notebooks are available, allowing users to reproduce the workflows, test the benchmarks, and adapt the method to their own instruments and datasets.

</details>


### [223] [Federated Learning with Multi-Partner OneFlorida+ Consortium Data for Predicting Major Postoperative Complications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16723)
*Yuanfang Ren,Varun Sai Vemuri,Zhenhong Hu,Benjamin Shickel,Ziyuan Guan,Tyler J. Loftus,Parisa Rashidi,Tezcan Ozrazgat-Baslanti,Azra Bihorac*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated learning across five centers using OneFlorida data improves prediction of postoperative complications and mortality, with AUROC/AUPRC comparable or superior to local and central models, while preserving privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Develop robust, generalizable, privacy-preserving predictive models for major postoperative complications and mortality using multicenter data and federated learning.

Method: Retrospective multicenter cohort (5 institutions) with 358,644 patients and 494,163 inpatient major surgical procedures (2012-2023). Develop and validate federated learning models to predict ICU admission, mechanical ventilation, AKI, and in-hospital mortality. Compare against local single-center models and a central pooled model. Internal and external validation. Performance assessed primarily with AUROC and AUPRC.

Result: Federated learning models achieved predictive performance that is comparable or superior to both local and central models across all outcomes and sites, demonstrating strong generalizability and privacy-preservation.

Conclusion: Federated learning is feasible and beneficial for multicenter clinical decision support in predicting major postoperative complications and mortality, enabling robust, generalizable models without centralized data sharing.

Abstract: Background: This study aims to develop and validate federated learning models for predicting major postoperative complications and mortality using a large multicenter dataset from the OneFlorida Data Trust. We hypothesize that federated learning models will offer robust generalizability while preserving data privacy and security. Methods: This retrospective, longitudinal, multicenter cohort study included 358,644 adult patients admitted to five healthcare institutions, who underwent 494,163 inpatient major surgical procedures from 2012-2023. We developed and internally and externally validated federated learning models to predict the postoperative risk of intensive care unit (ICU) admission, mechanical ventilation (MV) therapy, acute kidney injury (AKI), and in-hospital mortality. These models were compared with local models trained on data from a single center and central models trained on a pooled dataset from all centers. Performance was primarily evaluated using area under the receiver operating characteristics curve (AUROC) and the area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) values. Results: Our federated learning models demonstrated strong predictive performance, with AUROC scores consistently comparable or superior performance in terms of AUROC and AUPRC across all outcomes and sites. Our federated learning models also demonstrated strong generalizability, with comparable or superior performance in terms of both AUROC and AUPRC compared to the best local learning model at each site. Conclusions: By leveraging multicenter data, we developed robust, generalizable, and privacy-preserving predictive models for major postoperative complications and mortality. These findings support the feasibility of federated learning in clinical decision support systems.

</details>


### [224] [The Cost of Reasoning: Chain-of-Thought Induces Overconfidence in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16728)
*Robert Welch,Emir Konuk,Kevin Smith*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chain-of-thought reasoning in vision-language models degrades uncertainty estimates due to implicit answer conditioning; agreement-based consistency remains a robust and practical uncertainty proxy in reasoning-enabled VLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty quantification is essential in high-stakes VLM applications. While chain-of-thought (CoT) can improve accuracy, its impact on uncertainty reliability is unclear; understanding the mechanism and identifying robust UQ strategies is needed.

Method: Empirical analysis of VLMs with reasoning traces. The study examines how token-level probabilities align with the model's own reasoning versus the true correctness, assesses UQ reliability under CoT, and compares with agreement-based consistency as an alternative UQ signal.

Result: Reasoning traces lead to overconfident predictions: token probabilities increasingly reflect consistency with the model's internal reasoning trace rather than uncertainty about the true answer, degrading most UQ estimates. In contrast, agreement-based consistency remains robust and often improves when reasoning is used, making it a practical UQ method.

Conclusion: For uncertainty estimation in reasoning-enabled VLMs, favor agreement-based consistency and be cautious of CoT-induced miscalibration. Design UQ methods that decouple reasoning traces from uncertainty and consider robust consistency-based signals.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes settings where reliable uncertainty quantification (UQ) is as important as predictive accuracy. Extended reasoning via chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting or reasoning-trained models has become ubiquitous in modern VLM pipelines, yet its effect on UQ reliability remains poorly understood. We show that reasoning consistently degrades the quality of most uncertainty estimates, even when it improves task accuracy. We identify implicit answer conditioning as the primary mechanism: as reasoning traces converge on a conclusion before the final answer is generated, token probabilities increasingly reflect consistency with the model's own reasoning trace rather than uncertainty about correctness. In effect, the model becomes overconfident in its answer. In contrast, agreement-based consistency remains robust and often improves under reasoning, making it a practical choice for uncertainty estimation in reasoning-enabled VLMs.

</details>


### [225] [GeMA: Learning Latent Manifold Frontiers for Benchmarking Complex Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16729)
*Jia Ming Li,Anupriya,Daniel J. Graham*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GeMA introduces a manifold-frontier framework via ProMan-VAE to model production sets as latent manifolds, enabling nonlinear frontiers, endogenous peer groups, scale-invariant benchmarking, and a geometric robustness score. It matches classical methods under standard assumptions and excels in heterogeneity, non-convexity, or size-bias contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical frontier methods (DEA, SFA) rely on restrictive production-set assumptions and offer limited treatment of heterogeneity and scale effects; a flexible, nonlinear, geometry-based frontier is needed.

Method: ProMan-VAE: train a productivity-manifold variational autoencoder whose split-head encoder isolates technology and inefficiency; model the production set boundary as a low-dimensional manifold boundary; efficiency measured relative to this manifold; latent space yields peer groups, a quotient construction for scale invariance, and a local certification radius from decoder Jacobian and Lipschitz bound to quantify robustness.

Result: Synthetic data with non-convex frontiers, heterogeneity, and scale bias; real-world case studies: global urban rail systems (COMET), British rail operators (ORR), Penn World Table economies, and wind-farm data; GeMA matches established methods under classical assumptions and provides additional insight where heterogeneity, non-convexity, or size-related bias are pronounced.

Conclusion: GeMA offers a flexible, geometry-based frontier framework that integrates latent technology structure and robustness certification, uncovering endogenous peer groups and enabling scale-invariant benchmarking while maintaining performance in standard scenarios.

Abstract: Benchmarking the performance of complex systems such as rail networks, renewable generation assets and national economies is central to transport planning, regulation and macroeconomic analysis. Classical frontier methods, notably Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA) and Stochastic Frontier Analysis (SFA), estimate an efficient frontier in the observed input-output space and define efficiency as distance to this frontier, but rely on restrictive assumptions on the production set and only indirectly address heterogeneity and scale effects.
  We propose Geometric Manifold Analysis (GeMA), a latent manifold frontier framework implemented via a productivity-manifold variational autoencoder (ProMan-VAE). Instead of specifying a frontier function in the observed space, GeMA represents the production set as the boundary of a low-dimensional manifold embedded in the joint input-output space. A split-head encoder learns latent variables that capture technological structure and operational inefficiency. Efficiency is evaluated with respect to the learned manifold, endogenous peer groups arise as clusters in latent technology space, a quotient construction supports scale-invariant benchmarking, and a local certification radius, derived from the decoder Jacobian and a Lipschitz bound, quantifies the geometric robustness of efficiency scores.
  We validate GeMA on synthetic data with non-convex frontiers, heterogeneous technologies and scale bias, and on four real-world case studies: global urban rail systems (COMET), British rail operators (ORR), national economies (Penn World Table) and a high-frequency wind-farm dataset. Across these domains GeMA behaves comparably to established methods when classical assumptions hold, and provides additional insight in settings with pronounced heterogeneity, non-convexity or size-related bias.

</details>


### [226] [Understanding Quantization of Optimizer States in LLM Pre-training: Dynamics of State Staleness and Effectiveness of State Resets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16731)
*Kristi Topollai,Anna Choromanska*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Low-precision EMA states in quantized optimizers stall due to quantization; a predictive model and theory-guided resets recover performance while saving memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memory-efficient pre-training requires quantized optimizer states; observed stalling and nontrivial dynamics call for understanding and controlling resets.

Method: Develop a simple predictive model of one-step stalling probabilities, analyze accumulation over time, derive a theory-guided reset schedule; validate with controlled simulations and LLM pre-training.

Result: Suitable reset schedules restore responsiveness and recovers performance; significant memory savings on optimizer state.

Conclusion: In low-precision regimes, the timing of resets is crucial; the proposed model-guided resets offer a practical mechanism to balance memory efficiency and optimization performance.

Abstract: Quantizing optimizer states is becoming an important ingredient of memory-efficient large-scale pre-training, but the resulting optimizer dynamics remain only partially understood. We study low-precision exponential moving average (EMA) optimizer states and show how quantization can cause many nominal updates to round back to the same stored value, making the state effectively stale and slowing adaptation beyond what the nominal decay would suggest. We then develop a simple predictive model of stalling that estimates one-step stalling probabilities and characterizes how stalling builds up over time after the initialization. This perspective provides a mechanistic explanation for why optimizer-state resets help in low precision: once a quantized EMA becomes effectively stale, resetting it can temporarily restore responsiveness. Motivated by this picture, we derive a simple theory-guided method for choosing useful reset periods, showing that in low precision the key question is not only whether resets help, but when they should be applied. Experiments in controlled simulations and LLM pre-training show that suitable reset schedules recover the performance lost to low-precision state storage while substantially reducing optimizer-state memory.

</details>


### [227] [SpecMoE: Spectral Mixture-of-Experts Foundation Model for Cross-Species EEG Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16739)
*D. Darankoum,C. Habermacher,J. Volle,S. Grudinin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A foundation EEG model (SpecHi-Net) uses Gaussian-smoothed masking on short-time Fourier transform maps, combined with a three-expert SpecMoE framework, to achieve state-of-the-art performance across diverse EEG tasks and strong cross-subject/species generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome biases toward high-frequency components in self-supervised EEG pretraining on raw signals and to enable robust, multi-task EEG decoding with cross-subject and cross-species generalization.

Method: Apply time, frequency, and time-frequency Gaussian masking to STFT maps; train SpecHi-Net with a U-shaped hierarchical encoder-decoder; partition data into three subsets to train independent expert models; fuse experts via SpecMoE with a learned spectral gating mechanism.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple EEG tasks (sleep staging, emotion recognition, motor imagery, anomaly detection, drug effect prediction) and demonstrates strong cross-subject and cross-species generalization (human and murine EEG datasets).

Conclusion: The Gaussian-smoothed masking strategy combined with a mixture-of-experts fusion enables robust, generalizable EEG decoding, handling both high- and low-frequency neural patterns under aggressive masking.

Abstract: Decoding the orchestration of neural activity in electroencephalography (EEG) signals is a central challenge in bridging neuroscience with artificial intelligence. Foundation models have made strides in generalized EEG decoding, yet many existing frameworks primarily relying on separate temporal and spectral masking of raw signals during self-supervised pretraining. Such strategies often tend to bias learning toward high-frequency oscillations, as low-frequency rhythmic patterns can be easily inferred from the unmasked signal. We introduce a foundation model that utilizes a novel Gaussian-smoothed masking scheme applied to short-time Fourier transform (STFT) maps. By jointly applying time, frequency, and time-frequency Gaussian masks, we make the reconstruction task much more challenging, forcing the model to learn intricate neural patterns across both high- and low-frequency domains. To effectively recover signals under this aggressive masking strategy, we design SpecHi-Net, a U-shaped hierarchical architecture with multiple encoding and decoding stages. To accelerate large-scale pretraining, we partition the data into three subsets, each used to train an independent expert model. We then combine these models through SpecMoE, a mixture of experts framework guided by a learned spectral gating mechanism. SpecMoE achieves state-of-the-art performance across a diverse set of EEG decoding tasks, including sleep staging, emotion recognition, motor imagery classification, abnormal signal detection, and drug effect prediction. Importantly, the model demonstrates strong cross-species and cross-subject generalization, maintaining high accuracy on both human and murine EEG datasets.

</details>


### [228] [Bayesian Inference of Psychometric Variables From Brain and Behavior in Implicit Association Tests](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16741)
*Christian A. Kothe,Sean Mullen,Michael V. Bronstein,Grant Hanada,Marcelo Cicconet,Aaron N. McInnes,Tim Mullen,Marc Aafjes,Scott R. Sponheim,Alik S. Widge*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A sparse hierarchical Bayesian model is proposed to infer mental health-related psychometric variables from IAT data, aiming to exceed the predictive performance of D-score; validates on suicidality-related E-IAT and psychosis-related PSY-IAT with small samples, achieving AUCs up to ~0.76 overall and 0.79 in a MDD-subset, though with wide confidence intervals; performance is comparable to reference methods, and larger/independent validation is needed for clinical utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limited predictive power of the gold-standard D-score (reaction-time-based) by leveraging multi-modal data in a trainable, efficient model suitable for small cohorts typical of IAT studies.

Method: A sparse hierarchical Bayesian model, a multivariate generalization of the D-score with trainable parameters, designed for parameter efficiency. Analyzed data from two IAT variants: suicidality-related E-IAT (n=39) and psychosis-related PSY-IAT (n=34).

Result: Best configurations yield AUCs 0.73 (E-IAT) and 0.76 (PSY-IAT); corrected 95% CIs wide (±0.18); FDR q=0.10 (marginal). Restricting E-IAT to MDD improves AUC to 0.79 [0.62,0.97], significant at q=0.05. Performance on par with shrinkage LDA and EEGNet; much higher accuracy than near-chance D-scores; better cross-task consistency.

Conclusion: The framework shows promise for improved IAT-based assessment of mental health experiences (entrapment, psychosis) and potentially other conditions, but requires validation on larger, independent cohorts to establish clinical utility.

Abstract: Objective. We establish a principled method for inferring mental health related psychometric variables from neural and behavioral data using the Implicit Association Test (IAT) as the data generation engine, aiming to overcome the limited predictive performance (typically under 0.7 AUC) of the gold-standard D-score method, which relies solely on reaction times.
  Approach. We propose a sparse hierarchical Bayesian model that leverages multi-modal data to predict experiences related to mental illness symptoms in new participants. The model is a multivariate generalization of the D-score with trainable parameters, engineered for parameter efficiency in the small-cohort regime typical of IAT studies. Data from two IAT variants were analyzed: a suicidality-related E-IAT ($n=39$) and a psychosis-related PSY-IAT ($n=34$).
  Main Results. Our approach overcomes a high inter-individual variability and low within-session effect size in the dataset, reaching AUCs of 0.73 (E-IAT) and 0.76 (PSY-IAT) in the best modality configurations, though corrected 95% confidence intervals are wide ($\pm 0.18$) and results are marginally significant after FDR correction ($q=0.10$). Restricting the E-IAT to MDD participants improves AUC to 0.79 $[0.62, 0.97]$ (significant at $q=0.05$). Performance is on par with the best reference methods (shrinkage LDA and EEGNet) for each task, even when the latter were adapted to the task, while the proposed method was not. Accuracy was substantially above near-chance D-scores (0.50-0.53 AUC) in both tasks, with more consistent cross-task performance than any single reference method.
  Significance. Our framework shows promise for enhancing IAT-based assessment of experiences related to entrapment and psychosis, and potentially other mental health conditions, though further validation on larger and independent cohorts will be needed to establish clinical utility.

</details>


### [229] [A Practical Algorithm for Feature-Rich, Non-Stationary Bandit Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16755)
*Wei Min Loh,Sajib Kumer Sinha,Ankur Agarwal,Pascal Poupart*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Conditionally Coupled Contextual C3 Thompson Sampling (C3-CTS) for Bernoulli bandits, integrating an improved Nadaraya-Watson estimator on embedding space with Thompson sampling to handle contextual features, non-linear rewards, and non-stationary yet correlated reward distributions; yields empirical gains on OpenML and MIND datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Realistically extending contextual bandits to dense features, non-linear rewards, and time-varying but correlated reward processes to enable broader applications (e.g., recommendations).

Method: Propose conditionally coupled contextual C3 Thompson sampling for Bernoulli bandits; combines improved Nadaraya-Watson estimator in embedding space with online Thompson sampling to learn without retraining while modeling time-varying correlations.

Result: Outperforms baselines with 5.7% lower average cumulative regret on four OpenML datasets; 12.4% click lift on MIND compared to alternatives.

Conclusion: The approach effectively fuses nonparametric embedding-based regression with Bayesian exploration, addressing dense features, non-linear rewards, and non-stationary correlation, with practical performance gains.

Abstract: Contextual bandits are incredibly useful in many practical problems. We go one step further by devising a more realistic problem that combines: (1) contextual bandits with dense arm features, (2) non-linear reward functions, and (3) a generalization of correlated bandits where reward distributions change over time but the degree of correlation maintains. This formulation lends itself to a wider set of applications such as recommendation tasks. To solve this problem, we introduce conditionally coupled contextual C3 Thompson sampling for Bernoulli bandits. It combines an improved Nadaraya-Watson estimator on an embedding space with Thompson sampling that allows online learning without retraining. Empirical results show that C3 outperforms the next best algorithm by 5.7% lower average cumulative regret on four OpenML tabular datasets as well as demonstrating a 12.4% click lift on Microsoft News Dataset (MIND) compared to other algorithms.

</details>


### [230] [pADAM: A Plug-and-Play All-in-One Diffusion Architecture for Multi-Physics Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16757)
*Amirhossein Mollaali,Bongseok Kim,Christian Moya,Guang Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: pADAM is a unified generative framework that learns a shared probabilistic prior across heterogeneous PDE families to enable forward prediction, inverse inference, uncertainty quantification, and model selection within a single architecture, without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization across disparate physical laws and regimes remains difficult; existing solvers are typically restricted to single-equation settings, hindering transfer and multi-task inference in physics. A unified probabilistic approach could enable cross-regime prediction and uncertainty-aware scientific inference.

Method: Train a single generative model to learn a joint distribution over system states and, when available, physical parameters across multiple PDE families. Use this shared prior for forward prediction and inverse inference within one architecture. Incorporate conformal prediction to provide uncertainty with coverage guarantees. Employ the learned generative representation to perform probabilistic model selection from two sparse snapshots.

Result: The approach achieves accurate inference under sparse observations across benchmarks from scalar diffusion to nonlinear Navier–Stokes equations. It provides reliable uncertainty quantification with coverage guarantees via conformal prediction and can identify governing laws through probabilistic model selection from minimal data.

Conclusion: pADAM demonstrates the viability of generative multi-physics modeling for unified, uncertainty-aware scientific inference, enabling cross-regime prediction, inverse tasks, uncertainty quantification, and data-efficient model discovery without retraining.

Abstract: Generalizing across disparate physical laws remains a fundamental challenge for artificial intelligence in science. Existing deep-learning solvers are largely confined to single-equation settings, limiting transfer across physical regimes and inference tasks. Here we introduce pADAM, a unified generative framework that learns a shared probabilistic prior across heterogeneous partial differential equation families. Through a learned joint distribution of system states and, where applicable, physical parameters, pADAM supports forward prediction and inverse inference within a single architecture without retraining. Across benchmarks ranging from scalar diffusion to nonlinear Navier--Stokes equations, pADAM achieves accurate inference even under sparse observations. Combined with conformal prediction, it also provides reliable uncertainty quantification with coverage guarantees. In addition, pADAM performs probabilistic model selection from only two sparse snapshots, identifying governing laws through its learned generative representation. These results highlight the potential of generative multi-physics modeling for unified and uncertainty-aware scientific inference.

</details>


### [231] [SOMP: Scalable Gradient Inversion for Large Language Models via Subspace-Guided Orthogonal Matching Pursuit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16761)
*Yibo Li,Qiongxiu Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SOMP is a scalable gradient-inversion method using subspace-guided orthogonal matching pursuit to recover text from aggregated gradients, outperforming prior methods in large-batch, long-sequence regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gradient inversion poses a privacy risk for LLMs; existing attacks struggle with large batch sizes and long sequences due to signal mixing and high computation costs.

Method: Formulates text reconstruction from aggregated gradients as a sparse signal recovery problem. Exploits head-wise geometric structure and sample-level sparsity in transformer gradients. Uses subspace-guided orthogonal matching pursuit to progressively narrow the search space and disentangle mixed signals without exhaustive search.

Result: SOMP consistently outperforms prior methods across multiple LLM families, scales, and languages in the aggregated-gradient regime. For long sequences at batch size B=16, it achieves substantially higher reconstruction fidelity than strong baselines while remaining computationally competitive. Even under extreme aggregation (up to B=128), it recovers meaningful text, indicating privacy leakage persists in regimes where prior attacks fail.

Conclusion: Aggregated-gradient leakage remains a privacy concern for LLMs, and SOMP provides a scalable, effective attack framework by leveraging subspace structure and sparsity; this highlights the need for defenses targeting gradient leakage at scale.

Abstract: Gradient inversion attacks reveal that private training text can be reconstructed from shared gradients, posing a privacy risk to large language models (LLMs). While prior methods perform well in small-batch settings, scaling to larger batch sizes and longer sequences remains challenging due to severe signal mixing, high computational cost, and degraded fidelity. We present SOMP (Subspace-Guided Orthogonal Matching Pursuit), a scalable gradient inversion framework that casts text recovery from aggregated gradients as a sparse signal recovery problem. Our key insight is that aggregated transformer gradients retain exploitable head-wise geometric structure together with sample-level sparsity. SOMP leverages these properties to progressively narrow the search space and disentangle mixed signals without exhaustive search. Experiments across multiple LLM families, model scales, and five languages show that SOMP consistently outperforms prior methods in the aggregated-gradient regime.For long sequences at batch size B=16, SOMP achieves substantially higher reconstruction fidelity than strong baselines, while remaining computationally competitive. Even under extreme aggregation (up to B=128), SOMP still recovers meaningful text, suggesting that privacy leakage can persist in regimes where prior attacks become much less effective.

</details>


### [232] [Conservative Continuous-Time Treatment Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16789)
*Nora Schneider,Georg Manten,Niki Kilbertus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conservative continuous-time control for treatment optimization over irregular patient trajectories using a signature-based MMD regularizer on path space, yielding a computable upper bound on true cost and improved robustness vs non-conservative baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address extrapolation risk when optimizing dynamics learned from irregular, potentially shift-distributed patient data; naive model-based optimization can exploit model errors and propose out-of-support policies.

Method: Model dynamics as a controlled stochastic differential equation with treatment as continuous-time control. Introduce a signature-based MMD regularizer on path space to penalize induced trajectory distributions that diverge from observed data, yielding a computable upper bound on the true cost for optimization.

Result: Experiments on benchmark datasets show improved robustness and performance relative to non-conservative baselines, validating the approach.

Conclusion: A principled conservatism via path-space regularization yields safer, more robust treatment policies under model misspecification and irregular sampling, with practical computable objectives.

Abstract: We develop a conservative continuous-time stochastic control framework for treatment optimization from irregularly sampled patient trajectories. The unknown patient dynamics are modeled as a controlled stochastic differential equation with treatment as a continuous-time control. Naive model-based optimization can exploit model errors and propose out-of-support controls, so optimizing the estimated dynamics may not optimize the true dynamics. To limit extrapolation, we add a consistent signature-based MMD regularizer on path space that penalizes treatment plans whose induced trajectory distribution deviates from observed trajectories. The resulting objective minimizes a computable upper bound on the true cost. Experiments on benchmark datasets show improved robustness and performance compared to non-conservative baselines.

</details>


### [233] [Adaptive Moments are Surprisingly Effective for Plug-and-Play Diffusion Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16797)
*Christian Belardi,Justin Lovelace,Kilian Q. Weinberger,Carla P. Gomes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive moment estimation stabilizes noisy likelihood scores in guided diffusion sampling, enabling a simple, efficient method to achieve state-of-the-art results in image restoration and class-conditional generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Likelihood scores used in guided diffusion are often intractable and must be approximated, introducing gradient noise that degrades sampling quality. Stabilizing these estimates should improve alignment and performance.

Method: Apply an adaptive moment estimation (Adam-like) approach to the likelihood-score updates during diffusion sampling, dampening gradient noise without altering the model architecture.

Result: Empirically achieves state-of-the-art results on image restoration and class-conditional generation, outperforming more complex and computationally expensive methods; validated on both synthetic and real data.

Conclusion: Adaptive moment estimation offers a simple, effective, and computationally efficient way to mitigate gradient noise in guided diffusion, improving sampling alignment and overall performance.

Abstract: Guided diffusion sampling relies on approximating often intractable likelihood scores, which introduces significant noise into the sampling dynamics. We propose using adaptive moment estimation to stabilize these noisy likelihood scores during sampling. Despite its simplicity, our approach achieves state-of-the-art results on image restoration and class-conditional generation tasks, outperforming more complicated methods, which are often computationally more expensive.
