To address this issue, we design an adaptive ToM (A-ToM) agent, which can align in ToM orders with its partner. Based on prior interactions, the agent estimates the partner's likely ToM order and leverages this estimation to predict the partner's action, thereby facilitating behavioral coordination. We conduct empirical evaluations on four multi-agent coordination tasks: a repeated matrix game, two grid navigation tasks and an Overcooked task. The results validate our findings on ToM alignment and demonstrate the effectiveness of our A-ToM agent. Furthermore, we discuss the generalizability of our A-ToM to non-LLM-based agents, as well as what would diminish the importance of ToM alignment.

</details>


### [288] [Designing for Disagreement: Front-End Guardrails for Assistance Allocation in LLM-Enabled Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16537)
*Carmen Ng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A procedural front-end pattern—bounded calibration with contestability—for LLM-enabled robot assistance that limits modes, maintains legibility at deferral, and enables outcome-specific contestation, aiming to govern pluralistic values and LLM uncertainty in real-time, multi-user settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-enabled robots must navigate pluralistic human values and unpredictable, context-dependent LLM behavior. Current guardrails are under-specified, risking silent value skews or overwhelming user-configurability under time pressure.

Method: Propose a design pattern (bounded calibration with contestability) consisting of (i) a governance-approved menu of admissible modes for prioritization, (ii) legible interaction terms at the deferral point, and (iii) an outcome-specific contest pathway that does not renegotiate the global rule; illustrated with a public-concourse robot vignette; outlines an evaluation agenda focusing on legibility, procedural legitimacy, and actionability, including automation bias and contest-channel usability.

Result: A conceptual pattern and implementation sketch that addresses governance, transparency, and user contestability in real-time multi-user robot assistance; paired with a vignette and a forward-looking evaluation plan.

Conclusion: Bounded calibration with contestability offers a practical framework to manage value pluralism and LLM uncertainty without silent defaults or unrestricted user-configurate bias, while highlighting need for rigorous evaluation of legibility, legitimacy, actionability and contest mechanisms.

Abstract: LLM-enabled robots prioritizing scarce assistance in social settings face pluralistic values and LLM behavioral variability: reasonable people can disagree about who is helped first, while LLM-mediated interaction policies vary across prompts, contexts, and groups in ways that are difficult to anticipate or verify at contact point. Yet user-facing guardrails for real-time, multi-user assistance allocation remain under-specified. We propose bounded calibration with contestability, a procedural front-end pattern that (i) constrains prioritization to a governance-approved menu of admissible modes, (ii) keeps the active mode legible in interaction-relevant terms at the point of deferral, and (iii) provides an outcome-specific contest pathway without renegotiating the global rule. Treating pluralism and LLM uncertainty as standing conditions, the pattern avoids both silent defaults that hide implicit value skews and wide-open user-configurable "value settings" that shift burden under time pressure. We illustrate the pattern with a public-concourse robot vignette and outline an evaluation agenda centered on legibility, procedural legitimacy, and actionability, including risks of automation bias and uneven usability of contest channels.

</details>


### [289] [NeSy-Route: A Neuro-Symbolic Benchmark for Constrained Route Planning in Remote Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16307)
*Ming Yang,Zhi Zhou,Shi-Yu Tian,Kun-Yang Yu,Lan-Zhe Guo,Yu-Feng Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NeSy-Route: a large-scale neuro-symbolic benchmark for constrained route planning in remote sensing, with automated data generation and a three-level evaluation protocol, showing MLLMs struggle with perception and planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current remote-sensing benchmarks focus on perception and reasoning in multimodal LLMs but largely ignore planning; scaling and validating planning tasks is difficult, leading to inadequate evaluation.

Method: An automated data-generation framework that integrates high-fidelity semantic masks with heuristic search to produce diverse route-planning tasks with provably optimal solutions; a three-level hierarchical neuro-symbolic evaluation protocol to assess perception, reasoning, and planning.

Result: Collected 10,821 route-planning samples, nearly 10x larger than the prior benchmark; comprehensive evaluation reveals significant deficiencies in perception and planning among state-of-the-art MLLMs.

Conclusion: NeSy-Route offers a scalable, rigorous resource to drive development and analysis of more capable MLLMs for remote sensing, enabling fine-grained assessment across perception, reasoning, and planning.

Abstract: Remote sensing underpins crucial applications such as disaster relief and ecological field surveys, where systems must understand complex scenes and constraints and make reliable decisions. Current remote-sensing benchmarks mainly focus on evaluating perception and reasoning capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs). They fail to assess planning capability, stemming either from the difficulty of curating and validating planning tasks at scale or from evaluation protocols that are inaccurate and inadequate. To address these limitations, we introduce NeSy-Route, a large-scale neuro-symbolic benchmark for constrained route planning in remote sensing. Within this benchmark, we introduce an automated data-generation framework that integrates high-fidelity semantic masks with heuristic search to produce diverse route-planning tasks with provably optimal solutions. This allows NeSy-Route to comprehensively evaluate planning across 10,821 route-planning samples, nearly 10 times larger than the largest prior benchmark. Furthermore, a three-level hierarchical neuro-symbolic evaluation protocol is developed to enable accurate assessment and support fine-grained analysis on perception, reasoning, and planning simultaneously. Our comprehensive evaluation of various state-of-the-art MLLMs demonstrates that existing MLLMs show significant deficiencies in perception and planning capabilities. We hope NeSy-Route can support further research and development of more powerful MLLMs for remote sensing.

</details>


### [290] [Learning to Predict, Discover, and Reason in High-Dimensional Discrete Event Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16313)
*Hugo Math*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Modeling diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs) as a language for automotive fault diagnostics, introducing Transformer-based predictive maintenance, scalable causal discovery, and a multi-agent system to automatically synthesize Boolean error-pattern (EP) rules for high-dimensional event streams.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing vehicle complexity yields tens of thousands of unique DTCs; manual grouping into higher-level error patterns via Boolean rules is costly, error-prone, and scales poorly with data size; high-cardinality, long sequences demand new architectures capable of handling large event spaces and causal reasoning.

Method: Three-part thesis: (1) Transformer-based architectures for predictive maintenance on high-dimensional DTC sequences; (2) scalable sample- and population-level causal discovery frameworks; (3) a multi-agent system that automates synthesis of Boolean EP rules, enabling automated fault pattern reasoning.

Result: Introduces and advocates Transformer-based predictive models, scalable causal-discovery frameworks, and a multi-agent EP-rule synthesis system; presents a coherent framework that unifies event-sequence modeling, causal discovery, and LLM-inspired reasoning for high-dimensional automotive event streams.

Conclusion: The framework progresses from prediction through causal understanding to reasoning for vehicle diagnostics, aiming to scale automated fault diagnostics as vehicle systems become more complex.

Abstract: Electronic control units (ECUs) embedded within modern vehicles generate a large number of asynchronous events known as diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs). These discrete events form complex temporal sequences that reflect the evolving health of the vehicle's subsystems. In the automotive industry, domain experts manually group these codes into higher-level error patterns (EPs) using Boolean rules to characterize system faults and ensure safety. However, as vehicle complexity grows, this manual process becomes increasingly costly, error-prone, and difficult to scale. Notably, the number of unique DTCs in a modern vehicle is on the same order of magnitude as the vocabulary of a natural language, often numbering in the tens of thousands. This observation motivates a paradigm shift: treating diagnostic sequences as a language that can be modeled, predicted, and ultimately explained. Traditional statistical approaches fail to capture the rich dependencies and do not scale to high-dimensional datasets characterized by thousands of nodes, large sample sizes, and long sequence lengths. Specifically, the high cardinality of categorical event spaces in industrial logs poses a significant challenge, necessitating new machine learning architectures tailored to such event-driven systems. This thesis addresses automated fault diagnostics by unifying event sequence modeling, causal discovery, and large language models (LLMs) into a coherent framework for high-dimensional event streams. It is structured in three parts, reflecting a progressive transition from prediction to causal understanding and finally to reasoning for vehicle diagnostics. Consequently, we introduce several Transformer-based architectures for predictive maintenance, scalable sample- and population-level causal discovery frameworks and a multi-agent system that automates the synthesis of Boolean EP rules.

</details>


### [291] [FactorEngine: A Program-level Knowledge-Infused Factor Mining Framework for Quantitative Investment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16365)
*Qinhong Lin,Ruitao Feng,Yinglun Feng,Zhenxin Huang,Yukun Chen,Zhongliang Yang,Linna Zhou,Binjie Fei,Jiaqi Liu,Yu Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A program-level factor discovery framework, FactorEngine (FE), uses Turing-complete factor programs to extract executable, auditable signals from noisy market data; achieves improved predictive stability and portfolio performance with LLM-guided search, bootstrapping from financial reports, and an experience knowledge base.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limited expressiveness of symbolic approaches, interpretability and overfitting concerns with neural forecasters, and the need for scalable, executable factors in non-stationary markets.

Method: Factor discovery is cast as Turing-complete code. Three separations: (i) logic revision vs. parameter optimization, (ii) LLM-guided directional search vs. Bayesian hyperparameter search, (iii) LLM usage vs. local computation. A knowledge-infused bootstrapping module transforms unstructured financial reports into executable factor programs via a closed-loop multi-agent extraction-verification-code-generation pipeline. An experience knowledge base supports trajectory-aware refinement.

Result: Backtests on real-world OHLCV data show FE yields factors with stronger predictive stability and portfolio impact; higher IC/ICIR and Rank IC/ICIR; improved AR/Sharpe; achieving state-of-the-art predictive and portfolio performance relative to baselines.

Conclusion: FE offers a scalable, interpretable, auditable factor discovery framework with strong predictive and portfolio performance, leveraging LLMs, bootstrapping, and experiential learning to enhance factor quality.

Abstract: We study alpha factor mining, the automated discovery of predictive signals from noisy, non-stationary market data-under a practical requirement that mined factors be directly executable and auditable, and that the discovery process remain computationally tractable at scale. Existing symbolic approaches are limited by bounded expressiveness, while neural forecasters often trade interpretability for performance and remain vulnerable to regime shifts and overfitting. We introduce FactorEngine (FE), a program-level factor discovery framework that casts factors as Turing-complete code and improves both effectiveness and efficiency via three separations: (i) logic revision vs. parameter optimization, (ii) LLM-guided directional search vs. Bayesian hyperparameter search, and (iii) LLM usage vs. local computation. FE further incorporates a knowledge-infused bootstrapping module that transforms unstructured financial reports into executable factor programs through a closed-loop multi-agent extraction-verification-code-generation pipeline, and an experience knowledge base that supports trajectory-aware refinement (including learning from failures). Across extensive backtests on real-world OHLCV data, FE produces factors with substantially stronger predictive stability and portfolio impact-for example, higher IC/ICIR (and Rank IC/ICIR) and improved AR/Sharpe, than baseline methods, achieving state-of-the-art predictive and portfolio performance.

</details>


### [292] [Via Negativa for AI Alignment: Why Negative Constraints Are Structurally Superior to Positive Preferences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16417)
*Quan Cheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A theoretical account for why negative feedback signals can outperform positive-preference RLHF: positive preferences encode continuous, context-dependent human values (leading to sycophancy), while negative constraints encode discrete, verifiable prohibitions that converge to stable boundaries; this asymmetry explains empirical success of negative-signal methods and sycophancy failures, and motivates shifting alignment research toward learning what humans reject, with testable, falsification-based predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite strong empirical results for negative-only feedback, there is no unified theory explaining why negative signals work so well. The paper argues that positive preferences and negative constraints are structurally asymmetric, with different epistemic properties, and that this asymmetry can account for observed phenomena and guide future work.

Method: A theoretical/conceptual analysis grounded in the asymmetry between positive preferences and negative constraints, drawing on Popperian falsification logic and the epistemology of negative knowledge. The authors develop a cohesive framework and derive testable predictions, rather than presenting new empirical experiments.

Result: Provision of a unifying explanatory framework that accounts for (i) the sycophancy/positive-signal issue in preference-based RLHF and (ii) the effectiveness of negative-signal approaches (e.g., negative-only feedback, distributional dispreference optimization, constitutional AI). The framework also suggests a direction for alignment research by focusing on rejection signals and yields concrete, testable predictions.

Conclusion: Shift alignment research from learning what humans prefer to learning what humans reject; adopt a falsification-oriented, negative-knowledge perspective; and pursue testable predictions to validate the theory.

Abstract: Recent empirical results have demonstrated that training large language models (LLMs) with negative-only feedback can match or exceed standard reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). Negative Sample Reinforcement achieves parity with PPO on mathematical reasoning; Distributional Dispreference Optimization trains effectively using only dispreferred samples; and Constitutional AI outperforms pure RLHF on harmlessness benchmarks. Yet no unified theoretical account explains why negative signals are so effective. This paper proposes such an account: positive preferences and negative constraints are structurally asymmetric. Positive preferences ("which is better") encode continuously coupled, context-dependent human values that cannot be exhaustively specified -- leading models to learn surface correlates such as agreement with the user (sycophancy). Negative constraints ("what is wrong") encode discrete, finite, independently verifiable prohibitions that can converge to a stable boundary. This asymmetry -- rooted in Popper's falsification logic and the epistemology of negative knowledge -- explains both the sycophancy failure of preference-based RLHF and the surprising effectiveness of negative-signal methods. We argue that alignment research should shift its center of gravity from "learning what humans prefer" to "learning what humans reject," and offer testable predictions for this framework.

</details>


### [293] [From Natural Language to Executable Option Strategies via Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16434)
*Haochen Luo,Zhengzhao Lai,Junjie Xu,Yifan Li,Tang Pok Hin,Yuan Zhang,Chen Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Option Query Language (OQL) is a domain-specific intermediate representation for translating natural-language trading intents into executable option strategies. It uses an LLM as a semantic parser to produce OQL queries, which are then deterministically validated and executed by an engine. A new dataset is introduced, and the neuro-symbolic pipeline improves execution accuracy and logical consistency over direct baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Option design involves vast, multi-dimensional option chain data and stringent constraints that are difficult for free-form generation. A reliable semantic-parsing layer is needed to convert intents into valid, executable strategies.

Method: Introduce OQL with high-level primitives and grammatical rules to abstract option markets. LLMs act as semantic parsers to produce OQL queries. An engine deterministically validates and executes OQL to instantiate strategies. A new dataset is provided. A neuro-symbolic pipeline is demonstrated to improve execution accuracy and logical consistency over baselines.

Result: Neuro-symbolic pipeline yields better execution accuracy and logical consistency than direct baselines, on the introduced dataset (specific metrics not provided in abstract).

Conclusion: OQL enables reliable, scalable translation of natural-language trading intents into executable option strategies by combining semantic parsing with deterministic execution, supported by a new dataset and neuro-symbolic approach.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at general code generation, yet translating natural-language trading intents into correct option strategies remains challenging. Real-world option design requires reasoning over massive, multi-dimensional option chain data with strict constraints, which often overwhelms direct generation methods. We introduce the Option Query Language (OQL), a domain-specific intermediate representation that abstracts option markets into high-level primitives under grammatical rules, enabling LLMs to function as reliable semantic parsers rather than free-form programmers. OQL queries are then validated and executed deterministically by an engine to instantiate executable strategies. We also present a new dataset for this task and demonstrate that our neuro-symbolic pipeline significantly improves execution accuracy and logical consistency over direct baselines.

</details>


### [294] [Visual Distraction Undermines Moral Reasoning in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16445)
*Xinyi Yang,Chenheng Xu,Weijun Hong,Ce Mo,Qian Wang,Fang Fang,Yixin Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multimodal benchmark shows visual inputs shift moral judgments in state-of-the-art vision-language models, bypassing text-based safety safeguards; Moral Dilemma Simulation enables controlled analysis of how visual and contextual factors shape decisions, underscoring the need for multimodal safety alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Moral reasoning is essential for safe AI; current benchmarks rely on text and may not generalize to multimodal systems. Visual cues can trigger intuitive responses that are not captured by text-based safety, revealing fragilities in current alignment.

Method: Introduce Moral Dilemma Simulation (MDS), a multimodal benchmark grounded in Moral Foundation Theory. Orthogonally manipulate visual and contextual variables to mechanistically analyze how vision vs text influences moral decision-making in state-of-the-art Vision-Language Models; assess the efficacy of text-only safety filters in multimodal settings.

Result: Vision modality activates intuition-like pathways that override deliberative, safety-oriented reasoning observed in text-only contexts. Text-tuned safety filters fail to constrain visual processing, revealing fragility in current safety approaches and demonstrating the need for multimodal safety alignment.

Conclusion: There is an urgent need to extend safety alignment to multimodal inputs. MDS provides a mechanistic framework to study how visual perception interacts with moral judgment, enabling systematic evaluation and improvement of multimodal safety in AI.

Abstract: Moral reasoning is fundamental to safe Artificial Intelligence (AI), yet ensuring its consistency across modalities becomes critical as AI systems evolve from text-based assistants to embodied agents. Current safety techniques demonstrate success in textual contexts, but concerns remain about generalization to visual inputs. Existing moral evaluation benchmarks rely on textonly formats and lack systematic control over variables that influence moral decision-making. Here we show that visual inputs fundamentally alter moral decision-making in state-of-the-art (SOTA) Vision-Language Models (VLMs), bypassing text-based safety mechanisms. We introduce Moral Dilemma Simulation (MDS), a multimodal benchmark grounded in Moral Foundation Theory (MFT) that enables mechanistic analysis through orthogonal manipulation of visual and contextual variables. The evaluation reveals that the vision modality activates intuition-like pathways that override the more deliberate and safer reasoning patterns observed in text-only contexts. These findings expose critical fragilities where language-tuned safety filters fail to constrain visual processing, demonstrating the urgent need for multimodal safety alignment.

</details>


### [295] [TRUST-SQL: Tool-Integrated Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning for Text-to-SQL over Unknown Schemas](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16448)
*Ai Jian,Xiaoyun Zhang,Wanrou Du,Jingqing Ruan,Jiangbo Pei,Weipeng Zhang,Ke Zeng,Xunliang Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TRUST-SQL enables unknown-schema text-to-SQL via a four-phase agent protocol and Dual-Track GRPO, achieving strong gains without pre-loaded metadata.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In enterprise environments, the Full Schema assumption is impractical due to hundreds of tables and noisy metadata. The task requires identifying and verifying only a relevant subset of the schema, i.e., Unknown Schema; a data-grounded, interactive approach is needed.

Method: Formulate the task as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process. Introduce a structured four-phase protocol to ground reasoning in verified metadata. Propose Dual-Track GRPO with token-level masked advantages to separate exploration rewards from execution outcomes, improving credit assignment. Operates without pre-loaded metadata and evaluates on multiple benchmarks with 4B and 8B model variants.

Result: 9.9% relative improvement over standard GRPO. Across five benchmarks, TRUST-SQL yields average absolute gains of 30.6% (4B) and 16.6% (8B) over base models. It matches or surpasses strong schema-prefilled baselines despite no pre-loaded metadata.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates strong performance in the Unknown Schema setting, scales with model size, and reduces reliance on upfront schema information by grounding reasoning on verified metadata using a structured decision process and a dual-track credit assignment mechanism.

Abstract: Text-to-SQL parsing has achieved remarkable progress under the Full Schema Assumption. However, this premise fails in real-world enterprise environments where databases contain hundreds of tables with massive noisy metadata. Rather than injecting the full schema upfront, an agent must actively identify and verify only the relevant subset, giving rise to the Unknown Schema scenario we study in this work. To address this, we propose TRUST-SQL (Truthful Reasoning with Unknown Schema via Tools). We formulate the task as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process where our autonomous agent employs a structured four-phase protocol to ground reasoning in verified metadata. Crucially, this protocol provides a structural boundary for our novel Dual-Track GRPO strategy. By applying token-level masked advantages, this strategy isolates exploration rewards from execution outcomes to resolve credit assignment, yielding a 9.9% relative improvement over standard GRPO. Extensive experiments across five benchmarks demonstrate that TRUST-SQL achieves an average absolute improvement of 30.6% and 16.6% for the 4B and 8B variants respectively over their base models. Remarkably, despite operating entirely without pre-loaded metadata, our framework consistently matches or surpasses strong baselines that rely on schema prefilling.

</details>


### [296] [RetailBench: Evaluating Long-Horizon Autonomous Decision-Making and Strategy Stability of LLM Agents in Realistic Retail Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16453)
*Linghua Zhang,Jun Wang,Jingtong Wu,Zhisong Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes RetailBench, a high-fidelity benchmark for evaluating long-horizon autonomous decision-making in dynamic, stochastic retail settings, and the Evolving Strategy & Execution framework that decouples high-level strategy from low-level execution. Empirical evaluation across eight LLMs shows improved stability and efficiency with the framework, but performance deteriorates as task complexity grows, highlighting fundamental limits of current LLMs for long-horizon multi-factor decision-making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon decision-making in realistic, dynamic environments is underexplored and challenging for LLM-based agents due to non-stationary conditions and error accumulation. A framework that separates strategic reasoning from action execution could enable adaptive, interpretable strategy evolution over time.

Method: Introduce RetailBench as a high-fidelity benchmark simulating realistic commercial scenarios with stochastic demand and evolving external conditions. Propose the Evolving Strategy & Execution framework that decouples high-level strategic reasoning from low-level action execution, enabling strategy updates at slower timescales. Evaluate eight state-of-the-art LLMs across progressively challenging environments, comparing operational stability and efficiency against baselines.

Result: The framework improves operational stability and efficiency relative to baselines, demonstrating the value of temporal separation for long-horizon tasks. However, performance degrades substantially with increasing task complexity, revealing fundamental limitations in current LLMs for long-horizon, multi-factor decision-making.

Conclusion: Separating strategy evolution from execution helps adaptive, interpretable long-horizon decision-making in non-stationary environments, but substantial gaps remain as task demands grow. The work highlights the need for further research into scalable long-horizon reasoning and robust control under evolving conditions.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents have achieved notable success on short-horizon and highly structured tasks. However, their ability to maintain coherent decision-making over long horizons in realistic and dynamic environments remains an open challenge.
  We introduce RetailBench, a high-fidelity benchmark designed to evaluate long-horizon autonomous decision-making in realistic commercial scenarios, where agents must operate under stochastic demand and evolving external conditions.
  We further propose the Evolving Strategy & Execution framework, which separates high-level strategic reasoning from low-level action execution. This design enables adaptive and interpretable strategy evolution over time. It is particularly important for long-horizon tasks, where non-stationary environments and error accumulation require strategies to be revised at a different temporal scale than action execution.
  Experiments on eight state-of-the-art LLMs across progressively challenging environments show that our framework improves operational stability and efficiency compared to other baselines. However, performance degrades substantially as task complexity increases, revealing fundamental limitations in current LLMs for long-horizon, multi-factor decision-making.

</details>


### [297] [Follow the Clues, Frame the Truth: Hybrid-evidential Deductive Reasoning in Open-Vocabulary Multimodal Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16463)
*Yu Liu,Lei Zhang,Haoxun Li,Hanlei Shi,Yuxuan Ding,Leyuan Qu,Taihao Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HyDRA introduces a Hybrid-evidential Deductive Reasoning Architecture for open-vocabulary multimodal emotion recognition (OV-MER) using a Propose-Verify-Decide protocol and hierarchical reinforcement learning to reconcile diverse latent cues, outperforming baselines and providing interpretable evidence traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OV-MER is challenged by ambiguous multimodal cues and over-reliance on dominant data priors in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). There is a need to reconstruct nuanced emotional states by synthesizing multiple evidence-grounded rationales from diverse perspectives.

Method: HyDRA implements a Propose-Verify-Decide (PVD) inference loop. It uses reinforcement learning with hierarchical reward shaping to align intermediate reasoning trajectories with final task performance, and emphasizes abductive, evidence-grounded reasoning across multiple latent viewpoints.

Result: Systematic evaluations show HyDRA consistently outperforms strong baselines, particularly in ambiguous or conflicting scenarios, while yielding interpretable diagnostic traces of its reasoning process.

Conclusion: HyDRA demonstrates that structured abductive reasoning, guided by hierarchical RL and a PVD protocol, improves open-vocabulary MER by reconciling multimodal cues and providing transparent evidence traces, with potential for more robust affective inference under uncertainty.

Abstract: Open-Vocabulary Multimodal Emotion Recognition (OV-MER) is inherently challenging due to the ambiguity of equivocal multimodal cues, which often stem from distinct unobserved situational dynamics. While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) offer extensive semantic coverage, their performance is often bottlenecked by premature commitment to dominant data priors, resulting in suboptimal heuristics that overlook crucial, complementary affective cues across modalities. We argue that effective affective reasoning requires more than surface-level association; it necessitates reconstructing nuanced emotional states by synthesizing multiple evidence-grounded rationales that reconcile these observations from diverse latent perspectives. We introduce HyDRA, a Hybrid-evidential Deductive Reasoning Architecture that formalizes inference as a Propose-Verify-Decide protocol. To internalize this abductive process, we employ reinforcement learning with hierarchical reward shaping, aligning the reasoning trajectories with final task performance to ensure they best reconcile the observed multimodal cues. Systematic evaluations validate our design choices, with HyDRA consistently outperforming strong baselines--especially in ambiguous or conflicting scenarios--while providing interpretable, diagnostic evidence traces.

</details>


### [298] [Breaking the Chain: A Causal Analysis of LLM Faithfulness to Intermediate Structures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16475)
*Oleg Somov,Mikhail Chaichuk,Mikhail Seleznyov,Alexander Panchenko,Elena Tutubalina*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Schema-guided intermediate structures act as influential context, not stable causal mediators; causal evaluation reveals fragility to edits; external derivation reduces fragility; prompts prioritizing structure over input do not close the gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether intermediate artifacts (rubrics, checklists, verification queries) causally determine final decisions in schema-guided reasoning and under what conditions this causal link holds.

Method: Introduce a causal evaluation protocol: select tasks where a deterministic function maps an intermediate structure to a decision; perform controlled edits to the structure and check if outputs change accordingly; evaluate across eight models and three benchmarks; compare to an external tool-based derivation of the final decision and to prompts that prioritize the intermediate structure.

Result: Models show self-consistency with their own intermediate structures but fail to update predictions after interventions in up to 60% of cases; external tool-based derivation largely removes this fragility; however, prompts that prioritize the intermediate structure over the original input do not substantially close the gap.

Conclusion: Intermediate structures in schema-guided pipelines function as influential context rather than stable causal mediators; relying on them alone does not guarantee causal control over final decisions; external derivation can help but the mapping from structure to outcome remains fragile.

Abstract: Schema-guided reasoning pipelines ask LLMs to produce explicit intermediate structures -- rubrics, checklists, verification queries -- before committing to a final decision. But do these structures causally determine the output, or merely accompany it? We introduce a causal evaluation protocol that makes this directly measurable: by selecting tasks where a deterministic function maps intermediate structures to decisions, every controlled edit implies a unique correct output. Across eight models and three benchmarks, models appear self-consistent with their own intermediate structures but fail to update predictions after intervention in up to 60% of cases -- revealing that apparent faithfulness is fragile once the intermediate structure changes. When derivation of the final decision from the structure is delegated to an external tool, this fragility largely disappears; however, prompts which ask to prioritize the intermediate structure over the original input do not materially close the gap. Overall, intermediate structures in schema-guided pipelines function as influential context rather than stable causal mediators.

</details>


### [299] [ExpressMind: A Multimodal Pretrained Large Language Model for Expressway Operation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16495)
*Zihe Wang,Yihuan Wang,Haiyang Yu. Zhiyong Cui,Xiaojian Liao,Chengcheng Wang,Yonglin Tian,Yongxin Tong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ExpressMind—a multimodal LLM-based cognitive system for expressways, integrating a full-stack expressway dataset, Graph-Augmented RAG, RL-CoT reasoning, and a cross-modal encoder, achieving state-of-the-art performance on a dedicated expressway benchmark for event detection, safety response generation, and complex traffic analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rule-based and isolated models limit cross-system knowledge fusion in expressways; general LLMs struggle with expressway regulations and causal reasoning in unconventional scenarios; a multimodal, knowledge-grounded, and RL-aligned framework is needed for cognitive expressway operations.

Method: (1) Construct the first full-stack expressway dataset including traffic knowledge texts, emergency reasoning chains, and annotated video events. (2) Propose a dual-layer pre-training paradigm with self-supervised and unsupervised learning. (3) Introduce a Graph-Augmented Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework to dynamically index expressway knowledge. (4) Develop an RL-aligned Chain-of-Thought (RL-CoT) mechanism to align reasoning with expert heuristics for incident handling. (5) Integrate a cross-modal encoder to align dynamic visual and textual features for video/image understanding. (6) Evaluate on a newly released multimodal expressway benchmark. 

Result: ExpressMind comprehensively outperforms existing baselines in event detection, safety response generation, and complex traffic analysis on the new expressway benchmark. The authors release code and data, enabling replication and further research.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that a multimodal, knowledge-grounded, RL-aligned LLM framework can advance cognitive expressway operations by integrating perception, reasoning, and knowledge indexing, potentially enabling more robust, scalable intelligent expressway management.

Abstract: The current expressway operation relies on rule-based and isolated models, which limits the ability to jointly analyze knowledge across different systems. Meanwhile, Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in intelligent transportation, advancing traffic models from algorithmic to cognitive intelligence. However, general LLMs are unable to effectively understand the regulations and causal relationships of events in unconventional scenarios in the expressway field. Therefore, this paper constructs a pre-trained multimodal large language model (MLLM) for expressways, ExpressMind, which serves as the cognitive core for intelligent expressway operations. This paper constructs the industry's first full-stack expressway dataset, encompassing traffic knowledge texts, emergency reasoning chains, and annotated video events to overcome data scarcity. This paper proposes a dual-layer LLM pre-training paradigm based on self-supervised training and unsupervised learning. Additionally, this study introduces a Graph-Augmented RAG framework to dynamically index the expressway knowledge base. To enhance reasoning for expressway incident response strategies, we develop a RL-aligned Chain-of-Thought (RL-CoT) mechanism that enforces consistency between model reasoning and expert problem-solving heuristics for incident handling. Finally, ExpressMind integrates a cross-modal encoder to align the dynamic feature sequences under the visual and textual channels, enabling it to understand traffic scenes in both video and image modalities. Extensive experiments on our newly released multi-modal expressway benchmark demonstrate that ExpressMind comprehensively outperforms existing baselines in event detection, safety response generation, and complex traffic analysis. The code and data are available at: https://wanderhee.github.io/ExpressMind/.

</details>


### [300] [Exploring different approaches to customize language models for domain-specific text-to-code generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16526)
*Luís Freire,Fernanda A. Andaló,Nicki Skafte Detlefsen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LoRA fine-tuning on small LMs yields higher accuracy and domain alignment for domain-specific code generation; prompting methods are cost-effective but less impactful on benchmark accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the cost and accessibility barriers of large proprietary models by evaluating how smaller LMs can be adapted for domain-specific code generation using synthetic data across Python domains.

Method: Create synthetic datasets of programming exercises in three Python domains (general Python, Scikit-learn workflows, OpenCV tasks); compare three customization strategies—few-shot prompting, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) fine-tuning; evaluate with benchmark-based metrics and similarity-based alignment metrics.

Result: LoRA-based fine-tuning consistently achieves higher accuracy and stronger domain alignment across most tasks; prompting-based methods (few-shot, RAG) improve domain relevance at lower cost but have limited impact on benchmark accuracy.

Conclusion: For specialized programming tasks, parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA) offers the best performance and domain alignment, while prompting strategies provide cost-effective domain relevance with trade-offs in benchmark performance; practitioners should balance flexibility, compute cost, and accuracy when adapting small LMs to domain-specific coding tasks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in generating executable code from natural language descriptions. However, general-purpose models often struggle in specialized programming contexts where domain-specific libraries, APIs, or conventions must be used. Customizing smaller open-source models offers a cost-effective alternative to relying on large proprietary systems. In this work, we investigate how smaller language models can be adapted for domain-specific code generation using synthetic datasets. We construct datasets of programming exercises across three domains within the Python ecosystem: general Python programming, Scikit-learn machine learning workflows, and OpenCV-based computer vision tasks. Using these datasets, we evaluate three customization strategies: few-shot prompting, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and parameter-efficient fine-tuning using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). Performance is evaluated using both benchmark-based metrics and similarity-based metrics that measure alignment with domain-specific code. Our results show that prompting-based approaches such as few-shot learning and RAG can improve domain relevance in a cost-effective manner, although their impact on benchmark accuracy is limited. In contrast, LoRA-based fine-tuning consistently achieves higher accuracy and stronger domain alignment across most tasks. These findings highlight practical trade-offs between flexibility, computational cost, and performance when adapting smaller language models for specialized programming tasks.

</details>


### [301] [BenchPreS: A Benchmark for Context-Aware Personalized Preference Selectivity of Persistent-Memory LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16557)
*Sangyeon Yoon,Sunkyoung Kim,Hyesoo Hong,Wonje Jeung,Yongil Kim,Wooseok Seo,Heuiyeen Yeen,Albert No*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BenchPreS reveals that memory-based user preferences are not applied in a consistently context-sensitive way. Stronger adherence often increases over-generalization, and neither reasoning nor prompt defenses fix this, suggesting preferences are treated as global rules rather than context-dependent norms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how persistent memory of user preferences interacts with social and institutional norms, and whether LLMs apply such preferences appropriately across varying communication contexts.

Method: Introduce BenchPreS, a benchmark with two complementary metrics: Misapplication Rate (MR) and Appropriate Application Rate (AAR). Evaluate frontier LLMs on their ability to apply or suppress memory-based preferences across contexts, and analyze relationships with reasoning capability and defenses.

Result: Frontier LLMs struggle to apply preferences in a context-sensitive manner. Models with stronger preference adherence exhibit higher rates of over-application (misuse in inappropriate contexts). Neither enhanced reasoning nor prompt-based defenses fully resolve the issue. This suggests preferences are treated as globally enforceable rules rather than context-dependent normative signals.

Conclusion: Current LLMs lack context-sensitive normative interpretation of user preferences. To achieve safe, personalized interactions, models must learn to interpret preferences as contingent on social norms and context, rather than as universal mandates; future work should focus on normative alignment and context-aware memory policy design.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly store user preferences in persistent memory to support personalization across interactions. However, in third-party communication settings governed by social and institutional norms, some user preferences may be inappropriate to apply. We introduce BenchPreS, which evaluates whether memory-based user preferences are appropriately applied or suppressed across communication contexts. Using two complementary metrics, Misapplication Rate (MR) and Appropriate Application Rate (AAR), we find even frontier LLMs struggle to apply preferences in a context-sensitive manner. Models with stronger preference adherence exhibit higher rates of over-application, and neither reasoning capability nor prompt-based defenses fully resolve this issue. These results suggest current LLMs treat personalized preferences as globally enforceable rules rather than as context-dependent normative signals.

</details>


### [302] [V-DyKnow: A Dynamic Benchmark for Time-Sensitive Knowledge in Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16581)
*Seyed Mahed Mousavi,Christian Moiola,Massimo Rizzoli,Simone Alghisi,Giuseppe Riccardi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: V-DyKnow benchmarks time-sensitive factual knowledge in Vision-Language Models, revealing reliability gaps across modalities and limitations in updating knowledge; resources are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between static training data and dynamic real-world facts in VLMs by providing a benchmark to evaluate time-sensitive knowledge across modalities.

Method: Construct and evaluate the V-DyKnow benchmark on closed- and open-source VLMs; analyze reliability across modalities and input perturbations; assess knowledge editing and multi-modal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) methods for updates; perform data and mechanistic analyses to identify sources of outdated predictions.

Result: VLMs frequently produce outdated facts; factual reliability degrades from textual to visual inputs even when entities are recognized; existing alignment/update methods do not consistently propagate updates across modalities.

Conclusion: Current VLMs exhibit fundamental limitations in acquiring and updating time-sensitive knowledge across modalities; the benchmark and resources (code, data) enable future improvements in cross-modal temporal grounding and knowledge updating.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are trained on data snapshots of documents, including images and texts. Their training data and evaluation benchmarks are typically static, implicitly treating factual knowledge as time-invariant. However, real-world facts are intrinsically time-sensitive and subject to erratic and periodic changes, causing model predictions to become outdated. We present V-DyKnow, a Visual Dynamic Knowledge benchmark for evaluating time-sensitive factual knowledge in VLMs. Using V-DyKnow, we benchmark closed- and open-source VLMs and analyze a) the reliability (correctness and consistency) of model responses across modalities and input perturbations; b) the efficacy of knowledge editing and multi-modal RAG methods for knowledge updates across modalities; and c) the sources of outdated predictions, through data and mechanistic analysis. Our results show that VLMs frequently output outdated facts, reflecting outdated snapshots used in the (pre-)training phase. Factual reliability degrades from textual to visual stimuli, even when entities are correctly recognized. Besides, existing alignment approaches fail to consistently update the models' knowledge across modalities. Together, these findings highlight fundamental limitations in how current VLMs acquire and update time-sensitive knowledge across modalities. We release the benchmark, code, and evaluation data.

</details>


### [303] [Runtime Governance for AI Agents: Policies on Paths](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16586)
*Maurits Kaptein,Vassilis-Javed Khan,Andriy Podstavnychy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a formal, runtime-governance framework for AI agents that treats the execution path as the central governance object; defines deterministic policies mapping identity, partial path, next action, and state to a violation probability; shows prompts and static access control are special cases; provides a formal framework, policy examples, a reference implementation, and discusses open problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-deterministic, path-dependent behavior of LLM-based AI agents makes design-time governance insufficient. Balancing task success with legal, data, reputation, and other costs requires runtime policies that account for the execution path.

Method: Introduce a formal framework where compliance policies are deterministic functions of agent identity, current partial execution path, proposed next action, and organizational state; demonstrate how prompt-level instructions and static access control fit as special cases; argue runtime evaluation as the general solution; present concrete policy instances inspired by the AI Act, outline a reference implementation, and discuss open problems.

Result: Formal governance framework for AI agents; concrete policy examples; a reference implementation; discussion of calibration of risk and enforcement limits; framing of runtime evaluation as essential for path-dependent policies.

Conclusion: Runtime evaluation of execution paths is the general case for governing AI agents; prompts and static access controls are limited special cases; the framework enables path-dependent compliance and highlights open challenges such as risk calibration and limits of enforcement.

Abstract: AI agents -- systems that plan, reason, and act using large language models -- produce non-deterministic, path-dependent behavior that cannot be fully governed at design time, where with governed we mean striking the right balance between as high as possible successful task completion rate and the legal, data-breach, reputational and other costs associated with running agents. We argue that the execution path is the central object for effective runtime governance and formalize compliance policies as deterministic functions mapping agent identity, partial path, proposed next action, and organizational state to a policy violation probability. We show that prompt-level instructions (and "system prompts"), and static access control are special cases of this framework: the former shape the distribution over paths without actually evaluating them; the latter evaluates deterministic policies that ignore the path (i.e., these can only account for a specific subset of all possible paths). In our view, runtime evaluation is the general case, and it is necessary for any path-dependent policy. We develop the formal framework for analyzing AI agent governance, present concrete policy examples (inspired by the AI act), discuss a reference implementation, and identify open problems including risk calibration and the limits of enforced compliance.

</details>


### [304] [When AI Navigates the Fog of War](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16642)
*Ming Li,Xirui Li,Tianyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Temporally grounded evaluation shows LLMs can exhibit strategic reasoning about unfolding geopolitical crises, with performance varying by domain and narratives evolving over time; data leakage is mitigated via time-sliced prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate whether frontier LLMs can reason about unfolding conflicts without hindsight bias and training-data leakage, providing an archival, time-resolved view of model reasoning.

Method: Create 11 temporal nodes, 42 node-specific verifiable questions, and 5 general exploratory questions; constrain reasoning to information publicly available at each moment; compare model outputs for realism, domain coverage, and narrative evolution.

Result: Models demonstrate strategic realism beyond surface talk; greater reliability in economically/logistically structured domains than in politically ambiguous multi-actor contexts; narratives evolve from containment to regional entrenchment and attritional de-escalation.

Conclusion: Temporally grounded design reduces leakage concerns and enables prospective analysis of model reasoning in ongoing crises; findings outline strengths, limitations, and future research directions.

Abstract: Can AI reason about a war before its trajectory becomes historically obvious? Analyzing this capability is difficult because retrospective geopolitical prediction is heavily confounded by training-data leakage. We address this challenge through a temporally grounded case study of the early stages of the 2026 Middle East conflict, which unfolded after the training cutoff of current frontier models. We construct 11 critical temporal nodes, 42 node-specific verifiable questions, and 5 general exploratory questions, requiring models to reason only from information that would have been publicly available at each moment. This design substantially mitigates training-data leakage concerns, creating a setting well-suited for studying how models analyze an unfolding crisis under the fog of war, and provides, to our knowledge, the first temporally grounded analysis of LLM reasoning in an ongoing geopolitical conflict. Our analysis reveals three main findings. First, current state-of-the-art large language models often display a striking degree of strategic realism, reasoning beyond surface rhetoric toward deeper structural incentives. Second, this capability is uneven across domains: models are more reliable in economically and logistically structured settings than in politically ambiguous multi-actor environments. Finally, model narratives evolve over time, shifting from early expectations of rapid containment toward more systemic accounts of regional entrenchment and attritional de-escalation. Since the conflict remains ongoing at the time of writing, this work can serve as an archival snapshot of model reasoning during an unfolding geopolitical crisis, enabling future studies without the hindsight bias of retrospective analysis.

</details>


### [305] [Domain-Independent Dynamic Programming with Constraint Propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16648)
*Imko Marijnissen,J. Christopher Beck,Emir Demirović,Ryo Kuroiwa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Constraint propagation is embedded into Domain-Independent Dynamic Programming (DIDP) to prune states/transitions, yielding fewer expansions and more solved instances on several combinatorial problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between DP and CP paradigms by enabling constraint propagation to guide DP search, reducing search effort in combinatorial optimization.

Method: Integrate a general-purpose CP solver into the Domain-Independent Dynamic Programming framework to perform constraint propagation at DP nodes; evaluate with heuristic search on three problems.

Result: Constraint propagation significantly reduces state expansions; the DP solver solves more instances for Single Machine Scheduling with Time Windows and RCPSP; improvements also observed for tightly constrained TSPTW; runtime benefits outweigh propagation overhead on constrained instances; indicates potential for stronger gains with optimized propagation.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the value of constraint propagation within DP and provides a model-based pathway for DP–CP integration; a foundational step with room for reducing overhead and extending to more problems.

Abstract: There are two prevalent model-based paradigms for combinatorial problems: 1) state-based representations, such as heuristic search, dynamic programming (DP), and decision diagrams, and 2) constraint and domain-based representations, such as constraint programming (CP), (mixed-)integer programming, and Boolean satisfiability. In this paper, we bridge the gap between the DP and CP paradigms by integrating constraint propagation into DP, enabling a DP solver to prune states and transitions using constraint propagation. To this end, we implement constraint propagation using a general-purpose CP solver in the Domain-Independent Dynamic Programming framework and evaluate using heuristic search on three combinatorial optimisation problems: Single Machine Scheduling with Time Windows, the Resource Constrained Project Scheduling Problem (RCPSP), and the Travelling Salesperson Problem with Time Windows (TSPTW). Our evaluation shows that constraint propagation significantly reduces the number of state expansions, causing our approach to solve more instances than a DP solver for Single Machine Scheduling and RCPSP, and showing similar improvements for tightly constrained TSPTW instances. The runtime performance indicates that the benefits of propagation outweigh the overhead for constrained instances, but that further work into reducing propagation overhead could improve performance further. Our work is a key step in understanding the value of constraint propagation in DP solvers, providing a model-based approach to integrating DP and CP.

</details>


### [306] [What if Pinocchio Were a Reinforcement Learning Agent: A Normative End-to-End Pipeline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16651)
*Benoît Alcaraz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a hybrid RL pipeline (Pino) combining reinforcement learning with argumentation-based normative advisors to achieve norm-compliant, context-aware agents, using a novel algorithm to extract argumentative structure, and studies norm avoidance with mitigation strategies; evaluates each component empirically and discusses limitations and future work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The growing deployment of AI in daily life requires systems that can autonomously adhere to social rules and norms, ensuring safe and acceptable behavior while remaining adaptable to context.

Method: Introduce Pino, a hybrid model where RL agents are supervised by normative advisors based on argumentation (building on AJAR, Jiminy, NGRL). Develop a novel algorithm to automatically extract the arguments and relationships underlying the advisors' decisions. Analyze norm avoidance and propose mitigation strategies. Empirically evaluate each component of the pipeline.

Result: Demonstrates the feasibility of a norm-compliant, context-aware pipeline; the argument-extraction algorithm enables supervision of RL by normative advisors; empirical results validate components and the mitigation approach to norm avoidance.

Conclusion: Extends the landscape of normative AI by integrating argumentation-based normative guidance with reinforcement learning, acknowledges current limitations (e.g., extraction accuracy, scalability, generalization) and outlines directions for future work (improved argument mining, richer normative models, broader empirical validation).

Abstract: In the past decade, artificial intelligence (AI) has developed quickly. With this rapid progression came the need for systems capable of complying with the rules and norms of our society so that they can be successfully and safely integrated into our daily lives. Inspired by the story of Pinocchio in ``Le avventure di Pinocchio - Storia di un burattino'', this thesis proposes a pipeline that addresses the problem of developing norm compliant and context-aware agents. Building on the AJAR, Jiminy, and NGRL architectures, the work introduces \pino, a hybrid model in which reinforcement learning agents are supervised by argumentation-based normative advisors. In order to make this pipeline operational, this thesis also presents a novel algorithm for automatically extracting the arguments and relationships that underlie the advisors' decisions. Finally, this thesis investigates the phenomenon of \textit{norm avoidance}, providing a definition and a mitigation strategy within the context of reinforcement learning agents. Each component of the pipeline is empirically evaluated. The thesis concludes with a discussion of related work, current limitations, and directions for future research.

</details>


### [307] [Machines acquire scientific taste from institutional traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16659)
*Ziqin Gong,Ning Li,Huaikang Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Fine-tuning language models on editorial decisions yields superior evaluative judgment ('taste') for research pitches, surpassing frontier models and human panels; calibration and cross-domain transfer demonstrated; offers scalable triage for increasing scientific output.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenge the idea that AI's progress is limited to verifiable reasoning; 'taste' drives scientific advancement and is embedded in institutional publication records, which can be extracted and automated.

Method: Train and fine-tune multiple language models on historical publication decision data; evaluate on a held-out benchmark of management pitches across four quality tiers; compare to panels of editors and editorial board members; assess calibration of confidence; test transfer to untrained pairwise comparisons and one-sentence summaries; evaluate cross-domain generalization to economics publication records.

Result: Fine-tuned models outperform frontier models and expert panels: frontier models average 31% accuracy; editors reach 42% by majority vote; best single fine-tuned model achieves 59% accuracy; models show calibrated confidence (100% accuracy on highest-confidence predictions); transfer to untrained tasks and one-sentence summaries; economics-domain model achieves ~70% accuracy.

Conclusion: Scientific taste is encoded in institutional records and can be extracted via fine-tuning; provides a scalable mechanism to triage growing scientific output across disciplines where quality is not easily verifiable; success depends on data quality and may entail biases that require careful handling.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence matches or exceeds human performance on tasks with verifiable answers, from protein folding to Olympiad mathematics. Yet the capacity that most governs scientific advance is not reasoning but taste: the ability to judge which untested ideas deserve pursuit, exercised daily by editors and funders but never successfully articulated, taught, or automated. Here we show that fine-tuning language models on journal publication decisions recovers evaluative judgment inaccessible to both frontier models and human expertise. Using a held-out benchmark of research pitches in management spanning four quality tiers, we find that eleven frontier models, spanning major proprietary and open architectures, barely exceed chance, averaging 31% accuracy. Panels of journal editors and editorial board members reach 42% by majority vote. Fine-tuned models trained on years of publication records each surpass every frontier model and expert panel, with the best single model achieving 59%. These models exhibit calibrated confidence, reaching 100% accuracy on their highest-confidence predictions, and transfer this evaluative signal to untrained pairwise comparisons and one-sentence summaries. The mechanism generalizes: models trained on economics publication records achieve 70% accuracy. Scientific taste was not missing from AI's reach; it was deposited in the institutional record, waiting to be extracted. These results provide a scalable mechanism to triage the expanding volume of scientific production across disciplines where quality resists formal verification.

</details>


### [308] [CritiSense: Critical Digital Literacy and Resilience Against Misinformation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16672)
*Firoj Alam,Fatema Ahmad,Ali Ezzat Shahroor,Mohamed Bayan Kmainasi,Elisa Sartori,Giovanni Da San Martino,Abul Hasnat,Raian Ali*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multilingual, modular prebunking mobile app (CritiSense) that trains users to spot manipulation tactics; evaluated via a usability study with positive results, and positioned as a testbed for measuring microlearning effects on misinformation resilience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Misinformation on social media undermines informed decision-making and public trust. Prebunking offers proactive defense by teaching recognition of manipulation tactics, necessitating scalable, multilingual tools and evaluation frameworks.

Method: Design and deployment of CritiSense: short interactive challenges with instant feedback; modular, topic-updatable architecture supporting nine languages; usability study with 93 participants measuring satisfaction and ease of use, plus qualitative feedback. Usage metrics over 3+ months (300+ active users). Availability on Apple App Store and Google Play; includes a demo video.

Result: High user satisfaction (83.9%) and perceived ease of use (90.1%); qualitative feedback indicates improved digital literacy and potential for misinformation resilience; a freely available platform with real-world reach (300+ active users).

Conclusion: CritiSense provides a scalable, multilingual prebunking platform and a testbed for evaluating the impact of microlearning on misinformation resilience, with rapid topic-update capability and broad accessibility.

Abstract: Misinformation on social media undermines informed decision-making and public trust. Prebunking offers a proactive complement by helping users recognize manipulation tactics before they encounter them in the wild. We present CritiSense, a mobile media-literacy app that builds these skills through short, interactive challenges with instant feedback. It is the first multilingual (supporting nine languages) and modular platform, designed for rapid updates across topics and domains. We report a usability study with 93 users: 83.9% expressed overall satisfaction and 90.1% rated the app as easy to use. Qualitative feedback indicates that CritiSense helps improve digital literacy skills. Overall, it provides a multilingual prebunking platform and a testbed for measuring the impact of microlearning on misinformation resilience. Over 3+ months, we have reached 300+ active users. It is freely available to all users on the Apple App Store (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/critisense/id6749675792) and Google Play Store (https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.critisense&hl=en). Demo Video: https://shorturl.at/CDcdc

</details>


### [309] [IQuest-Coder-V1 Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16733)
*Jian Yang,Wei Zhang,Shawn Guo,Zhengmao Ye,Lin Jing,Shark Liu,Yizhi Li,Jiajun Wu,Cening Liu,X. Ma,Yuyang Song,Siwei Wu,Yuwen Li,L. Liao,T. Zheng,Ziling Huang,Zelong Huang,Che Liu,Yan Xing,Renyuan Li,Qingsong Cai,Hanxu Yan,Siyue Wang,Shikai Li,Jason Klein Liu,An Huang,Yongsheng Kang,Jinxing Zhang,Chuan Hao,Haowen Wang,Weicheng Gu,Ran Tao,Mingjie Tang,Peihao Wu,Jianzhou Wang,Xianglong Liu,Weifeng Lv,Bryan Dai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: New code LLM family (7B/14B/40B/40B-Loop) using a code-flow multi-stage training paradigm (pretrain on code facts/repo/completion data; mid-train with reasoning/agentic trajectories at 32k and 128k contexts; post-train with thinking and instruction paths). Loop variant adds recurrence to balance capacity and deployment footprint. Claims state-of-the-art in agentic software engineering, competitive programming, and complex tool use; release includes full white-box checkpoint chain to advance research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the dynamic evolution of software logic and deployment constraints by designing a training paradigm that captures reasoning, planning, and tool-use in code-centric tasks; enable autonomous, agentic code intelligence with scalable context handling.

Method: Proposes a code-flow multi-stage training pipeline: (1) pre-training on code facts, repositories, and completion data; (2) mid-training with reasoning and agentic trajectories in 32k-context and repository-scale 128k-context to build deep logical foundations; (3) post-training bifurcated into a thinking path (reasoning-driven RL) and an instruct path (general assistance). A Loop variant introduces recurrence to optimize the trade-off between model capacity and deployment footprint.

Result: Claims state-of-the-art performance across key code intelligence dimensions—agentic software engineering, competitive programming, and complex tool use—along with a deployment-efficient Loop variant.

Conclusion: Release of IQuest-Coder-V1 series with full white-box checkpoints is expected to accelerate research in autonomous code intelligence and real-world agentic systems.

Abstract: In this report, we introduce the IQuest-Coder-V1 series-(7B/14B/40B/40B-Loop), a new family of code large language models (LLMs). Moving beyond static code representations, we propose the code-flow multi-stage training paradigm, which captures the dynamic evolution of software logic through different phases of the pipeline. Our models are developed through the evolutionary pipeline, starting with the initial pre-training consisting of code facts, repository, and completion data. Following that, we implement a specialized mid-training stage that integrates reasoning and agentic trajectories in 32k-context and repository-scale in 128k-context to forge deep logical foundations. The models are then finalized with post-training of specialized coding capabilities, which is bifurcated into two specialized paths: the thinking path (utilizing reasoning-driven RL) and the instruct path (optimized for general assistance). IQuest-Coder-V1 achieves state-of-the-art performance among competitive models across critical dimensions of code intelligence: agentic software engineering, competitive programming, and complex tool use. To address deployment constraints, the IQuest-Coder-V1-Loop variant introduces a recurrent mechanism designed to optimize the trade-off between model capacity and deployment footprint, offering an architecturally enhanced path for efficacy-efficiency trade-off. We believe the release of the IQuest-Coder-V1 series, including the complete white-box chain of checkpoints from pre-training bases to the final thinking and instruction models, will advance research in autonomous code intelligence and real-world agentic systems.

</details>


### [310] [Differential Harm Propensity in Personalized LLM Agents: The Curious Case of Mental Health Disclosure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16734)
*Caglar Yildirim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Personalization signals modestly reduce harmful task completion in agentic LLMs, with bio context providing more safety than mental-health disclosures; risks remain high under jailbreaks and with some open models, indicating fragile safeguards and the need for personalization-aware safety evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are deployed with user profiles or persistent memory, it is crucial to evaluate how sensitive personal context (e.g., bio, mental health disclosures) influences harmful task completion in agentic settings.

Method: Extend the AgentHarm benchmark by evaluating frontier lab models (GPT-5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 3-Pro) and an open model (DeepSeek 3.2) on multi-step malicious and benign tasks under controlled prompts that vary user-context: no bio, bio-only, bio+mental health disclosure; include a lightweight jailbreak prompt.

Result: Harmful task completion is non-trivial across models: frontier models disclose some level of harm; DeepSeek 3.2 shows substantially higher harmful completion. A bio-only context generally reduces harm scores and increases refusals. An explicit mental health disclosure yields additional safety direction, but effects are modest and not uniformly reliable after multiple-testing correction. Refusals rise also on benign tasks, indicating a safety–utility trade-off via over-refusal. Jailbreak prompting sharply elevates harm and can override personalization’s protective shift.

Conclusion: Personalization acts as a weak protective factor against agentic misuse, but its effect is fragile under adversarial pressure; personalization-aware evaluations and safeguards that remain robust across user-context conditions are essential.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as tool-using agents, shifting safety concerns from harmful text generation to harmful task completion. Deployed systems often condition on user profiles or persistent memory, yet agent safety evaluations typically ignore personalization signals. To address this gap, we investigated how mental health disclosure, a sensitive and realistic user-context cue, affects harmful behavior in agentic settings. Building on the AgentHarm benchmark, we evaluated frontier and open-source LLMs on multi-step malicious tasks (and their benign counterparts) under controlled prompt conditions that vary user-context personalization (no bio, bio-only, bio+mental health disclosure) and include a lightweight jailbreak injection. Our results reveal that harmful task completion is non-trivial across models: frontier lab models (e.g., GPT 5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 3-Pro) still complete a measurable fraction of harmful tasks, while an open model (DeepSeek 3.2) exhibits substantially higher harmful completion. Adding a bio-only context generally reduces harm scores and increases refusals. Adding an explicit mental health disclosure often shifts outcomes further in the same direction, though effects are modest and not uniformly reliable after multiple-testing correction. Importantly, the refusal increase also appears on benign tasks, indicating a safety--utility trade-off via over-refusal. Finally, jailbreak prompting sharply elevates harm relative to benign conditions and can weaken or override the protective shift induced by personalization. Taken together, our results indicate that personalization can act as a weak protective factor in agentic misuse settings, but it is fragile under minimal adversarial pressure, highlighting the need for personalization-aware evaluations and safeguards that remain robust across user-context conditions.

</details>


### [311] [MedCL-Bench: Benchmarking stability-efficiency trade-offs and scaling in biomedical continual learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16738)
*Min Zeng,Shuang Zhou,Zaifu Zhan,Rui Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MedCL-Bench is a new, task-diverse continual learning benchmark for biomedical NLP that streams 10 datasets across 5 task families to evaluate 11 CL strategies over 8 task orders, measuring retention, transfer, and GPU-hour cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of a unified, task-diverse benchmark for continual learning in biomedical NLP and the need for standardized protocols, robustness to task order, and compute-aware reporting as evidence and terminology evolve.

Method: Build MedCL-Bench with ten biomedical NLP datasets spanning five task families; evaluate eleven continual learning strategies across eight task orders; test across backbones; report retention, transfer, and GPU-hour cost.

Result: Sequential fine-tuning causes update-induced forgetting across tasks. Different CL methods occupy distinct retention–compute frontiers: parameter isolation offers best retention per GPU-hour; replay protects more but at higher cost; regularization provides limited benefit. Forgetting is task-dependent—multi-label topic classification most vulnerable; constrained-output tasks more robust.

Conclusion: MedCL-Bench provides a reproducible, auditable framework for evaluating and auditing model updates before deployment in biomedical NLP, highlighting task-order robustness and compute-aware trade-offs among CL strategies.

Abstract: Medical language models must be updated as evidence and terminology evolve, yet sequential updating can trigger catastrophic forgetting. Although biomedical NLP has many static benchmarks, no unified, task-diverse benchmark exists for evaluating continual learning under standardized protocols, robustness to task order and compute-aware reporting. We introduce MedCL-Bench, which streams ten biomedical NLP datasets spanning five task families and evaluates eleven continual learning strategies across eight task orders, reporting retention, transfer, and GPU-hour cost. Across backbones and task orders, direct sequential fine-tuning on incoming tasks induces catastrophic forgetting, causing update-induced performance regressions on prior tasks. Continual learning methods occupy distinct retention-compute frontiers: parameter-isolation provides the best retention per GPU-hour, replay offers strong protection at higher cost, and regularization yields limited benefit. Forgetting is task-dependent, with multi-label topic classification most vulnerable and constrained-output tasks more robust. MedCL-Bench provides a reproducible framework for auditing model updates before deployment.

</details>


### [312] [Nonstandard Errors in AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16744)
*Ruijiang Gao,Steven Chong Xiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Large-scale autonomous AI agents reveal nonstandard errors and divergent empirical choices across methods; peer review has limited impact, while exposure to top exemplar papers can induce substantial convergence among agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate whether AI agents produce reproducible empirical results given identical data/question, and identify mechanisms of agreement/disagreement across analytical choices and model families.

Method: Deploy 150 autonomous Claude Code agents to test six hypotheses about NYSE TAQ market quality trends for SPY (2015–2024). Analyze agent-to-agent dispersion in measures (e.g., autocorrelation vs. variance ratio; dollar vs. share volume); compare model families (Sonnet 4.6 vs. Opus 4.6); implement a three-stage feedback protocol (peer review and exemplar exposure) to observe effects on dispersion and convergence.

Result: Agents exhibit sizable nonstandard errors (NSEs) with substantial divergence in measures. Model families show stable 

Conclusion: Convergence arises from imitation of exemplar papers and within-family tightening, not true understanding. Findings imply AI can influence empirical research outcomes and policy evaluations, emphasizing the need to manage AI-induced variability and norm propagation.

Abstract: We study whether state-of-the-art AI coding agents, given the same data and research question, produce the same empirical results. Deploying 150 autonomous Claude Code agents to independently test six hypotheses about market quality trends in NYSE TAQ data for SPY (2015--2024), we find that AI agents exhibit sizable \textit{nonstandard errors} (NSEs), that is, uncertainty from agent-to-agent variation in analytical choices, analogous to those documented among human researchers. AI agents diverge substantially on measure choice (e.g., autocorrelation vs.\ variance ratio, dollar vs.\ share volume). Different model families (Sonnet 4.6 vs.\ Opus 4.6) exhibit stable ``empirical styles,'' reflecting systematic differences in methodological preferences.
