In a three-stage feedback protocol, AI peer review (written critiques) has minimal effect on dispersion, whereas exposure to top-rated exemplar papers reduces the interquartile range of estimates by 80--99\% within \textit{converging} measure families. Convergence occurs both through within-family estimation tightening and through agents switching measure families entirely, but convergence reflects imitation rather than understanding. These findings have implications for the growing use of AI in automated policy evaluation and empirical research.

</details>


### [313] [Anticipatory Planning for Multimodal AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16777)
*Yongyuan Liang,Shijie Zhou,Yu Gu,Hao Tan,Gang Wu,Franck Dernoncourt,Jihyung Kil,Ryan A. Rossi,Ruiyi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TraceR1 presents a two-stage RL framework for anticipatory trajectory reasoning in multimodal agents, leading to better planning stability, execution robustness, and generalization across seven benchmarks compared to reactive and single-stage baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal agents are largely reactive, optimizing actions in isolation and lacking long-horizon planning that aligns with global goals, which hampers planning coherence and success in complex, multi-step tasks.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) trajectory-level reinforcement learning that forecasts short-horizon action sequences with rewards enforcing global consistency; (2) grounded reinforcement fine-tuning using execution feedback from frozen tool agents to refine step-level accuracy and executability.

Result: Evaluated on seven benchmarks spanning online/offline computer-use and multimodal tool-use tasks, showing substantial improvements in planning stability, execution robustness, and generalization over reactive and single-stage baselines.

Conclusion: Anticipatory trajectory reasoning is a key principle for building multimodal agents capable of robust planning and acting in complex real-world environments.

Abstract: Recent advances in multimodal agents have improved computer-use interaction and tool-usage, yet most existing systems remain reactive, optimizing actions in isolation without reasoning about future states or long-term goals. This limits planning coherence and prevents agents from reliably solving high-level, multi-step tasks. We introduce TraceR1, a two-stage reinforcement learning framework that explicitly trains anticipatory reasoning by forecasting short-horizon trajectories before execution. The first stage performs trajectory-level reinforcement learning with rewards that enforce global consistency across predicted action sequences. The second stage applies grounded reinforcement fine-tuning, using execution feedback from frozen tool agents to refine step-level accuracy and executability. TraceR1 is evaluated across seven benchmarks, covering online computer-use, offline computer-use benchmarks, and multimodal tool-use reasoning tasks, where it achieves substantial improvements in planning stability, execution robustness, and generalization over reactive and single-stage baselines. These results show that anticipatory trajectory reasoning is a key principle for building multimodal agents that can reason, plan, and act effectively in complex real-world environments.

</details>


### [314] [Beyond Accuracy: Evaluating Forecasting Models by Multi-Echelon Inventory Cost](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16815)
*Swata Marik,Swayamjit Saha,Garga Chatterjee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A pipeline fuses forecasting and inventory optimization, using seven forecasting methods and deep sequence models, evaluated on M5 data in single- and two-echelon newsvendor settings; Temporal CNN and LSTM standout in reducing costs and boosting fill rates, with robust, scalable performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge forecasting accuracy with operational inventory decisions to reduce costs and improve service levels in modern supply chains; leverage rich M5 Walmart data to test a unified forecasting–inventory framework.

Method: Develop a unified pipeline combining traditional forecasting, ML regressors, and deep sequence models within an inventory simulation framework; evaluate seven forecasting approaches on the M5 dataset; assess operational impact in single- and two-echelon newsvendor systems; perform sensitivity and multi-echelon analyses to test robustness and scalability.

Result: Temporal CNN and LSTM models significantly reduce inventory costs and improve fill rates compared with statistical baselines; sensitivity and multi-echelon analyses indicate robustness and scalability of the approach across configurations.

Conclusion: The proposed data-driven forecasting–inventory pipeline provides a practical decision-support tool for modern supply chains, offering robust performance and scalability across echelons and forecasting horizons.

Abstract: This study develops a digitalized forecasting-inventory optimization pipeline integrating traditional forecasting models, machine learning regressors, and deep sequence models within a unified inventory simulation framework. Using the M5 Walmart dataset, we evaluate seven forecasting approaches and assess their operational impact under single- and two-echelon newsvendor systems. Results indicate that Temporal CNN and LSTM models significantly reduce inventory costs and improve fill rates compared to statistical baselines. Sensitivity and multi-echelon analyses demonstrate robustness and scalability, offering a data-driven decision-support tool for modern supply chains.

</details>


### [315] [Is Conformal Factuality for RAG-based LLMs Robust? Novel Metrics and Systematic Insights](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16817)
*Yi Chen,Daiwei Chen,Sukrut Madhav Chikodikar,Caitlyn Heqi Yin,Ramya Korlakai Vinayak*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Conformal factuality offers distribution-free reliability for RAG-LMs but often reduces usefulness and is brittle under distribution shifts; lightweight entailment verifiers can be far more efficient and competitive; overall, reliability methods must balance factuality and informativeness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs hallucinate; RAG grounds claims but lacks a statistical guarantee of correctness. Conformal factuality could provide a reliability guarantee, but its impact on task utility and robustness is unclear.

Method: Systematic analysis across generation, scoring, calibration, robustness, and efficiency; introduce new informativeness-aware metrics; evaluate on three benchmarks and multiple model families; compare LLM-based confidence scoring with entailment-based verifiers.

Result: Findings: (i) conformal filtering yields low usefulness at high factuality due to vacuous outputs; (ii) guarantees degrade under distribution shifts/distractors; calibration data must match deployment; (iii) entailment-based verifiers achieve similar or better performance with >100x fewer FLOPs than LLM-based scorers.

Conclusion: Factuality-informativeness trade-offs and fragility of conformal filtering under shifts; need new approaches prioritizing reliability with robustness and usefulness; provide actionable guidance for efficient, reliable RAG pipelines.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) frequently hallucinate, limiting their reliability in knowledge-intensive applications. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and conformal factuality have emerged as potential ways to address this limitation. While RAG aims to ground responses in retrieved evidence, it provides no statistical guarantee that the final output is correct. Conformal factuality filtering offers distribution-free statistical reliability by scoring and filtering atomic claims using a threshold calibrated on held-out data, however, the informativeness of the final output is not guaranteed. We systematically analyze the reliability and usefulness of conformal factuality for RAG-based LLMs across generation, scoring, calibration, robustness, and efficiency. We propose novel informativeness-aware metrics that better reflect task utility under conformal filtering. Across three benchmarks and multiple model families, we find that (i) conformal filtering suffers from low usefulness at high factuality levels due to vacuous outputs, (ii) conformal factuality guarantee is not robust to distribution shifts and distractors, highlighting the limitation that requires calibration data to closely match deployment conditions, and (iii) lightweight entailment-based verifiers match or outperform LLM-based model confidence scorers while requiring over $100\times$ fewer FLOPs. Overall, our results expose factuality-informativeness trade-offs and fragility of conformal filtering framework under distribution shifts and distractors, highlighting the need for new approaches for reliability with robustness and usefulness as key metrics, and provide actionable guidance for building RAG pipelines that are both reliable and computationally efficient.

</details>


### [316] [Surg$Σ$: A Spectrum of Large-Scale Multimodal Data and Foundation Models for Surgical Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16822)
*Zhitao Zeng,Mengya Xu,Jian Jiang,Pengfei Guo,Yunqiu Xu,Zhu Zhuo,Chang Han Low,Yufan He,Dong Yang,Chenxi Lin,Yiming Gu,Jiaxin Guo,Yutong Ban,Daguang Xu,Qi Dou,Yueming Jin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SurgΣ introduces SurgΣ-DB, a large-scale multimodal data foundation and associated surgical foundation models, spanning 6 specialties and 18 tasks with rich image/video annotations and hierarchical reasoning, to improve cross-task generalization and interpretability in surgical AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Surgical AI systems currently suffer from task-specific limitations and poor generalization due to fragmented, heterogeneous datasets. Large-scale multimodal foundation models show cross-task potential but are hampered by lack of systematically curated data.

Method: Develop SurgΣ-DB by consolidating open-source datasets, curated in-house collections, and web data into a unified schema; provide image- and video-level annotations across 18 tasks; cover 6 clinical specialties; incorporate hierarchical reasoning annotations; train and demonstrate surgical foundation models built on SurgΣ-DB.

Result: Demonstrates practical benefits of large-scale multimodal annotations, unified semantic design, and structured reasoning annotations for improved cross-task generalization and interpretability; includes evidence from recently developed surgical foundation models; data volume exceeds 5.98 million conversations.

Conclusion: A large-scale, standardized, multimodal surgical data backbone enables cross-task generalization and richer interpretability for surgical AI, with SurgΣ-DB and its foundation models serving as a platform to advance generalizable, interpretable surgical intelligence.

Abstract: Surgical intelligence has the potential to improve the safety and consistency of surgical care, yet most existing surgical AI frameworks remain task-specific and struggle to generalize across procedures and institutions. Although multimodal foundation models, particularly multimodal large language models, have demonstrated strong cross-task capabilities across various medical domains, their advancement in surgery remains constrained by the lack of large-scale, systematically curated multimodal data. To address this challenge, we introduce Surg$Σ$, a spectrum of large-scale multimodal data and foundation models for surgical intelligence. At the core of this framework lies Surg$Σ$-DB, a large-scale multimodal data foundation designed to support diverse surgical tasks. Surg$Σ$-DB consolidates heterogeneous surgical data sources (including open-source datasets, curated in-house clinical collections and web-source data) into a unified schema, aiming to improve label consistency and data standardization across heterogeneous datasets. Surg$Σ$-DB spans 6 clinical specialties and diverse surgical types, providing rich image- and video-level annotations across 18 practical surgical tasks covering understanding, reasoning, planning, and generation, at an unprecedented scale (over 5.98M conversations). Beyond conventional multimodal conversations, Surg$Σ$-DB incorporates hierarchical reasoning annotations, providing richer semantic cues to support deeper contextual understanding in complex surgical scenarios. We further provide empirical evidence through recently developed surgical foundation models built upon Surg$Σ$-DB, illustrating the practical benefits of large-scale multimodal annotations, unified semantic design, and structured reasoning annotations for improving cross-task generalization and interpretability.

</details>


### [317] [Prompt Programming for Cultural Bias and Alignment of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16827)
*Maksim Eren,Eric Michalak,Brian Cook,Johnny Seales*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Extends cultural alignment for LLMs by reproducing survey-based cultural metrics on open-weight models and introducing DSPy-based prompt programming to optimize cultural conditioning, finding optimization often surpasses manual culture prompts and yields more transferable, stable culturally aligned responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Culture shapes reasoning and decision-making; LLMs exhibit cultural biases that can misalign with target populations. To ensure trustworthy, relevant analyses in strategic tasks, cultural alignment is needed beyond proprietary systems. The work aims to validate a survey-grounded alignment framework on open models and improve alignment via modular, optimizable prompts.

Method: Reproduce social-science survey-based projection and distance metrics on open-weight LLMs to assess cultural skew and benefits of culture conditioning. Introduce prompt programming with DSPy to treat prompts as modular, optimizable programs and optimize against cultural-distance objectives. Compare culture-optimized prompts to culture-specific prompt engineering.

Result: Prompt optimization often improves cultural alignment over manually engineered culture prompts, suggesting DSPy-based prompt compilation offers a more stable and transferable route to culturally aligned LLM responses on open-weight models.

Conclusion: DSPy-driven prompt compilation provides a robust, transferable approach to culturally align LLM outputs across models, enhancing reproducibility and applicability beyond proprietary systems.

Abstract: Culture shapes reasoning, values, prioritization, and strategic decision-making, yet large language models (LLMs) often exhibit cultural biases that misalign with target populations. As LLMs are increasingly used for strategic decision-making, policy support, and document engineering tasks such as summarization, categorization, and compliance-oriented auditing, improving cultural alignment is important for ensuring that downstream analyses and recommendations reflect target-population value profiles rather than default model priors. Previous work introduced a survey-grounded cultural alignment framework and showed that culture-specific prompting can reduce misalignment, but it primarily evaluated proprietary models and relied on manual prompt engineering. In this paper, we validate and extend that framework by reproducing its social sciences survey based projection and distance metrics on open-weight LLMs, testing whether the same cultural skew and benefits of culture conditioning persist outside closed LLM systems. Building on this foundation, we introduce use of prompt programming with DSPy for this problem-treating prompts as modular, optimizable programs-to systematically tune cultural conditioning by optimizing against cultural-distance objectives. In our experiments, we show that prompt optimization often improves upon cultural prompt engineering, suggesting prompt compilation with DSPy can provide a more stable and transferable route to culturally aligned LLM responses.

</details>


### [318] [Learning to Present: Inverse Specification Rewards for Agentic Slide Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16839)
*Karthik Ragunath Ananda Kumar,Subrahmanyam Arunachalam*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OpenEnv RL framework for LLM-driven HTML slide generation with multi-component rewards; 0.5% parameter fine-tuning of a 7B model yields near Claude Opus 4.6 quality; releases SlideRL dataset and code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated presentation generation requires coherent content creation, visual design, and audience-aware communication. Current methods struggle with integrated planning, tool use, and holistic evaluation; the authors propose an OpenEnv-compatible RL environment with a composite reward, including an inverse-specification signal, to guide LLM agents.

Method: Develop an OpenEnv-compatible reinforcement learning environment in which LLM agents research topics, plan content, and generate professional HTML slides via tool use. Introduce a multi-component reward system: structural validation, render quality, LLM-based aesthetic scoring, content quality metrics, and an inverse specification reward (LLM attempts to recover the original specification from generated slides). Fine-tune Qwen2.5-Coder-7B via GRPO using prompts derived from expert demonstrations collected with Claude Opus 4.6 (0.5% of parameters). Evaluate on 48 business briefs across six models; open-source dataset SlideRL (288 multi-turn rollouts) and code release.

Result: The 7B model achieves 91.2% of Claude Opus 4.6's quality while surpassing the base model by 33.1%. Across six models, instruction adherence and tool-use compliance, not raw parameter count, drive agentic task performance.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that instruction and tool-use alignment can yield strong performance with small parameter updates; the inverse-specification reward provides a holistic quality signal; SlideRL dataset and accompanying code will support future research in automated presentation generation and tool-enabled LLM agents.

Abstract: Automated presentation generation remains a challenging task requiring coherent content creation, visual design, and audience-aware communication. This work proposes an OpenEnv-compatible reinforcement learning environment where LLM agents learn to research topics, plan content, and generate professional HTML slide presentations through tool use. We introduce a multi-component reward system combining structural validation, render quality assessment, LLM-based aesthetic scoring, content quality metrics, and an inverse specification reward that measures how faithfully generated slides convey their intended purpose. The inverse specification reward, an "inverse task" where an LLM attempts to recover the original specification from generated slides, provides a holistic quality signal. Our approach fine-tunes Qwen2.5-Coder-7B via GRPO, training only 0.5% of parameters on prompts derived from expert demonstrations collected using Claude Opus 4.6. Experiments on 48 diverse business briefs across six models demonstrate that our fine-tuned 7B model achieves 91.2% of Claude Opus 4.6's quality while improving 33.1% over the base model. The six-model comparison reveals that instruction adherence and tool-use compliance, rather than raw parameter count, determine agentic task performance. We contribute SlideRL, an open-source dataset of 288 multi-turn rollout trajectories across all six models: https://huggingface.co/datasets/KarthikRagunathAnandaKumar/sliderl-multi-turn-rollouts Code: https://github.com/pushing-the-frontier/slide-forge-llm

</details>


### [319] [Internalizing Agency from Reflective Experience](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16843)
*Rui Ge,Yichao Fu,Yuyang Qian,Junda Su,Yiming Zhao,Peng Zhao,Hao Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LEAFE enables feedback-grounded recovery by reflective experience, improving long-horizon agent performance beyond outcome-focused methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outcome-driven post-training methods optimize final success signals and neglect rich environmental feedback, causing distribution-sharpening and limited problem-solving capacity in long-horizon tasks.

Method: During exploration, the agent summarizes environment feedback into actionable experience, backtracks to earlier decisions, explores revised actions, and then distills corrections into the model via supervised fine-tuning.

Result: LEAFE yields consistent gains in Pass@1 and Pass@k across interactive tasks under fixed budgets, outperforming GRPO and Early Experience, with up to 14% gains on Pass@128.

Conclusion: Internalizing recovery agency from reflective experience enables more effective backtracking and corrective action, improving long-horizon, feedback-grounded agentic performance.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents that must plan, act, and recover from mistakes through long-horizon interaction with environments that provide rich feedback. However, prevailing outcome-driven post-training methods (e.g., RL with verifiable rewards) primarily optimize final success signals, leaving rich environment feedback underutilized. Consequently, they often lead to distribution sharpening: the policy becomes better at reproducing a narrow set of already-successful behaviors, while failing to improve the feedback-grounded agency needed to expand problem-solving capacity (e.g., Pass@k) in long-horizon settings.
  To address this, we propose LEAFE (Learning Feedback-Grounded Agency from Reflective Experience), a framework that internalizes recovery agency from reflective experience. Specifically, during exploration, the agent summarizes environment feedback into actionable experience, backtracks to earlier decision points, and explores alternative branches with revised actions. We then distill these experience-guided corrections into the model through supervised fine-tuning, enabling the policy to recover more effectively in future interactions. Across a diverse set of interactive coding and agentic tasks under fixed interaction budgets, LEAFE consistently improves Pass@1 over the base model and achieves higher Pass@k than outcome-driven baselines (GRPO) and experience-based methods such as Early Experience, with gains of up to 14% on Pass@128.

</details>


### [320] [SocialOmni: Benchmarking Audio-Visual Social Interactivity in Omni Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16859)
*Tianyu Xie,Jinfa Huang,Yuexiao Ma,Rongfang Luo,Yan Yang,Wang Chen,Yuhui Zeng,Ruize Fang,Yixuan Zou,Xiawu Zheng,Jiebo Luo,Rongrong Ji*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SocialOmni benchmark for evaluating social-interactive capabilities of omni-modal LLMs, focusing on speaker separation/identification, interruption timing, and interruption generation; reveals perceptual accuracy and interruption quality are decoupled; provides diagnostics to bridge perception-interaction gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address gap in evaluating conversational interactivity of OLMs, beyond static accuracy, by testing dynamic cues in real dialogues.

Method: Constructed SocialOmni with 2,000 perception samples and 209 interaction-generation instances, plus AV inconsistency scenarios; evaluated 12 leading OLMs; exams across three dimensions: who is speaking, when to interject, how to phrase interruption.

Result: Notable variance across models in social-interaction tasks; perceptual accuracy does not predict ability to generate contextually appropriate interruptions; robustness to AV inconsistencies tested; diagnostics reveal actionable signals.

Conclusion: Diagnostics can guide bridging perception-interaction gap; future OLMs can be improved by integrating perception and interaction modules; SocialOmni provides a framework for evaluating social competence in conversational AI.

Abstract: Omni-modal large language models (OLMs) redefine human-machine interaction by natively integrating audio, vision, and text. However, existing OLM benchmarks remain anchored to static, accuracy-centric tasks, leaving a critical gap in assessing social interactivity, the fundamental capacity to navigate dynamic cues in natural dialogues. To this end, we propose SocialOmni, a comprehensive benchmark that operationalizes the evaluation of this conversational interactivity across three core dimensions: (i) speaker separation and identification (who is speaking), (ii) interruption timing control (when to interject), and (iii) natural interruption generation (how to phrase the interruption). SocialOmni features 2,000 perception samples and a quality-controlled diagnostic set of 209 interaction-generation instances with strict temporal and contextual constraints, complemented by controlled audio-visual inconsistency scenarios to test model robustness. We benchmarked 12 leading OLMs, which uncovers significant variance in their social-interaction capabilities across models. Furthermore, our analysis reveals a pronounced decoupling between a model's perceptual accuracy and its ability to generate contextually appropriate interruptions, indicating that understanding-centric metrics alone are insufficient to characterize conversational social competence. More encouragingly, these diagnostics from SocialOmni yield actionable signals for bridging the perception-interaction divide in future OLMs.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [321] [You've Got a Golden Ticket: Improving Generative Robot Policies With A Single Noise Vector](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15757)
*Omkar Patil,Ondrej Biza,Thomas Weng,Karl Schmeckpeper,Wil Thomason,Xiaohan Zhang,Robin Walters,Nakul Gopalan,Sebastian Castro,Eric Rosen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Constant initial-noise inputs (golden tickets) can significantly improve pretrained generative robot policies without retraining; found via Monte-Carlo policy evaluation; improves 38/43 tasks across simulated and real-world benchmarks; enables Pareto-frontier behavior and transfer across tasks; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate how initial noise input affects performance of frozen, pretrained diffusion/flow matching policies and whether a simple, training-free method can improve downstream rewards.

Method: Use Monte-Carlo policy evaluation with the pretrained policy frozen. Inject a fixed (constant) initial noise input instead of sampling from the prior; run rollouts to obtain sparse rewards. Search for high-performing tickets by evaluating many fixed noises, without training any networks or altering the policy.

Result: Improves performance in 38 out of 43 tasks across simulation and real-world robot manipulation benchmarks. Relative success-rate gains up to 58% in simulated tasks; up to 60% improvement within 50 search episodes in real-world tasks. Tickets also yield diversity that defines Pareto frontiers across objectives and can transfer to related tasks.

Conclusion: Golden tickets provide a simple, deployable, model-free augmentation for pretrained generative policies that can yield substantial performance gains and meaningful multi-task trade-offs without additional infrastructure. The authors release code with pretrained policies and tickets for VLAs, diffusion, and flow-matching policies.

Abstract: What happens when a pretrained generative robot policy is provided a constant initial noise as input, rather than repeatedly sampling it from a Gaussian? We demonstrate that the performance of a pretrained, frozen diffusion or flow matching policy can be improved with respect to a downstream reward by swapping the sampling of initial noise from the prior distribution (typically isotropic Gaussian) with a well-chosen, constant initial noise input -- a golden ticket. We propose a search method to find golden tickets using Monte-Carlo policy evaluation that keeps the pretrained policy frozen, does not train any new networks, and is applicable to all diffusion/flow matching policies (and therefore many VLAs). Our approach to policy improvement makes no assumptions beyond being able to inject initial noise into the policy and calculate (sparse) task rewards of episode rollouts, making it deployable with no additional infrastructure or models. Our method improves the performance of policies in 38 out of 43 tasks across simulated and real-world robot manipulation benchmarks, with relative improvements in success rate by up to 58% for some simulated tasks, and 60% within 50 search episodes for real-world tasks. We also show unique benefits of golden tickets for multi-task settings: the diversity of behaviors from different tickets naturally defines a Pareto frontier for balancing different objectives (e.g., speed, success rates); in VLAs, we find that a golden ticket optimized for one task can also boost performance in other related tasks. We release a codebase with pretrained policies and golden tickets for simulation benchmarks using VLAs, diffusion policies, and flow matching policies.

</details>


### [322] [Simulation Distillation: Pretraining World Models in Simulation for Rapid Real-World Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15759)
*Jacob Levy,Tyler Westenbroek,Kevin Huang,Fernando Palafox,Patrick Yin,Shayegan Omidshafiei,Dong-Ki Kim,Abhishek Gupta,David Fridovich-Keil*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SimDist distills simulator priors into a latent world model and uses online planning plus supervised dynamics finetuning for fast sim-to-real adaptation, transferring reward/value models from simulation to enable dense planning signals without deployment-time value learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotics suffers from sim-to-real dynamics mismatch. While RL enables adaptation, exploration and long-horizon credit assignment in low-data real-world settings hinder performance.

Method: Extract structural priors from a simulator into a latent world model. Use online planning and supervised dynamics finetuning for real-world adaptation. Transfer reward and value models from simulation to provide planning signals directly from raw perception, avoiding value learning during deployment. Real-world adaptation reduces to short-horizon system identification.

Result: In both precise manipulation and quadruped locomotion tasks, SimDist substantially improves data efficiency, stability, and final performance compared to prior methods.

Conclusion: SimDist enables fast, stable sim-to-real adaptation by leveraging simulator priors and transferred reward/value models to provide planning signals without deployment-time value learning, effectively reducing the problem to short-horizon system identification and achieving strong cross-task performance.

Abstract: Simulation-to-real transfer remains a central challenge in robotics, as mismatches between simulated and real-world dynamics often lead to failures. While reinforcement learning offers a principled mechanism for adaptation, existing sim-to-real finetuning methods struggle with exploration and long-horizon credit assignment in the low-data regimes typical of real-world robotics. We introduce Simulation Distillation (SimDist), a sim-to-real framework that distills structural priors from a simulator into a latent world model and enables rapid real-world adaptation via online planning and supervised dynamics finetuning. By transferring reward and value models directly from simulation, SimDist provides dense planning signals from raw perception without requiring value learning during deployment. As a result, real-world adaptation reduces to short-horizon system identification, avoiding long-horizon credit assignment and enabling fast, stable improvement. Across precise manipulation and quadruped locomotion tasks, SimDist substantially outperforms prior methods in data efficiency, stability, and final performance. Project website and code: https://sim-dist.github.io/

</details>


### [323] [CorrectionPlanner: Self-Correction Planner with Reinforcement Learning in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15771)
*Yihong Guo,Dongqiangzi Ye,Sijia Chen,Anqi Liu,Xianming Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Autoregressive planning with a self-correction loop (Propose→Evaluate→Correct) that uses a collision critic and a self-correction trace in motion-token space; trained with imitation learning plus model-based RL on a world model; achieves safer and better planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most learning-based planners lack explicit self-correction to prevent unsafe actions, leading to unsafe trajectories even after proposed actions. A mechanism for online correction can improve safety without extensive hand-engineered constraints.

Method: At each planning step, the policy proposes a motion token. A learned collision critic predicts if the action would cause a collision within a short horizon. If a collision is predicted, the sequence of unsafe tokens becomes a self-correction trace; the next motion token is generated conditioned on this trace and the loop repeats until a safe token is found or safety criteria are met. The trace serves as a reasoning-like process in motion-token space. Training comprises imitation learning followed by model-based reinforcement learning using rollouts from a pretrained world model that captures reactive agent behavior.

Result: Closed-loop evaluations show the method reduces collision rate by over 20% on Waymax and achieves state-of-the-art planning scores on nuPlan.

Conclusion: Self-correction in motion-token space provides a concrete mechanism for online safety in planning, yielding substantial safety gains and strong planning performance in autonomous driving scenarios.

Abstract: Autonomous driving requires safe planning, but most learning-based planners lack explicit self-correction ability: once an unsafe action is proposed, there is no mechanism to correct it. Thus, we propose CorrectionPlanner, an autoregressive planner with self-correction that models planning as motion-token generation within a propose, evaluate, and correct loop. At each planning step, the policy proposes an action, namely a motion token, and a learned collision critic predicts whether it will induce a collision within a short horizon. If the critic predicts a collision, we retain the sequence of historical unsafe motion tokens as a self-correction trace, generate the next motion token conditioned on it, and repeat this process until a safe motion token is proposed or the safety criterion is met. This self-correction trace, consisting of all unsafe motion tokens, represents the planner's correction process in motion-token space, analogous to a reasoning trace in language models. We train the planner with imitation learning followed by model-based reinforcement learning using rollouts from a pretrained world model that realistically models agents' reactive behaviors. Closed-loop evaluations show that CorrectionPlanner reduces collision rate by over 20% on Waymax and achieves state-of-the-art planning scores on nuPlan.

</details>


### [324] [Emergent Dexterity via Diverse Resets and Large-Scale Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15789)
*Patrick Yin,Tyler Westenbroek,Zhengyu Zhang,Joshua Tran,Ignacio Dagnino,Eeshani Shilamkar,Numfor Mbiziwo-Tiapo,Simran Bagaria,Xinlei Liu,Galen Mullins,Andrey Kolobov,Abhishek Gupta*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A scalable, reset-driven on-policy RL framework for long-horizon dexterous manipulation that uses a single reward, fixed hyperparameters, and no curricula or demonstrations, improving exploration, robustness, and sim-to-real transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current reinforcement learning in massively parallel physics simulations is brittle and task-specific, demanding extensive engineering (rewards, curricula, demonstrations) and failing on long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation tasks with limited compute scaling.

Method: Introduce \Method, which uses simulator resets to systematically expose the RL agent to diverse robot–object interactions underlying dexterous manipulation. Resets are generated programmatically with minimal human input, converting extra compute into broader behavioral coverage under on-policy RL with fixed hyperparameters, no curricula, and no demonstrations.

Result: Demonstrates scalability to long-horizon dexterous tasks beyond prior methods, learns robust policies over wider initial conditions than baselines, and distills into visuomotor policies that exhibit robust retrying and achieve higher zero-shot real-world success rates.

Conclusion: Reset-driven exploration provides broad, scalable coverage of dexterous manipulation tasks, enabling more robust sim-to-real transfer and reducing per-task engineering requirements.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning in massively parallel physics simulations has driven major progress in sim-to-real robot learning. However, current approaches remain brittle and task-specific, relying on extensive per-task engineering to design rewards, curricula, and demonstrations. Even with this engineering, they often fail on long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation tasks and do not meaningfully scale with compute, as performance quickly saturates when training revisits the same narrow regions of state space. We introduce \Method, a simple and scalable framework that enables on-policy reinforcement learning to robustly solve a broad class of dexterous manipulation tasks using a single reward function, fixed algorithm hyperparameters, no curricula, and no human demonstrations. Our key insight is that long-horizon exploration can be dramatically simplified by using simulator resets to systematically expose the RL algorithm to the diverse set of robot-object interactions which underlie dexterous manipulation. \Method\ programmatically generates such resets with minimal human input, converting additional compute directly into broader behavioral coverage and continued performance gains. We show that \Method\ gracefully scales to long-horizon dexterous manipulation tasks beyond the capabilities of existing approaches and is able to learn robust policies over significantly wider ranges of initial conditions than baselines. Finally, we distill \Method \ into visuomotor policies which display robust retrying behavior and substantially higher success rates than baselines when transferred to the real world zero-shot. Project webpage: https://omnireset.github.io

</details>


### [325] [Robust Dynamic Object Detection in Cluttered Indoor Scenes via Learned Spatiotemporal Cues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15826)
*Juan Rached,Yixuan Jia,Kota Kondo,Jonathan P. How*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A LiDAR-only dynamic object detection framework that fuses temporal occupancy-grid motion segmentation with a learned BEV dynamic prior, improving recall and F1 in cluttered environments while maintaining precision and localization error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pure geometric LiDAR pipelines miss dynamic obstacles near static structures or partially observed objects; vision-based methods add semantic cues but are limited by closed-set detectors and camera FOV, reducing robustness to novel or out-of-frustum objects. A robust, end-to-end LiDAR-only solution is desirable.

Method: Fuse temporal occupancy-grid-based motion segmentation with a learned bird's-eye-view (BEV) dynamic prior. A fusion module prioritizes 3D detections when available and uses the learned dynamic grid to recover detections that would be lost due to proximity-induced false negatives.

Result: Compared with state-of-the-art on motion-capture ground truth, the method achieves 28.67% higher recall and 18.50% higher F1 score in substantially cluttered environments, with precision and position error comparable to the baseline.

Conclusion: A LiDAR-only framework that integrates temporal motion cues with a learned BEV prior improves detection robustness to dynamic obstacles in cluttered scenes, particularly near static structures, without sacrificing localization accuracy or precision.

Abstract: Reliable dynamic object detection in cluttered environments remains a critical challenge for autonomous navigation. Purely geometric LiDAR pipelines that rely on clustering and heuristic filtering can miss dynamic obstacles when they move in close proximity to static structure or are only partially observed. Vision-augmented approaches can provide additional semantic cues, but are often limited by closed-set detectors and camera field-of-view constraints, reducing robustness to novel obstacles and out-of-frustum events. In this work, we present a LiDAR-only framework that fuses temporal occupancy-grid-based motion segmentation with a learned bird's-eye-view (BEV) dynamic prior. A fusion module prioritizes 3D detections when available, while using the learned dynamic grid to recover detections that would otherwise be lost due to proximity-induced false negatives. Experiments with motion-capture ground truth show our method achieves 28.67% higher recall and 18.50% higher F1 score than the state-of-the-art in substantially cluttered environments while maintaining comparable precision and position error.

</details>


### [326] [Gaze-Aware Task Progression Detection Framework for Human-Robot Interaction Using RGB Cameras](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15951)
*Linlin Cheng,Koen Hindriks,Artem V. Belopolsky*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Calibration-free, RGB-only gaze-based HRI framework using a monocular camera to infer task progression with three AOIs (tablet, robot face, elsewhere); achieves 77.6% task completion detection accuracy; slightly higher interaction latency than button input but enhances comfort, social presence, and perceived naturalness; demonstrates feasibility of low-cost gaze cues in everyday HRI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable gaze-based interaction without specialized hardware, addressing practicality gaps of appearance-based gaze estimation in HRI and enabling intuitive, low-cost interfaces using standard RGB cameras.

Method: Utilizes a single monocular RGB camera (640x480) and state-of-the-art gaze estimation to monitor attention across three AOIs (tablet, robot face, elsewhere). A calibration-free framework detects task progression by natural gaze shifts (users look at the robot face to signal completion). Systematic parameter optimization balances detection accuracy and interaction latency. Evaluation conducted in a 'First Day at Work' scenario, compared against button-based interaction.

Result: Task completion detection accuracy of 77.6%. Compared with button-based interaction, the proposed method has slightly higher response latency but preserves information retention and significantly improves comfort, social presence, and perceived naturalness. Most participants did not consciously use eye movements to guide interaction, indicating gaze is a natural communicative cue.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of intuitive, low-cost, RGB-only gaze-based HRI for natural and engaging interactions. The approach shows potential to replace or augment traditional controls in everyday settings, though real-world generalization and latency trade-offs warrant further study.

Abstract: In human-robot interaction (HRI), detecting a human's gaze helps robots interpret user attention and intent. However, most gaze detection approaches rely on specialized eye-tracking hardware, limiting deployment in everyday settings. Appearance-based gaze estimation methods remove this dependency by using standard RGB cameras, but their practicality in HRI remains underexplored. We present a calibration-free framework for detecting task progression when information is conveyed via integrated display interfaces. The framework uses only the robot's built-in monocular RGB camera (640x480 resolution) and state-of-the-art gaze estimation to monitor attention patterns. It leverages natural behavior, where users shift focus from task interfaces to the robot's face to signal task completion, formalized through three Areas of Interest (AOI): tablet, robot face, and elsewhere. Systematic parameter optimization identifies configurations that balance detection accuracy and interaction latency. We validate our framework in a "First Day at Work" scenario, comparing it to button-based interaction. Results show a task completion detection accuracy of 77.6%. Compared to button-based interaction, the proposed system exhibits slightly higher response latency but preserves information retention and significantly improves comfort, social presence, and perceived naturalness. Notably, most participants reported that they did not consciously use eye movements to guide the interaction, underscoring the intuitive role of gaze as a communicative cue. This work demonstrates the feasibility of intuitive, low-cost, RGB-only gaze-based HRI for natural and engaging interactions.

</details>


### [327] [ExpertGen: Scalable Sim-to-Real Expert Policy Learning from Imperfect Behavior Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15956)
*Zifan Xu,Ran Gong,Maria Vittoria Minniti,Ahmet Salih Gundogdu,Eric Rosen,Kausik Sivakumar,Riedana Yan,Zixing Wang,Di Deng,Peter Stone,Xiaohan Zhang,Karl Schmeckpeper*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diffusion-policy-based expert prior is learned from imperfect demonstrations and frozen during RL optimization of its initial noise to steer toward high task success, enabling scalable sim-to-real transfer for robust, dexterous manipulation without reward engineering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-efficient, scalable acquisition of expert-like policies for robotics to enable robust sim-to-real transfer; large volumes of high-quality real-world demonstrations are expensive, so automated simulation-based expert learning can fill the gap.

Method: Train a diffusion-policy prior on imperfect demonstrations (which can be synthesized by LLMs or human-provided). Freeze the diffusion policy and use reinforcement learning to optimize the initial noise of the diffusion process to maximize task success with sparse rewards. This yields an expert policy that stays within human-like behavior manifolds. Then distill the state-based expert into visuomotor policies via DAgger for real hardware deployment.

Result: ExpertGen achieves 90.5% overall success on industrial assembly tasks and 85% on long-horizon manipulation tasks, outperforming baselines. Policies show dexterous control and robustness to varied initial states and failures. Sim-to-real transfer validated by distilling to visuomotor policies and deploying on real robots without reward engineering.

Conclusion: Freezing the diffusion prior regularizes exploration and enables effective learning from sparse rewards, enabling scalable, safe, and robust sim-to-real transfer of expert policies without requiring extensive reward shaping.

Abstract: Learning generalizable and robust behavior cloning policies requires large volumes of high-quality robotics data. While human demonstrations (e.g., through teleoperation) serve as the standard source for expert behaviors, acquiring such data at scale in the real world is prohibitively expensive. This paper introduces ExpertGen, a framework that automates expert policy learning in simulation to enable scalable sim-to-real transfer. ExpertGen first initializes a behavior prior using a diffusion policy trained on imperfect demonstrations, which may be synthesized by large language models or provided by humans. Reinforcement learning is then used to steer this prior toward high task success by optimizing the diffusion model's initial noise while keep original policy frozen. By keeping the pretrained diffusion policy frozen, ExpertGen regularizes exploration to remain within safe, human-like behavior manifolds, while also enabling effective learning with only sparse rewards. Empirical evaluations on challenging manipulation benchmarks demonstrate that ExpertGen reliably produces high-quality expert policies with no reward engineering. On industrial assembly tasks, ExpertGen achieves a 90.5% overall success rate, while on long-horizon manipulation tasks it attains 85% overall success, outperforming all baseline methods. The resulting policies exhibit dexterous control and remain robust across diverse initial configurations and failure states. To validate sim-to-real transfer, the learned state-based expert policies are further distilled into visuomotor policies via DAgger and successfully deployed on real robotic hardware.

</details>


### [328] [Safety Case Patterns for VLA-based driving systems: Insights from SimLingo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16013)
*Gerhard Yu,Fuyuki Ishikawa,Oluwafemi Odu,Alvine Boaye Belle*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RAISE is a safety-case design methodology for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) based driving systems that extends Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment (HARA) with instruction-specific patterns, defines safe scenarios and outcomes, and provides a design technique for safety cases; demonstrated via a SimLingo case study to support evidence-based safety claims.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA-based driving systems enable flexible, instruction-responsive driving but introduce new hazards from natural-language inputs, potentially leading to unsafe behavior. A rigorous safety case approach is needed to build trust and ensure safe operation.

Method: Introduce RAISE patterns tailored to instruction-based driving, extend HARA with safe scenarios and outcomes, and provide a design technique to construct safety cases for VLA-based driving systems; validate via a case study on SimLingo.

Result: A structured safety-case design framework that enables rigorous, evidence-based safety claims for VLA-based driving systems, demonstrated through a SimLingo case study.

Conclusion: RAISE offers a viable, evidence-based approach to safety-case design for VLA-based driving systems, aiding the development of trustworthy and safer autonomous driving technologies.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA)-based driving systems represent a significant paradigm shift in autonomous driving since, by combining traffic scene understanding, linguistic interpretation, and action generation, these systems enable more flexible, adaptive, and instruction-responsive driving behaviors. However, despite their growing adoption and potential to support socially responsible autonomous driving while understanding high-level human instructions, VLA-based driving systems may exhibit new types of hazardous behaviors. Such as the addition of natural language inputs (e.g., user or navigation instructions) into the multimodal control loop, which may lead to unpredictable and unsafe behaviors that could endanger vehicle occupants and pedestrians. Hence, assuring the safety of these systems is crucial to help build trust in their operations. To support this, we propose a novel safety case design approach called RAISE. Our approach introduces novel patterns tailored to instruction-based driving systems such as VLA-based driving systems, an extension of Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment (HARA) detailing safe scenarios and their outcomes, and a design technique to create the safety cases of VLA-based driving systems. A case study on SimLingo illustrates how our approach can be used to construct rigorous, evidence-based safety claims for this emerging class of autonomous driving systems.

</details>


### [329] [Geometry-Aligned LLM Fine-Tuning for Sequential Narrow-Opening Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16028)
*Al Jaber Mahmud,Xuan Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A two-stage geometry-aware LLM fine-tuning framework (failure-driven LoRA SFT + GRPO with geometric verification) that outputs fixed-length waypoint sequences for sequential narrow openings, achieving state-of-the-art success in simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon geometric reasoning is required to navigate multiple sequential narrow openings, since early decisions constrain later reachable configurations. The paper integrates geometry with LLM-based planning to produce coordinated, feasible sequences.

Method: Bi-level training: (1) failure-driven LoRA supervised fine-tuning on human demonstrations with structured failure feedback and enforced output format; (2) further refine LoRA adapters via Group Relative Policy Optimization with geometric verification, densifying waypoint sequences via a model-based planner and scoring with a deterministic geometry-derived reward for continuous-motion feasibility.

Result: In simulations, the method achieves the highest success rate in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution environments and qualitatively demonstrates long-horizon geometric reasoning by selecting exit poses that ease entry into subsequent openings.

Conclusion: The framework enables geometry-aligned long-horizon planning for sequential openings, leveraging LLMs augmented with failure-aware SFT and geometry-verified GRPO, with strong simulated performance and potential for broader geometry-constrained tasks.

Abstract: We study rigid-body motion planning through multiple sequential narrow openings, which requires long-horizon geometric reasoning because the configuration used to traverse an early opening constrains the set of reachable configurations for subsequent ones. To achieve this, we propose a geometry-aligned large language model (LLM) fine-tuning framework that generates fixed-length, machine-readable waypoint sequences that are both geometrically feasible and coordinated across openings. Our approach uses a bi-level training pipeline. First, we perform failure-driven LoRA supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on human demonstrations, which incorporates structured failure feedback to teach the model common failure modes and enforce the output format. Second, we refine the same LoRA adapters using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with geometric verification: each sampled waypoint sequence is densified by a model-based planner and scored with a deterministic geometry-derived reward to achieve continuous-motion feasibility. To validate the effectiveness of our proposed method, we provide both quantitative and qualitative results from simulations. Our method achieves the highest success rate in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution environments and qualitatively exhibits long-horizon geometric reasoning by selecting exit poses that facilitate entry into subsequent openings.

</details>


### [330] [Compact Optical Single-axis Joint Torque Sensor Using Redundant Photo-Reflectors and Quadratic-Programming Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16040)
*Hyun-Bin Kim,Byeong-Il Ham,Kyung-Soo Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A non-contact optical joint torque sensor uses a redundant array of photo-reflectors to measure elastic deformation, with a quadratic-program calibration to achieve high resolution and low drift, enabling precise, high-bandwidth torque control in collaborative robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current torque estimation in collaborative robots suffers from gearbox stiction/friction and current-to-torque nonlinearity, especially at low/ static torques, limiting precision and safety during physical interaction.

Method: Optically measure micro-deformation of an elastic structure using four-direction photo-reflectors arranged redundantly; calibrate with a quadratic-programming approach that exploits redundancy to suppress noise; compact form factor (96 mm diameter, 12 mm thick); validate with temperature compensation and motor-level torque/admittance control.

Result: Maximum torque error 0.083% of full scale; RMS error 0.0266 Nm for z-axis; 3-sigma resolution of 0.0224 Nm at 1 kHz without filtering; 2.14× improvement over least-squares calibration; robust to temperature-induced zero drift; improved low-torque tracking and disturbance rejection vs current-sensor-based control.

Conclusion: The proposed non-contact optical torque sensor delivers high accuracy and bandwidth with reduced drift, offering improved joint torque sensing for safe and precise robotics control, enabled by the redundant optical sensing and quadratic-program calibration.

Abstract: This study proposes a non-contact photo-reflector-based joint torque sensor for precise joint-level torque control and safe physical interaction. Current-sensor-based torque estimation in many collaborative robots suffers from poor low-torque accuracy due to gearbox stiction/friction and current-torque nonlinearity, especially near static conditions. The proposed sensor optically measures micro-deformation of an elastic structure and employs a redundant array of photo-reflectors arranged in four directions to improve sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio. We further present a quadratic-programming-based calibration method that exploits redundancy to suppress noise and enhance resolution compared to least-squares calibration. The sensor is implemented in a compact form factor (96 mm diameter, 12 mm thickness). Experiments demonstrate a maximum error of 0.083%FS and an RMS error of 0.0266 Nm for z-axis torque measurement. Calibration tests show that the proposed calibration achieves a 3 sigma resolution of 0.0224 Nm at 1 kHz without filtering, corresponding to a 2.14 times improvement over the least-squares baseline. Temperature chamber characterization and rational fitting based compensation mitigate zero drift induced by MCU self heating and motor heat. Motor-level validation via torque control and admittance control confirms improved low torque tracking and disturbance robustness relative to current-sensor-based control.

</details>


### [331] [The Era of End-to-End Autonomy: Transitioning from Rule-Based Driving to Large Driving Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16050)
*Eduardo Nebot,Julie Stephany Berrio Perez*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The abstract surveys a shift from modular, rule-based autonomous driving to end-to-end learning with large driving models (LDMs). It highlights supervised end-to-end (FSD-like) as an emerging dominant strategy, with driver role becoming safety oversight; discusses architectures, deployment strategies, safety, and broader implications for embodied AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by performance and scalability gains from end-to-end driving, the need to handle long-tail real-world scenarios, and the commercial push from major players toward supervised E2E offerings and robotaxi deployments.

Method: Qualitative analytical review of recent developments and architectures across leading vendors (Tesla FSD, Rivian, NVIDIA, etc.), assessment of deployment strategies and safety considerations, and synthesis of industry implications and future directions; includes forward-looking statements about 2026 deployments and cross-domain applicability.

Result: End-to-end learning, particularly supervised E2E (FSD-like), is reported to handle long-tail driving scenarios and become a dominant commercial strategy; the driver role shifts to safety oversight; early evidence supports E2E viability in complex environments; potential expansion to other embodied AI systems.

Conclusion: The field is transitioning toward end-to-end architectures with supervised E2E as a central product category. Anticipated deployments from 2026 and broader cross-domain applicability imply significant architectural and safety paradigm shifts, warranting further empirical validation and governance considerations.

Abstract: Autonomous driving is undergoing a shift from modular rule based pipelines toward end to end (E2E) learning systems. This paper examines this transition by tracing the evolution from classical sense perceive plan control architectures to large driving models (LDMs) capable of mapping raw sensor input directly to driving actions. We analyze recent developments including Tesla's Full Self Driving (FSD) V12 V14, Rivian's Unified Intelligence platform, NVIDIA Cosmos, and emerging commercial robotaxi deployments, focusing on architectural design, deployment strategies, safety considerations and industry implications. A key emerging product category is supervised E2E driving, often referred to as FSD (Supervised) or L2 plus plus, which several manufacturers plan to deploy from 2026 onwards. These systems can perform most of the Dynamic Driving Task (DDT) in complex environments while requiring human supervision, shifting the driver's role to safety oversight. Early operational evidence suggests E2E learning handles the long tail distribution of real world driving scenarios and is becoming a dominant commercial strategy. We also discuss how similar architectural advances may extend beyond autonomous vehicles (AV) to other embodied AI systems, including humanoid robotics.

</details>


### [332] [Ultrafast Sampling-based Kinodynamic Planning via Differential Flatness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16059)
*Thai Duong,Clayton W. Ramsey,Zachary Kingston,Wil Thomason,Lydia E. Kavraki*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A fast parallelized kinodynamic motion planning method (AkinoPDF) using differential flatness to map to a flat output space, enabling analytic time-parameterized BVP solutions and back-projection to dynamic trajectories; achieves microsecond–millisecond planning, compatible with any sampling-based planner, validated on simulations and real cluttered/dynamic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the computational bottlenecks of enforcing dynamics constraints in kinodynamic planning (solving two-point BVPs or simulating dynamics) while leveraging the speedups from parallelized sampling-based planners that are currently geometric-only.

Method: Leverage differential flatness to transform the motion planning problem from the original state space to a flat-output space. In the flat space, derive an analytical time-parameterized solution for the BVP and dynamics integration. Back-project the resulting flat-space trajectory to a closed-form dynamically feasible trajectory in the original state space. Use SIMD-style parallelization for fast validation, and ensure compatibility with any sampling-based planner.

Result: Demonstrates planning times in the microseconds to milliseconds range across simulated benchmarks and real experiments in cluttered and dynamic environments. Applicable to a broad class of differentially flat systems, including manipulators, ground and aerial vehicles, and more.

Conclusion: AkinoPDF provides a fast, exact, and broadly compatible kinodynamic planning framework by exploiting differential flatness. It addresses a key bottleneck in kinodynamic planning and enables near real-time planning for complex robots. Potential limitations include the requirement of differential flatness for the system and the complexity of real-world model mismatches.

Abstract: Motion planning under dynamics constraints, i.e., kinodynamic planning, enables safe robot operation by generating dynamically feasible trajectories that the robot can accurately track. For high-\dof robots such as manipulators, sampling-based motion planners are commonly used, especially for complex tasks in cluttered environments. However, enforcing constraints on robot dynamics in such planners requires solving either challenging two-point boundary value problems (BVPs) or propagating robot dynamics over time, both of which are computational bottlenecks that drastically increase planning times. Meanwhile, recent efforts have shown that sampling-based motion planners can generate plans in microseconds using parallelization, but are limited to geometric paths. This paper develops AkinoPDF, a fast parallelized sampling-based kinodynamic motion planning technique for a broad class of differentially flat robot systems, including manipulators, ground and aerial vehicles, and more. Differential flatness allows us to transform the motion planning problem from the original state space to a flat output space, where an analytical time-parameterized solution of the BVP and dynamics integration can be obtained. A trajectory in the flat output space is then converted back to a closed-form dynamically feasible trajectory in the original state space, enabling fast validation via ``single instruction, multiple data" parallelism. Our method is fast, exact, and compatible with any sampling-based motion planner. We extensively verify the effectiveness of our approach in both simulated benchmarks and real experiments with cluttered and dynamic environments, requiring mere microseconds to milliseconds of planning time.

</details>


### [333] [Large Reward Models: Generalizable Online Robot Reward Generation with Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16065)
*Yanru Wu,Weiduo Yuan,Ang Qi,Vitor Guizilini,Jiageng Mao,Yue Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Zero-shot, VLM-based reward generation enables online RL refinement of an IL policy for long-horizon robotic manipulation, achieving substantial sample-efficient gains within 30 iterations without manual reward engineering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of designing generalizable reward functions for RL in robotics by leveraging foundation vision-language models to provide flexible, observation-based feedback for online policy refinement.

Method: Train a large-scale VLM reward model on multi-source data (real robot trajectories, human–object interactions, diverse simulations). The VLM yields multifaceted rewards (process, completion, and temporal contrastive) from current visual observations. Start from an IL-trained base policy and perform closed-loop RL using VLM rewards in test environments (zero-shot). Evaluate on long-horizon manipulation benchmarks requiring sequential execution and precise control.

Result: The approach yields a significant boost in the initial IL policy's success rate within just 30 RL iterations, demonstrating strong sample efficiency. The VLM-based rewards provide reliable feedback that mitigates execution errors and eliminates the need for hand-crafted reward engineering.

Conclusion: VLM-generated signals can effectively guide online policy refinement in robotics, enabling efficient, reward-free engineering-driven RL and scalable improvement from IL policies in challenging tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has shown great potential in refining robotic manipulation policies, yet its efficacy remains strongly bottlenecked by the difficulty of designing generalizable reward functions. In this paper, we propose a framework for online policy refinement by adapting foundation VLMs into online reward generators. We develop a robust, scalable reward model based on a state-of-the-art VLM, trained on a large-scale, multi-source dataset encompassing real-world robot trajectories, human-object interactions, and diverse simulated environments. Unlike prior approaches that evaluate entire trajectories post-hoc, our method leverages the VLM to formulate a multifaceted reward signal comprising process, completion, and temporal contrastive rewards based on current visual observations. Initializing with a base policy trained via Imitation Learning (IL), we employ these VLM rewards to guide the model to correct sub-optimal behaviors in a closed-loop manner. We evaluate our framework on challenging long-horizon manipulation benchmarks requiring sequential execution and precise control. Crucially, our reward model operates in a purely zero-shot manner within these test environments. Experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly improves the success rate of the initial IL policy within just 30 RL iterations, demonstrating remarkable sample efficiency. This empirical evidence highlights that VLM-generated signals can provide reliable feedback to resolve execution errors, effectively eliminating the need for manual reward engineering and facilitating efficient online refinement for robot learning.

</details>


### [334] [Towards the Vision-Sound-Language-Action Paradigm: The HEAR Framework for Sound-Centric Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16086)
*Chang Nie,Tianchen Deng,Guangming Wang,Zhe Liu,Hesheng Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces Vision-Sound-Language-Action (VSLA) as a continuous, multi-modal control paradigm and HEAR framework; adds streaming audio context, temporal learning, and causal persistence; provides OpenX-Sound pretraining data and HEAR-Bench benchmark; shows sound-centric manipulation requires temporal persistence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To close the gap where audio is treated as static prompts or ignored in VLA systems, enabling real-time, sound-centric state verification and reducing missed acoustic events during action chunking.

Method: Proposes VSLA with four components: Historizer (streaming audio history), Envisioner (multi-sensory reasoning), Advancer (audio world model predicting near-future audio codes), Realizer (flow-matching policy for smooth actions); pretraining with OpenX-Sound; evaluation on HEAR-Bench with strict causal timing; leverages proprioception.

Result: Preliminary results indicate that robust sound-centric manipulation benefits from causal persistence and explicit temporal learning; demonstrates feasibility of continuous VSLA and improved perception-action alignment.

Conclusion: VSLA and HEAR constitute a practical step toward multi-sensory foundation models for embodied agents, enabling robust perception and interaction in dynamic environments; code and videos released.

Abstract: While recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have begun to incorporate audio, they typically treat sound as static pre-execution prompts or focus exclusively on human speech. This leaves a significant gap in real-time, sound-centric manipulation where fleeting environmental acoustics provide critical state verification during task execution. Consequently, key sounds are easily missed due to low-frequency updates or system latency. This problem is exacerbated by action chunking with open-loop execution, which creates a Blind Execution Interval where acoustic events are lost between discrete audio observation windows. Recognizing the necessity of continuous auditory awareness, we formalize Vision-Sound-Language-Action (VSLA) as a continuous control paradigm conditioned on vision, streaming audio, language, and proprioception under delayed decision loops. As an instantiation, we introduce HEAR, a VSLA framework integrating four components: (i) a streaming Historizer to maintain a compact, causal audio context across execution gaps; (ii) an Envisioner adapted from omni foundation models to reason over multi-sensory inputs; (iii) an Advancer, formulated as an audio world model, to learn temporal dynamics by predicting near-future audio codes; and (iv) a flow-matching Realizer policy to generate smooth action chunks. To address the scarcity of pretraining data and evaluations for VSLA, we construct OpenX-Sound for pretraining, alongside HEAR-Bench, the first sound-centric manipulation benchmark with strict causal timing rules. Our results suggest that robust sound-centric manipulation necessitates causal persistence and explicit temporal learning. This framework provides a practical step toward multi-sensory foundation models for embodied agents, enabling robots to perceive and interact with dynamic environments. Code and videos are available at https://hear.irmv.top.

</details>


### [335] [SE(3)-LIO: Smooth IMU Propagation With Jointly Distributed Poses on SE(3) Manifold for Accurate and Robust LiDAR-Inertial Odometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16118)
*Gunhee Shin,Seungjae Lee,Jei Kong,Youngwoo Seo,Hyun Myung*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes SE(3)-LIO, a LiDAR-inertial odometry framework that propagates poses on the SE(3) manifold and uses correlation-aware uncertainty in motion compensation to improve odometry accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current IMU propagation methods propagate rotation and translation separately on their own manifolds, causing rotational effects to be inadequately reflected in translation and leading to uncertain relative transformations during motion compensation.

Method: Represent and propagate the pose on the SE(3) manifold so translation propagation fully accounts for rotation; quantify the relative-transformation uncertainty by modeling correlations between predicted poses and incorporate this into the measurement noise during motion compensation.

Result: Validation on diverse datasets demonstrates effectiveness of SE(3)-LIO; source code and materials are provided at the authors' project site.

Conclusion: SE(3)-LIO provides more accurate odometry through improved IMU propagation and uncertainty-aware motion compensation, integrating propagation and compensation into a unified SE(3) framework.

Abstract: In estimating odometry accurately, an inertial measurement unit (IMU) is widely used owing to its high-rate measurements, which can be utilized to obtain motion information through IMU propagation. In this paper, we address the limitations of existing IMU propagation methods in terms of motion prediction and motion compensation. In motion prediction, the existing methods typically represent a 6-DoF pose by separating rotation and translation and propagate them on their respective manifold, so that the rotational variation is not effectively incorporated into translation propagation. During motion compensation, the relative transformation between predicted poses is used to compensate motion-induced distortion in other measurements, while inherent errors in the predicted poses introduce uncertainty in the relative transformation. To tackle these challenges, we represent and propagate the pose on SE(3) manifold, where propagated translation properly accounts for rotational variation. Furthermore, we precisely characterize the relative transformation uncertainty by considering the correlation between predicted poses, and incorporate this uncertainty into the measurement noise during motion compensation. To this end, we propose a LiDAR-inertial odometry (LIO), referred to as SE(3)-LIO, that integrates the proposed IMU propagation and uncertainty-aware motion compensation (UAMC). We validate the effectiveness of SE(3)-LIO on diverse datasets. Our source code and additional material are available at: https://se3-lio.github.io/.

</details>


### [336] [SignNav: Leveraging Signage for Semantic Visual Navigation in Large-Scale Indoor Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16166)
*Jian Sun,Yuming Huang,He Li,Shuqi Xiao,Shenyan Guo,Maani Ghaffari,Qingbiao Li,Chengzhong Xu,Hui Kong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SignNav: end-to-end embodied navigation leveraging signage semantics via a Spatial-Temporal Aware Transformer (START); introduces LSI-Dataset and DAgger-based training; achieves 80% SR and 0.74 NDTW on val-unseen; feasible in real-world without maps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans navigate using signage in novel, large-scale indoor environments; current embodied navigation lacks semantic interpretation of signage and robust reasoning under dynamic, sparse cues.

Method: Proposes START with a spatial-aware module grounding signage semantics into the physical world and a temporal-aware module capturing long-range dependencies across history and current observation. Trains end-to-end with two-stage DAgger. Builds LSI-Dataset with dynamic signage for training/evaluation. Evaluates on val-unseen and tests real-world deployment without pre-built maps.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance: 80% success rate and 0.74 NDTW on val-unseen split. Real-world deployment demonstrates practicality without predefined maps.

Conclusion: SignNav demonstrates viability of semantic hint grounding for embodied navigation in LSI environments. START effectively grounds signage semantics spatially and captures temporal dependencies, enabling robust decision-making under dynamic, sparse cues. The LSI-Dataset and DAgger training approach support progress toward real-world SignNav systems.

Abstract: Humans routinely leverage semantic hints provided by signage to navigate to destinations within novel Large-Scale Indoor (LSI) environments, such as hospitals and airport terminals. However, this capability remains underexplored within the field of embodied navigation. This paper introduces a novel embodied navigation task, SignNav, which requires the agent to interpret semantic hint from signage and reason about the subsequent action based on current observation. To facilitate research in this domain, we construct the LSI-Dataset for the training and evaluation of various SignNav agents. Dynamically changing semantic hints and sparse placement of signage in LSI environments present significant challenges to the SignNav task. To address these challenges, we propose the Spatial-Temporal Aware Transformer (START) model for end-to-end decision-making. The spatial-aware module grounds the semantic hint of signage into physical world, while the temporal-aware module captures long-range dependencies between historical states and current observation. Leveraging a two-stage training strategy with Dataset Aggregation (DAgger), our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, recording an 80% Success Rate (SR) and 0.74 NDTW on val-unseen split. Real-world deployment further demonstrates the practicality of our method in physical environment without pre-built map.

</details>


### [337] [Enforcing Task-Specified Compliance Bounds for Humanoids via Anisotropic Lipschitz-Constrained Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16180)
*Zewen He,Yoshihiko Nakamura*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Anisotropic Lipschitz-constrained policy (ALCP) for RL in humanoid locomotion maps a task-space stiffness bound to a state-dependent Lipschitz-like constraint on the policy Jacobian, enforced with a hinge-squared spectral-norm penalty; this yields direction-dependent compliance and improves stability, robustness, and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable physically interpretable and verifiable compliance in model-free RL for humanoid locomotion, addressing the limitation of scalar, global Lipschitz constraints and the misalignment with real-world stiffness requirements.

Method: Introduce ALCP that ties a task-space stiffness upper bound to a state-dependent Jacobian constraint; enforce via a hinge-squared spectral-norm penalty during RL training, enabling anisotropic (direction-dependent) compliance.

Result: ALCP improves locomotion stability and impact robustness, while reducing oscillations and energy usage in humanoid robot experiments.

Conclusion: Anisotropic, physically interpretable Lipschitz-style constraints can enhance RL-based locomotion by enabling task-aligned, direction-dependent compliance without sacrificing optimization tractability.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has demonstrated substantial potential for humanoid bipedal locomotion and the control of complex motions. To cope with oscillations and impacts induced by environmental interactions, compliant control is widely regarded as an effective remedy. However, the model-free nature of RL makes it difficult to impose task-specified and quantitatively verifiable compliance objectives, and classical model-based stiffness designs are not directly applicable.
