Lipschitz-Constrained Policies (LCP), which regularize the local sensitivity of a policy via gradient penalties, have recently been used to smooth humanoid motions. Nevertheless, existing LCP-based methods typically employ a single scalar Lipschitz budget and lack an explicit connection to physically meaningful compliance specifications in real-world systems. In this study, we propose an anisotropic Lipschitz-constrained policy (ALCP) that maps a task-space stiffness upper bound to a state-dependent Lipschitz-style constraint on the policy Jacobian. The resulting constraint is enforced during RL training via a hinge-squared spectral-norm penalty, preserving physical interpretability while enabling direction-dependent compliance. Experiments on humanoid robots show that ALCP improves locomotion stability and impact robustness, while reducing oscillations and energy usage.

</details>


### [338] [PanguMotion: Continuous Driving Motion Forecasting with Pangu Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16196)
*Quanhao Ren,Yicheng Li,Nan Song*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PanguMotion uses Pangu-1B Transformer blocks to enable continuous, temporally-aware motion forecasting by augmenting existing architectures; experiments transform independent scenes into continuous sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap where current motion forecasting treats scenes discretely, neglecting temporal continuity and historical context in real-world driving.

Method: Integrate Transformer blocks from Pangu-1B as feature enhancement modules within autonomous driving motion prediction frameworks; reassemble Argoverse 2 data via RealMotion to create continuous sequences.

Result: Experiments conducted on Argoverse 2 with RealMotion; no quantitative results reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates feasibility of leveraging LLM-based Transformers for continuous driving scenario forecasting; further results are implied but not provided in the abstract.

Abstract: Motion forecasting is a core task in autonomous driving systems, aiming to accurately predict the future trajectories of surrounding agents to ensure driving safety. Existing methods typically process discrete driving scenes independently, neglecting the temporal continuity and historical context correlations inherent in real-world driving environments. This paper proposes PanguMotion, a motion forecasting framework for continuous driving scenarios that integrates Transformer blocks from the Pangu-1B large language model as feature enhancement modules into autonomous driving motion prediction architectures. We conduct experiments on the Argoverse 2 datasets processed by the RealMotion data reorganization strategy, transforming each independent scene into a continuous sequence to mimic real-world driving scenarios.

</details>


### [339] [Enabling Dynamic Tracking in Vision-Language-Action Models via Time-Discrete and Time-Continuous Velocity Feedforward](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16218)
*Johannes Hechtl,Philipp Schmitt,Georg von Wichert,Wolfram Burgard*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Integrating velocity feedforward into vision-language-action policies for robot manipulation resolves the compliance–responsiveness trade-off. The paper proposes two model-agnostic velocity extraction methods (finite-difference and cubic B-spline) compatible with action-chunking, and shows faster execution with finite differences and high success with B-splines on a cube-in-hole task after fine-tuning a VLA model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rigid industrial robots face a trade-off between compliance (gentle, safe contact dynamics) and responsiveness (fast, accurate tracking). Traditional behavior cloning outputs discrete poses with little velocity/acceleration information, requiring high stiffness. Incorporating velocity feedforward terms can improve tracking and safety without sacrificing compliance.

Method: Two velocity targets extraction approaches from VLA policies: (1) time-discrete finite-difference to approximate velocity targets, bridging existing models; (2) continuous Cubic B-Spline action space to produce C^2 continuous trajectories suitable for high-frequency control. Both are model-agnostic and require only modifications to teleoperation, data processing, and low-level controllers; compatible with any action-chunking architecture. The π_0.5 model was fine-tuned and evaluated on a contact-rich cube-in-hole task.

Result: Finite-difference velocity targets significantly improved task execution speed; Cubic B-Spline approach yielded high overall success rates and enabled smoother higher-order derivatives without sacrificing compliance.

Conclusion: Incorporating velocity feedforward into VLA policies via these two strategies mitigates the compliance–responsiveness trade-off in compliant robots. The finite-difference method enhances speed, while the B-Spline method preserves safety and provides smoother dynamics; both are model-agnostic and practical to integrate.

Abstract: While vision-language-action (VLA) models have shown great promise for robot manipulation, their deployment on rigid industrial robots remains challenging due to the inherent trade-off between compliance and responsiveness. Standard Behavior Cloning (BC) approaches predict discrete poses at low frequencies, omitting the velocity and acceleration feedforward terms typically used by low-level compliant controllers. This requires to rely on high stiffness for accurate tracking, thereby sacrificing safe contact dynamics. In this paper, we demonstrate the importance of integrating velocity feedforward terms into VLA policies to resolve this trade-off. We propose two methods for extracting velocity targets from VLAs: a time-discrete finite-difference approximation that serves as a highly effective bridge for existing models, and a continuous Cubic B-Spline action space that natively yields $C^2$ continuous trajectories for high-frequency control. Crucially, both approaches are strictly model-agnostic and compatible with any standard action-chunking architecture, requiring modifications only to teleoperation, data processing, and the low-level controller. We fine-tune the $π_{0.5}$ model and evaluate both of our approaches on a demanding, contact-rich cube-in-hole task. Our results indicate that incorporating the velocity feedforward term via finite differences significantly improves task execution speed, while the continuous B-Spline approach maintains high overall success rates and provides a foundation for smoother higher-order derivatives without compromising compliance.

</details>


### [340] [PA-LVIO: Real-Time LiDAR-Visual-Inertial Odometry and Mapping with Pose-Only Bundle Adjustment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16228)
*Hailiang Tang,Tisheng Zhang,Liqiang Wang,Xin Ding,Man Yuan,Xiaoji Niu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Real-time pose-only LVIO (PA-LVIO) using a marginalization-free frame-to-map LiDAR model and IMU-centric online calibration to achieve accurate odometry and high-quality RGB-rendered maps; demonstrates real-time performance on PC and ARM with strong comparisons to state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time LiDAR-visual-inertial odometry and mapping is essential for navigation across robotics platforms. Existing LVIO methods face drift and calibration challenges; a real-time, accurate, and robust solution with frame-to-map constraints and pixel-level extrinsics is needed.

Method: A pose-only bundle adjustment framework (PA-LVIO) fuses LiDAR and visual data with a marginalization-free frame-to-map LiDAR measurement model. It includes IMU-centric online spatial-temporal calibration for pixel-wise LiDAR-camera alignment, yielding accurate odometry and extrinsics to build RGB-rendered maps.

Result: Extensive experiments on 28 sequences over 50+ km across wheeled robots, UAVs, and handheld devices show PA-LVIO achieves superior or comparable odometry accuracy and mapping quality to state-of-the-art LVIO methods, and runs in real-time on desktop and ARM hardware.

Conclusion: PA-LVIO effectively combines pose-only BA, marginalization-free F2M LiDAR measurements, and online calibration to deliver accurate real-time odometry and high-quality RGB-rendered maps, suitable for diverse platforms.

Abstract: Real-time LiDAR-visual-inertial odometry and mapping is crucial for navigation and planning tasks in intelligent transportation systems. This study presents a pose-only bundle adjustment (PA) LiDAR-visual-inertial odometry (LVIO), named PA-LVIO, to meet the urgent need for real-time navigation and mapping. The proposed PA framework for LiDAR and visual measurements is highly accurate and efficient, and it can derive reliable frame-to-frame constraints within multiple frames. A marginalization-free and frame-to-map (F2M) LiDAR measurement model is integrated into the state estimator to eliminate odometry drifts. Meanwhile, an IMU-centric online spatial-temporal calibration is employed to obtain a pixel-wise LiDAR-camera alignment. With accurate estimated odometry and extrinsics, a high-quality and RGB-rendered point-cloud map can be built. Comprehensive experiments are conducted on both public and private datasets collected by wheeled robot, unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV), and handheld devices with 28 sequences and more than 50 km trajectories. Sufficient results demonstrate that the proposed PA-LVIO yields superior or comparable performance to state-of-the-art LVIO methods, in terms of the odometry accuracy and mapping quality. Besides, PA-LVIO can run in real-time on both the desktop PC and the onboard ARM computer.

</details>


### [341] [Industrial cuVSLAM Benchmark & Integration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16240)
*Charbel Abi Hana,Kameel Amareen,Mohamad Mostafa,Dmitry Slepichev,Hesam Rabeti,Zheng Wang,Mihir Acharya,Anthony Rizk*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Benchmark study finds a hybrid cuVSLAM front-end and custom back-end offers the best mapping accuracy for VO/VSLAM in real-world logistics, with validation on embedded Jetson.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate and compare VO/VSLAM systems in real-world logistics environments to identify an effective stack for production robotic navigation, using ground-truth (Vicon) and LiDAR-SLAM references.

Method: Controlled trajectories (translational, rotational, mixed); real facility dataset (~1.7 km); Absolute Pose Error (APE) against Vicon and LiDAR-SLAM reference; compare multiple VO approaches; evaluate cuVSLAM front-end + custom back-end; deploy on NVIDIA Jetson.

Result: Hybrid cuVSLAM front-end + custom back-end achieves the strongest mapping accuracy; cuVSLAM recommended as core VO component in the robotics stack; demonstrated feasibility on Jetson.

Conclusion: cuVSLAM-based VO integrates well as the core component for robust, accurate mapping in real-world logistics and supports edge deployment.

Abstract: This work presents a comprehensive benchmark evaluation of visual odometry (VO) and visual SLAM (VSLAM) systems for mobile robot navigation in real-world logistical environments. We compare multiple visual odometry approaches across controlled trajectories covering translational, rotational, and mixed motion patterns, as well as a large-scale production facility dataset spanning approximately 1.7 km. Performance is evaluated using Absolute Pose Error (APE) against ground truth from a Vicon motion capture system and a LiDAR-based SLAM reference. Our results show that a hybrid stack combining the cuVSLAM front-end with a custom SLAM back-end achieves the strongest mapping accuracy, motivating a deeper integration of cuVSLAM as the core VO component in our robotics stack. We further validate this integration by deploying and testing the cuVSLAM-based VO stack on an NVIDIA Jetson platform.

</details>


### [342] [MG-Grasp: Metric-Scale Geometric 6-DoF Grasping Framework with Sparse RGB Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16270)
*Kangxu Wang,Siang Chen,Chenxing Jiang,Shaojie Shen,Yixiang Dai,Guijin Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MG-Grasp: a depth-free 6-DoF grasping framework that uses a two-view 3D foundation model to reconstruct metric-scale, multi-view consistent dense point clouds from sparse RGB images, enabling high-quality RGB-based 6-DoF grasping and achieving state-of-the-art performance on GraspNet-1Billion and real-world data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RGB-only 6-DoF grasp methods struggle with inaccurate geometry and lack reliable 3D structure, while depth sensors add hardware requirements and potential failure modes. A depth-free approach aims to provide physically reliable manipulation by recovering geometric structure from RGB data.

Method: Leverages a two-view 3D foundation model with known camera intrinsics and extrinsics to reconstruct metric-scale, multi-view-consistent dense point clouds from sparse RGB inputs, then generates stable 6-DoF grasps without relying on depth sensors.

Result: Experimental evaluation on GraspNet-1Billion and real-world scenarios shows MG-Grasp achieves state-of-the-art performance among RGB-based 6-DoF grasping methods.

Conclusion: MG-Grasp demonstrates that depth-free 6-DoF grasping is viable and competitive by reconstructing accurate 3D geometry from RGB via a two-view foundation model, enabling reliable manipulation without depth sensing; success on benchmark and real data implies practical potential, though the approach may depend on foundation model quality and accurate calibration.

Abstract: Single-view RGB-D grasp detection remains a com- mon choice in 6-DoF robotic grasping systems, which typically requires a depth sensor. While RGB-only 6-DoF grasp methods has been studied recently, their inaccurate geometric repre- sentation is not directly suitable for physically reliable robotic manipulation, thereby hindering reliable grasp generation. To address these limitations, we propose MG-Grasp, a novel depth- free 6-DoF grasping framework that achieves high-quality object grasping. Leveraging two-view 3D foundation model with camera intrinsic/extrinsic, our method reconstructs metric- scale and multi-view consistent dense point clouds from sparse RGB images and generates stable 6-DoF grasp. Experiments on GraspNet-1Billion dataset and real world demonstrate that MG-Grasp achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) grasp performance among RGB-based 6-DoF grasping methods.

</details>


### [343] [GenZ-LIO: Generalizable LiDAR-Inertial Odometry Beyond Indoor--Outdoor Boundaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16273)
*Daehan Lee,Hyungtae Lim,Seongjun Kim,Soonbin Rho,Changhyeon Lee,Sanghyun Park,Junwoo Hong,Eunseon Choi,Hyunyoung Jo,Soohee Han*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GenZ-LIO introduces scale-adaptive voxel downsampling via a PID-inspired feedback controller, a hybrid point-to-plane/point-to-point state update to mitigate degeneracy, and voxel-pruned correspondence search to reduce computation, achieving robust and efficient LiDAR-inertial odometry across indoor and outdoor environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LiDAR-inertial odometry (LIO) performance is sensitive to variations in spatial scale and point density, especially during indoor–outdoor transitions. A robust, scalable, and efficient LIO is needed that generalizes across scene scales.

Method: 1) Adaptive voxel size controlled by a PID-like feedback mechanism to drive the voxelized point count toward a scale-informed setpoint; 2) A hybrid metric for state update that fuses point-to-plane and point-to-point residuals to mitigate degeneracy; 3) A voxel-pruned correspondence search to discard non-promising voxel candidates and reduce computation.

Result: Experimental evaluation shows GenZ-LIO achieves robust odometry and improved computational efficiency across confined indoor, open outdoor, and transitional environments.

Conclusion: GenZ-LIO provides a generalizable LIO framework that remains robust to scale variations and reduces computational burden, with code to be released publicly.

Abstract: Light detection and ranging (LiDAR)-inertial odometry (LIO) enables accurate localization and mapping for autonomous navigation in various scenes. However, its performance remains sensitive to variations in spatial scale, which refers to the spatial extent of the scene reflected in the distribution of point ranges in a LiDAR scan. Transitions between confined indoor and expansive outdoor spaces induce substantial variations in point density, which may reduce robustness and computational efficiency. To address this issue, we propose GenZ-LIO, a LIO framework generalizable across both indoor and outdoor environments. GenZ-LIO comprises three key components. First, inspired by the principle of the proportional-integral-derivative (PID) controller, it adaptively regulates the voxel size for downsampling via feedback control, driving the voxelized point count toward a scale-informed setpoint while enabling stable and efficient processing across varying scene scales. Second, we formulate a hybrid-metric state update that jointly leverages point-to-plane and point-to-point residuals to mitigate LiDAR degeneracy arising from directionally insufficient geometric constraints. Third, to alleviate the computational burden introduced by point-to-point matching, we introduce a voxel-pruned correspondence search strategy that discards non-promising voxel candidates and reduces unnecessary computations. Experimental results demonstrate that GenZ-LIO achieves robust odometry estimation and improved computational efficiency across confined indoor, open outdoor, and transitional environments. Our code will be made publicly available upon publication.

</details>


### [344] [Agile Interception of a Flying Target using Competitive Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16279)
*Timothée Gavin,Simon Lacroix,Murat Bronz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Competitive reinforcement learning (PPO) for agile drone interception: an interceptor drone with a catching net, trained against an agile target in a high-fidelity quadrotor simulator with GPU-accelerated low-level control; outperforms heuristics; real-world indoor demo.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Require autonomous interception of agile drones in dynamic environments; challenge is rapid, nonlinear quadrotor dynamics and timing for catching; need robust learning-based policies transferable from simulation to real hardware.

Method: Two agents (interceptor and target) are trained with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) in a competitive setting. A high-fidelity simulation integrates realistic quadrotor dynamics and a JAX-based low-level controller for fast GPU parallelism. Training uses low-level commands (collective thrust and body rates). Evaluation compares catch rate, time to catch, and crash rate against heuristic baselines; demonstration in scaled real-world indoor arena.

Result: Trained policies outperform baselines on interception metrics (catch rate, time to catch, crash rate). Real-world indoor demonstration shows feasibility of transferring learned policies to agile drones.

Conclusion: Competitive RL with high-fidelity simulation and GPU-accelerated low-level control is effective for agile drone interception, showing promise for real-world deployment and further optimization.

Abstract: This article presents a solution to intercept an agile drone by another agile drone carrying a catching net. We formulate the interception as a Competitive Reinforcement Learning problem, where the interceptor and the target drone are controlled by separate policies trained with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). We introduce a high-fidelity simulation environment that integrates a realistic quadrotor dynamics model and a low-level control architecture implemented in JAX, which allows for fast parallelized execution on GPUs. We train the agents using low-level control, collective thrust and body rates, to achieve agile flights both for the interceptor and the target. We compare the performance of the trained policies in terms of catch rate, time to catch, and crash rate, against common heuristic baselines and show that our solution outperforms these baselines for interception of agile targets. Finally, we demonstrate the performance of the trained policies in a scaled real-world scenario using agile drones inside an indoor flight arena.

</details>


### [345] [OGScene3D: Incremental Open-Vocabulary 3D Gaussian Scene Graph Mapping for Scene Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16301)
*Siting Zhu,Ziyun Lu,Guangming Wang,Chenguang Huang,Yongbo Chen,I-Ming Chen,Wolfram Burgard,Hesheng Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OGScene3D introduces an incremental open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding pipeline that builds consistent semantic maps and scene graphs using a confidence-aware Gaussian representation, hierarchical optimization, temporal memory, and progressive graph construction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots require open-vocabulary, incrementally built 3D scene understanding. Existing methods rely on pre-built complete semantic maps, which restrict exploration and dynamic environments. A robust system should produce locally and globally consistent semantic maps with temporal integration for downstream tasks.

Method: Propose a confidence-based Gaussian semantic representation that captures predictions and reliability. Develop a hierarchical 3D semantic optimization with local correspondences and global refinement to enforce semantic consistency. Implement a long-term global optimization leveraging historical observations. Integrate 2D-3D semantic consistency with Gaussian rendering to refine the entire scene. Employ a progressive graph construction approach that dynamically creates and updates nodes and semantic relationships for continuous 3D graph building.

Result: Extensive experiments on standard datasets and real-world scenes demonstrate OGScene3D’s effectiveness in open-vocabulary scene understanding, showing improved robustness and capability to incrementally update semantic maps and scene graphs compared with baselines.

Conclusion: OGScene3D enables continuous, globally consistent 3D semantic maps and scene graphs suitable for robotic exploration and downstream tasks, by combining a probabilistic semantic representation, hierarchical optimization, temporal memory, and dynamic graph construction.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary scene understanding is crucial for robotic applications, enabling robots to comprehend complex 3D environmental contexts and supporting various downstream tasks such as navigation and manipulation. However, existing methods require pre-built complete 3D semantic maps to construct scene graphs for scene understanding, which limits their applicability in robotic scenarios where environments are explored incrementally. To address this challenge, we propose OGScene3D, an open-vocabulary scene understanding system that achieves accurate 3D semantic mapping and scene graph construction incrementally. Our system employs a confidence-based Gaussian semantic representation that jointly models semantic predictions and their reliability, enabling robust scene modeling. Building on this representation, we introduce a hierarchical 3D semantic optimization strategy that achieves semantic consistency through local correspondence establishment and global refinement, thereby constructing globally consistent semantic maps. Moreover, we design a long-term global optimization method that leverages temporal memory of historical observations to enhance semantic predictions. By integrating 2D-3D semantic consistency with Gaussian rendering contribution, this method continuously refines the semantic understanding of the entire scene.Furthermore, we develop a progressive graph construction approach that dynamically creates and updates both nodes and semantic relationships, allowing continuous updating of the 3D scene graphs. Extensive experiments on widely used datasets and real-world scenes demonstrate the effectiveness of our OGScene3D on open-vocabulary scene understanding.

</details>


### [346] [Toward Deep Representation Learning for Event-Enhanced Visual Autonomous Perception: the eAP Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16303)
*Jinghang Li,Shichao Li,Qing Lian,Peiliang Li,Xiaozhi Chen,Yi Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR:  introduces eAP, a large-scale event-camera dataset for autonomous perception, enabling event-enhanced 3D vehicle detection under challenging illumination and object TTC estimation with a geometry-aware representation learning framework, achieving 200 FPS; dataset/code/models to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation:  addresses the gap where deep visual perception struggles under challenging illumination and lacks large-scale event-camera datasets for autonomous driving; proposes eAP to facilitate study and development of event-enhanced perception tasks.

Method:  introduces the eAP dataset; demonstrates improvement of a 3D vehicle detection network with events in poor lighting; proposes a geometry-aware representation learning framework for TTC estimation that achieves 200 FPS. 

Result:  first demonstration of using event data to improve a state-of-the-art 3D vehicle detection network under challenging illumination; best-performing event-based object TTC estimation network; achieves 200 FPS; dataset and code/models to be released.

Conclusion:  eAP enables systematic study of event-based representation learning in autonomous perception, providing a valuable resource for developing robust perception systems under adverse illumination and supporting future research in 3D detection and TTC estimation.

Abstract: Recent visual autonomous perception systems achieve remarkable performances with deep representation learning. However, they fail in scenarios with challenging illumination.While event cameras can mitigate this problem, there is a lack of a large-scale dataset to develop event-enhanced deep visual perception models in autonomous driving scenes. To address the gap, we present the eAP (event-enhanced Autonomous Perception) dataset, the largest dataset with event cameras for autonomous perception. We demonstrate how eAP can facilitate the study of different autonomous perception tasks, including 3D vehicle detection and object time-to-contact (TTC) estimation, through deep representation learning. Based on eAP, we demonstrate the ffrst successful use of events to improve a popular 3D vehicle detection network in challenging illumination scenarios. eAP also enables a devoted study of the representation learning problem of object TTC estimation. We show how a geometryaware representation learning framework leads to the best eventbased object TTC estimation network that operates at 200 FPS. The dataset, code, and pre-trained models will be made publicly available for future research.

</details>


### [347] [ADAPT: Adaptive Dual-projection Architecture for Perceptive Traversal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16328)
*Shuo Shao,Tianchen Huang,Wei Gao,Shiwu Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive dual-projection perception (ADAPT) enables efficient, robust agile humanoid locomotion by using a horizontal elevation map and a vertical distance map, with a learnable sensing range that adapts to speed and clutter.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Balance perceptual fidelity with computational efficiency in 3D humanoid locomotion; existing methods rely on rigid sensing and incur high observation dimensionality.

Method: Introduce ADAPT: a dual-projection architecture using a horizontal elevation map for terrain geometry and a vertical distance map for traversable-space constraints; treat sensing range as a learnable action so the policy can expand or contract the perceptual horizon depending on speed and clutter; compare to voxel-based baselines; achieve faster training and lower compute.

Result: Drastic reduction in observation dimensionality and computational overhead compared to voxel-based baselines; significantly faster training; successful zero-shot transfer to a Unitree G1 Humanoid; robust traversal across diverse 3D environments.

Conclusion: Adaptive dual-projection perception with a learnable sensing range enables efficient, robust 3D humanoid navigation and effective transfer to hardware, outperforming fixed-range baselines.

Abstract: Agile humanoid locomotion in complex 3D en- vironments requires balancing perceptual fidelity with com- putational efficiency, yet existing methods typically rely on rigid sensing configurations. We propose ADAPT (Adaptive dual-projection architecture for perceptive traversal), which represents the environment using a horizontal elevation map for terrain geometry and a vertical distance map for traversable- space constraints. ADAPT further treats its spatial sensing range as a learnable action, enabling the policy to expand its perceptual horizon during fast motion and contract it in cluttered scenes for finer local resolution. Compared with voxel-based baselines, ADAPT drastically reduces observation dimensionality and computational overhead while substantially accelerating training. Experimentally, it achieves successful zero-shot transfer to a Unitree G1 Humanoid and signifi- cantly outperforms fixed-range baselines, yielding highly robust traversal across diverse 3D environtmental challenges.

</details>


### [348] [Faulty Coffees: Barriers to Adoption of an In-the-wild Robo-Barista](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16336)
*Bruce W. Wilson,David A. Robb,Mei Yii Lim,Helen Hastie,Matthew Peter Aylett,Theodoros Georgiou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Five-week field test of a Furhat robot in an over-50s housing complex comparing narrative vs non-narrative dialogue for a Robo-Barista; engagement remained low and practical, technical, and social factors shaped outcomes; the paper foregrounds these in-the-wild difficulties and offers longitudinal HRI lessons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate whether task-based narratives can sustain long-term engagement with a service robot in real-world, everyday settings, and to identify practical challenges in conducting longitudinal HRI research.

Method: Five-week in-situ deployment of a Furhat robot in an over-50s housing complex in Stockton, England. Residents received a free daily coffee by interacting with the robot, which operated under either a narrative or a non-narrative dialogue condition. The study foregrounded technical breakdowns, accessibility barriers, curiosity trials, and social dynamics while examining repeat interactions.

Result: Repeat engagement was low; participants exhibited curiosity trials without retention. The deployment faced multiple issues: technical breakdowns, accessibility barriers, and complex social dynamics of the housing setting, which constrained sustained use.

Conclusion: The authors highlight the in-the-wild realities of longitudinal HRI experiments and propose lessons for conducting such research, emphasizing the need to anticipate and address practical constraints, context-specific dynamics, and robustness of deployments to draw meaningful conclusions.

Abstract: We set out to study whether task-based narratives could influence long-term engagement with a service robot. To do so, we deployed a Robo-Barista for five weeks in an over-50's housing complex in Stockton, England. Residents received a free daily coffee by interacting with a Furhat robot assigned to either a narrative or non-narrative dialogue condition. Despite designing for sustained engagement, repeat interaction was low, and we encountered curiosity trials without retention, technical breakdowns, accessibility barriers, and the social dynamics of a housing complex setting. Rather than treating these as peripheral issues, we foreground them in this paper. We reflect on the in-the-wild realities of our experiment and offer lessons for conducting longitudinal Human-Robot Interaction research when studies unravel in practice.

</details>


### [349] [Encoding Predictability and Legibility for Style-Conditioned Diffusion Policy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16368)
*Adrien Jacquet Crétides,Mouad Abrini,Hamed Rahimi,Mohamed Chetouani*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A post-training diffusion-policy framework that steers trajectory generation toward legibility or efficiency via scene-conditioned modulation, enabling expressive motions only when goals are ambiguous while preserving efficiency otherwise, without retraining the base policy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Balancing expressive (legible) motion with efficiency in human-robot collaboration is essential for safety and trust; excessive legibility costs time/energy and wastes effort in low-ambiguity scenarios; a modular approach is needed that adapts to environment ambiguity without retraining core policies.

Method: Freeze a pre-trained diffusion policy; train a lightweight scene encoder and a conditioning predictor to modulate the diffusion process. At inference, use an ambiguity detector to select conditioning that emphasizes legibility in ambiguous cases or efficiency otherwise; evaluates on manipulation and navigation tasks.

Result: The approach increases legibility in ambiguous settings while maintaining optimal efficiency when legibility is unnecessary; achieves this without retraining the base policy.

Conclusion: SCDP provides a modular, post-training mechanism to trade off legibility and efficiency in HRI, adaptable across tasks; preserves the original policy performance while enabling context-driven expressive motion.

Abstract: Striking a balance between efficiency and transparent motion is a core challenge in human-robot collaboration, as highly expressive movements often incur unnecessary time and energy costs. In collaborative environments, legibility allows a human observer a better understanding of the robot's actions, increasing safety and trust. However, these behaviors result in sub-optimal and exaggerated trajectories that are redundant in low-ambiguity scenarios where the robot's goal is already obvious. To address this trade-off, we propose Style-Conditioned Diffusion Policy (SCDP), a modular framework that constrains the trajectory generation of a pre-trained diffusion model toward either legibility or efficiency based on the environment's configuration. Our method utilizes a post-training pipeline that freezes the base policy and trains a lightweight scene encoder and conditioning predictor to modulate the diffusion process. At inference time, an ambiguity detection module activates the appropriate conditioning, prioritizing expressive motion only for ambiguous goals and reverting to efficient paths otherwise. We evaluate SCDP on manipulation and navigation tasks, and results show that it enhances legibility in ambiguous settings while preserving optimal efficiency when legibility is unnecessary, all without retraining the base policy.

</details>


### [350] [Controlling Fish Schools via Reinforcement Learning of Virtual Fish Movement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16384)
*Yusuke Nishii,Hiroaki Kawashima*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Reinforcement-learning driven virtual fish can steer real fish schools toward target directions; using 2D display agents to circumvent robotic constraints, the approach demonstrates non-invasive control of collective behavior with RL policies outperforming baselines in both simulation and real experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address challenges in modeling real fish behavior and deploying physical robotic agents; investigate whether model-free RL can influence collective animal behavior via artificial, screen-displayed stimuli.

Method: Train virtual fish in a 2D simulation with model-free reinforcement learning; present learned policies to live fish via a screen; evaluate in both simulation and real-world experiments; compare against baselines including no stimulus and a 'stay-at-edge' heuristic; perform statistical analyses.

Result: In simulation, RL learns effective movement policies even when real fish frequently ignore the virtual stimulus; in real experiments, the learned policy directs schools toward specified target directions; statistical analyses show the method significantly outperforms the baselines.

Conclusion: An early demonstration that reinforcement learning can be used to influence collective animal behavior through artificial agents; suggests potential for non-invasive, scalable control of animal groups and motivates further work on policy generalization and transfer.

Abstract: This study investigates a method to guide and control fish schools using virtual fish trained with reinforcement learning. We utilize 2D virtual fish displayed on a screen to overcome technical challenges such as durability and movement constraints inherent in physical robotic agents. To address the lack of detailed behavioral models for real fish, we adopt a model-free reinforcement learning approach. First, simulation results show that reinforcement learning can acquire effective movement policies even when simulated real fish frequently ignore the virtual stimulus. Second, real-world experiments with live fish confirm that the learned policy successfully guides fish schools toward specified target directions. Statistical analysis reveals that the proposed method significantly outperforms baseline conditions, including the absence of stimulus and a heuristic "stay-at-edge" strategy. This study provides an early demonstration of how reinforcement learning can be used to influence collective animal behavior through artificial agents.

</details>


### [351] [Onboard MuJoCo-based Model Predictive Control for Shipboard Crane with Double-Pendulum Sway Suppression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16407)
*Oscar Pang,Lisa Coiffard,Paul Templier,Luke Beddow,Kamil Dreczkowski,Antoine Cully*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Real-time predictive control for maritime crane sway using MuJoCo MPC and cross-entropy planning, enabling sway damping under external perturbations on resource-constrained hardware without heavy offline learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address heavy payload sway in underactuated maritime cranes amplified by wind and waves; current methods rely on simplified models or data-hungry RL with limited generalization; need real-time predictive control on embedded hardware without extensive offline training.

Method: A real-time pipeline using MuJoCo MPC with a cross-entropy method planner to evaluate action sequences via physics-simulated rollouts. Sampling-based optimization balances dynamic target tracking and sway damping without relying on detailed analytical models; demonstrated on resource-constrained embedded hardware and robust to unmodeled changes (e.g., a second payload).

Result: Controller runs in real time on embedded hardware and outperforms PID and RL baselines in mitigating base perturbations.

Conclusion: Shows feasibility of model-based predictive control in nonlinear, underactuated maritime crane settings without extensive offline training or complex analytical models, with robustness to unmodeled discrepancies and payload variations.

Abstract: Transferring heavy payloads in maritime settings relies on efficient crane operation, limited by hazardous double-pendulum payload sway. This sway motion is further exacerbated in offshore environments by external perturbations from wind and ocean waves. Manual suppression of these oscillations on an underactuated crane system by human operators is challenging. Existing control methods struggle in such settings, often relying on simplified analytical models, while deep reinforcement learning (RL) approaches tend to generalise poorly to unseen conditions. Deploying a predictive controller onto compute-constrained, highly non-linear physical systems without relying on extensive offline training or complex analytical models remains a significant challenge. Here we show a complete real-time control pipeline centered on the MuJoCo MPC framework that leverages a cross-entropy method planner to evaluate candidate action sequences directly within a physics simulator. By using simulated rollouts, this sampling-based approach successfully reconciles the conflicting objectives of dynamic target tracking and sway damping without relying on complex analytical models. We demonstrate that the controller can run effectively on a resource-constrained embedded hardware, while outperforming traditional PID and RL baselines in counteracting external base perturbations. Furthermore, our system demonstrates robustness even when subjected to unmodeled physical discrepancies like the introduction of a second payload.

</details>


### [352] [Early-Terminable Energy-Safe Iterative Coupling for Parallel Simulation of Port-Hamiltonian Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16424)
*Qi Wei,Jianfeng Tao,Hongyu Nie,Wangtao Tan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces an energy-safe, adaptive Douglas–Rachford splitting for partitioned, port-Hamiltonian subsystems in scattering coordinates, enabling finite-iteration coupling with an explicit residual and convergence to a monolithic update as iterations increase.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finite-iteration coupling in partitioned time stepping can inject spurious energy and violate power consistency even for passive subsystems; there is a need for energy-consistent, real-time parallel simulation with controllable accuracy.

Method: Embed a Douglas–Rachford splitting scheme in wave (scattering) coordinates. Enforce lossless interconnection as an orthogonal constraint in the wave domain; each subsystem provides a discrete-time scattering port map from its one-step integrator. Prove an augmented-storage inequality under a discrete passivity condition on time steps and a mild impedance-tuning condition.

Result: Prove discrete passivity of the coupled macro-step for any finite inner-iteration budget; the residual captures the mismatch. As inner iterations grow, the scheme converges to the monolithic discrete-time update. Experiments on a coupled-oscillator benchmark validate the passivity certificates (round-off ~1e-14) and show RMS error decays monotonically with inner budget.

Conclusion: Offers a principled framework for energy-consistent, real-time parallel simulation under varying computational budgets, with adaptive accuracy–compute trade-offs and convergence to the full coupled update as computation increases.

Abstract: Parallel simulation and control of large-scale robotic systems often rely on partitioned time stepping, yet finite-iteration coupling can inject spurious energy by violating power consistency--even when each subsystem is passive. This letter proposes a novel energy-safe, early-terminable iterative coupling for port-Hamiltonian subsystems by embedding a Douglas--Rachford (DR) splitting scheme in scattering (wave) coordinates. The lossless interconnection is enforced as an orthogonal constraint in the wave domain, while each subsystem contributes a discrete-time scattering port map induced by its one-step integrator. Under a discrete passivity condition on the subsystem time steps and a mild impedance-tuning condition, we prove an augmented-storage inequality certifying discrete passivity of the coupled macro-step for any finite inner-iteration budget, with the remaining mismatch captured by an explicit residual. As the inner budget increases, the partitioned update converges to the monolithic discrete-time update induced by the same integrators, yielding a principled, adaptive accuracy--compute trade-off, supporting energy-consistent real-time parallel simulation under varying computational budgets. Experiments on a coupled-oscillator benchmark validate the passivity certificates at numerical roundoff (on the order of 10e-14 in double precision) and show that the reported RMS state error decays monotonically with increasing inner-iteration budgets, consistent with the hard-coupling limit.

</details>


### [353] [Coverage First Next Best View for Inspection of Cluttered Pipe Networks Using Mobile Manipulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16471)
*Joshua Raymond Bettles,Jiaxu Wu,Bruno Vilhena Adorno,Joaquin Carrasco,Atsushi Yamashita*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Next-best-view planning for autonomous inspection in confined environments; combines information-gain-based coverage with stochastic obstacle handling via chance-constrained vector-field-inequalities; validated on a mobile manipulator inspecting a pipe network.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce operator exposure and enable robust autonomous reconstruction and surface coverage in unknown, cluttered, and confined settings; address simultaneous exploration, mapping, and collision avoidance under measurement uncertainty.

Method: Reformulates coverage path planning as information gain maximization (next-best-view). Extends vector-field-inequalities with stochastic (chance) constraints for uncertain geometric primitives within a constrained optimal control framework. Experimental evaluation on a mobile manipulator inspecting a pipe network.

Result: The system autonomously plans and executes inspection and coverage paths to reconstruct and cover a simplified pipe network. It online-estimates geometric primitives and avoids collisions between viewpoints, demonstrating effective exploration-exploitation and safety under uncertainty.

Conclusion: The approach enables autonomous, information-driven inspection in confined environments with online primitive estimation and probabilistic collision avoidance, providing a viable path for robotic inspection in hazardous settings.

Abstract: Robotic inspection of radioactive areas enables operators to be removed from hazardous environments; however, planning and operating in confined, cluttered environments remain challenging. These systems must autonomously reconstruct the unknown environment and cover its surfaces, whilst estimating and avoiding collisions with objects in the environment. In this paper, we propose a new planning approach based on next-best-view that enables simultaneous exploration and exploitation of the environment by reformulating the coverage path planning problem in terms of information gain. To handle obstacle avoidance under uncertainty, we extend the vector-field-inequalities framework to explicitly account for stochastic measurements of geometric primitives in the environment via chance constraints in a constrained optimal control law. The stochastic constraints were evaluated experimentally alongside the planner on a mobile manipulator in a confined environment to inspect a pipe network. These experiments demonstrate that the system can autonomously plan and execute inspection and coverage paths to reconstruct and fully cover the simplified pipe network. Moreover, the system successfully estimated geometric primitives online and avoided collisions during motion between viewpoints.

</details>


### [354] [When Rolling Gets Weird: A Curved-Link Tensegrity Robot for Non-Intuitive Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16503)
*Lauren Ervin,Harish Bezawada,Vishesh Vikas*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Semi-circular curved-link tensegrity robot TeXploR2 balances rolling efficiency with stability; validated via simulations and a tetherless prototype, showing novel ground-contact control and shock absorption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome speed limitations of straight-link tensegrity and stability issues of spherical robots on unstructured terrain—relevant for space exploration.

Method: Introduce semi-circular curved-links with discontinuities at arc endpoints; internal shifting masses that roll along curved links to modulate ground contact points; build on a geometric static modeling framework; perform quasistatic simulations of piecewise continuous locomotion; fabricate a tetherless prototype for experiments; conduct initial impact tests.

Result: Discovered non-intuitive rolling behaviors; quantified positional displacement between inertial and body frames; demonstrated successful dynamic, untethered locomotion; validated shock-absorption and conformability in the tensegrity structure.

Conclusion: Dynamic model to be finalized and experimentally validated; plan to add more curved-links and additional ground contact points to improve controllability; extend testing in real-world environments.

Abstract: Conventional mobile tensegrity robots constructed with straight links offer mobility at the cost of locomotion speed. While spherical robots provide highly effective rolling behavior, they often lack the stability required for navigating unstructured terrain common in many space exploration environments. This research presents a solution with a semi-circular, curved-link tensegrity robot that strikes a balance between efficient rolling locomotion and controlled stability, enabled by discontinuities present at the arc endpoints. Building upon an existing geometric static modeling framework [1], this work presents the system design of an improved Tensegrity eXploratory Robot 2 (TeXploR2). Internal shifting masses instantaneously roll along each curved-link, dynamically altering the two points of contact with the ground plane. Simulations of quasistatic, piecewise continuous locomotion sequences reveal new insights into the positional displacement between inertial and body frames. Non-intuitive rolling behaviors are identified and experimentally validated using a tetherless prototype, demonstrating successful dynamic locomotion. A preliminary impact test highlights the tensegrity structure's inherent shock absorption capabilities and conformability. Future work will focus on finalizing a dynamic model that is experimentally validated with extended testing in real-world environments as well as further refinement of the prototype to incorporate additional curved-links and subsequent ground contact points for increased controllability.

</details>


### [355] [LIMBERO: A Limbed Climbing Exploration Robot Toward Traveling on Rocky Cliffs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16531)
*Kentaro Uno,Masazumi Imai,Kazuki Takada,Teruhiro Kataonami,Yudai Matsuura,Antonin Ringeval-Meusnier,Keita Nagaoka,Mikio Eguchi,Ryo Nishibe,Kazuya Yoshida*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A 10 kg quadrupedal climber LIMBERO uses spine-type grippers with a single-motor actuation to achieve >150 N grasp on rugged terrain, introduces a geometry-based graspability visualization, and demonstrates climbing steep rocky surfaces at 1 G, a milestone for its scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Legged climbing robots offer access to rough, inclined terrain critical for lunar/planetary exploration where wheels fail; a lightweight, high-grip gripper with integrated actuation and terrain-aware grasp planning could significantly enhance locomotion on uneven surfaces.

Method: Design of spine-type grippers with coupled finger-closing and spine-hooking motions actuated by a single motor; development of an efficient algorithm to visualize a geometry-based graspability index on continuous rough terrain; integration into LIMBERO and experimental validation on steep rocky surfaces under 1 G.

Result: Gripper achieves grasping forces >150 N while weighing 525 g; LIMBERO demonstrates stable ascent on steep rocky surfaces in 1 G, a performance not previously achieved for limbed climbing robots of this size; graspability visualization algorithm demonstrated and integrated.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that a lightweight quadrupedal climber with spine-type grippers and geometry-based grasp planning can achieve challenging climbs on rugged terrain at 1 G, advancing the capabilities of small-to-midweight legged climbing robots for planetary exploration; future work could address robustness across terrains, efficiency, and operation under reduced gravity.

Abstract: In lunar and planetary exploration, legged robots have attracted significant attention as an alternative to conventional wheeled robots, which struggle to traverse rough and uneven terrain. To enable locomotion over highly irregular and steeply inclined surfaces, limbed climbing robots equipped with grippers on their feet have emerged as a promising solution. In this paper, we present LIMBERO, a 10 kg-class quadrupedal climbing robot that employs spine-type grippers for stable locomotion and climbing on rugged and steep terrain. We first introduce a novel gripper design featuring coupled finger-closing and spine-hooking motions, tightly actuated by a single motor, which achieves exceptional grasping performance (>150 N) despite its lightweight design (525 g). Furthermore, we develop an efficient algorithm to visualize a geometry-based graspability index on continuous rough terrain. Finally, we integrate these components into LIMBERO and demonstrate its ability to ascend steep rocky surfaces under a 1 G gravity condition, a performance not previously achieved yet for limbed climbing robots of this scale.

</details>


### [356] [Kamino: GPU-based Massively Parallel Simulation of Multi-Body Systems with Challenging Topologies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16536)
*Vassilios Tsounis,Guirec Maloisel,Christian Schumacher,Ruben Grandia,Agon Serifi,David Müller,Chris Amevor,Tobias Widmer,Moritz Bächer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Kamino is a GPU-based constrained rigid-body dynamics solver integrated with Newton and Python Warp, enabling high-throughput, loop-capable, heterogeneous robotics simulations for data-driven RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing rigid-body simulators often approximate closed-loop (looped) mechanisms by tree topologies or added constraints, hindering high-fidelity, data-driven RL on complex robots with kinematic loops. There is a need for native loop-closure handling, heterogeneous batching on a single GPU, and high-fidelity contact dynamics without convexification.

Method: Kamino implements a GPU-accelerated constrained dynamics solver that formulates forward dynamics with nonlinear complementarity problems (NCP) to compute constraint forces. It natively supports kinematic loops and mimic joints, enables batched simulation of diverse robots on one GPU, and is integrated into the Newton framework via Python using NVIDIA Warp.

Result: Demonstrates reinforcement learning policy training on DR Legs (a six-loop biped) and achieves 4096 parallel environments simulated on a single GPU, yielding a feasible walking policy with high-fidelity dynamics.

Conclusion: Kamino enables high-fidelity, loop-capable, parallelized rigid-body simulations on GPUs for data-driven robotics, reducing the burden of loop-closure handling and enabling scalable RL and other data-driven methods on heterogeneous robot populations.

Abstract: We present Kamino, a GPU-based physics solver for massively parallel simulations of heterogeneous highly-coupled mechanical systems. Implemented in Python using NVIDIA Warp and integrated into the Newton framework, it enables the application of data-driven methods, such as large-scale reinforcement learning, to complex robotic systems that exhibit strongly coupled kinematic and dynamic constraints such as kinematic loops. The latter are often circumvented by practitioners; approximating the system topology as a kinematic tree and incorporating explicit loop-closure constraints or so-called mimic joints. Kamino aims at alleviating this burden by natively supporting these types of coupling. This capability facilitates high-throughput parallelized simulations that capture the true nature of mechanical systems that exploit closed kinematic chains for mechanical advantage. Moreover, Kamino supports heterogeneous worlds, allowing for batched simulation of structurally diverse robots on a single GPU. At its core lies a state-of-the-art constrained optimization algorithm that computes constraint forces by solving the constrained rigid multi-body forward dynamics transcribed as a nonlinear complementarity problem. This leads to high-fidelity simulations that can resolve contact dynamics without resorting to approximate models that simplify and/or convexify the problem. We demonstrate RL policy training on DR Legs, a biped with six nested kinematic loops, generating a feasible walking policy while simulating 4096 parallel environments on a single GPU.

</details>


### [357] [Conservative Offline Robot Policy Learning via Posterior-Transition Reweighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16542)
*Wanpeng Zhang,Hao Luo,Sipeng Zheng,Yicheng Feng,Haiweng Xu,Ziheng Xi,Chaoyi Xu,Haoqi Yuan,Zongqing Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PTR is a reward-free, conservative offline post-training method that reweights samples by a posterior over post-action targets to handle heterogeneous robot datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline post-training often uses uniform weights, which misallocates credit among demonstrations of varying quality and utility in heterogeneous data. There is a need for a principled, data-driven credit assignment that does not rely on tractable policy likelihoods.

Method: For each sample, PTR constructs a latent target from the observed post-action consequence and pools it among candidate mismatched targets. A transition scorer estimates a softmax posterior over target indices. The posterior-to-uniform ratio defines a PTR score, which is transformed into a clipped-and-mixed weight and applied to a self-normalized, weighted regression objective. This requires no tractable policy likelihood and is compatible with diffusion and flow-matching action heads.

Result: The method reallocates credit according to how attributable each sample’s post-action consequence is under the current representation, improving conservative offline adaptation to heterogeneous robot data.

Conclusion: PTR provides a principled, reward-free credit assignment mechanism for offline policy adaptation that mitigates the negative impact of low-quality or misaligned demonstrations without requiring policy likelihoods.

Abstract: Offline post-training adapts a pretrained robot policy to a target dataset by supervised regression on recorded actions. In practice, robot datasets are heterogeneous: they mix embodiments, camera setups, and demonstrations of varying quality, so many trajectories reflect recovery behavior, inconsistent operator skill, or weakly informative supervision. Uniform post-training gives equal credit to all samples and can therefore average over conflicting or low-attribution data. We propose Posterior-Transition Reweighting (PTR), a reward-free and conservative post-training method that decides how much each training sample should influence the supervised update. For each sample, PTR encodes the observed post-action consequence as a latent target, inserts it into a candidate pool of mismatched targets, and uses a separate transition scorer to estimate a softmax identification posterior over target indices. The posterior-to-uniform ratio defines the PTR score, which is converted into a clipped-and-mixed weight and applied to the original action objective through self-normalized weighted regression. This construction requires no tractable policy likelihood and is compatible with both diffusion and flow-matching action heads. Rather than uniformly trusting all recorded supervision, PTR reallocates credit according to how attributable each sample's post-action consequence is under the current representation, improving conservative offline adaptation to heterogeneous robot data.

</details>


### [358] [A Pin-Array Structured Climbing Robot for Stable Locomotion on Steep Rocky Terrain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16543)
*Keita Nagaoka,Kentaro Uno,Kazuya Yoshida*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Passive compliant pin-array grippers enable stable climbing on irregular surfaces with reduced control complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Climbing robots struggle with unpredictable surface topographies, requiring reliable ground attachment without heavy sensing or control.

Method: A mobile climbing robot is equipped with compliant pin-array grippers where each pin has a vertically split design (elastic element + metal spine) enabling mechanical interlocking with microscale surface features. The system emphasizes passive compliance and mechanical redundancy, supported by statistical modeling that identifies grasping uncertainty (variability in pin forces and contact counts). Validation includes indoor tests on inclined walls (10–30°) and outdoor tests on natural rocky terrain.

Result: Robust and stable locomotion achieved in both indoor and outdoor experiments; the design yields practical robustness while minimizing control complexity.

Conclusion: A design focused on passive compliance and mechanical redundancy provides a robust, real-world solution for climbing robots, enabling reliable attachment on irregular surfaces with reduced sensing and control requirements.

Abstract: Climbing robots face significant challenges when navigating unstructured environments, where reliable attachment to irregular surfaces is critical. We present a novel mobile climbing robot equipped with compliant pin-array structured grippers that passively conform to surface irregularities, ensuring stable ground gripping without the need for complicated sensing or control. Each pin features a vertically split design, combining an elastic element with a metal spine to enable mechanical interlocking with microscale surface features. Statistical modeling and experimental validation indicate that variability in individual pin forces and contact numbers are the primary sources of grasping uncertainty. The robot demonstrated robust and stable locomotion in indoor tests on inclined walls (10-30 degrees) and in outdoor tests on natural rocky terrain. This work highlights that a design emphasizing passive compliance and mechanical redundancy provides a practical and robust solution for real-world climbing robots while minimizing control complexity.

</details>


### [359] [ASCENT: Transformer-Based Aircraft Trajectory Prediction in Non-Towered Terminal Airspace](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16550)
*Alexander Prutsch,David Schinagl,Horst Possegger*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A lightweight transformer-based framework for real-time, multi-modal 3D aircraft trajectory forecasting in non-towered airspace, achieving state-of-the-art results on TrajAir and TartanAviation via a motion encoder and query-based decoder with parameterized outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance safety in high-density non-towered terminal airspace by providing accurate, diverse, low-latency trajectory predictions that reflect real-world traffic patterns.

Method: Transformer-based motion encoder + query-based decoder; multi-modal predictions; domain-aware 3D coordinate normalization; parameterized outputs; ablation studies.

Result: Superior performance vs baselines on TrajAir and TartanAviation; encoder captures motion dynamics; decoder aligns with structured traffic; ablations confirm contributions; low-latency real-time predictions.

Conclusion: ASCENT demonstrates efficacy for real-time trajectory prediction in non-towered terminal airspace; supports diverse maneuver hypotheses with practical latency.

Abstract: Accurate trajectory prediction can improve General Aviation safety in non-towered terminal airspace, where high traffic density increases accident risk. We present ASCENT, a lightweight transformer-based model for multi-modal 3D aircraft trajectory forecasting, which integrates domain-aware 3D coordinate normalization and parameterized predictions. ASCENT employs a transformer-based motion encoder and a query-based decoder, enabling the generation of diverse maneuver hypotheses with low latency. Experiments on the TrajAir and TartanAviation datasets demonstrate that our model outperforms prior baselines, as the encoder effectively captures motion dynamics and the decoder aligns with structured aircraft traffic patterns. Furthermore, ablation studies confirm the contributions of the decoder design, coordinate-frame modeling, and parameterized outputs. These results establish ASCENT as an effective approach for real-time aircraft trajectory prediction in non-towered terminal airspace.

</details>


### [360] [Scalable Inspection Planning via Flow-based Mixed Integer Linear Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16593)
*Adir Morgan,Kiril Solovey,Oren Salzman*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MILP reformulation with a network-flow view enables scalable, high-quality solutions for Graph Inspection Planning (GIP) using a specialized Branch-and-Cut solver; achieves tighter lower bounds and scales to very large instances (up to 15k vertices and thousands of POIs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GIP is a hard, combinatorial problem requiring POI coverage and path connectivity. Sampling-based discrete approximations exist but do not scale well and still face strong optimality gaps. A scalable exact method is needed for real-world sizes in manufacturing, medical robotics, and infrastructure inspection.

Method: Reformulate core GIP constraints as a network flow; develop MILP models that exploit flow structure; implement a specialized Branch-and-Cut solver to leverage combinatorial properties of flows; evaluate on medical, infrastructure, and synthetic large-scale instances.

Result: The approach yields substantially tighter lower bounds than prior formulations, reducing optimality gaps by 30-50% on large instances; demonstrates unprecedented scalability by delivering non-trivial solutions for problems with up to 15,000 vertices and thousands of POIs, where previous methods fail to provide meaningful guarantees.

Conclusion: This work significantly advances the state-of-the-art in GIP by delivering scalable MILP-based solutions with improved solution quality, enabling large-scale, real-world inspection planning tasks.

Abstract: Inspection planning is concerned with computing the shortest robot path to inspect a given set of points of interest (POIs) using the robot's sensors. This problem arises in a wide range of applications from manufacturing to medical robotics. To alleviate the problem's complexity, recent methods rely on sampling-based methods to obtain a more manageable (discrete) graph inspection planning (GIP) problem. Unfortunately, GIP still remains highly difficult to solve at scale as it requires simultaneously satisfying POI-coverage and path-connectivity constraints, giving rise to a challenging optimization problem, particularly at scales encountered in real-world scenarios. In this work, we present highly scalable Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) solutions for GIP that significantly advance the state-of-the-art in both runtime and solution quality. Our key insight is a reformulation of the problem's core constraints as a network flow, which enables effective MILP models and a specialized Branch-and-Cut solver that exploits the combinatorial structure of flows. We evaluate our approach on medical and infrastructure benchmarks alongside large-scale synthetic instances. Across all scenarios, our method produces substantially tighter lower bounds than existing formulations, reducing optimality gaps by 30-50% on large instances. Furthermore, our solver demonstrates unprecedented scalability: it provides non-trivial solutions for problems with up to 15,000 vertices and thousands of POIs, where prior state-of-the-art methods typically exhaust memory or fail to provide any meaningful optimality guarantees.

</details>


### [361] [Dexterous grasp data augmentation based on grasp synthesis with fingertip workspace cloud and contact-aware sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16609)
*Liqi Wu,Haoyu Jia,Kento Kawaharazuka,Hirokazu Ishida,Kei Okada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Teleoperation-based demonstration collection combined with a fingertip-contact-aware sampling-based grasp generator (FSG) and AutoWS fingertip workspace clouds enables real-time, hand-structure-agnostic grasp generation and efficient data augmentation for data-driven grasp training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-driven grasping requires large, diverse datasets, and the diversity of robot hand structures makes generalizable grasp generation difficult; there is a need for efficient data collection and IK-free pose generation.

Method: Three components: (1) teleoperation-based collection of a small set of grasp pose demonstrations; (2) FSG, a fingertip-contact-aware sampling-based grasp generator, to augment demonstrations; (3) AutoWS, which transforms demonstrated grips into structured fingertip workspace clouds embedding hand-structure info to bypass inverse kinematics.

Result: Experiments on YCB objects show the method achieves higher speed and greater valid-pose generation rate than baselines; enables real-time grasp generation for hands with arbitrary structures; yields human-like grasps when combined with demonstrations, serving as an efficient data augmentation tool for data-driven grasp training.

Conclusion: The framework provides a robust and efficient pathway for generating diverse, plausible grasp data across varying hand anatomies, facilitating faster, more generalizable data-driven grasp models.

Abstract: Robotic grasping is a fundamental yet crucial component of robotic applications, as effective grasping often serves as the starting point for various tasks. With the rapid advancement of neural networks, data-driven approaches for robotic grasping have become mainstream. However, efficiently generating grasp datasets for training remains a bottleneck. This is compounded by the diverse structures of robotic hands, making the design of generalizable grasp generation methods even more complex. In this work, we propose a teleoperation-based framework to collect a small set of grasp pose demonstrations, which are augmented using FSG--a Fingertip-contact-aware Sampling-based Grasp generator. Based on the demonstrated grasp poses, we propose AutoWS, which automatically generates structured workspace clouds of robotic fingertips, embedding the hand structure information directly into the clouds to eliminate the need for inverse kinematics calculations. Experiments on grasping the YCB objects show that our method significantly outperforms existing approaches in both speed and valid pose generation rate. Our framework enables real-time grasp generation for hands with arbitrary structures and produces human-like grasps when combined with demonstrations, providing an efficient and robust data augmentation tool for data-driven grasp training.

</details>


### [362] [Routing and Control for Marine Oil-Spill Cleanup with a Boom-Towing Vessel Fleet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16626)
*Snir Carmeli,Adir Morgan,Kiril Solovey*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A scalable multi-robot framework for oil-spill containment/cleanup using ASV duos, formulating a risk-weighted minimum-latency problem, solved by MILP with a warm-start heuristic. It includes two boom-towing controllers (feedback linearization with stability guarantee and PID) and shows simulation-based validation of tracking under coupled dynamics, enabling near-optimal planning for multiple spills on commodity hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in coordinating large robotic fleets across multiple simultaneous oil spills, enabling rapid, risk-aware response to minimize environmental damage and economic losses.

Method: Formulates multi-spill response as a risk-weighted minimum-latency problem. Solves with a hybrid optimization approach combining mixed-integer linear programming and a tailored warm-start heuristic to produce near-optimal routing for tens of spills. For execution, develops two tracking controllers for boom-towing ASV duos: a feedback-linearization controller with proven asymptotic stability and a baseline PID controller. Validation via simulations with coupled vessel-boom dynamics.

Result: Demonstrates near-optimal routing for scenarios with tens of spills within minutes on commodity hardware. Simulation results show accurate path tracking for both controllers, indicating feasibility of coordinated large-scale response.

Conclusion: Offers a scalable, holistic framework for rapid, risk-aware multi-robot oil-spill response, integrating planning and control components to handle large numbers of spills efficiently in realistic simulations.

Abstract: Marine oil spills damage ecosystems, contaminate coastlines, and disrupt food webs, while imposing substantial economic losses on fisheries and coastal communities. Prior work has demonstrated the feasibility of containing and cleaning individual spills using a duo of autonomous surface vehicles (ASVs) equipped with a towed boom and skimmers. However, existing algorithmic approaches primarily address isolated slicks and individual ASV duos, lacking scalable methods for coordinating large robotic fleets across multiple spills representative of realistic oil-spill incidents. In this work, we propose an integrated multi-robot framework for coordinated oil-spill confinement and cleanup using autonomous ASV duos. We formulate multi-spill response as a risk-weighted minimum-latency problem, where spill-specific risk factors and service times jointly determine cumulative environmental damage. To solve this problem, we develop a hybrid optimization approach combining mixed-integer linear programming, and a tailored warm-start heuristic, enabling near-optimal routing plans for scenarios with tens of spills within minutes on commodity hardware. For physical execution, we design and analyze two tracking controllers for boom-towing ASV duos: a feedback-linearization controller with proven asymptotic stability, and a baseline PID controller. Simulation results under coupled vessel-boom dynamics demonstrate accurate path tracking for both controllers. Together, these components provide a scalable, holistic framework for rapid, risk-aware multi-robot response to large-scale oil spill disasters.

</details>


### [363] [Reconciling distributed compliance with high-performance control in continuum soft robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16630)
*Vito Daniele Perfetta,Daniel Feliu Talegon,Ebrahim Shahabi,Cosimo Della Santina*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Fully continuum soft manipulator with high-bandwidth closed-loop control achieves fast, precise dynamic task execution without discretization, by combining direct-drive actuation, tendon routing for coupled bending/twisting, and reduced-order nonlinear control; reports state-of-the-art speed and millimeter precision for soft robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the prevailing view that distributed softness and dynamic precision are incompatible, and to enable high-performance control of a fully compliant, discretization-free soft arm.

Method: Integration of direct-drive actuation, a tendon routing scheme enabling coupled bending and twisting, and a structured nonlinear control architecture based on reduced-order strain modeling of underactuated systems; co-design of modeling, actuation, and control for high-bandwidth loop closure, with experimental validation.

Result: Dynamic Cartesian task execution with millimeter-scale precision; fastest reported task-execution speed among soft robots; approximately fourfold speed improvement over prior soft-robot approaches while maintaining full body softness.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that distributed compliance and high-performance dynamic control can coexist in a fully soft continuum manipulator, enabling operational capabilities approaching rigid robots without sacrificing morphological richness; supports a path toward truly soft robots with high-speed, precise manipulation.

Abstract: High-performance closed-loop control of truly soft continuum manipulators has remained elusive. Experimental demonstrations have largely relied on sufficiently stiff, piecewise architectures in which each actuated segment behaves as a distributed yet effectively rigid element, while deformation modes beyond simple bending are suppressed. This strategy simplifies modeling and control, but sidesteps the intrinsic complexity of a fully compliant body and makes the system behave as a serial kinematic chain, much like a conventional articulated robot. An implicit conclusion has consequently emerged within the community: distributed softness and dynamic precision are incompatible. Here we show this trade-off is not fundamental. We present a highly compliant, fully continuum robotic arm - without hardware discretization or stiffness-based mode suppression - that achieves fast, precise task-space convergence under dynamic conditions. The platform integrates direct-drive actuation, a tendon routing scheme enabling coupled bending and twisting, and a structured nonlinear control architecture grounded in reduced-order strain modeling of underactuated systems. Modeling, actuation, and control are co-designed to preserve essential mechanical complexity while enabling high-bandwidth loop closure. Experiments demonstrate accurate, repeatable execution of dynamic Cartesian tasks, including fast positioning and interaction. The proposed system achieves the fastest reported task-execution speed among soft robots. At millimetric precision, execution speed increases nearly fourfold compared with prior approaches, while operating on a fully compliant continuum body. These results show that distributed compliance and high-performance dynamic control can coexist, opening a path toward truly soft manipulators approaching the operational capabilities of rigid robots without sacrificing morphological richness.

</details>


### [364] [Kinema4D: Kinematic 4D World Modeling for Spatiotemporal Embodied Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16669)
*Mutian Xu,Tianbao Zhang,Tianqi Liu,Zhaoxi Chen,Xiaoguang Han,Ziwei Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Kinema4D introduces a 4D action-conditioned robotic simulator that decouples robot control from environmental reactions, using URDF-based kinematics for precise 4D robot trajectories and a 4D generative model (via pointmaps) to synthesize reactive environmental dynamics; accompanied by Robo4D-200k (≈201k episodes) with 4D annotations, enabling promising 4D, geometry-consistent, embodiment-agnostic simulations and potential zero-shot transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video-based simulators either operate in 2D or rely on static cues, failing to capture the full 4D spatiotemporal nature of robot–world interactions. A true 4D simulator with precise control and responsive environment modeling is needed for faithful embodied AI research.

Method: Disentangle interaction into two components: (1) Precise 4D robot controls generated from URDF-based kinematics to yield a 4D control trajectory; (2) Generative 4D environmental reactions by projecting the robot trajectory into a spatiotemporal signal (pointmap) and conditioning a generative model to synthesize reactive dynamics, producing synchronized RGB/pointmap sequences. Dataset Robo4D-200k with 201,426 episodes and high-quality 4D annotations.
